question: Will the United States conduct negotiations with China in the same way?
interview_answer: Yes, completely. I have strong respect for China and its leaders. Completely yes.

question: What additional political pressure or influence can you apply to help push a bill forward in the House?
interview_answer:
We have an economic analysis showing our economy would be about a trillion dollars larger if we passed immigration reform. We also have data indicating that the housing sector would be healthier if immigrants, after paying a penalty and back taxes, were able to fully participate in the housing market. We have strong indications that our technological leadership and research strength would improve if immigration reform is enacted.

We know the Senate bill enhances border security, layering new, unmatched resources on top of the unprecedented investments I’ve already made at the border. So if your top concern is border security, this is exactly the kind of bill you should be supporting. We also know the Senate bill sets up a system that holds employers accountable when they hire undocumented workers. People regularly say that’s a serious problem—and I agree. This legislation actually puts in place the enforcement mechanisms to hold those employers responsible.

So when I hear objections to immigration reform, I go down the list of issues critics raise, I compare them to what’s actually in the Senate bill, and I conclude that, on every point they say they care about, the Senate bill makes things better, not worse.

Now, some will say it doesn’t completely eliminate the problem. I don’t know of any law that solves a problem 100 percent. Social Security lifted millions of seniors out of poverty, but some seniors are still poor. The Civil Rights Act and the Voting Rights Act massively reduced discrimination, but discrimination still exists. That doesn’t make those bad laws; it just reflects the reality that almost no human problem is perfectly solvable.

What I see right now is a strong bipartisan vote emerging from the Senate. The Speaker and others have acknowledged that the House has to act, and I will press them, when they return, to actually move a bill that can pass. It doesn’t have to be word-for-word the Senate bill. I’d prefer they simply bring the Senate bill up for a vote. But if they have additional ideas, I’m confident the Senate is open to considering them. The key is: put a bill on the floor and allow a vote.

I am fully convinced that the Senate bill—which tightens border security, requires undocumented workers to pay a fine, pay a penalty, move to the back of the line, modernizes our legal immigration system, and enforces employer accountability—would pass the House if it were brought up.

So the main hurdle is not a lack of majority support in the House—just as there’s a majority in the Senate ready to back it. The real obstacle is internal Republican caucus dynamics. And that’s exactly what Americans don’t want us fixating on. They don’t want Washington consumed by partisan maneuvering; they want us to fix problems.

On this issue, there is unusually broad agreement. It’s hard to find another topic where labor unions, the Chamber of Commerce, evangelicals, students, and many others are all on the same side supporting a bill. So we should move forward and pass it. Thank you all very much.

question: Do you still believe that a renewed Cold War is the last thing Putin wants?
interview_answer:
My view is that he still does not want an all‑out war, first of all. Second, do I believe he will push the limits with the West, with the United States and NATO, as far as he can? Yes, I do. But I also believe he will pay a heavy and significant price for doing that, a price he currently underestimates, and I think he will come to regret it.

Now, as for his mindset—let me put this carefully in a public setting—I believe he’s grappling with what he sees as the greatest tragedy to befall Mother Russia: the fall of the Berlin Wall, the collapse of the empire, the loss of the near abroad, and the breakup of the Soviet Union.

Yet look at what he actually controls. He presides over a country spanning eight time zones, facing a warming tundra that will not naturally refreeze, possessing substantial oil and gas reserves, and he is trying to position Russia between China and the West in the global order.

So I’m not entirely convinced, David, that he himself is fully decided on his next move. My expectation is that he will send forces in. He has to take some kind of action.

And I have made clear to him the two main assurances he says he wants: First, that Ukraine will never join NATO. Second, that NATO—or the alliance—will not deploy strategic weapons in Ukraine. On the second demand, we might be able to reach an arrangement, depending on what he does along the Russian frontier—in the European part of Russia, along that border.

On the first demand, we have multiple international and European agreements that affirm each nation’s right to choose its own alignments. And in any case, the odds that Ukraine joins NATO in the near future are quite low, given the substantial democratic and other reforms they still need, and the question of whether key Western allies would currently back Ukraine’s entry.

So there is space for negotiation if he chooses that path. But I believe, consistent with his pattern, that his actions are going to cost him dearly—and I should probably stop there. Still, I am convinced this will damage him significantly.

question: Over the next two years, what, if anything, do you plan to do differently to alter how people view the nation’s trajectory, especially as you consider a potential 2024 presidential campaign?
interview_answer:
Nothing different, because people are only now beginning to see what we’ve actually done. As they learn more about our actions, support increases. Can you name anyone who wants us to undo our reforms that lowered prescription drug costs and push prices back up? Or anyone who wants us to abandon the new investments in roads, bridges, broadband, and similar projects? I don’t know anyone. The real issue is that the major laws we’ve passed—some with bipartisan backing—take time before people feel the impact.

Take infrastructure: we’ve approved over a trillion dollars for it, but there still aren’t that many shovels in the ground. It’s a slow process. Recently, I called to congratulate a Californian and then a newly elected congressman from Scranton, Pennsylvania. He asked if I could help secure high-speed rail between Scranton and New York City. I told him: Yes, we can. We will. That will ease travel, remove a lot of cars from the highways, and we already have more funding available now—already enacted—than we initially invested in creating Amtrak.

Another example: during the campaign, I promised we would cap insulin costs for seniors at $35 a month instead of roughly $400. But that cap doesn’t kick in until next year. So many of these measures are only just beginning to take effect. The same applies to our climate and environmental initiatives—they require time to ramp up.

So I’m not changing direction. In fact, there are areas where I intend to go further. We passed the most significant, sensible gun safety law in 30 years, with bipartisan support, but it did not include an assault weapons ban. I intend to ban assault weapons and fight hard to do it. So no, I’m not altering our course.

I ran on three core goals, and I’m sticking with them, even though I recognize that some people reasonably doubt I’m right. First, restoring the soul of the nation—bringing back decency, honor, and integrity in how we treat one another. That is beginning to take hold; public dialogue is slowly becoming more civil and respectful. Second, rebuilding the economy from the middle out and the bottom up so everyone has a fair shot. I reject trickle-down economics; very little ever reaches working people that way. Third, doing everything I can to bring the country together. It’s very difficult to remain the world’s leading democracy if you can’t foster some level of unity.

So I am not going to make any fundamental changes to my approach.

question: Is ASEAN prepared to take on the more prominent international role that Washington is encouraging it to assume?
interview_answer:
I’ve been away and am only now catching up on the media coverage, so I haven’t yet received a comprehensive briefing on Israel’s plans or on what exactly they have conveyed to our administration. That said, actions of this sort are never constructive for advancing peace talks. I’m worried that we are not seeing both sides exert the extra effort needed to achieve a real breakthrough that could finally establish a framework in which Israel is secure and able to live in peace, side by side, with a sovereign Palestinian state.

We are nevertheless going to continue working on this, because resolving the conflict is in the interest of the international community, in the interest of the Israeli people, and in the interest of the Palestinian people. But each of these step-by-step moves can erode confidence between the parties.

Although the previous question wasn’t addressed to me, I want to weigh in briefly on the matter of China. We want China to thrive and do well. It benefits the United States if China stays on its current development trajectory.

From a humanitarian standpoint, lifting millions out of poverty is inherently positive. It also creates a rapidly growing market for American products and services, so China’s prosperity and security are, from our perspective, good things. We have no desire to contain or block that progress. We want China to fulfill its development objectives.

What we do insist on is that all countries act within an agreed international framework and shared rules, recognizing mutual obligations. That applies equally to the United States, to China, to Indonesia—indeed, to all nations. The more we rely on international arrangements in which countries acknowledge not only their rights but also their duties, and commit to honoring them and holding one another to account, the better off everyone will be.

Yes, my vision for the future of our region—Asia, including East and Southeast Asia—is that it remain on a path of development, especially economic development. This region should continue to be stable, peaceful, and secure.

In that context, the regional community we are building in Asia and East Asia, and the newer architecture through the East Asia Summit, gives us a responsibility in at least one major area: ensuring that regional cooperation, particularly economic cooperation, makes a strong contribution to the global economy in ways that benefit all humanity.

On the other side of the coin, we also carry the responsibility of safeguarding stability and security in our region. I am not invoking any doctrine of one power counterbalancing another. My view is that we need a kind of dynamic equilibrium in the Asia-Pacific, including East and Southeast Asia. The creation of regional groupings like the East Asia Summit—bringing together the 10 ASEAN countries along with China, the Republic of Korea, Japan, India, Australia, New Zealand, and now Russia and the United States—strengthens my confidence that we can more effectively preserve peace, stability, and order in this part of the world.

Under those conditions, economic cooperation can proceed effectively, and Indonesia very much hopes that relations between China and the United States remain on a positive track, because any serious deterioration between those two nations would have severe consequences not only for Asian countries but for the entire world.

For that reason, we hope economic ties between the United States and China keep moving forward in a healthy way, regardless of geopolitical tensions. We also intend to help foster an East Asian, Southeast Asian, and broader Asia-Pacific region that is stable and productive.

Those are my overall views on the regional architecture and on the future of cooperation in our region. Thank you very much.

question: Are you pleased with how Vice President Harris has handled voting rights?
interview_answer: Absolutely, without question.

question: As China continues to grow, how does it define its own status and function in global affairs?
interview_answer:
Thank you for raising this. There is a broad range of fields in which China and the United States both need to, and are able to, cooperate. And as the global situation undergoes complicated, shifting, and far‑reaching changes, there will be even more areas where our two countries must work together.

In recent years, China and the United States have jointly addressed the fallout of the international financial crisis. We have also cooperated through dialogue to help tackle regional flashpoints such as the Korean Peninsula nuclear issue, the Iranian nuclear question, and the conflict in Syria, among others. We have teamed up to confront major global challenges including climate change, terrorism, and the Ebola outbreak.

The strategic importance of China–U.S. relations is growing. China regards itself as a participant in, a builder of, and a contributor to the current international order. China’s economic growth in itself is a major contribution to global efforts to overcome the financial crisis and to spur the recovery and expansion of the world economy.

China has provided more personnel for United Nations peacekeeping operations than any other permanent member of the U.N. Security Council. So far, we have dispatched over 20,000 members of our armed forces to various U.N. peacekeeping missions. Our navy has also carried out 45 escort operations in the pirate‑infested waters off the Gulf of Aden. China resolutely opposes all forms of terrorism and has consistently backed international cooperation in counterterrorism.

The Chinese people feel deep sympathy for the West African nations suffering from the Ebola epidemic. To date, we have offered four rounds of assistance totaling 750 million RMB. And right now, more than 300 Chinese medical workers are on the ground in Africa, helping the affected regions.

As China’s development continues, we will assume more and more international responsibilities that match our capabilities and standing. We will remain firmly committed to working with other countries to share the opportunities created by China’s development and to confront diverse challenges together. We will make our due contribution to safeguarding and advancing peace, stability, and development in the Asia‑Pacific and around the world.

Both I and my U.S. counterparts believe that when China and the United States cooperate, we can serve as an anchor for global stability and a driving force for world peace. China is ready to work with the United States to bolster confidence, apply our collective wisdom, and take concrete steps to deepen coordination and cooperation at the bilateral, regional, and global levels, while effectively managing sensitive differences. In this way, we can make new progress in building a new model of major‑country relations between China and the United States that serves the fundamental interests of our two peoples and people across the globe.

China and the United States are major countries with different historical and cultural traditions, social systems, and stages of development. It is therefore natural that we will not agree on every issue. But our common interests have always outweighed our differences. Both sides should respect each other’s core interests and major concerns and handle disagreements constructively through dialogue and consultation so as to preserve the overall interest of steady, healthy growth in China–U.S. relations.

I also had a frank exchange with the U.S. side on human rights. Since the founding of the People’s Republic of China, and especially over the more than three decades of reform and opening, China has made tremendous strides in human rights. This is a reality recognized by people around the world.

On human rights, we do not view our work as finished. It is always an ongoing endeavor, and there is always room to do better. China is willing to conduct human rights dialogue with the United States on the basis of equality and mutual respect, so that we can deal with our differences constructively, deepen mutual understanding, and learn from one another.

In my discussions, I also made clear that the Occupy Central movement in Hong Kong is illegal. We firmly support the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region (HKSAR) government in handling this matter in accordance with the law, in order to preserve social stability in Hong Kong and protect the lives and property of Hong Kong residents. Hong Kong affairs are purely China’s internal affairs, and foreign governments should not interfere in any way. At the same time, we will protect the lawful rights and interests of foreign nationals and businesses in Hong Kong. And I think it is self‑evident that law and order must be upheld by law everywhere, not only in Hong Kong but in other parts of the world as well.

China and the United States are both influential countries. It is entirely normal that international media will express differing views about us. I do not think we should be overly concerned about such differences of opinion. I also do not regard any regional free trade arrangement as being aimed against China. China supports open regionalism, and we believe different regional cooperation initiatives and mechanisms should interact positively with one another—and this is what we see happening now.

China safeguards our citizens’ freedom of expression and the legitimate rights and interests of media organizations in line with the law. At the same time, media outlets must abide by China’s laws and regulations. When a car breaks down on the road, we need to step out and examine where the fault lies. Likewise, when an issue is identified as a problem, there must be an underlying cause. In Chinese we say: the party who causes a problem should be the one to help resolve it. So we should look closely at the issue and see where the root cause is.

Thank you.

question: Do you continue to maintain that Al Qaida has been crippled?
interview_answer:
In that same National Defense University address I gave in May, which I mentioned earlier, I stated that the core of Al Qaida is on the defensive, that it has been severely degraded. But I also made clear that Al Qaida and other extremist groups have spread into regional offshoots that still pose serious risks. I’d point you back to that May speech, where I said explicitly that although these groups are now less likely to launch spectacular attacks on our homeland like 9/11, they do retain the ability to target our embassies. They can, in some cases, strike our commercial interests. They can generate instability and disruption in countries where security institutions are fragile. And that is exactly the pattern we’re seeing now.

So there is no contradiction in saying that the tightly organized, relatively centralized Al Qaida network that struck us on 9/11 has been shattered, is now very weak, and has limited ability to conduct complex operations, and at the same time saying that we still face regional groups, like AQAP, that are dangerous, that can, for example, drive a truck bomb into an embassy compound and kill people.

That reality means we have to pursue a strategy focused on reinforcing partners so they develop their own capability to handle what can be contained as local or regional threats if those states are stronger and have more effective counterterrorism tools and so on. It also means we must remain alert and continue to target known terrorists who may be plotting attacks or trying over time to expand their capabilities, because they will always probe for weaknesses and test what they might be able to get away with.

This is a continuing effort. We are not going to eradicate terrorism altogether. What we can do is diminish it and bolster our alliances so that it can’t present the kind of catastrophic danger we experienced on 9/11. And I’m not going to get into particular operations that have occurred. As I laid out in that May speech, I went into detail about how we decide on potential lethal actions, and I would refer you back to that explanation.

question: Will the President attempt to prevent the release of his tax returns, or permit Congress to obtain them?
interview_answer:
So, let me say again: my tax returns are currently under audit. They have been audited for a long time. They’re extremely complicated. Most people wouldn’t be able to make sense of them. They’re prepared by some of the largest and most highly regarded law firms in the United States, and the same goes for the accounting side. The accounting firm involved is a very big, very influential firm, widely respected. The law firm is first-rate—you know its reputation. These professionals assemble and submit the returns. But the reality is, most people don’t really understand tax returns.

What I did provide, though, is a financial disclosure that I believe runs over a hundred pages, which is on file. When people reviewed that document and saw how extensive it was, they were not happy, because it showed the full scale of everything. They saw the scope and the level of detail. You can actually learn much more from that disclosure than you could from a tax return. And I think we’ve now filed that disclosure three times. You get far more information from that than from any tax return.

Now, when you’re under audit—and I’m under an almost constant audit, because there are so many entities involved and the business is very large, much larger than most people realize. It’s a very successful company, but it’s big and complicated. The paperwork is probably stacked feet high. It’s a very intricate set of documents. I don’t think the public would understand them. But if the audit were completed, I would be open-minded about releasing the returns. I want to be clear: I do not want to release them while the audit is ongoing. And, in fact, most lawyers—even those who don’t represent me—often say that when you’re in the middle of an audit, you don’t expose those materials like that. You finish the audit first, and then you consider releasing them.

So once that audit is over, if it does conclude, I would definitely be willing to consider making them public.

question: I’d like to ask both of you, as national leaders, how you see the path forward on tackling climate change.
interview_answer:
To begin, I regard climate change as humanity’s core existential danger. It is the gravest challenge facing people everywhere. We must hold global warming to no more than 1.5 degrees Celsius.

Here in the United States, we’ve made major headway by focusing on three core approaches. First, we’re pushing every sector of our economy to transition so it can use and benefit from abundant, cheaper renewable power—solar, wind, hydrogen, including green hydrogen. There’s a broad spectrum of clean technologies we’re actively deploying.

Second, we’re sharply increasing conservation. I pledged that by 2030 the United States would place 30 percent of our land and our waters—including our oceans—under conservation so that they cannot be developed. We are well on track toward that goal. Those conserved areas act as carbon sinks, pulling carbon out of the atmosphere.

Third, we’ve approved substantial new funding streams to address climate impacts, not only domestically but globally. Working with the G‑7, for instance, we’re investing in climate‑friendly infrastructure across Africa—such as building the largest solar installation in Angola, and creating a rail corridor spanning the southern portion of the continent from the Atlantic to the Indian Ocean, so goods can move more cheaply and with much lower carbon emissions.

We’re collaborating with other nations to preserve their carbon sinks, so they don’t have to follow the same high‑pollution development path we did. We’re examining mechanisms to compensate countries for not exploiting critical ecosystems, including areas like the Amazon in Brazil.

At the same time, we’re sharing key technologies we’ve refined—like low‑cost solar power, which is now cheaper than fossil fuels, and advances in wind energy. Because of this, we are, in my view, on a solid trajectory to fulfill the commitments we made under the Paris Agreement. And we stand ready to share this progress with India, which has expressed a strong commitment to similar goals.

Your Excellency rightly noted that for India, the environment and climate are deeply rooted in our cultural traditions. For us, protecting nature is a matter of faith, not convenience. This is not something we do merely because it is practical; we genuinely believe in it.

We reject the idea of exploiting nature. For life on Earth to function harmoniously, we can harness nature, but we must not plunder it—and we have always held this view.

Grounded in these values, we have adopted measures not only for India’s benefit but as part of global efforts. You may be aware that among G‑20 countries, with respect to the pledges made in Paris, India is the only one that has fully met all of its commitments.

Furthermore, in the field of solar energy, we announced in Glasgow a target of achieving 500 gigawatts of renewable capacity. By 2030, we have also pledged to make Indian Railways net‑zero in emissions.

You must appreciate the immense scale of our rail network: every day, the number of passengers transported on Indian trains is roughly equivalent to the entire population of Australia. Yet we have resolved that this vast system will reach net‑zero.

We have also adopted clear targets for solar power and for ethanol—such as a 10 percent ethanol blend in fuel—which we achieved ahead of schedule.

We are advancing rapidly in green hydrogen as well. Our ambition is for India to become a global hub for green hydrogen, and we are actively building that capability.

India spearheaded the creation of the International Solar Alliance, which many countries have joined and with which we continue to work closely. We have supported island nations in expanding their solar capacity, giving them new confidence that India stands alongside them in their fight against climate change. We are determined not only to meet our own obligations, but also to assist others in this area.

We’ve seen that when natural disasters strike, the focus is rightly on the loss of life, but there is also massive damage to infrastructure. Given the intensifying crises brought on by climate change, we must create infrastructure that can withstand these shocks.

That is why we have established a global coalition known as CDRI—the Coalition for Disaster Resilient Infrastructure. And, as you may recall, in Glasgow I proposed a new global initiative, which was recently launched by the U.N. Secretary‑General and me: Mission LiFE. LiFE stands for “Lifestyle for Environment.” It calls on every individual to live in a way that is both environmentally responsible and development‑oriented. We are working vigorously to promote this way of life.

I am confident that India’s efforts in all these domains are not driven by guilt over having damaged the planet—because India has not been a major cause of global environmental harm. We are acting out of concern for our children and grandchildren. We refuse to hand future generations a world that makes life harder for them.

So, as part of our global duty, even though India has not created this problem, we are taking a leading, constructive role in the solution.

For wealthier nations, there has long been discussion about technology transfer and financial support. Some countries genuinely need access to advanced technology and climate finance. Our expectation is that once we move quickly to fulfill these commitments, we will be in a much stronger position to overcome the climate challenge.

Let me add a final point. In the United States, over the past 300 years, our model of development has caused real environmental damage. That recognition is precisely why I was able to persuade Congress to pass the most significant climate investment package in world history: $369 billion dedicated to tackling the climate crisis.

We are now deploying those funds at scale, and I believe you will see substantial progress as a result.

Thank you all very much.

question: What concrete assurances can you offer Americans that you are not trying to personally enrich yourself through the office of the Presidency?
interview_answer:
Let me be very clear: serving as President is costing me, in total foregone income and business opportunities, about three to five billion dollars. The only thing that matters to me is this country. I have zero interest in personal gain; if I did, I never would have run. People have asked, “What do you think the financial impact is?” And when you add up all the opportunities I’ve passed on, it’s enormous. I used to be paid very large sums to give speeches; now I give speeches constantly and I earn nothing for them. That’s fine with me. I also used to do major projects and highly profitable deals that I’ve stopped doing. I don’t want to pursue those anymore, because the deals I’m focused on now are deals that benefit the United States, and I consider that far more important.

Doral is inside the city of Miami. It’s a great location in an extremely successful part of Florida. Crucially, it’s about 5 minutes from a major international airport—one of the largest in the world. All of these delegations arrive with big entourages, and that access matters. My team reviewed 12 different potential venues. They were all decent, but some were 2 hours from a major airport, some 4 hours, and many had serious constraints—certain things were not permitted or available.

At Doral, there are multiple outstanding buildings—what we call bungalows. Each one has 50 to 70 high-end rooms with excellent views. There are top-tier conference facilities, great restaurants, everything you’d want for this kind of event. We wouldn’t even have to invest the same level of work that went into this site here, although they’ve done a terrific job; they really have.

Doral also offers the broader Miami area and hundreds of acres of land. That means abundant parking and all the necessary infrastructure. The ballrooms are among the largest and finest in Florida, and the facilities are essentially new. My team strongly preferred it. From my perspective, I am not going to profit from this. I firmly believe I will not make money from it. I do not want to make money from it. I am indifferent to making money here. If my goal were profit, I wouldn’t willingly absorb a loss of three to five billion dollars. At some point, I will lay out those numbers in detail.

I simply think Doral is an outstanding choice. Miami is an exceptional host city, and that property, because of how it’s laid out, allows each country to have its own villa or bungalow. And those bungalows contain many individual units, so the logistics work extremely well.

When my staff completed their site visits across the country, they toured various locations. I won’t specify them now, but you’ll see the list when they present their findings. They came back and said, “This is where we believe it should be.” The review included military officials, Secret Service personnel, and others who truly understand the operational requirements. Their focus was on security, logistics, and suitability—not on me. The decision was about selecting the best possible venue, and that’s what they concluded. Jonathan.

question: For how long can Greece realistically sustain its ties with foreign lenders?
interview_answer:
You're correct that the situation in Greece is not directly comparable to that of the United States, for several reasons that go beyond the relative size of our economies. When I took office, we were in the middle of a very deep downturn: we were shedding about 800,000 jobs every month, and the economy was shrinking more rapidly than it did at the worst point of the Great Depression. Yet we managed to step in, draw on lessons from past crises, stabilize the situation, and then return to growth.

One of the key lessons we tried to put into practice is that you have to marry structural reforms and responsible fiscal management with a strategy for growth. When your economy expands and tax revenues rise, that makes it easier to bring down debt. If, instead, you focus only on cutting spending while the economy is already shrinking, you can make the contraction worse—and that can actually increase debt.

We also had an important advantage: the dollar serves as the world’s reserve currency. Even in the depths of the crisis, investors continued to buy U.S. Treasuries. And we weren’t tied into a larger monetary framework like the euro area, which gave us more room to maneuver.

The main takeaway from our experience—and it’s true that our recovery was faster and stronger than in much of Europe—is that, especially when an economy is still weak, putting people back to work and stimulating economic activity can, over time, help reduce structural deficits and overall debt levels.

I believe Greece is now following the right course. You’ve undertaken some very tough structural changes. I recognize that this has been hard on the Greek people—that it’s painful, and that the politics around it are very difficult—but in a globalized economy, Greece was inevitably going to have to make structural adjustments. Every country does. The United States has had to reform our education system, modernize our infrastructure, revisit regulations that failed to rein in excesses on Wall Street, and push through health care reform. We had to pursue a whole series of structural changes.

They’re not identical to what Greece has had to do, but they were necessary. The fact that the Prime Minister and his government have been willing to advance these reforms should make Greece more competitive and more attractive to investors over the long term. The Greek people are enterprising, and this country has significant assets. My hope is that more investors around the world will recognize the opportunities that exist here.

At the same time, even with those reforms, our consistent argument has been that, after such a sharp collapse and with unemployment so high, you also need a growth strategy alongside fiscal and structural measures. And it is very hard to see how you get the scale of growth required without some form of debt relief.

The politics of this inside Europe are complicated. And to be fair to some of the northern European governments, which I know are not always well‑liked here, they also face domestic political pressures. Their publics and institutions are often skeptical of certain debt‑relief arrangements.

But given that Greece has already embarked on many of these difficult reforms, has demonstrated a willingness to change, and that the Greek people have endured serious sacrifices for many years, there ought now to be a chance for all sides to accept that a lasting, long‑term settlement—rather than a new, contentious negotiation every year or every six months—would be in everyone’s interest. With Greece’s economy growing again, this may well be the right moment to secure that more durable solution.

question: Seeking clarification on your view of Holder.
interview_answer: Mr. Prime Minister, let me respond directly: you’re correct, I have full confidence in Eric Holder in his role as Attorney General. He is an excellent Attorney General who performs his duties with honesty and professionalism, and I fully expect him to continue doing so. All right? You’re focusing on the empty part of the glass. I prefer to see the glass as half full rather than half empty. What we want to achieve is greater awareness and responsiveness from the international community to what is happening in [country]. That is what we, as Turkey, are working toward, and I am convinced the United States is pursuing the same aim, along with other nations, the United Nations Security Council, and the Arab League. Additional countries, even if they are not members of these organizations, are also attentive to developments in [country]. Our objective is to speed this process up, and I will travel to other nations—my Foreign Minister will also travel—to explore how we can accelerate developments in a way that prevents further loss of life and enables a transition to a democratic order in [country]. We want the oppressive regime and dictatorship in [country] to end and to be replaced by democracy. I see this as a shared obligation for all states that support democracy, and this is the course of action we will collectively continue to pursue.

question: What concrete guarantees do you have that sending F-16s will not intensify this conflict?
interview_answer: I have a categorical commitment from President Zelenskyy that they will not be used to strike inside Russia’s own territory. However, they will be used wherever Russian forces are operating within Ukraine’s borders. Thank you all very much. I appreciate it.

question: If you meet Kim Jong Un at the DMZ tomorrow, would you actually cross over the line into North Korean territory?
interview_answer:
Yes, I would. I definitely would. I’d be completely at ease doing that. I’d have no hesitation at all.

Regarding China, we essentially decided today that we’ll resume the trade talks—which I had previously put on hold—and move forward with negotiations. We also agreed that I will not impose tariffs on the additional $325 billion worth of Chinese goods that I’m currently in a position to tariff if I choose, where, depending on how you look at it, we’re already fairly far along.

We covered a range of other issues as well. We talked about Huawei. I said we’d leave that for the final phase of the discussions; we’ll see how it goes. One thing I am going to permit, though—and this surprises a lot of people—is that we will continue to ship and sell a very large volume of components to Huawei that go into the products they manufacture. I said that’s acceptable, and that we will keep supplying those items. These are U.S. companies, John, producing these goods, and the technology is very sophisticated—highly advanced.

In some areas we’re the only ones capable of making this technology; no one else has it. What’s been achieved in Silicon Valley is remarkable, and no one has matched it. I agreed—without much difficulty—to let our firms keep selling that technology, so American businesses can continue operating. They were running into serious difficulties; these companies were unhappy they couldn’t sell to Huawei even though they weren’t involved in any of the concerns surrounding Huawei itself. So I made that change.

We also discussed education and students. Someone raised the concern that it had become harder for Chinese students to come here. If it’s being perceived that way, I don’t share that view. We want Chinese students to attend our excellent colleges and universities. They’ve been outstanding students and real assets. We talked that through, and I said they’ll be treated just like students from any other country.

And we’re moving toward a system where, if you graduate from a university here—because our top companies, including in Silicon Valley, need this talent—we deal with a real problem: you can finish first in your class at one of our best schools, and then you’re told you must leave. We’re going to fix that with what we’re calling a “smart person’s waiver.” We’ll make it possible not only for them to stay, but potentially to obtain green cards. We want to retain this talent here. We need to keep them here. Yes, go ahead, John.

question: What is your message to President Moon about North–South cooperation and sanctions?
interview_answer: I have a lot of respect for President Moon. I get along with him extremely well. In fact, I have very good relationships with almost all foreign leaders. Many people are surprised by that, but it’s true. Some of them have taken advantage of the United States in ways you wouldn’t imagine. And when they realize that I see it—which I do in every instance—it tends to make them think twice. Still, we maintain many strong partnerships. I’ll be phoning President Moon very shortly, basically as soon as I’m near the phone on the plane. He’ll be among the first people I call. I’ll also speak with Prime Minister Abe of Japan to brief him on our position and our plans. I’ll be making those calls. And President Moon really is putting in a tremendous effort. He very much wants an agreement, and he’s been very supportive. Thank you. Next question.

question: Why didn’t you meet directly with Jacob Blake’s family?
interview_answer: I spoke with their pastor—a terrific person—the family’s pastor. I decided it was best not to do anything where attorneys were part of it. They asked to talk, but they wanted lawyers on the call, and I felt that wasn’t appropriate, so I declined. Instead, I talked with the family’s pastor, who is an excellent man, a really good man, and we had a very good conversation. I might, at some later time, have that meeting. But they did insist that a lawyer be on the line, and I said no, that’s not appropriate, and I simply offered my best wishes through him. But again, I did speak directly with their pastor. Yes, go ahead.

question: Has the administration played a role in those talks?
interview_answer:
Yes, we have been directly involved. And what they’re working on right now is a temporary agreement, because we believe that, in the end, shifting funding to the States through block grants is the real solution. That’s a very strong approach. We think it will not only reduce costs, but also give people much better health care with a far smaller jump in premiums—you can see what’s happened with those—and significantly lower deductibles so people can actually use their coverage.

Lamar has been working extremely hard with Democrats—his colleagues across the aisle—and especially with Patty Murray. They’re putting together a plan and are getting very close to a short-term arrangement. That arrangement would likely last about one to two years and would carry us through this transition period, because—as you probably know—we either already have the votes or are very close to having the votes. And we will secure the votes to give us a real chance at truly strong health care in this country.

So yes, they are actively negotiating, but it’s for a limited, short-term fix so we avoid a very risky gap—including a risky period for insurers, by the way. For that one- to two-year span, we will have a solid interim plan. But our goal is to put in place an ultimately much better, long-term health care solution. All right?

question: Why is it considered such a significant issue for a President to briefly speak by phone with Taiwan’s leader?
interview_answer:
Let me walk through how I see it. I’d place myself somewhere in the middle. I believe every element of our foreign policy should periodically be reexamined. One of the things I’ve said before is that, while I’m very proud of the foreign policy work I’ve done and I think I’m a stronger President now than when I began, spending eight years inside this bubble shapes your perspective. Our democracy, and the country, gain from having fresh viewpoints come in.

So I think it’s not just appropriate, but actually the responsibility of a new President to go back over the existing policies, decide what still holds up, and what may need to change. That’s what I did when I took office, and I fully expect any successor to go through the same process.

Given how central the U.S.–China relationship is—its importance for the global economy, our national security, our role in the Asia‑Pacific, and China’s growing weight in world affairs—there is probably no bilateral relationship that is more consequential, or where a breakdown or open conflict would do more harm to everyone involved. So if he wants to revisit aspects of that relationship, that in itself is fine.

What I’ve told the President‑elect, though, is that on foreign policy generally, you want to proceed in a structured, careful, and purposeful manner. And because there is only one sitting President at any given time, my counsel has been that before he starts engaging extensively with foreign leaders—beyond standard congratulatory or protocol calls—he should first have his full team in place, have them thoroughly briefed on what has been done, where the risks lie, where the openings are, and what we’ve learned over the last eight years. That way, as he chooses to shift foreign policy in new directions, he’s doing so with a complete picture and ensuring the entire U.S. Government is coordinated and communicating the same message.

On China specifically—and using Taiwan as the concrete example—there has long been an implicit understanding among the United States, China, and, in practice, Taiwan: do not disrupt the existing status quo. Taiwan functions in a different manner than mainland China. China regards Taiwan as part of China, but recognizes it has to deal with Taiwan as a distinct entity with its own systems. For their part, the Taiwanese have accepted that as long as they can continue to operate with a meaningful degree of autonomy, they will not push ahead with a formal declaration of independence.

This arrangement, although imperfect for all parties, has preserved peace and enabled Taiwan to become a prosperous society whose people enjoy a significant level of self‑rule. But you have to recognize that, for China, Taiwan ranks at the very top of their priorities. The principle of “one China” is central to their national identity.

So if you are going to disrupt that long‑standing understanding, you must carefully think through the consequences, because China will not react to that the way they might react to other disputes. They will treat it as more fundamental than even the tensions we’ve seen in the South China Sea. It touches the core of how they define themselves as a nation, and their response could be very far‑reaching.

That doesn’t mean you are locked into every past precedent. It does mean you have to move only after detailed planning and with an awareness of how they are likely to respond.

All right. Isaac Dovere from Politico.

question: Commands given on that particular date.
interview_answer:
Ed, I will release every piece of information we have. What I can say right now is this: the moment I was informed that our people were in danger, I gave a clear instruction to my national security team—take whatever steps are necessary to ensure their safety. That is the same instruction I issue any time I learn Americans are at risk, civilian or military, because protecting them is our top priority.

Now, on the question of a mandate, I have one central mandate. My mandate is to stand up for middle‑class families and for those working hard to join the middle class. That’s the charge I’ve been given. That’s what voters told us they wanted. They said: Focus on helping us. Don’t get bogged down in partisan games, don’t cater to party interests, don’t bend to special interests. Just work as hard as you can to help us move forward, because we’re out here working hard and many of us are still having a tough time. That is the mandate I’m operating under.

I don’t assume that winning an election means everyone suddenly shares my views on every issue. I’m well aware of the history and commentary about Presidents overreaching in their second terms, and we are very mindful of that risk. At the same time, I wasn’t reelected to sit back and enjoy the victory. I was reelected to deliver results for American families and small businesses across this country, who are still coming back from a severe recession but remain optimistic about what lies ahead.

I share that optimism. During the campaign I said something that may have sounded like standard campaign talk, but now that the campaign is done, I want to repeat it—and I hope you’ll take it at face value. When you travel around America, you can’t help but be moved by the determination, resilience, work ethic, and basic decency of the American people. It pushes you to put in even more effort. You meet parents who’ve faced long odds and still manage to get by and send their kids to college. You meet young people doing remarkable work in struggling neighborhoods because they believe in the American promise and want it to be real for everyone. You meet farmers supporting each other through droughts, and business owners who kept their doors open through the recession even when it meant not taking a paycheck themselves.

After talking with people like that, you come away thinking: they deserve a better level of government service than they’ve had. They deserve all of us in Washington waking up every day asking, how can I make life a bit easier for them? That doesn’t mean every policy will be flawless, or that we won’t face some very hard problems we have to work through. But I do know the federal government can have a positive impact.

We see that now on the New Jersey shoreline and in New York. People there are still facing real hardship. The response hasn’t been perfect, but it has been strong, rapid, forceful, and sustained, and many people have received help because of it. That’s a good model for how I want the federal government to function overall, and I’m going to push as hard as I can to make sure that’s how it operates.

Christi Parsons, Chicago Tribune. Go ahead.

question: How does naming Senator Max Baucus as the next Ambassador to China reflect on the prospects for passing a major tax reform bill during the President’s second term?
interview_answer: It means Max Baucus is going to serve as an excellent Ambassador to China, and I want the Senate to confirm him quickly. And my belief and expectation is that if Democrats and Republicans in both the House and the Senate are genuinely committed to tax reform, it shouldn’t hinge on a single person; it should rest on all of us working together. My team and I are fully prepared and eager to work with both parties to discuss how we can streamline the Tax Code, make it more equitable, ensure it helps generate more employment, and better serve middle‑class Americans.

question: Have you taken a practical view of your plan and its limits?
interview_answer:
Well—appreciate the followup—nothing’s changed just because we’re in a different room. Anyway—yes. As I explained to you last November, about this same time, I was among those Americans who disapproved of the situation in Iraq, because it looked like everything we had done up to then was on the verge of collapsing. Put simply, sectarian bloodshed was surging. And I had a decision to make: either pull back, as some urged, and just hope that the turmoil and killing that might erupt in the capital wouldn’t spread nationwide—or send additional forces to stop that turmoil and violence before it took hold. I chose to send more troops. So that was a sober, realistic judgment on my part.

What’s also realistic is recognizing what’s at stake if we lose in Iraq. People aren’t going to be satisfied simply with pushing America out of that country. Al Qaeda wants to strike us here at home. That is their goal. That is what they are aiming to do. They hold an ideology that says the world should live under their vision, and one way they think they can advance that ideology is by attacking Americans and U.S. interests. The same extremists who are blowing up innocent civilians in Iraq are the ones who attacked us here on September 11th, and that’s why events in Iraq directly affect our security at home.

So I have taken a realistic view of what defeat would mean. I have taken a realistic view of what must occur on the ground for us to succeed. And it has been tough going, and the American people recognize that it is tough going. One reason it is so hard is that our television screens are filled with brutal attacks carried out by people who have harmed us before. And that should drive home to Americans that what happens in Iraq and in other parts of the world directly affects the safety of the United States of America. Yes, ma’am.

question: Is there even one existing policy you would leave unchanged?
interview_answer: Frankly, hardly any at all. I’m almost entirely on the other side.

question: Does the Russian state possess any compromising information about President Trump or his relatives?
interview_answer:
President Trump’s position on Crimea is widely known: he has consistently said the annexation was illegal and still does. Our stance is different. We held a referendum there in full accordance with the UN Charter and international law, and from our perspective the matter is settled on that basis.

Now, regarding so‑called compromising material. I have indeed heard the allegations that we supposedly gathered damaging information on Mr. Trump during a visit he made to Moscow. Let me be very clear, colleague: when Mr. Trump was in Moscow at that time, I had no idea he was even in the city. I respect President Trump highly, but back then he was a private businessman, and no one ever informed me that he was here.

Take the St. Petersburg International Economic Forum as an example. There were more than 500 American business leaders of very senior rank. I cannot recall all of their surnames. Do you seriously think we try to collect compromising files on every single one of them? It is hard to come up with something more nonsensical than that.

So I urge you to simply dismiss these claims and stop dwelling on them. And I must add: if such material actually existed, it would have surfaced a long time ago. If you watched Peter Strzok’s testimony in recent days—as I did from Brussels—you would see it was a disgrace to the FBI and to our country, and clearly part of a baseless witch hunt.

Thank you all very much. Thank you. Thank you.

question: Is it possible to prevent a new cold war with China?
interview_answer: To address your question directly, yes, I am firmly convinced that a new cold war is not inevitable. I’ve had numerous meetings with Xi Jinping, and in those discussions we have both spoken frankly and plainly on every major issue. Based on those talks, I do not believe China is on the verge of launching an invasion of Taiwan. I also reiterated that our Taiwan policy has not shifted in any way; it remains exactly what it has been. I emphasized that we support a peaceful resolution of cross-strait differences so that the situation never escalates to that point. I am confident he fully grasped my position, just as I understood his. I also believe the United States is better positioned than any other nation, both economically and politically, to adapt to global changes. And President Xi and I agreed to establish a framework in which, on matters where further work is needed, our chiefs of staff—our relevant Cabinet officials and other appropriate representatives—will meet and work through the specifics of each issue we brought up. We covered a substantial number of topics in those discussions.

question: What are you seeking to achieve in return?
interview_answer:
From a symbolic standpoint, this trip really matters. I’ll be the first American President ever to go to Laos. Given the history between our two countries, it’s helpful to look at how our relationship with Vietnam has changed over time—a country I just visited. At the beginning, when you’re trying to rebuild trust, you can accomplish a lot by focusing on the unresolved issues from the war. In Laos, that primarily means dealing with unexploded bombs that still contaminate large areas of rural land. Laos is still a relatively low‑income, developing country, so it doesn’t have the resources on its own to fully address that problem. We should step in and assist. I fully expect that over the days we’re there, we’ll be able to commit to very specific forms of aid so that children playing outside, farmers working their fields, or entrepreneurs trying to start a business are no longer at risk of being killed or injured by hidden munitions.

We’re also firmly committed to accounting for those who were lost in that conflict. As with Vietnam, any additional information we can uncover about our missing in action and prisoners of war not only brings profound comfort and closure to families and fits our national values, it also serves as a concrete gesture of good faith from the Lao side and helps both nations move into a new stage of partnership.

So I expect much of our initial dialogue to focus on those legacy issues, but it will go well beyond that. We already have a program that supports all the countries along the Mekong in managing development and environmental challenges, something we’ve pursued through ASEAN for several years. Expanding that work with Laos will be important. Deepening exchanges between our peoples is another traditional pillar that I think we’ll emphasize.

I also believe that Laos, looking at the rapid economic gains made by neighbors like Vietnam and China, will be very eager to find ways to plug into the global economy and accelerate its own growth, and we can play a constructive role in that process.

So we’ll be pursuing a wide‑ranging agenda. If you think about my visit to Ho Chi Minh City—the crowds on the streets and the remarkable goodwill you could feel—that began with the same kinds of steps we’re now preparing to take with Laos. The difference is that this time we can move more quickly and achieve more in a shorter period than the 10 or 15 years it took in Vietnam, because we’ve learned from that experience. Laos is clearly ready to engage, and we’re ready to engage with them.

So I’m genuinely eager to see a country that I’m told is extraordinarily beautiful, and to use this visit to launch that broader cooperation.

question: In your view, what specific role could Vietnam play in helping advance talks between the United States and North Korea?
interview_answer: On this matter, Vietnam is firmly committed to fully complying with all relevant UN Security Council resolutions, and we back the denuclearization of the Korean Peninsula. We will make every possible effort and take all actions within our capacity to help achieve the denuclearization of the Korean Peninsula. Thank you.

question: Are they still moving forward with their program?
interview_answer: He stated that the testing is not going to begin. He said he will not conduct tests of rockets, missiles, or anything related to nuclear weapons. All I can tell you is that this is exactly what he said. And we’ll see. Yes, next question. Go ahead. You there in the back, in red.

question: Is there any scenario where you’d lift the sanctions before major progress on human rights occurs?
interview_answer: No. I require substantial progress. I need to be confident that these abuses will no longer occur. And again, once you begin making that progress, there will come a stage where, even though the work won’t be fully complete for some time because it can’t be done instantly or automatically, you’ve crossed a line you can’t reverse. When we’ve clearly reached that stage, that’s when I’ll seriously consider changing course. Yes. Please, go ahead. You, go ahead. You first.

question: How do you respond to the decision by Scooter Libby’s lawyers to call Vice President Cheney as a witness in the current CIA leak trial?
interview_answer: I saw that reported in the paper this morning, and it struck me as noteworthy. Beyond that, I’m not going to say anything more about a case that’s still under way. I just found it noteworthy. Ann.

question: Are you going to lose confidence in this process?
interview_answer: No. I expect him to follow through. I truly mean that. If I didn’t, I wouldn’t be involved in this at all. I genuinely believe he will. And a big part of that is the engine test facility, on top of the other commitments they’ve already made. It’s a very powerful engine testing site that, again, we can detect because of the heat it gives off. And yes, I’m—I'm very satisfied. I'll say this—I’m very satisfied with those two elements—the two you mentioned. But I think you may be talking about what’s not listed, which is the engine test facility. I think he’s—I think—honestly, I think he’s going to carry out these steps. I could be mistaken. I mean, I might be standing here in six months saying, You know what, I got it wrong. I’m not sure I’d ever flat‑out admit that, but I’d come up with—I’d come up with some sort of explanation. Okay, one or two more. One last one. Let’s go.

question: Do you feel concerned about your position here today, and about possibly having a trust problem with the American public?
interview_answer: No, I’m feeling energetic, positive about things, and I chose to come here specifically to walk you through the NIE. I have already said it’s dangerous, and nothing in the NIE alters my judgment about the threat it poses internationally. In fact, it reinforces it. I’m treating this NIE as a tool to keep mobilizing support among our partners and allies.

question: May I raise a domestic issue with you?
interview_answer: Yes.

question: What is your position on tapping the strategic petroleum reserve?
interview_answer: The strategic petroleum reserve exists for genuine emergencies. Using it now doesn’t solve the core problem we face. We have to deal with that deeper issue—which, honestly, I’ve been laying out since I first took office—which is a two‑part strategy: investing in new technologies to develop alternative energy sources, while also expanding oil and gas production here in the United States. And this is the moment to follow through on that. I’ve heard people say, “That will take 7 years.” But if we had started 7 years ago, we would be having a different discussion right now. I’m not claiming we would have completely reshaped the global energy picture, but we certainly would be relying more on our own supply and sending less of our money abroad.

question: What has the President been telling our allies about how the next U.S. President will be able to uphold any commitments made now?
interview_answer:
What I tell them is very simple: “Judge me by what I do.” In other words, I’m not offering elaborate reassurances; I’m just doing the work. As I’ve said many times, people don’t really question whether I mean what I say, and they generally trust that I follow through on my word. I’m not giving anyone assurances that I don’t think are overwhelmingly likely to be honored.

We’re at a particular moment, and let me frame it this way: A lot of people expected that my appearance at the G7 would fall flat—that there’d be no real enthusiasm for renewed American leadership or confidence in where the United States stands. I’d argue the opposite is what actually happened.

The leaders I’m engaging with at NATO and the G7 understand our recent history. They understand, in broad terms, the character of the American people and where the large middle of the country stands—not as Democrats or Republicans, but as Americans. Their view, which I share, is that we remain a fundamentally decent, honorable nation.

They, like we have, witnessed events that startled and disturbed them—things they didn’t expect could occur in the United States. But they also believe, as I do, that the American public is not going to tolerate that kind of behavior indefinitely.

I don’t want to get bogged down in numbers—there’s that old Disraeli line about “lies, damned lies, and statistics”—but it is fair to say this: The Republican Party has shrunk in size; its leadership is split; and while the Trump faction dominates the party internally, it still represents only a sizable minority of the overall American electorate.

Now, we’ll see how it evolves. I believe that by clearly stating what we think is true—and by refusing to play the game of nonstop exaggeration that’s so common right now—we’re showing, as the saying goes, that “the proof of the pudding is in the eating.”

When I said I was going to focus on defeating the virus and getting millions of vaccine doses into people’s arms, it wasn’t about me alone. I understood the American people. I knew I would get crucial support from the Pentagon, from local law enforcement, from hospitals, from retired doctors, and many others. I trusted that, and look at how fast we moved.

Yes, we still face obstacles: some people are politically opposed to vaccines, others are simply afraid of needles, and there are many reasons in between. We still have work to do. But I never doubted that we could rally the level of cooperation we’ve seen and vaccinate tens of millions of Americans.

So, I think many here and abroad have been stunned by the consequences of President Trump’s fake populism. And I’m disappointed that so many Republican senators—colleagues who, frankly, know better—have hesitated to support, say, a full investigation, because they’re afraid of facing a primary challenge.

But in the end, we’ve been through comparable periods in our history—times when politicians were wary of risking their reelection because of the volatile state of their party. I see this phase as something we’re moving through. I don’t mean it will fade away easily. That’s precisely why my success on my priorities is so critical—whether on vaccinations, the economy, or infrastructure. We have to show that we can deliver results and keep delivering them.

I believe we will. As I’ve said, we’ll know more about where we stand six months down the road, but I think you’re going to see real progress, and, God willing, you’ll see a regrouping of many Republicans—especially younger Republicans rising in the party—around that progress and around a more stable course for our politics and our commitments.

question: Do you believe that if you had personally taken a more hands-on role, our situation right now would be any different?
interview_answer: No, not at all. I’m fully engaged. No—look, they’re in touch with me constantly. They update me on everything they’re doing with Nancy and Chuck. In my view, Democrats are just using this as cover to play politics—Chuck Schumer even put out a tough statement today saying schools must reopen because it helps the economy, but he doesn’t follow his own advice. My team—Steve Mnuchin and our terrific Chief of Staff, Mark Meadows—are on the phone with me nonstop. I’m completely involved in this, and we’re going to resolve it in a much simpler way. We let them try it their way, but they see the whole thing as a way to boost themselves in the election. People worry about foreign countries rigging elections; well, Democrats are rigging it too, because that’s exactly what they’re doing. If you look at their behavior, even in these talks, it’s election interference—unfair election interference. But we’re going to win regardless. Yes, next question.

question: Would you be willing to sit down with Mr. Rouhani?
interview_answer:
If the situation were properly set up, yes, I would absolutely sit down with him. But in the meantime, they have to behave responsibly. You know exactly what that implies. They cannot carry out the threats they’ve been talking about, because if they do, they will face extremely strong military force. We really wouldn’t have any alternative. So I believe they’re going to act responsibly. I truly think that’s what they want to do.

And by the way, President Macron informed me of every move he made yesterday. Many of you suggested he just showed up and acted on his own—he actually briefed me well before he came in about what was going on, what the plan was. I didn’t feel a meeting yesterday was right. It was too early, and certain issues need to be settled first.

But President Macron laid out precisely what was taking place, who was coming, the schedule, and where they would be talking. Afterward, he reported back in detail on how it went. I think his conversation was very constructive.

Prime Minister Abe of Japan, a major buyer of their oil, was deeply engaged as well. He knows the leadership there, so he was an extremely constructive influence. And I have to say, the President has handled this extremely well, and we’ll see what the final outcome is. Maybe it succeeds; maybe it doesn’t. I always say that: maybe it works, maybe it doesn’t.

But here’s the reality: the prior agreement was a terrible deal. We handed over $150 billion. We sent $1.8 billion in cash, and we received nothing in return. Nothing. And that deal was so short term that it runs out in a very brief time. You don’t negotiate such a short agreement with a country. Nations endure for a long time, and you don’t make short-lived deals when that much money is involved.

So I’m optimistic about where this can go. I’m hopeful it will be successful. I know many people from that country who live in New York City. I have a lot of friends from there. They’re remarkable people. Truly remarkable. The last thing I want is a major conflict. But we will do whatever is necessary. All right?

Next question. No. No, you first. Go ahead. No, go ahead. Yes. Yes.

question: Do you fear you’ll end up regretting this?
interview_answer: No. I made a choice, and I stand firmly by it. My concern now is that the rest of the international community must act with the same speed and urgency. As I mentioned in these remarks, we have committed $100 million to help relocate people into Darfur. I also do not regret imposing strong sanctions on Sudanese leaders and on companies controlled by certain figures in Sudan. That was the correct course of action. I am working to persuade others to adopt similar measures. As you know, putting together a broad, global sanctions regime is not easy; different countries often have conflicting priorities and commercial stakes. Our view is that human suffering must come before economic interests. So I am at ease with the choice I made; I am not at ease with how slow the overall response has been. Even so, we will keep addressing this crisis. At every stop on this trip, I have raised Darfur. And the President has raised it as well. I’ve spoken at such length you might have lost track of the question that was posed to you. I still remember it, Mr. President. We certainly discussed Darfur, along with other trouble spots, particularly on our continent. And I want to concur with the President: the problems exist, but the best strategy is to help Africans build their own capacity to confront these challenges. Just as important, as he said, is that we invest time and resources in monitoring and in preventing new crises from emerging, because they keep arising in different places. It is essential to recognize that today it is Darfur; tomorrow, unfortunately, it could be another region. These crises should not be treated as problems the United States alone must resolve. They are responsibilities of the international community, and, when they occur in Africa, of Africans themselves. Above all, Africans must develop the capacity to respond, and they should receive support to build that capacity so they can prevent crises and be ready when they arise. Then they can manage and solve these problems. In my view, President Bush’s approach is realistic, because you do not want the United States to be summoned as the solution to every difficulty. Otherwise, there is a backlash; people start faulting the United States for intervening everywhere and interpret that as serving hidden interests. The better path is to enable people to address their own conflicts, while the United States, with its significant capabilities, and the wider international community provide backing, instead of simply criticizing America for not stepping in to fix everything. The responsibility for both the problems and their solutions should remain with the places where they occur and the people who live there. That, I believe, is the soundest way forward. And we did, in fact, discuss this, including our own role and how it can be strengthened. The President is ready to assist us—he has consistently offered support—so that we can keep making progress.

question: Your view of Boris Johnson?
interview_answer: I know Boris. I like him. I’ve liked him for many years. I think he’d perform very well. I know Jeremy. I think he’d perform very well, too. I don’t know Michael. But would Jeremy do a good job? You tell me. []All right? All right. Thanks a lot, everyone. Thanks. Thank you very much.

question: Do you believe they’re genuinely earnest about this?
interview_answer:
Yes, I do. I believe they are extremely eager to reach an agreement. That became even clearer very late last night, when I saw an alert—what you’d call breaking news. It said that the Vice Premier of China—not some low-level official, but the Vice Premier himself—publicly stated that he wants to see a deal concluded, and that he wants it done under calm conditions. He specifically used the word “calm.” On that point, I agree with him.

China has taken a very serious economic hit over the past several months. They’ve already lost about 3 million jobs, and that number is going to climb well beyond 3 million. Their supply chain is coming apart—a breakdown on a scale nobody has really seen before. Once a supply chain fractures like that, putting it back together is extremely difficult. You know that.

So I think they very strongly want to reach an agreement. And the longer they hold off, the harder it becomes to restore that system, if it can be fully restored at all. That’s why I’m convinced they want a deal.

Our tariffs have impacted them very sharply in a relatively short time. The United States will soon have taken in over $100 billion in tariffs. And I’ll repeat this: reporters—the media—generally do not acknowledge this. But if you look specifically at imports from China—we’re talking about China here, not other countries—those goods give China a certain leverage. However, that leverage only lasts for so long.

They’ve been intervening in their currency, weakening it, and pouring a lot of money into their system. Because of these moves, consumer prices here haven’t risen much, if at all, since they’re determined to keep their people employed. If prices go up significantly, they can’t keep everyone working, and they lose competitiveness. The global marketplace is a very sophisticated, very tough market. Many markets are.

Frankly, I don’t think China can keep doing what they’re doing indefinitely—maybe they can, maybe they can’t, but I don’t think so. And they’re very smart. I consider President Xi a very strong leader and a very intelligent man. He can’t just absorb a loss of 3 million jobs in such a short time, especially when that number is going to multiply. That will severely damage China’s trade apparatus, and he simply cannot allow that to happen.

So when you ask, “Do you think they want to?”—maybe on one level they might hesitate, maybe they might not. But my view is that they do want a deal. In fact, I’m not sure they really have any alternative. That’s not meant as a threat; it’s just how I see the situation. Meanwhile, the United States—which previously wasn’t collecting anything meaningful from China—will, in a relatively brief period, surpass $100 billion in tariff revenue. So yes, I think they are very anxious to make a deal. All right? Next question.

question: Are you open to holding a summit with Vladimir Putin to tackle these issues and pursue a solution?
interview_answer:
On that last point—on your final question—the answer is yes. When we discussed whether we would have the three initial meetings, we also said that, depending on how those went and if circumstances warranted, we could move toward a summit. We considered the idea of a summit even before the Ukraine situation arose, in the context of broader strategic doctrine and our long-term strategic relationship. So I still view a summit as a real option, first of all.

Second, I am deeply worried. I’m very worried this could turn into—look, the only type of war worse than one that’s planned is one that happens by accident. My concern is that this could spiral—very quickly spiral—out of control, especially for the reason you mentioned: Ukraine’s borders and what Russia might or might not decide to do.

My hope is that Vladimir Putin recognizes that, short of triggering an all-out nuclear conflict, he is not in a strong position to dominate the globe. I don’t believe he actually thinks he can, but it remains a serious worry. That’s why we must proceed with great caution and spell out plainly that there will be consequences—costs that could be extremely damaging to his country.

And yes, naturally you have to be worried when a nuclear-armed state invades—if he invades. That hasn’t occurred since World War II. It would be the most significant development for global war and peace since World War II.

question: Can you clarify if the event in Lebanon was a deliberate attack or an accidental incident?
interview_answer: From everything we’ve seen, especially the size and nature of the blast, it strongly points to an attack. I’ve spoken with several of our top military commanders, and their assessment is the same. They don’t view this as a typical industrial or manufacturing accident. Their judgment—and they’re in the best position to know—is that this was an attack, involving some sort of bomb, yes.

question: Why have you decided not to hold a joint press briefing with Putin at this summit? Why pass up the opportunity to publicly confront Russia’s destabilizing behavior on a global stage?
interview_answer:
[…] Let me spell this out plainly: I agree that our relationship is at a low point, and where it goes from here depends on whether he starts behaving in line with international norms, which he often has not. As I told him when I was a candidate and again after I won, before I was sworn in, I made clear I would determine whether he had, in fact, tried to interfere in our election; whether he was tied to major cyber intrusions, and so on; and that, if he had, I would respond. I did that review. I had full access to the intelligence. He was involved in those actions. I responded, and I stated plainly that I am prepared to respond again.

On the question of the press event itself: in my experience—and I’m not saying the press shouldn’t be informed—a shared press conference is not a competition over who performs better on camera or who can publicly embarrass the other. My goal is to make absolutely clear to him what the terms are for improving our relationship with Russia.

We are not seeking a clash. We are trying to address the behavior we believe violates international norms, that’s the first point. Second, where cooperation is possible, we’re open to it—for example, on strategic stability and arms doctrine, and potentially in other areas. There’s even discussion of possible cooperation on climate.

So my conclusion is that the most effective way to handle this is for the two of us to meet privately and talk directly. I’m confident you understand I’ll be very blunt […] with him about our concerns. Afterward, I’ll state clearly how I judge the meeting went, and he’ll state his view of how it went.
