I do not want the focus shifted to side issues like: Did they shake hands, who spoke longer, who looked stronger. He can describe what he says the meeting covered, and I will describe what I believe it covered. That is the approach I’m taking instead of a joint press conference.

question: Have you requested that John Kelly come out and deny the Atlantic story?
interview_answer: No, I have not, and I hold no ill will toward John. I don’t hold ill will toward anyone. No. I was very encouraged to see that one of his associates—because I know Zach is close to him and worked for him—Zach Fuentes issued a statement—I think late last night—making clear that the report was false.

question: Whether the current situation inside is okay
interview_answer: You’re seriously asking that? Are you asking if I think it’s okay? Of course not. That’s exactly why we’re in the process of quickly transferring a thousand of those children out. That’s why I ordered Fort Bliss to be opened. That’s why, from the very beginning, I’ve been pushing to secure more space so children—especially children—can be housed safely while we address everything else that’s going on. The situation as it stands is completely not acceptable. Ken [Ken Thomas, Wall Street Journal].

question: What would you say to those critics who argue that by approving legislation to build a barrier along the U.S.–Mexico border, you sent the signal that Latin Americans are not welcome in the United States?
interview_answer:
I did approve a law to upgrade our border. It wasn’t just about putting up a fence; it involved a range of infrastructure—new technology like sensors, barriers, berms. We have a very long border, and it has to be enforced. It must be enforced not only to stop undocumented crossings, but also to stop narcotics. It must be enforced to block potential terrorists, and it must be enforced to control weapons flows—sometimes weapons moving south, I’m told. So we need effective border controls. That’s what sovereign nations do: they secure their borders.

So we updated and strengthened the border. It went far beyond a simple fence. And I understand that some people felt it sent a message that they’re not wanted. My view is the opposite: People are welcome, but they must come in accordance with the law. There are thousands of foreign nationals living in our country who aren’t citizens but are here legally. The real issue is how we deal with those who have been here for years because our current law has not been functioning properly. And I’ve already laid out my position on that.

I also point out that persuading people that a comprehensive immigration plan can succeed requires convincing the American public that we’re fulfilling our basic duty to enforce the law. Many citizens were saying, “You don’t care about whether we have a secure border.” Congress responded by saying, “Yes, we do care.” That enforcement step was meant as the opening move toward a broader bill. In other words, Members of Congress were effectively saying, “Let’s address border security first, then tackle a full reform package.” At least, that’s what I’m urging them to say.

So I’m pushing them to move from that first step to the full package. We’ve taken action on border enforcement; people are being returned. In fact, I find it notable—and I honestly didn’t foresee this—that the responsible press in Guatemala, reflecting public concern there, and the President of Guatemala, also reflecting those concerns, pressed me about deportations. That was a top issue for them. That tells you that enforcement is actually happening.

The American people have to be convinced, Elaine, that their Government is serious about its responsibilities. Once they see that, it becomes easier to persuade skeptical Members of Congress to support a comprehensive immigration solution.

Now, I’ve consistently backed a comprehensive approach. If you look back at my earlier speeches—you may not have listened to them, but you could go back and read them—[laughter] well, that’s not entirely fair, but anyway, my position has been the same. I came to see how important this is when I was Governor of Texas. I used to say that family values don’t end at the Rio Grande, and that people come here to do jobs Americans aren’t doing because they need to support their families. That’s why I said it that way.

And the reason trade matters—programs like Labradores Mayas, for example—is that I also believe most people in Guatemala would rather have decent jobs at home than be forced to travel to another country to work. The more we can build prosperity in our own region, the more we can expand trade that actually produces employment and stability, the less likely it is that someone who’s just trying to feed a family will feel compelled to come to the United States.

So I appreciate your interest in this subject, Mr. President. This has been an excellent press conference. Thank you. I’d like to finish on that point: the American Dream for everyone. We can realize that dream in our own nations by investing in education, upgrading infrastructure, implementing CAFTA, which is a powerful tool for producing and exporting goods, attracting investment, and creating jobs. I’m confident that if we remain committed on those fronts, the American Dream will truly be a dream shared by all the peoples of the American continent.

Thank you very much. Thank you all.

question: On boosting defense spending, enemy base strike options, and the Japan-U.S. alliance?
interview_answer: Absolutely not.

question: Isn't that precisely the job voters chose them for last November?
interview_answer:
From my perspective, what the American people want is for Congress to back our troops who are in danger, to get them the funding they need, and not to have politicians in Washington dictating battlefield tactics to our commanders. It’s acceptable to disagree with the overall policy, but once you have forces in harm’s way, it’s a completely different matter to then withhold the resources they require.

There’s clearly been a lot of political maneuvering here in Washington. You’ve watched this closely, so you know what I mean. Not only have lawmakers been posturing to satisfy different factions within their party, but they have also started tacking on new streams of spending just to round up votes for an emergency war funding bill.

My concerns, David, are several. First, Congress should not be micromanaging the war; Congress should not underfund our armed forces; and Congress should not turn an emergency war appropriations bill into a vehicle for unrelated pet projects. Second, as I said in my remarks, pushing this past mid‑April and then into May will begin to erode the readiness of our military. So my view is: enough games. They need to return, pass a bill. If they want to keep playing politics, that’s their choice—but I will veto that bill. They should still move it quickly, get it to my desk, and I’ll veto it. Then we can focus on the core task: providing money for our troops without conditions and without fixed withdrawal dates.

What’s remarkable to me is that, first, the United States Senate voted overwhelmingly to confirm General Petraeus, after he laid out what he believes is required to achieve success in Iraq, and then turns around and refuses to fund his mission. Second, we’ve already deployed about 40 percent of the additional forces, and yet some people are demanding we start pulling back before the new strategy has even had a chance to work.

They need to cut short their recess, send a bill to my desk, and if it’s full of conditions, mandates, withdrawal timetables, and pork‑barrel spending, I will veto it. After that, we can get on with actually finishing this job, and we can do it fast. There’s no reason for a long delay. We can move a straightforward bill, get our troops the funds they require, and continue the mission of winning this war.

question: At what point, exactly, should Americans expect prices to drop?
interview_answer:
Let me be clear: the main reason prices are elevated right now is that COVID has disrupted global supply chains. I’m not talking down to you—you’re aware of that—but that is factor number one.

Second, if you look specifically at gasoline and crude oil costs, they’re high largely because, so far, Russia and the OPEC countries have declined to significantly increase production. We’ll know relatively soon whether they decide to open the taps more and what impact that will have.

Third, consider what we’re facing as we head into this Thanksgiving. We’re in a very different position than we were a year ago. Last Thanksgiving, as I’ve mentioned, I had dinner just with my wife, my daughter, and my son‑in‑law. This year, the situation is much improved. The economy is stronger, and wages have been rising faster than prices overall, which means we’ve produced real economic growth.

Now, that does not mean the current disruptions are imaginary; they are real and they hit family budgets. That’s one reason I’ve highlighted the need to address the behavior and possible price gouging we’re seeing in beef, poultry, and similar products. We’re examining whether those companies may be violating antitrust laws in how they set those prices.

So there are several moving parts here. But the bottom line is this: despite how painful higher prices are for many families right now, most people would still rather be in this year’s economic situation than where we were last Thanksgiving.

question: When you look back on your time in office, how would you like your legacy on race relations to be remembered?
interview_answer:
I really do think the historians should be the ones to render final verdicts on legacy. But what I can do—and this is probably the most honest way to answer you—is describe the approach I’ve tried to take in leading the country on race.

Above all, my hope is that I’ve used my voice to help Americans confront our difficult racial history; to urge people to truly listen to one another; to insist that the legacy of slavery, Jim Crow, and systemic discrimination didn’t disappear with the Civil Rights Act, the Voting Rights Act, or with my election; to acknowledge that, while conditions have improved—a lot—we still have significant work ahead; and, as we did with the policing task force we created, to push people toward practical, specific steps that can lessen, even if they can’t fully erase, the effects of racial bias.

If I’ve spoken about these questions honestly and constructively, then my hope is that, even if my efforts don’t resolve everything immediately, they at least bring problems to light, define them clearly, and give the country room to grapple with them and design realistic remedies. And I would hope that way of looking at things contributes to ongoing progress so that not only Malia and Sasha, but their children as well, can grow up in an America that is fairer, more cohesive, and more equal.

That change won’t be instantaneous, and that’s all right. We plant seeds, and others may one day enjoy the shade from the trees we’ve planted. And I’d like to believe that, as faithfully as I knew how, I’ve spoken candidly and responsibly about these matters.

question: What is your reply to that point?
interview_answer:
Yes, I do have a response. They may well be trying to drag things out. When you’re dealing with a closed system that lacks a truly free press or strong opposition voices, it’s difficult to read what’s actually happening. He has made certain statements, and he is clearly testing our relationship—trying to see whether the five of us will stay together or split apart. The only responsible course I see is to keep moving ahead inside the six‑party structure.

We’ve already taken our position. The five parties have agreed: there is a specific path forward. We obviously want him to live up to his obligations in a way that can be checked and confirmed. But when you’re dealing with a country where reliable information is scarce, you really do have to wait and see whether they’re acting in good faith. That’s very different from our own system, where you have many people inside the government talking to you all the time—sometimes with permission, sometimes without. In his country it doesn’t work like that. It’s shut off from the outside, and the direction of the state is determined by the will of a single leader.

Still, we remain optimistic. We’ve reaffirmed our shared intent to preserve the six‑party framework to address several matters, beginning with the nuclear question. And I can assure you, Steven Lee, it is far more effective to have multiple countries speaking with one voice on this than to have one country standing alone. If we’re going to see a real breakthrough, it’s going to come through this six‑party process.

I am, in fact, cautiously optimistic. Time will tell. I’ve been working on this issue for a long time. There have been periods when everything seemed on track, everyone looked ready to fulfill their commitments, and then, for one reason or another, we hit a setback. What matters, though, is that we have never walked away from trying to resolve this peacefully and through diplomacy.

If you fully grasp how that society works, you’ll better understand why we’re seeing delays now. If it were not so closed and centralized, we probably would have settled this by now. We have to be persistently patient, ladies and gentlemen. We need to allow time to reach a complete resolution.

That said, persuading them to abandon their nuclear weapons program is very hard, but it can be done. It is not out of reach, and I am convinced of that. To settle this, I am persuaded that, as the President said, the six‑party talks are the most effective tool and format we have. At this moment, we are waiting for their formal declaration; after that, we can proceed to verification.

So I would say it’s also your role to help create an international climate that encourages them to keep their word and submit an accurate declaration, and to recognize that it is in their own interest to do so. A full and honest declaration will help improve daily life for their people, and that is the smartest long‑term choice their leadership can make.

question: What purpose did the fiscal commission ultimately serve? If a majority agreed on tax changes, entitlement adjustments, and bipartisan collaboration, why weren’t the commission’s proposals actually carried out?
interview_answer:
I don’t accept the idea that it was simply set aside; that’s not accurate. The commission still offers a foundation for ongoing discussions. Part of the problem here is that in Washington—and I’ll be blunt—you all tend to be very impatient. If something doesn’t get done immediately, people jump to the conclusion that it will never happen. I’ve heard this same complaint for the last two years on virtually every issue we’ve addressed, whether it was health care, “don’t ask, don’t tell,” or Egypt. We see a major change unfold over a few weeks, and the reaction is, why did it take that long? So there’s a built‑in tendency to think that if something isn’t finished right away, it’s dead.

The fiscal commission laid out a basic outline. I support many parts of that outline and disagree with others. It’s true it received 11 votes, which was encouraging. It’s also true that, for instance, the Republican chair in charge of the House budget process did not endorse it. He has significant influence over any final budget agreement, and he has reservations. That means I need to sit down with him and find out what changes he wants to see.

I also need to talk with the Democrats who voted against it. There are elements in the report that I oppose on principle, and there are areas where I think the commission either didn’t go far enough or went too far. So what we are engaged in now is a back‑and‑forth process in both the House and the Senate, where each side narrows the gaps until we develop something that can actually pass.

That’s what I’m aiming for. My objective is to genuinely fix the problem, not to generate a splashy headline on day one. I want us, a year or two from now, to be able to say that we truly began to move the ball forward on this issue.

question: Can you clarify whether you remain firmly opposed to any law setting a date to withdraw troops?
interview_answer: Are you referring to this current transitional phase? Yes, without question. I do not believe Congress should be directing the war; their responsibility is to provide funding for our forces. I’m certainly willing to hear their views, but trying to manage a war through congressional resolutions is, in my view, a formula for defeat, and we cannot afford defeat. I will cooperate with Congress; I will hear them out. Congress absolutely has the authority to allocate money. But the notion that they should dictate to our commanders how to conduct operations, or what troop levels to maintain, does not, in my judgment, add up. It doesn’t add up now, and it would set a bad example going forward. The Commander in Chief’s job is to consult with Congress, not to have Congress run the war.

question: How are you feeling right now?
interview_answer: I’m feeling great, really great. Thanks.

question: What is your approach to requesting the last $350 billion in bailout funds from Congress?
interview_answer: I’ve discussed this issue with the President‑elect. I told him that if he decides he needs the remaining $350 billion, I’m prepared to formally request it—meaning, if he thinks it should be done before I leave office. The optimal path forward is to secure sufficient support in the Senate to approve that request. And that’s the full extent of what I can tell you about it, because that’s the full extent of what I know.

question: The question essentially asks whether concrete sanction plans have already been prepared and are ready to be put into effect in the event of Russian military action against Ukraine.
interview_answer: Yes, we are fully prepared to do that, and I can assure you of it. Thank you for raising this. Let me state this very plainly: We have thoroughly worked out and put in place everything needed so that we can immediately impose the required sanctions if Russia carries out military aggression against Ukraine. That advance preparation is essential. We must do this beforehand so that Russia understands clearly that these measures will be extensive and very tough. As part of our strategy, we are not disclosing every detail publicly, because Russia also needs to see that there could be additional steps beyond what is known. At the same time, it is absolutely clear that we are ready with far‑reaching actions. We will implement these actions together with our allies and partners, including the United States, and we will take every necessary measure. You can be certain that there will be no sanctions on which we diverge; we will move in lockstep. Let me also underscore to our American friends: we will stand united, we will act in concert, and every necessary measure will be taken by all of us, collectively.

question: Given how much time has passed and the strong influence of gun rights organizations such as the NRA, why do you believe this latest Washington effort on guns will actually be different from past attempts?
interview_answer:
First, this isn’t going to be a traditional commission. Joe will convene a core group of relevant Cabinet officials who are directly involved with this issue. We’re also going to bring in a wide range of stakeholders. We’ll be engaging Members of Congress who are seriously focused on this topic. We don’t have to invent everything from the ground up; there are already many ideas and proposals that have been discussed and vetted, and we expect to surface new approaches as well.

Their job will be to review every solid idea available—and even examine some weaker ones before discarding them—and then deliver a concrete package of recommendations in roughly a month. I’m counting on us not forgetting what happened in Newtown so quickly, not losing our sense of urgency just a few weeks later.

Once I receive those recommendations, I will put forward detailed, specific measures. I’ll outline them in my State of the Union address, and then we will work with Members of Congress who want to act to get as many of those measures passed as we can.

Saying, “This is horrific, this must never happen again,” and then failing to maintain the focus needed to get real action in the coming months would be illogical. I have more faith in the American public than that. I have more faith in the parents—the mothers and fathers I’ve been speaking with across the country, from every political background, including many gun owners—who are telling me plainly that this time, we need to approach this differently and actually follow through.

question: The question is asking whether that definition is going to be updated.
interview_answer: I’m sticking with what I’ve said—the answer is yes. Go get the booster dose. It’s included as part of the same requirement. It gives you stronger protection. Okay. Alex Alper, Reuters./North Atlantic Treaty Organization

question: In your view, what prevented you from persuading European leaders to adopt your approach to managing the financial crisis and implementing austerity policies?
interview_answer:
Let me start with the second part of your question. Ultimately, this is a negotiation among Cypriots themselves: between Turkish Cypriots and Greek Cypriots. The positive development is that both current leaders appear genuinely determined to reach compromises and to craft a framework that serves both communities. If they can reach a genuine convergence in their positions, the next step is ensuring that all of us in the international community—Turkey, Greece, the United States and others—back that deal in a way that allows it to be approved on both sides. We have devoted substantial effort to this; Vice President Biden has been deeply engaged. We are encouraged by the progress so far, and I believe there is a real window over the coming weeks and months to finally settle this issue. If the two sides can agree on a fair arrangement—which, by definition, will not give either side everything it wants—and if we can design transitional mechanisms to move from the current situation to the future both envision, then I think a solution is within reach. We will do everything we can to support that process.

On your question about Europe’s economy: my situation in the United States was, in some respects, less complicated. For my first two years in office, my party controlled both chambers of Congress, and I was dealing with the politics of just one country. Now, even that is difficult—Congress is not easy to handle—but it is still more straightforward than having to coordinate among numerous parliaments, commissions, unions, and other actors across an entire continent. European leaders had to proceed by consensus, and not all euro‑area countries were in the same economic condition, even though they shared a common currency. That structural reality made their job significantly harder. So I want to be clear: I recognize and respect the degree of difficulty European leaders faced, often in crises they did not create.

What I have tried to do is share our best policy lessons. One is that it’s critical to deal with troubled banks quickly and transparently, because the sooner you acknowledge losses and restructure, the sooner those banks can recapitalize and start lending again. Another is that when the economy is contracting, targeted public spending—especially on job creation and infrastructure—can actually boost growth, increase tax receipts, and thereby help reduce deficits over time. Then, once the economy is back on a stronger footing, you move more aggressively to repair the public finances.

To illustrate, in the United States our deficits increased sharply during the first two years of my administration because we were implementing emergency measures to stabilize the economy. Since then, our deficit has been cut by roughly two‑thirds, primarily because growth resumed and tax revenues rose. Those are policy lessons that, in my view, can be relevant to many countries.

In Europe, however, decisions had to be made within a far more complex architecture: multiple sovereign governments, a shared currency that includes some but not all EU members, and institutions like the European Parliament and the European Council layered on top. That means a lot of actors at the table and a lot of meetings to reach any major decision. Within that system, all we could do was offer the clearest advice possible and respect that European leaders, operating under those constraints, might choose a different course.

question: Once a vaccine is available, who should be vaccinated first?
interview_answer: Right now, we don’t have enough information about it. We’re hopeful it’s effective—truly hopeful—but some trials have been paused, and we think it’s critical to let the full process play out. There are several vaccine candidates we believe will be successful, yet they all need to move through the proper trial phases. Our progress is very advanced. We’ll be sharing concrete news fairly soon, including on treatments, which I consider extremely important. Yes, go ahead.

question: Can you explain precisely what role, if any, your administration played in influencing or encouraging Australia as it made that decision?
interview_answer:
All right. To begin with, on the issue of India, we have not exerted any influence, as far as I can tell, on Australia’s choice to look at what kind of relationship it wants with India regarding the peaceful civilian use of nuclear power. My view is that you have very capable officials here who can see for themselves that India is a major actor on the world stage and that it makes sense to strengthen the partnership between Australia and India. So I don’t think Julia or any other Australian leader needs guidance from me to recognize that. India is in your regional environment, and you are going to make your own one-on-one decisions about how you want to proceed.

From what I can see—without getting into every technical detail—the conversations happening here in Australia now about that relationship and about nuclear cooperation with India are consistent with international legal obligations and with the decisions taken under the NPT framework. I’ll be following what Australia ultimately decides with interest. But this is not a matter being negotiated between the United States and Australia; it is fundamentally an issue between Australia and India.

On the question of carbon emissions, I agree with your Prime Minister and with the overwhelming majority of scientists worldwide that climate change is a serious challenge, that human activities are a major driver of it, and that all of us share a duty to cut our carbon output.

Every nation is working out how to do that in the way that best fits its circumstances. Here in Australia, under the Prime Minister’s leadership, you’ve adopted an ambitious plan. In the United States, while we have not enacted what we refer to as a cap-and-trade system or emissions trading scheme, we have, for instance, moved to double fuel efficiency standards for cars, which will significantly reduce the amount of carbon released into the atmosphere.

We’ve also put substantial funding into clean energy research. We are firmly convinced that through greater efficiency and a broad set of other measures, we can honor the commitments we made in Copenhagen and Cancun. And as we look ahead over the next several years, my expectation is that the United States, as one of the larger carbon emitters, will be able to identify additional ways to bring down our emissions. I believe that’s good for the planet, and over time I think it’s also good for our economies, because I’m convinced that businesses, power producers, and consumers everywhere are going to have to change how they use energy and how they think about carbon.

Another conviction that I think the Prime Minister and I share is that the advanced industrialized countries cannot solve this on their own. So in multilateral settings—and I will keep pressing this in Durban—we insist that if we are taking significant actions, then emerging economies such as China and India also need to be part of the overall effort. That doesn’t mean they must adopt identical policies to ours. We recognize that on a per-person basis, their emissions are still much lower than ours. But it does mean they must also take their obligations seriously.

In the end, what we are seeking is a system in which all nations are doing their part. It’s going to be difficult, especially while many economies are still under strain. But I believe that in the long run, this is a path that can bring benefits as well as meet our climate responsibilities.

question: What is the single biggest and most pressing security danger that Barack Obama will have to confront as president?
interview_answer: The most immediate danger he’ll face, and that future presidents will also confront, is an attack on the United States itself. I’d like to be able to say that isn’t true, but there are still adversaries who want to hurt America and Americans. That remains the primary threat. North Korea continues to pose a challenge. Within the intelligence community, there’s ongoing disagreement about exactly how serious that threat is. One of my worries is that they may be pursuing a highly enriched uranium program. That’s why it’s crucial that the six-party talks produce a tough and comprehensive verification system. Put simply, if we’re going to move our relationship with North Korea forward, their government has to live up to its promises by accepting robust verification procedures, so we can be sure they are not, for instance, developing a highly enriched uranium capability. So they are still a danger, and Iran remains dangerous as well. Yes.

question: Do you honestly believe you’ll be able to reach an agreement that would meaningfully overhaul the current asylum system?
interview_answer:
No, because if we were actually allowed to do it, we could change the asylum system almost instantly. I used to say it would take 45 minutes; now I say 15 minutes. We could finish it in a day, even in an hour. Fixing the asylum rules would be very fast. We could close the loopholes very fast. These loopholes are terrible.

One reason Mexico handles this so well is that they have extremely tough immigration policies. They don’t have the kind of rules and, frankly, the kind of stupidity we have. Here, if someone sets one foot on our soil, we’re forced to bring them into the court system. We have to register them. Then we have to catch and release them. They then go live in our country and are supposed to return in three years for a hearing, and around 2 percent actually come back—as you know very well, maybe better than most people.

So they’re meant to return, but almost nobody does. Two percent show up. It’s a terrible system. What we’re doing now is: they enter illegally, and we are sending them out legally. But some very good Democrats asked me to pause that, and I did, for a couple of weeks. There’s about a week left on that pause. Aside from that, we did manage, on a very bipartisan basis—and I want to thank Speaker Pelosi, because she really cooperated with us—to secure humanitarian funding. They had no money for that, and we got that done.

question: Do you intend to visit Moscow next spring following President Putin’s invitation to you?
interview_answer: Absolutely.

question: According to the guidance from their legal team, where does the President’s authority stop?
interview_answer:
My view on this hasn’t shifted. When I spoke with immigrant-rights advocates, they wanted me, through executive orders, to basically recreate the broad immigration reform bill that stalled in Congress. Enacting a full, Senate-style comprehensive immigration package on my own would go beyond what the law lets me do. There are actions I am not legally allowed to take. There are clear limits on how far prosecutorial discretion can go when we’re enforcing the immigration statutes already on the books.

So we’ve been in continuous consultation with the Office of Legal Counsel, which is charged with defining the legal boundaries of presidential action—what we can and cannot do—and helping us determine where it is lawful, for instance, to say we are not going to try to remove all 11 million undocumented people in this country. At the same time, we face real resource limits at the border—not so much in catching people, but in processing cases, having enough immigration judges, and so on. That means we have to ask what is within our legal authority in terms of shifting resources and resetting enforcement priorities, since we simply cannot do everything. Any executive measures I take will rest on that legal and practical analysis.

Let me restate something I’ve said many times: there is a straightforward way to resolve concerns that I’m overusing executive power. Congress should send me a bill on immigration that I can sign. If Congress passes legislation that secures the border, modernizes our legal immigration system, and creates a real process for the 11 million people already here—people working in restaurants, on farms, cleaning hotel rooms, whom everyone knows we’re not going to deport en masse—and lets them come out of the shadows, pay taxes, pay a fine, and comply with the law, I will sign that bill immediately. And once that’s in place, I would gladly set aside any executive actions we’ve taken on this issue, because we would finally have a comprehensive law that deals with the problem directly.

question: How would you characterize China’s part in enabling the interaction that has taken place so far between Washington and Pyongyang?
interview_answer: I think China has played a major role, bigger than most people realize. Along the border, as you know, about 93 percent of what enters North Korea comes through China. So China has tremendous leverage there. At the same time, I believe North Korea is making its own decisions. They’re not following anyone’s orders. Its leader is very forceful. And they’re able to accomplish things that are quite remarkable. But the fact remains that around 93 percent still comes through China. China does have influence, and it has been very helpful. And Russia has also been very helpful. As you know, there’s a relatively small stretch of shared border, but still meaningful, roughly 28 miles. Activity can move through there as well. And they’ve been helpful too. Yes, go ahead.

question: As a follow‑up, do you think China is being genuine about the statements it made this morning?
interview_answer: Absolutely.

question: Will the U.S. administration officially back Brazil’s bid to join the OECD?
interview_answer: Yes, we are going to back it. We’ll have a strong partnership in many different areas. This is simply a step we’re taking as a gesture of respect for the President and for Brazil. We’ll have some requests of our own, but they won’t necessarily be related to this issue. Overall, I expect a very balanced, fair relationship. All right?

question: If that's the case, what exactly will you do to fix it?
interview_answer:
People’s frustration is completely understandable. We just went through a government shutdown and came close, for the first time in more than two centuries, to not honoring our financial obligations. When that crisis finally ended, people were relieved. Then almost immediately, they saw that the President’s health care reform law had a website that didn’t function properly and that people were receiving cancellation notices for their plans. So I fully get why they’re upset—I’d be upset too.

A lot of Americans watch what’s happening in Washington and conclude that not enough is being done that actually improves their daily lives. And no matter what Congress does or doesn’t do, I’m the President, and people expect me to step in and address these problems.

So, in terms of what I’m going to do about it, I’m going to keep working as hard as I possibly can on the things Americans care about most. It’s reasonable for people to expect me to earn back some trust, especially on this health care law, but also on a broader set of issues.

That responsibility is mine. We mishandled the rollout of this law. There are many aspects of it that have gone very well and drew little attention because they weren’t controversial: for example, allowing young adults to stay on their parents’ insurance until age 25, and providing seniors with larger discounts on prescription drugs. For the first three years, a lot of elements were implemented successfully.

But we always knew that setting up these insurance marketplaces—places where people could compare plans and, through competition, get more affordable coverage for their families—would be complex and closely watched. We should have gotten that part right on day one, instead of fixing it several weeks later.

I’m confident that when we look back a year from now, people will see that the law is functioning effectively and helping many Americans. To regain the public’s confidence, my plan is straightforward: keep working relentlessly, identify the specific problems, and systematically correct them. That means ensuring the website works properly and making sure people who received cancellation notices get the assistance they need. We’re going to keep addressing each of these issues, one by one, until we have fully completed the job.

question: Do you believe Joe Biden, the Democratic Party, and Kamala Harris should formally label and condemn Antifa as a domestic terrorist group?
interview_answer: Yes, they should. I believe they’re scared to do it. As far as I’m concerned, Antifa is basically an arm of their campaign. Democrats pretend, “Well, we’re not really sure what Antifa is.” Just look at Portland. Look at any city you want; they’re everywhere. They were active here too. Two months ago, we put in place a 10‑year prison term for anyone who toppled statues. Since then, we’ve barely seen them.We had to send federal personnel—Homeland Security, Border Patrol, and other DHS units—into Portland because we had to protect a federal courthouse worth about $500 million. Antifa wanted to burn it down and tear it apart. Our people were more than capable of defending it, but the city’s mayor and the state’s governor refused to do it themselves.We sent our teams in and they performed incredibly well. They handled that mission without difficulty. But while that courthouse was secured, Antifa moved to other parts of the city and kept destroying property. We are urging the governor to request the National Guard, to bring in real security. We are ready to step in—we could end that situation in one hour.We deployed only defensive forces to make sure that courthouse wasn’t burned or demolished. We also protected several other federal facilities because local officials failed to protect them.I want to add this: the local police forces are excellent. They have strong officers who could restore order if they were actually allowed to work—same thing in Chicago, same thing in New York, and in some other cities. Chicago has about 25,000 police officers; they’re very capable, but their leadership won’t let them do their jobs.In New York, you’ve got the NYPD—New York’s Finest—but their motivation has been destroyed. Their careers have been undercut because politicians won’t let them enforce the law. They could fix the situation in New York very quickly; there would be no issue, including on terrorism, if they were given the authority. Instead, the city cut the police budget by $1 billion. One billion dollars. And crime has skyrocketed—200 percent, 250 percent, depending on the week. The situation in New York is out of control.We could step in and fix it easily, but we shouldn’t have to; those cities should handle it on their own. The key is returning respect and backing to law enforcement. Policing is an honor, and leaders have to restore that honor to their departments. New York is a great city; so is Chicago. And in Portland, the police could do the job, too. If the local officials refuse to act, we are fully prepared—if they ask us—to send in the National Guard and resolve it within one hour. It would be over very fast.

question: What guidance would you offer Democrats who view the GOP’s recent tactics as a blueprint for regaining political power?
interview_answer:
Let me answer that in reverse. First, I believe Nancy Pelosi is a truly exceptional and historic political figure. A huge share of what our administration achieved was possible because of her intelligence, determination, and mastery of the legislative process. I generally stay out of intraparty leadership fights, and, frankly, as I’m exiting the stage, I’m even less inclined to intervene. But I can’t overstate my admiration for Nancy Pelosi. She brings firm progressive convictions together with remarkable political acumen. She takes on the hard fights, not just the easy ones. She has made decisions that angered parts of her own base because she believed they were the right choices for the country as a whole. In my view, that makes her an extraordinary leader.

Now, on Democrats, Republicans, and how Democrats should approach a new administration, I think you start by giving the incoming team a chance. I absolutely do not want them to copy what Mitch McConnell did when I won: convene right away and declare that their only mission was to block me on everything, even with the economy on the brink of a depression, just to pick up seats in the midterms and try to defeat me later.

That is not why voters send us to Washington—to engage in those kinds of partisan games. So that is not the counsel I’d give Democrats. My advice is: be clear about your core priorities and values, stand up for them, and be willing to engage in tough battles to defend them. When the new administration proposes something that you genuinely believe will help the American people, look for ways to collaborate. When you believe their agenda will do harm, say so plainly and lay out your case.

The basic test should be: is this good for the American people? That guiding principle has served me well. It allows me—after eight years—to look back and say I consistently tried to do what I thought was right. That doesn’t mean you avoid mistakes, but it does mean you’ve honored your oath and the promises you made to your constituents.

As for broader reflections on the presidency that I’ve had while traveling, my central conclusion—and the main piece of advice I’d offer the incoming President—is that the United States truly is an indispensable nation in today’s global system. I say that as someone who has made a point of expressing respect for all countries and peoples and who has repeatedly stressed that many of our toughest problems cannot be solved by America acting alone. But I also recognize that the basic post–World War II framework—and the order that carried through the end of the Cold War—was built around a set of ideals and norms that have benefited most people on the planet, not just Americans.

Those ideals include democracy, the rule of law, a free press, an independent judiciary, open markets, and a social safety net that blunts some of capitalism’s harshest impacts. They also include a commitment to human rights, investments in public health and development not only at home but abroad, and cooperation through multilateral institutions like the United Nations to uphold international standards and rules.

That framework is what created the modern world. There have been periods when we failed to live up to these principles and have rightly faced charges of hypocrisy. In Latin America, there have been moments when nations were treated dismissively, and sometimes they had good reason to feel that way. We have also fallen short of our own values domestically. But that overall system explains why the world today is far wealthier, much safer, and, yes, less violent, healthier, better educated, and more tolerant than it was half a century ago.

Maintaining that system takes constant effort; it doesn’t sustain itself automatically. I’ve made this point in Europe and in other places where there’s a backlash against this modern order. Look at Europe before that system was put in place: two world wars within 30 years. In the second conflict, roughly 60 million people lost their lives—not hundreds of thousands, not a couple of million, but 60 million—with whole continents destroyed.

Or look at Asia and the broader Asia-Pacific region before that order existed: recurring famines that killed millions, not just anxiety about low wages, but large populations dying because they lacked food, clean water, or access to basic medicines—people dying of cholera or other easily preventable diseases if only someone had a dose of penicillin.

So my view is that all of us share the obligation to strengthen that order, preserve it, and make it more inclusive, so it offers greater hope and prosperity to more parts of the globe. Every country has a responsibility to uphold the dignity and value of its citizens. And the United States cannot solve every problem or remake every society. If a government is determined to oppress its people, deny education to girls, or steal development funds and stash them in offshore accounts, our capacity to fix that from the outside is limited.

But the American President—and the United States as a whole—must be on the side of what is right. We have to make the case for those principles and defend them, even when we can’t fully realize them everywhere, all the time. If we stop doing that, the whole system erodes. And there is no other country ready to step in and play that role. There simply isn’t.

There are other major powers, of course. Take China: without China’s participation, we could not have secured the Paris climate accord. But China was not the one pulling together 200 countries, drafting the text, shaping the policy architecture, and driving the negotiations. Or take Russia: it’s a major military power, but right now it’s not the country leading efforts to rebuild after a hurricane in Haiti. We are.

I’ve said before that this is a burden we ought to shoulder with pride. And I would like every President—not just the forty-fifth, but all who follow—to recognize that this is both a heavy responsibility and an extraordinary honor. If you are given the chance to wield that influence for good, you should embrace it fully and do the job well.

Thank you all.

question: Why don’t you put it on more frequently yourself, or have White House staff use it more regularly?
interview_answer:
Look, I get tested, and honestly I’m often amazed when I see someone just sitting there—like Joe. Joe seems extremely comfortable staying in a mask. Maybe he doesn’t want to show his face; I really don’t know what his issue is. He’ll be far away from anyone, absolutely no one around—he doesn’t attract crowds—and he’s got those circles, big circles, people spaced way out. There’s absolutely no need for him to be masked in that situation.

In my case, we’re tested—I’m tested, my people are tested. Anyone coming into the Oval Office, it’s a serious process. Whoever they are—even foreign leaders—they all get tested. So my situation is different. And if I didn’t have that kind of setup, I’d probably wear a mask more often. But I have worn masks. I especially prefer to use them when I’m in hospitals, more to protect others than myself. All right? Thank you. Yes.

question: Have you made a resolution for the New Year?
interview_answer: For the New Year, I’m resolving to treat the White House press corps more kindly. [] I mean that, definitely.

question: You've argued you can defeat Trump again. Do you believe you're uniquely able to do that?
interview_answer:
I might not be the sole person who could beat him, but I understand him deeply. And I understand the threat he poses to our democratic system. We’ve already seen what that looks like once.

On the questions about my age and the polls, I keep hearing about surveys showing my favorability in the low‑ to mid‑40s, around 42 to 46 percent. But everyone running for reelection in a similar position in the cycle has been there. That’s not unusual. You’re portraying it as if I’m uniquely underwater. That’s simply not the case.

Second, when those same polls ask people to judge the job I’ve actually done, the numbers are strongly positive. Around 58 percent approve of what we’ve accomplished—from the CHIPS Act to the broader agenda we’ve put in place.

We’ve created roughly 12 million new jobs. We’ve added about 800,000 manufacturing jobs. The economy is growing. We’re in a position where, on climate, we’ve committed more funding and support to confront the climate crisis than any other country on Earth.

So we’re making progress. And the reason I’m running again is that there’s more work that has to be completed.

Also, think about the situation I walked into when I took office. I inherited a country with sharply increased debt from his four years in charge. I inherited a United States whose credibility around the world had been badly damaged by the “America First” approach.

At my first G‑7 meeting, I said, “America is back.” And one of the other leaders asked me, “For how long? For how long?” There was real doubt about whether the United States could still lead the free world. We’ve restored that leadership, and we’re leading again.

Those same polls you’re citing—look at what they say about whether I’ve managed to unify NATO and our European allies, along with partners in Asia. By any reasonable measure, we have done that. But there’s still more we need to accomplish.

On my age, I honestly lose track when I try to say the number out loud—it doesn’t quite register with me.

What I can say is this: voters are going to watch a campaign unfold, and they’ll decide for themselves whether I’m up to the job or not. I respect them for scrutinizing that closely. I’d do the same in their shoes. I examined it very carefully myself before deciding to run.

I feel strong. I’m energized by what lies ahead. I think we’re very close to turning a corner in a way this country hasn’t in a long time.

I know you’ve heard me say again and again that we’re at an inflection point, but it’s true. What happens over the next two, three, four years will shape the next three or four decades. And I have never been more confident about America’s potential than I am right now.

question: What would you like to say to him now?
interview_answer:
On North Korea, my core message is this: South Korea, the United States, and the broader international community all share a single, overarching goal—we want North Korea to give up its nuclear weapons and to move toward becoming a responsible member of the international community. That outcome would advance peace on the Korean Peninsula and globally, and it would also be in North Korea’s own long‑term interest for development and stability. That is how I see it.

To persuade North Korea to choose that path and to change its outlook, we have to act together. In that context, China’s role and leverage are significant, so China’s active participation in these efforts is essential. We have discussed this and we see it in a similar way.

As for China and Russia’s positions, I believe that both of them—as well as the rest of the international community—agree on the need for a nuclear‑free Korean Peninsula and are working closely to press North Korea to head in the right direction. China, for example, has supported U.N. Security Council resolutions responding to North Korea’s missile launches and nuclear tests and is implementing those resolutions seriously.

Russia, likewise, is firmly committed to denuclearization of the Korean Peninsula. It has strongly backed the adoption of U.N. Security Council resolutions on North Korea, and it pushed to include a clear, tough message to North Korea in the G‑8 Foreign Ministers’ joint statement. These constructive steps by China and Russia are crucial for delivering a single, consistent message to North Korea that nuclear weapons are unacceptable and for urging its leadership to make a better choice.

I do not know Kim Jong Un personally. I have never spoken with him, so I cannot offer any judgment about his personal traits. What we can judge are his actions, which so far have been provocative and appear to lead his country into a dead end.

I want to stress that we share the view that we will maintain a robust deterrent and will not offer incentives in response to provocations. At the same time, we remain ready to see North Korea take a peaceful route: to dismantle its nuclear program, honor its international obligations, reengage constructively with the world, and gradually create conditions in which both security and prosperity for the North Korean people can be realized.

If the course North Korea has followed so far has failed to deliver a strong, prosperous nation, then this is an appropriate moment for Kim Jong Un to look at that record and choose a different course. If he does so, not only would we welcome it, but so would the international community at large.

China, Russia, Japan, and the other key participants in the Six‑Party Talks have made this point repeatedly. But a real shift will require a concrete change in North Korea’s behavior. There is a saying in English: Do not focus on what I say; look at what I do. Up to now, we have not seen the kinds of actions from North Korea that would demonstrate it is ready to move in a new, more constructive direction.

question: How does the President assess the likelihood that North Korea will conduct a nuclear test?
interview_answer:
Yes. Both our government in Seoul and our Ministry of National Defense judge that North Korea is already fully prepared to conduct a fourth nuclear test, so they could proceed with it whenever they decide to do so. That is our official assessment. We are not sure exactly when they will choose to act, but we do believe they are now completely ready.

This makes the situation highly tense. Your question also mentioned adopting a more flexible approach—the Dresden initiative is precisely such an example, aimed at creating a win‑win outcome for both Koreas and improving living conditions for people in the North. It is a flexible policy. Yet North Korea is responding with threats of new provocations, including warnings about carrying out another nuclear test. This is something we have to weigh very seriously.

On the transfer of wartime operational control, South Korea and the United States have agreed that the fundamental direction is to reinforce the combined KORUS defense posture. We are clear that this must not produce any deterioration in South Korea’s defense readiness. In light of current escalating threats, the target date is 2015, but we have jointly agreed that the timing and conditions for the transfer can be reconsidered.

Therefore, at this moment, I do not think it is appropriate for me to specify an exact date or precise conditions. The defense authorities of both countries will work together to coordinate these details, and we will urge them to move forward with that coordination.

question: How do you view Afghanistan’s prospects going forward in light of this specific issue?
interview_answer:
Madam, the young person I granted clemency to was actually a 14‑year‑old boy from Pakistan’s South Waziristan tribal region. His father sent him to a madrassa for religious schooling because he could no longer afford regular school fees; the family’s money was going toward treatment for the boy’s mother, who had a serious heart condition. After the father enrolled him in the madrassa, the boy vanished. Several months later, the father learned that his son had been arrested in Afghanistan, so he came here to find out what had happened.

When I saw that this was a teenager—indeed, a minor in the eyes of the law—an innocent child whom terrorists had exploited to make him kill himself and murder other innocent people, I concluded it was the right and just choice to pardon him. I wanted to give him another chance at an education and at a normal life, and to send his mother the clear assurance that her son would be returned to her. I am very satisfied with that decision.

This case teaches us something important about the nature of our common enemies: they are people who recruit children, indoctrinate them, and drive them to their deaths. The world needs to understand plainly the kind of evil we are confronting—the utterly ruthless individuals we are up against, who feel nothing for children, infants, or adolescents. We now know that terrorists are literally trading in suicide bombers. Our government offices have received phone calls from handlers offering to sell these would‑be bombers to us. It has turned into a market, a vile business, run by merchants of death. It is therefore our responsibility to eliminate them. Thank you very much. Thank you very much. Thank you.

question: At this point, are you prepared to reach an agreement?
interview_answer: I am willing to reach an agreement only if it is a fair agreement and a strong one for the United States. If it is not, I will not agree to it. We have billions and billions of dollars already coming in, and I believe we are going to arrive at a deal. I truly do. I’m even more convinced of that now than I was some time ago. We are going to reach one, and it will be a fair agreement. But keep this in mind: when you talk about fairness, China has been drawing more than five hundred billion dollars a year out of this country, year after year, for a very long time. It needed to be stopped. As a private citizen, I used to speak out about this. It’s one of the reasons I’m President now. And we are in a very strong spot. Our economy is performing well. Our unemployment rate is the lowest it has been in over 50 years. Unemployment for African Americans, for Asians, for Hispanics is at the best levels ever recorded—historic figures. We are in a very strong position. Our consumers are solid. Walmart just reported results that were truly staggering. That’s an excellent indicator, really the ultimate one—how retailers are doing and how major companies are doing. We are performing extremely well. And I think we are going to reach an agreement with China. And I think that, in time, we will reach an agreement with them as well.

question: Can you explain exactly what particular measures you’re talking about here?
interview_answer:
To start, one example would be improving how we collect and analyze information on firearms that end up with criminals and how we more efficiently trace those guns; there are likely several actions we can pursue through executive or administrative authority rather than waiting on new laws from Congress.

Regarding people rushing out to buy more guns, we've already seen for years that opponents of basic gun safety or gun control reforms are very effective at stoking fear among gun owners that the Federal Government is on the verge of confiscating all their firearms. There is almost certainly a financial incentive behind that message, because it clearly boosts sales and profits.

I’ve consistently maintained that responsible gun owners—people who own a firearm for self‑defense, hunting, or sport—have nothing to fear from the kinds of proposals we’re discussing. The core question is not whether we respect the Second Amendment; we do. The core question is whether we can adopt reasonable measures that prevent someone like the Newtown shooter from walking into a school and slaughtering children with terrifying speed. It’s hard to believe we can’t take at least some action to reduce that risk.

The difficulty is that even the smallest suggestion of prudent, narrowly tailored legislation tends to ignite this narrative that mass confiscation is imminent and that everyone’s guns are about to be seized. That’s the political reality we face. If you look at the first four years of my administration, what we’ve actually done is focus on tightening and enforcing existing laws. And it’s very difficult for anyone to make a serious case that gun owners’ rights have been violated during that time.

question: Have you thought about doing that?
interview_answer: Yes, I would absolutely take that step. In fact, I’m prepared to require it if that’s what you want. I believe it’s necessary, and I also believe some Governors were far too lenient about nursing homes. The problem was clear from the very start. Look at Washington State, where about 26 to 28 people died very early on. My focus would have been nursing homes—and I did say nursing homes. That’s where the attention needed to be. Some States—many, actually—are doing this now, but in my view every State should be. They have the resources to do it. They need to be doing nursing home testing. That’s a major point of weakness.

question: Is an additional economic stimulus package necessary now?
interview_answer: No, not at this point, because we need to watch how the economy develops and how well the first stimulus actually works. Remember, the initial stimulus bill was the very first major step we took, just a couple of weeks after I took office. At that time, no one fully grasped how deep this recession would be. Only much later did we receive data showing how sharply the economy had fallen. So it’s understandable that our early projections, especially on unemployment, turned out to be off. Given current trends, it’s now quite evident that unemployment is likely to rise above 10 percent, since job growth usually lags behind renewed business investment and hiring, even after the broader economy begins to improve. And we have not yet reached a real recovery. So I expect that we’re facing a tough year ahead, and a challenging stretch overall.

question: Is there any existing policy you’d actually want to keep in effect?
interview_answer: Honestly, very few, I’d say. I’m at the far other end on that.

question: And what about Biden?
interview_answer: Listen, Biden and his son are outright corrupt. And you’re aware of that. His son walks away with millions of dollars. He has no expertise at all. You know it, and so do we. Alright, go ahead and ask your next question.

question: If the answer is yes, what steps is the U.S. taking to increase mask production?
interview_answer: We can secure a large supply. In fact, we’ve already placed substantial orders in case the need arises. We might not end up needing that amount—you know that—but we’re planning for the worst possible case. We’ll be fully supplied very quickly. However, I don’t believe we’ll get anywhere close to that situation. I truly don’t think we’ll be anywhere near it. Our borders are tightly managed. Flights coming in from the relevant regions we’ve discussed are tightly managed. I don’t see us ever getting close to that point. Next question.

question: Are you open to holding direct talks with TikTok in this deal?
interview_answer: No, we’re not engaging with them. We’re in discussions with the firms involved. Technically, it’s a business, but it is a business operating inside China, which effectively means China. Any agreement must deliver major advantages to the United States, and we require absolute security. All right?

question: Describe the President’s attitude and way of communicating.
interview_answer: No, I did not see him acting either tougher or more accommodating than usual. To me, he was as he has always been: plainspoken and to the point. Do I believe he’s prepared to find middle ground on a range of matters? Yes. I think he recognizes that the U.S. elections—which still leave a few things unsettled—nonetheless sent a very clear signal globally that the United States intends to remain fully engaged. The Republicans who were re‑elected, together with the Democrats, share the belief that we will stay active on the world stage and that we have a firm sense of our direction. So I don’t perceive any increase or decrease in his level of confrontation. We spoke very frankly with each other about areas of disagreement and about issues where we weren’t entirely sure of the other’s stance. And we decided—and have already done so—to put in place channels for our senior officials to meet in depth, to work through those questions, or, if they remain unresolved, to clarify exactly on what grounds they remain unresolved.

question: Is the President’s move contingent on that legislative package, or entirely separate from it?
interview_answer: I’m not acting until the Democrats pass it. All right?

question: What judgment has the President reached about the military strategy promoted by Donald Rumsfeld?
interview_answer:
No. I firmly back the concept of a smaller, more flexible Army that can rapidly deploy to confront 21st‑century threats. I also endorsed his review of our force posture and his proposals to relocate units from older installations—bases that, for instance, were originally positioned to counter the Soviet Union in Europe. He has made real, substantive reforms at the Defense Department, and I am a strong supporter of those changes.

That said, endorsing those reforms does not rule out boosting the overall troop levels of the Army and Marine Corps. I lean toward believing that expanding end strength is wise because I recognize we are entering a long-term struggle against radicals and extremists, and we must ensure our military can sustain operations over many years. I am not forecasting a specific battlefield, but I am saying that it will take time for the ideology of freedom to prevail over the ideology of hatred.

I feel this very deeply: we are at the outset of an ideological confrontation that will decide whether your children live in security. If we fail in the Middle East—if we fail in Iraq, or retreat into isolationism—we will leave a generation of young Americans facing a constant danger from abroad. Therefore, we will succeed in Iraq. And we will stand by emerging democracies when we encounter them—democracies like Lebanon, and, we hope, a Palestinian state living peacefully alongside Israel, as well as the new democracy in Iraq.

It serves our national interest to link security measures with a political strategy that frees and empowers people, giving them the ability to shape their own destinies. I am convinced that most people in the Middle East desire exactly that—to be able to direct their own future—and it is in our interest to help them achieve it.

question: Do you anticipate any harm to U.S.–China relations, given that you still rely on Beijing’s cooperation in handling Iran and North Korea’s nuclear challenges?
interview_answer:
First, I have great respect for the Dalai Lama. Second, I stand firmly for religious liberty, and so does he. Third, I enjoy attending Congressional Gold Medal ceremonies. I believe it’s important for the President to show up and recognize people whom Congress has chosen to honor, and I intend to be there. 

I informed President Hu directly that I would be attending the ceremony—in fact, I raised the issue myself. I told him I’m going because I want to pay tribute to this man. I have repeatedly told the Chinese leadership that respecting religious freedom serves their own long‑term interests. I’ve also urged them to meet with the Dalai Lama, and I’ll repeat that message today at the ceremony on Capitol Hill. If they were to sit down with him, they would see that he is committed to peace and reconciliation. I believe it would benefit China to welcome him there and to engage with him.

So my appearance today is not a surprise to the Chinese government; as I’ve said, I discussed it openly with President Hu so he would fully understand my reasons. They’re unhappy about it, of course, but I do not believe it will seriously harm our relationship. In fact, I don’t think relations are ever damaged when an American President speaks out in favor of religious tolerance and religious freedom as a national good. I make that point every time I meet with him.

David. Good to have you back.

question: Why do you believe it’s reasonable to rely on Russia and Vladimir Putin?
interview_answer:
I do not believe it’s reasonable to simply rely on Russia and Vladimir Putin, and that’s exactly why we have to probe whether we can achieve a real halt in the fighting—one that truly stops the aerial bombardment and the civilian deaths and devastation we’ve seen the Assad regime carry out. And Russia may not deliver that outcome, either because it chooses not to, or because it does not actually have enough leverage over Assad. That is what we intend to find out.

So we are entering this process with no illusions. We are fully aware that Russia has backed a brutal regime and a leader in Assad who has wrecked his own country just to hold on to power. What began as peaceful demonstrations has ended in the collapse of an entire, relatively advanced society. Whenever you’re trying to negotiate any kind of arrangement with a regime and a state like that, you have to approach it with real skepticism.

At the same time, if we can secure a true suspension of hostilities that stops indiscriminate bombing, shields civilians, opens the door for humanitarian aid, and creates some kind of route to start the tough political talks inside Syria, then we are obligated to attempt it, because the alternative is endless civil war.

I’ve been grappling with this for many years. I’m fairly sure a good share of my gray hair comes from Syria briefings. And there’s not a single meeting I leave without asking whether there is anything else we haven’t tried—whether there is a Plan F, G, or H that might bring this conflict to a close so Syrians can rebuild their lives, we can restore peace, and we can ease the refugee crisis that has emerged. The choices are constrained when you’re dealing with a civil war like this, with a dictator indifferent to his people, with vicious terrorist groups ready to impose their own brand of tyranny, and with moderate opposition forces and ordinary citizens who are often outgunned and outnumbered. It is an extremely tough situation. But we still have to give diplomacy a chance.

There will be some clear minimum requirements before we move beyond basic military deconfliction to broader cooperation with Russia. That includes real restraint from the regime, which, up to now, we have not seen.

At the outset of this latest attempt at a cessation of hostilities, we did see some lives spared and a modest reduction in violence. But violations of the truce have increased to the point that it almost doesn’t function, especially in the northwest of the country. So we are going to see whether we can secure an arrangement that endures. If we cannot, then Russia will have unmistakably shown itself to be an irresponsible player on the global stage that backs a murderous regime, and it will have to face that judgment in the international arena.

All right? Thank you all very much.

question: Is there anything you’d like to say to Jimmy Lai?
interview_answer: I want to extend my best wishes to him. From everything I’ve been told, he’s an extraordinary person, and he’s clearly very courageous. So I send him my very best. At the same time, because of what has happened to him and, more broadly, in Hong Kong, we have terminated all the substantial financial advantages we were providing Hong Kong. We were effectively underwriting Hong Kong’s success with major incentives, and that’s what fueled the city’s role in global finance and trade. I’ve ended those benefits. That means the United States will now keep that business. We had given them enormous support and encouragement to help them thrive as a free society. But since their freedom has essentially been stripped away, we will retain all of those incentives here at home, worth many billions of dollars, and that business will flow into our own markets, including places like the New York Stock Exchange and Nasdaq.

question: Lowering insurance premiums
interview_answer: To start, what we’re doing is—if you look at the Department of Labor and also what Secretary Azar is doing—they’ve introduced some excellent health plans that are increasing competition and pushing costs way down. We’re also eliminating the individual mandate, because it treated many people very unfairly. At the same time, we’re still protecting those who truly need coverage. The individual mandate was a terrible policy, because people who couldn’t really afford insurance were being forced to pay a penalty just for not buying it. And the coverage they were being pushed toward wasn’t very good in the first place.

question: Considering leaving the U.S.–Japan defense pact
interview_answer: No, that’s not something I’m considering at all. What I’m saying is that the arrangement itself is lopsided. And I’ve been telling him that for the past six months. I said, look, if Japan is attacked, we step in, we go to war—we are fully engaged. We’re drawn into a fight and firmly obligated to defend Japan. But if the United States is attacked, they don’t have the same obligation. That’s not fair. That’s the kind of agreement we’ve signed. That’s—every agreement looks like that. It’s as if the people who negotiated them either didn’t care or didn’t know what they were doing. But those are the kinds of deals we’re stuck with. That’s just standard. But I have—I told him—we’re going to have to revise it. Because—look, I don’t expect anyone to attack us. But if, in the less likely case, someone did, and we’re defending them, then they need to be required to defend us. And he understands that. And he’s not going to object to that. Yes, go ahead.

question: What additional steps can be taken to stop that from occurring?
interview_answer:
Ed, I’m optimistic about where inflation stands and about job creation. We’ve now had 49 straight months of job growth without interruption, which is a record in the United States. I’m also glad to see the federal deficit coming down. At the same time, like our Treasury Secretary, I fully recognize that the housing sector is weak. We also saw GDP growth driven in part by exports, so there are strong aspects of our economy. But there’s no doubt the housing market is soft.

The key issue is, what actions do we take to assist homeowners? I do not believe we should rescue the lenders, but I do believe we should adopt policies that help people remain in their homes. That’s why modernizing the FHA is so important. Expanding FHA’s scope will allow more homeowners to refinance into more stable loans.

One of the problems in housing has come from the way mortgages have been bundled into financial assets, which often makes it difficult for a borrower to even identify someone who can help them refinance. In the past, you could walk into your neighborhood savings and loan, work out the mortgage face-to-face, and if you later hit financial trouble, that same person was there to help you restructure. Today, in many cases, the original lender no longer owns the mortgage.

Turning mortgages into securities has supplied a lot of liquidity, and that’s beneficial. But it also creates a challenge: how do we make sure people understand the terms of the loans they took out, and how do we help them refinance so they can stay in their homes? That’s exactly what Secretary Paulson and Secretary Jackson have been focused on, working closely with private-sector partners to make refinancing more accessible.

We also need to revise the tax laws. Right now, if you refinance your home, you can face an added tax burden. If our goal is to help people keep their homes, then we need to change the tax code. That’s why I recently spoke with Senator Stabenow and thanked her for sponsoring important tax legislation that will make it easier for homeowners to stay put.

So there are concrete measures we can take, Ed. At the same time, we have to recognize that some of this has to run its course. When there are more homes for sale than there are buyers, prices usually drop, and the market has to adjust. I’m not in the business of making forecasts, but I can say that I’m confident about many of the economic indicators we’re seeing in the United States.

Peter [Peter Baker, Washington Post].

question: And regarding fentanyl?
interview_answer:
Yes, we are addressing that issue. Just as I mentioned about migration—how, at first, most migrants came from Central America and Mexico, but recently we are seeing many more from Venezuela, Nicaragua, Colombia, and Ecuador—the situation has changed. These shifts reflect how people are being forced to leave their hometowns and countries for multiple reasons.

With drugs, something similar is happening. It’s no longer mainly cannabis, marijuana, or poppies. It’s not just cocaine either. Now we are facing fentanyl and other chemical substances, which are among the most dangerous drugs and extremely harmful to people because they are causing such a high number of deaths.

So we are confronting this in an organized and systematic way. In Mexico, this challenge led us to place all port and customs operations under military control, specifically all maritime customs posts, because fentanyl and other precursor chemicals arrive from Asia and are then processed in clandestine laboratories. We are working to block the entry of these precursors and to dismantle and destroy those labs.

The Navy is now responsible for operating and overseeing ports and sea customs. For example, there used to be a large volume of chemical trafficking through the ports of Manzanillo and Lázaro Cárdenas. Now the Navy controls those customs posts.

And all land customs along the northern border have been put under the command of the Ministry of National Defense. In this way, we are actively fighting fentanyl and its precursors, and we are doing so because we genuinely care. No human suffering is foreign to us. We truly want to help address what is happening in the United States—the many fentanyl overdose deaths there.

But as we discussed today, this is not a problem affecting only the United States. If we fail to confront this scourge, we will suffer its consequences in our own country too. That is why we must act in a coordinated manner. That is exactly what we have been doing, and this was part of our discussions at the summit. Our joint commitment appears in the communiqué that will be released. Our goal is to protect life.

As I explained to Prime Minister Trudeau, President Biden, and their teams, my administration has only two major government-wide publicity campaigns. One of them is focused on discouraging drug use: a campaign that says, essentially, “Don’t do drugs.” We must emphasize that dimension. Gang culture and organized crime have acquired a kind of public glamour. There are television series about cartels and organized crime that present a lifestyle many viewers find attractive: huge houses, luxury, fancy cars, very attractive men and women, all impeccably dressed, covered in jewelry, wielding great power, casually calling a police chief, a general, or even a president on the phone. That image is being promoted widely.

By contrast, we see very little content showing the real harm caused by fentanyl—for example, how a young person’s life can be destroyed in six months, and how doses can contain substances like muriatic acid.
