Is that information routinely shared with the public? Are people clearly warned about this? No, they are not. So we will launch an information campaign to change that.

I also told the President and the Prime Minister: look at vaping. It is often advertised as harmless, with claims like, “It’s just a few ingredients; nothing serious.” But our own research shows that these devices contain more than 30 dangerous, carcinogenic substances. Yet, because of lobbying, corruption, and the way advertising is handled, their use continues to be allowed and normalized.

Many parents do not realize the harm that vaporizers are causing to their children. This is something we need to confront. But it is not solely the government’s responsibility. The media also have a crucial role. You can help a great deal by spreading accurate information—radio, television, all outlets should devote time to explaining the health damage caused by drugs, to guiding people, and to making it clear that one can be successful and happy without falling into drug addiction and those deadly traps.

That is the substance of what we have been talking about. I have probably taken more time than I should, especially given the cold weather. Thank you very much to all of you, sincerely. Thank you. I want it on record that I am not sure which questions I may have left unanswered, but I am available to respond later. Thank you very much.

question: Chances that a third U.S.–North Korea summit will take place before this year ends.
interview_answer:
Yes. To respond directly, yes, I have received an invitation to go to Panmunjom with President Trump this afternoon. That said, as I noted earlier, today’s primary focus will be on discussions between the United States and North Korea. Naturally, President Trump and Chairman Kim will meet again and hold follow‑up talks, and I believe—and am confident—that this will be highly important for the negotiations that will continue after today’s encounter.

In addition, there will indeed be talks between the United States and North Korea today. But regarding inter‑Korean talks, those will take place later, not today.

On your question about a third U.S.–North Korea summit, my view is that the outcome of today’s meeting—how the reunion and the talks proceed this afternoon—will largely determine whether and when a third summit is held. I therefore do anticipate some movement forward.

As for your final point, what I explained earlier was that if North Korea proceeds with a truly sincere dismantlement of its nuclear facilities in Yongbyon, that would mark our arrival at the starting point of irreversible, substantial denuclearization. If that step is genuinely carried out, then the international community would be in a position to consider a limited easing of sanctions. My earlier comments should be understood in that context.

question: Can you clarify your position on the moratorium?
interview_answer: No. I plan to review all of their proposals. We’ll examine them.

question: What has the President discovered about Democrats’ outlook and mindset?
interview_answer:
That’s an interesting way to frame it. Let me start by saying I see Democrats as patriotic Americans who care deeply about this country—going back to Hutch’s thoughtful question. I genuinely do. I was very thankful for the way they received me at the State of the Union. They treated me in a cordial and respectful way that allowed me to lay out what I stand for. I was also grateful for the welcome I got when I spoke at the Democratic retreat in Virginia.

My takeaway from that gathering was that we have a lot of common ground. We’re all people who actually filled out the paperwork and ran for office; we’ve all put our families through the strain of political life; we all wanted to serve the country; we all care intensely about what happens in Washington, in America, and around the world.

My hope is that we can translate that into concrete, positive legislation, because there’s a widespread assumption that our disagreements over Iraq will make it impossible to cooperate in other areas. I don’t accept that view. And I hope events prove me right—and the way to test that is simple: will I be able to sign bills that both sides helped to craft?

One prime example is the budget. There appears to be consensus that we should balance the budget. I’ve put forward a specific path to do that, showing that we can reach balance in five years without raising taxes. I want to work closely with Democratic leaders, as well as with Republicans, to make that happen.

Another promising area is immigration reform. As you know, I strongly believe in securing our borders, and we’ve already taken steps to strengthen enforcement. But I also think real border security requires a temporary‑worker program so people aren’t forced to sneak into the country to find work. They should be able to come here in an orderly way, which would ease the burden on our Border Patrol. That way, agents can focus less on workers doing jobs Americans aren’t doing and more on terrorists, criminals, and gun smugglers, to protect both our country and Mexico.

I also recognize we must address the situation of the roughly 12 million people already here illegally. I’ve said many times that we cannot realistically round them up and deport them all. That doesn’t make sense to me; maybe some people think that’s the answer, but I don’t. At the same time, I don’t support giving them automatic citizenship—which, in my view, amounts to amnesty. We’re ready to work with both Democrats and Republicans on a comprehensive immigration package that deals with all of these elements.

Energy policy is another opportunity for cooperation. We’ve already done considerable work to promote alternative energy sources. The United States has done more than any other nation to develop alternatives and renewables, with the goals of sustaining economic growth, achieving greater energy independence, and protecting the environment. I’m eager to partner with Democrats on the energy independence proposals I’ve laid out.

One of those proposals is a set of mandatory fuel standards that would rely on alternative fuels for vehicles. Ethanol is the most obvious place to begin, but we also need to invest in technologies that allow us to produce fuel from non‑corn feedstocks—like switchgrass and wood chips, for example.

The problem with depending heavily on corn is that when demand for corn stays high, prices rise, and then your hog farmer becomes unhappy with the alternative energy strategy. So it’s critical that new technologies come online quickly, both to relieve the pressure on corn used for ethanol and to stabilize the broader corn market. That’s something we can tackle with Congress.

Health care offers another area for joint work. I recently received a letter from a bipartisan group of Senators expressing interest in working on health‑care issues. They said they liked some of my proposals. My point is that we have a chance to cooperate on helping the uninsured get access to private health coverage so they can receive quality care. There’s real room for collaboration there.

The Governors will be in Washington soon, and I’ll have Secretary Leavitt brief them on the affordable grants initiative, which is part of our broader health‑care strategy, including tax‑code reforms.

Finally, the No Child Left Behind Act has to be reauthorized. I fully recognize that, if you read the papers—which I do, at least at times—and listen closely, you’ll hear criticism from both parties. Some say there’s too much testing; others say they don’t like the accountability requirements, or raise other objections. But the law is working. It’s a sound approach.

There is an income gap in this country, which I discussed in my remarks on Wall Street. It’s clear to me that our children must receive a strong education so that, in a global economy, the high‑quality jobs of the 21st century remain here at home. That starts with solid schooling. So I will forcefully argue that No Child Left Behind should be renewed—it’s producing results, it’s a vital law—and I’ll reach out to Democrats and Republicans alike to get it reauthorized.

So, Mike, there are many areas where we can work together. I’d also say we’re still in the early stages of this Congress. It’s a two‑year term, and we have time to collaborate on significant legislation. I’m encouraged by how things have begun. In fact, this afternoon I’ll be meeting with members of both parties, from both chambers, to talk about how we can keep moving forward on our legislative agenda.

I’ve told Democrats—and they’ve told me—that neither side is going to walk away from its core principles. I don’t expect them to drop theirs, and they shouldn’t expect me to abandon mine. But there are still ways to cooperate and produce legislative results that serve the common good. That’s what the American people want to see, and I believe it’s achievable. Will it require effort? Yes, it will. But that’s fine. That’s what we’re all being paid to do.

Richard [Richard Wolffe, Newsweek].

question: Is the Speaker’s leadership contest making these talks harder?
interview_answer:
Yes, the race for Speaker is making these talks harder. That was a rhetorical question; of course it adds complications. It will make the negotiations more difficult. But on the debt ceiling itself, we are not reopening that fight.

Let me quickly remind everyone what the debt ceiling is about. Raising the debt limit does not give us permission to spend new money; it simply allows us to pay the obligations we have already taken on. It is how the United States preserves its strong credit rating and the full faith and credit of our government. Traditionally, we do not play games with it. If we do, it can have major consequences for the global economy and could send our financial system into the kind of spiral we saw in 2007 and 2008. It is a very bad idea to use it as leverage.

So we are not going to bargain over whether to raise it. It has to be increased within the next five weeks. Even though the current continuing resolution funds the Government for ten weeks, the deadline to lift the debt ceiling is five. That means we have less time to deal with that issue.

Here is the key point: Mitch McConnell, John Boehner, myself, Nancy Pelosi, and Harry Reid have all been in touch about working out a budget deal. Yes, Speaker Boehner’s decision to resign makes that effort more complicated. But I still believe there is a workable route to a sensible agreement that lifts the sequester caps so we can adequately fund both defense and nondefense priorities, while still exercising responsible control over our deficits—and that we can reach that agreement soon. The numbers are straightforward; the arithmetic is what it is.

I’ve urged that we begin that process right away, push through over the coming weeks, and avoid dragging in side issues that could derail a budget deal.

I know, for instance, that many Republicans are very upset about Planned Parenthood. I strongly disagree with their position and believe they misrepresent what Planned Parenthood does. But I recognize their conviction and I respect that they care deeply about it. Still, they cannot let that dispute endanger the entire U.S. economy—just as I should not put the entire budget at risk to force action on gun violence. I am just as passionate about that issue, and I think the evidence supports my view. But if I were to tell Republicans that unless they passed gun safety laws to reduce mass shootings I would shut down the Government and refuse to sign a debt ceiling increase, that would be irresponsible. And the American people would rightly reject that approach.

The same standard applies to them. Some battles have to be fought on their own terms. If they want to cut off funding for Planned Parenthood, there is a legitimate way to attempt that: pass a bill and override my veto. That holds for many other issues where they disagree with me. That is how our democratic system operates, and I accept that.

But at the end of the day, you still have to govern. I hope the next Speaker recognizes that the reason Speaker Boehner or Mitch McConnell could not repeal Obamacare, could not abolish the Department of Education, or could not deport every undocumented immigrant in this country is not that they lacked conservative convictions. It is that our system of government requires compromise, and it prevents any one side from getting everything it wants—just as I cannot enact everything I want on immigration reform or gun safety.

And when I don’t get everything I want, that does not entitle me to throw a fit and threaten the economy, risking the livelihoods of hard‑working Americans who are only now recovering from a severe recession. That would be the wrong course of action.

question: Are you saying you would veto any bill that provides money to the Postal Service?
interview_answer: Absolutely not. Definitely not.

question: Mitt Romney’s remark that an Iranian nuclear weapon would transform the world
interview_answer:
Okay, Mike, you’ve really put a couple of issues on the table, so let me begin with Iran, since that’s dominated the headlines these past several days. When I took office, Iran was united internally, advancing steadily, and had already made serious headway on its nuclear program, while the international community was split over how to respond. Over the last three years, we’ve built and implemented an unprecedented regime of tough, crippling sanctions on Iran. Those sanctions are inflicting real, significant economic pain. The international community is now acting in concert, and Iran finds itself politically isolated.

I’ve made it clear that we will not accept Iran obtaining a nuclear weapon. My policy is not to tolerate or contain a nuclear-armed Iran; my policy is to stop Iran from getting a nuclear weapon in the first place. If Iran were to succeed, it could spark a regional arms race, undermine decades of work on nonproliferation, and increase the risk that nuclear materials could end up with terrorists. We’ve been working very closely with all our allies, including Israel, to pursue this strategy.

At this point, I believe there is still a window in which this can be resolved through diplomacy. That view is not mine alone; it is shared by our top U.S. intelligence officials and by leading Israeli intelligence officials. So we will keep tightening the pressure, while also keeping open a path the Iranian leadership can take to rejoin the international community—by providing credible assurances that they are complying with their obligations and not seeking a nuclear weapon.

That’s the record of what we’ve actually done. By contrast, people on the campaign trail don’t carry the responsibilities of the presidency. They are not the Commander in Chief. When I hear how casually some of them talk about going to war, I think about the real costs of war: the sacrifice we ask of our young men and women in uniform, the long-term effects on their lives, and the consequences for our national security and our economy.

This is not some kind of political exercise, and there is nothing casual about decisions on war and peace. When I listen to some of these candidates, there’s a lot of swagger and tough talk, but when you press them on exactly what they would do differently, they end up describing the very steps we’ve already taken over the past three years. That tells me their posture is more about politics than about solving a very hard problem.

One thing we have not done is start a war. If any of these candidates think now is the time to go to war with Iran, they should say that plainly. And they should be prepared to tell the American people exactly why they would choose that course and what the consequences would be. Anything short of that is just rhetoric.

question: Are you going to accept overseas funding for this?
interview_answer: Yes, I’ll likely end up taking some overseas contributions, but I can’t say for sure yet, Ken. We just rolled this deal out, and honestly I’ve been tied up with other things, like gas prices and my Africa visit, and I haven’t reviewed the fundraising plan yet. So, in truth, I really can’t fully answer your question right now.

question: What guidance should Obama receive on handling Pakistan’s government in relation to terrorists? Should he prioritize diplomatic engagement or rely on military force?
interview_answer:
Yes, thanks. To begin, I belong to a different political party than President‑elect Obama, but I want him to succeed. I want his policies to work. That’s why we’ve put real effort into making this transition as smooth as possible.

Next, I find it notable that he chose Secretary Gates—who has served as my Secretary of Defense for the last couple of years—to stay on and serve as his Secretary of Defense as well. That choice should clearly signal to people in Afghanistan that this handover will be orderly and consistent.

I also expect you’ll see additional American forces deployed here as soon as we can get them to those regions of the country where the Taliban is most active. I recall when President Karzai, President Musharraf, and I had that much‑discussed dinner at the White House. The core issues we discussed then are essentially unchanged: extremists, terrorists, and those who use car bombs and suicide attacks to achieve political ends threaten all of us. Pakistan needs a broad, integrated plan to help its government confront those who inflict terrible harm on their own citizens and on the people of Afghanistan.

So, directly answering you, what’s needed is a joint strategy. We must cooperate and work together in a constructive manner. And we’re moving forward on that path. I recently met President Zardari—I believe it was in New York. I had not met him before, and I reiterated that I’m someone who is determined to defend our country and our citizens. The top responsibility of a U.S. President is to protect the American people from attacks.

He replied that I didn’t need to explain extremist violence to him—terrorists murdered his wife. So there is intensive consultation under way with President Zardari. I understand you’ll soon be meeting with him yourself. That’s positive, and I welcome it.

Olivier [Olivier Knox, Agence France‑Presse]. Military Operations in Afghanistan/

question: Did they bring you down to the bunker?
interview_answer: No, instead we were brought over into the Oval Office.

question: Can you make it clear to your neighboring countries that you intend to stay fully engaged in South American trade?
interview_answer:
I am visiting South and Central America to promote a positive, constructive diplomacy carried out by my administration on behalf of the American people. The core of my message to the nations in our region is that we care about people’s lives and believe their circumstances can be improved in several concrete ways—first and foremost, through investment. So we are asking: how can we maintain a productive dialogue with our neighbors about how to ensure investment benefits are more widely shared?

I am also emphasizing that U.S. taxpayers are extremely generous in terms of direct assistance. Since I took office, we have doubled our annual bilateral aid to Latin America from $800 million to $1.6 billion. Most of these resources are directed toward social justice initiatives, such as education and health care.

I am fully aware—and I witnessed it again yesterday in São Paulo—that many American NGOs, faith-based organizations, and individual citizens also show their concern for the poor through concrete projects that give people real opportunities. Yesterday in São Paulo, we visited a very affluent area that was ringed by a favela. In the middle of a setting marked by despair, there was a small center of hope. Some of its funding came from concerts held in the United States, where ordinary Americans contributed so that the program could continue.

This trip is therefore a clear demonstration of our commitment to cooperate with the countries in our neighborhood. I have traveled to Central and South America many times as President because I understand that a stable and prosperous region directly serves U.S. interests.

I would describe our approach as quiet, effective diplomacy—diplomacy focused on helping people, on raising living standards, and on reflecting the deep compassion of the American people.

And, Mr. President, I am grateful for the opportunity to meet with you and to discuss how we can jointly advance our national interests and those of our wider region. Turning directly to your question: Uruguay’s strategy for its integration into the international economy is already well established and very clear. We support an open model of integration and are strong supporters of the regional integration process. We are part of this regional framework and have no desire to leave it. Our trade ties and cultural and historical bonds with our sister nations in the region are solid and enduring. At the same time, we do not want a closed model of integration but an open one.

In our view, MERCOSUR should be able to connect with other blocs and other countries around the globe, and each member—Uruguay included—must retain its sovereign right to develop bilateral relationships with other integration schemes or individual nations. That is the framework in which we are acting, and it is the framework under which we are conducting our discussions with the President of the United States.

Thank you, Mr. President.

question: Is a letter like that really sufficient?
interview_answer:
No, it isn’t. I believe the Federal Reserve has a very significant role to play, and a big part of that role is psychological. If you look at it, the Fed wields enormous influence—some of it through market psychology and some of it through concrete actions. This is the kind of step they should have taken earlier and gone even further with, especially because other countries that are far less strong than we are are already doing more. We’re the leading nation in the world. It’s our currency, we have every advantage, and yet our interest rates are not the lowest. Our benchmark rate is higher.

Look at Germany, look at Japan, and look at other economies: many of them actually have negative interest rates. We’re not in that position because of the decisions our Fed has made. And now we’re facing this situation. You can see Germany cutting rates and pumping huge amounts of money into their economy—a massive injection. I haven’t heard our Fed step up and say they’ll take comparable action.

Our Federal Reserve should be setting the pace, not trailing behind. Right now the Fed has been following others, not leading. We need a Federal Reserve that leads. At a minimum, our rates should match those of other countries, and ideally they should be lower. We should not be paying higher interest, but that’s what we’re stuck with, and we’re the top-tier economy.

Think about it: we are by far the largest economy on Earth. China was supposed to surpass us this year—they’ve been predicting that for about five years, that in 2019 they’d overtake us. They’re nowhere near doing that, and they won’t get close as long as there’s competent leadership here. Our relationship with China is fine, but on the specific question of the Fed, our Fed needs to act like a leader. It simply has not been a leader at all.

question: Could it be that, by stating that so early, you moved too fast in taking direct military involvement in this conflict off the table?
interview_answer: Absolutely not.

question: What is President Trump’s view of how dangerous Russia is to this area?
interview_answer:
Yes, I do. Absolutely, I do. I consider the situation very tense and confrontational. In my view, Russia has behaved in an aggressive way. They understand and respond to power. They respect strength, like anyone else does. And we now possess unmatched strength—more than ever. Under the prior administration our forces were being run down. Our aircraft were aging, worn out, sometimes not even airworthy. They were using salvaged parts. That’s not acceptable for the United States. We build the best aircraft and missiles in the world.

We have, to put it mildly, massively upgraded our armed forces. Our military is essentially being rebuilt right now, with literally hundreds and hundreds of new planes, missiles, and every system you can think of. The defense industry has never had it better, because I secured $700 billion from Congress this year and $716 billion last year—far above what was expected.

I saw this in two ways. First, as a military necessity—because that always comes before everything else, even jobs. Second, as an engine for jobs. We manufacture all of this here, which means hundreds of thousands of jobs to equip us with the finest military on earth. And Russia takes note of that. They recognize that strength. So I agree with the President of Poland. I think his assessment is correct. And given Poland’s long, difficult history with Russia, he absolutely has grounds to feel that way. Okay?

question: How do you respond to criticism that your administration hasn’t been tough enough in bringing criminal cases against Wall Street firms?
interview_answer:
First, on the question of criminal cases on Wall Street: one of the core problems with the Lehman collapse, the broader financial meltdown, and the entire subprime mortgage mess is that much of what happened wasn’t clearly illegal under existing law; it was unethical, inappropriate, or wildly irresponsible. That’s precisely why we had to enact Dodd–Frank—so that many of those practices would be explicitly barred going forward.

The financial industry is extremely inventive and constantly seeking ways to generate profit—that’s what they do. If there are gaps in the rules, gray areas they can exploit, or opportunities for regulatory arbitrage, they’ll exploit them. So, without weighing in on any specific prosecutions—because that is not my role, that is the Attorney General’s responsibility—I want to be clear: a major source of the public’s anger, and of my own, is that many behaviors that never should have been permitted were not actually violations of the law at the time, even though they caused enormous damage. That is exactly why we moved to establish stronger financial regulations that shield Americans from reckless decisions and irresponsible conduct.

Now, on Solyndra and Fast and Furious: I have said consistently that I have full confidence in Attorney General Holder and how he manages the Department of Justice. He has taken a very aggressive approach to targeting gun trafficking and illicit money flows that support the major drug cartels in Mexico, and we’ve seen significant cooperation between our government and the Mexican government on these issues.

He has stated that he did not know about the specific tactics used in Fast and Furious. I certainly did not. And I think it’s fair to say that both of us would have been deeply disturbed if anyone had proposed deliberately letting guns move across the border when the United States could have stopped it.

He has directed the Inspector General to investigate exactly how this operation unfolded, and I have full faith in him and in that investigative process to determine who made those decisions and how they were approved.

Regarding Solyndra: this loan guarantee program was created before I became President and has historically drawn support from both Democrats and Republicans. The concept behind it is simple: if we want to compete and win in the 21st-century global economy, we must lead in cutting-edge technologies and advanced manufacturing. Clean energy is one of those future-oriented sectors that we need rooted here in the United States if we want long-term economic success.

The loan guarantee program was intended to solve a specific market problem. Many small, innovative firms can secure early-stage “angel” funding—those first few million dollars—but then struggle to attract the larger amounts needed to scale up, especially with novel technologies. At the same time, countries like China and those in Europe are heavily subsidizing these industries, offering major incentives for companies to locate there instead of here. Even when the underlying technology is invented in America, they lure firms overseas by promising help with startup costs, low- or no-interest loans, favorable siting, and other support.

That’s a key reason why we’ve lost our lead in areas like solar and wind energy, even when the innovation started in the United States. The purpose of the loan guarantees was to help close that competitive gap—to encourage these companies to build and hire in America.

From the outset, we understood that a program like this would necessarily involve risk. If there were no risk, we wouldn’t need federal support in the first place. But overall, the portfolio of loans has performed well. It has helped launch, for example, advanced battery manufacturing here in the United States and has supported job creation. We anticipated that some firms would fail; Solyndra is one of those failures. But the decision to back that company was made on the substantive merits. The process was transparent and based on policy judgment, and naturally there were internal debates around such a complex initiative.

I remain confident that the choices were guided by what would benefit the U.S. economy, the American people, and our efforts to put people back to work.

Let me add one final point. I’m aware that a Republican Member of Congress overseeing this issue—someone whose party broadly supported the loan guarantee concept in the past—has now argued that we simply cannot compete with China on solar power. I fundamentally reject that view. I am not prepared to hand over leadership in critical technologies that will shape the strength of our middle class. So we are going to keep pressing to ensure that manufacturing, new enterprises, and new technologies are developed and located here in the United States. Some bets will not pay off, but I will not back down just because other countries are pouring subsidies into these industries.

question: As the nation’s leader, what exactly will you do to keep the country from lurching from one crisis to the next?
interview_answer:
There are a few key pieces to that. First, I intend to keep pushing forward wherever possible on issues that matter most to middle‑class Americans and to people working hard to join the middle class. If you put the budget battles aside for a moment, we’ve already managed to reauthorize the Violence Against Women Act. Bipartisan talks on comprehensive immigration reform are actively moving ahead. There’s strong, cross‑party interest in strengthening our schools, including early childhood education. And there have been serious, constructive conversations on ways to cut gun violence.

My plan is to keep pressing on those family‑centered priorities. We won’t accomplish every goal overnight, but we can still make substantial progress across multiple fronts. That is the first part of my approach.

On the budget, I’ve been laying out a clear case to the American people: we need a balanced strategy for cutting the deficit, and we must recognize that deficit reduction by itself is not an economic program. One of our big problems right now is that not just Congress, but Washington as a whole, is fixated almost exclusively on deficits and often ignores the core question of how we generate jobs. I want both issues on the table at the same time.

For instance, we could immediately put a lot of people back to work rebuilding roads and bridges. This is delayed maintenance that we know cannot be avoided forever. I visited a bridge that links Senator Mitch McConnell’s state with Speaker John Boehner’s state; it was in terrible shape, and everyone understands it needs to be fixed. I’m confident people who use it want to see that happen. The question is how we agree to do it. If we decide to move forward, that project would directly create jobs, help businesses, shorten commutes, and improve safety. Investments like that must be part of the discussion, instead of having an endless fight focused only on where to cut spending.

So my broader point, Ari, is this: I want our constant focus—our "true north"—to be how we help American families thrive. Reducing the deficit is one component of that agenda, and an important one, but it is not the only component. I’m not willing to let disagreement over this single issue freeze progress on everything else.

As I mentioned to Jessica, I’m also hoping that, over time—perhaps after Republicans decide they have held firm on the sequester, feel they’ve made their point, and the caucus is more satisfied—there will be an opening for a more serious, substantive negotiation about the actual drivers of the deficit and how to address them.

The advantage we have as a country is that, even when we hit these gridlocks and have intense partisan clashes, the public tends to be steady, pragmatic, and rooted in common sense. Over time, that practical, commonsense outlook usually prevails. I believe that will be the outcome here as well.

In the meantime, to underscore my view of the sequester: we will get past it. It is not going to be the kind of catastrophic event some have described. It is simply a bad policy—harmful and unnecessary. It will cause real pain for individuals and will drag on the broader economy.

But if Congress comes around in a week, or a month, or even in three months, we will still have plenty of room to accelerate economic growth and move the American people’s agenda forward in a major way. So I see the sequester as a temporary hurdle, not a permanent derailment, on what I believe are very strong long‑term prospects for American growth and strength. Thank you very much. Okay?

question: What specific steps can the United States take to help lower global food costs?
interview_answer:
Yes, I do, and I look at this a bit differently. My understanding is that roughly 85 percent of the increase in world food prices comes from weather conditions, rising demand, and higher energy costs—the basic expense of producing food—and about 15 percent is tied to ethanol, or the growth of ethanol use. Now, high gasoline prices are actually going to drive more money into ethanol as a substitute for gas. And the reality is, it’s in America’s national interest for our farmers to produce energy here at home rather than for us to keep buying energy from unstable regions or from countries that may not be friendly to us.

On the global side, we’re very worried about food prices for Americans, and we’re equally worried about people overseas who don’t have enough to eat. So we are concerned about shortages. Last year, we provided very substantial food aid, and we will continue to be generous this year. In fact, we just drew down about $200 million from the Emerson Trust as part of our ongoing work to respond to food scarcity.

One policy I believe would be—and I know would be—highly effective is for us to purchase food directly from local farmers overseas. That would not only help relieve shortages but also help build the agricultural systems those countries need to become self-reliant and self-sustaining. I’ve formally proposed this approach, but Congress hasn’t acted on it yet, and I strongly urge them to do so. That’s my answer to you, Jim.

question: Regarding unemployment benefits, how far under $600 per week will you accept?
interview_answer: The economy is improving, and I believe we’re on track for very strong growth, especially if the measures I just mentioned are implemented. We want people to return to their jobs and want to be working, rather than ending up in a situation where they’re earning more from benefits than they anticipated. That makes it difficult for employers to bring workers back. That’s why that earlier decision was made. I actually opposed that original approach, but it went through. It still turned out okay because it gave people a critical lifeline, a real lifeline. Now we’re setting it up again, and the current idea is to cover about 70 percent of prior wages. The overall concept of the benefit stays the same, but it would be paid out in somewhat smaller weekly amounts so that people are motivated to return to work, instead of receiving so much that they don’t really need to go back.

question: Isn’t it the moment to overhaul your approach?
interview_answer:
First, let’s look carefully at what we are actually doing. Our military approach is to exert intense pressure on ISIL through sustained airstrikes, while providing training and support on the ground to Iraqi forces. We are now partnering with Syrian forces as well, tightening the noose around ISIL and disrupting its supply routes. Internationally, we are coordinating to choke off their funding streams, including the oil they attempt to sell abroad. We are also conducting targeted strikes on high-value figures, such as the militant featured in the execution videos of captured civilians, and the leader of ISIL in Libya. So our efforts extend beyond Iraq and Syria alone.

On the military side, we are not standing still; we are speeding up what we are already doing. When we identify effective local forces, we deepen our cooperation with them. I have already approved additional Special Operations forces on the ground to improve coordination and effectiveness.

On the counterterrorism side, remember that since the day I took office, we have been alert to the possibility of these types of attacks. Our government maintains a high level of vigilance, and we are constantly strengthening our coordination with European and other partners to pursue every terrorist network we can identify. Every few weeks, I convene my full national security team to review every single threat stream. Whenever we have relevant information, we promptly share it with allies around the globe, including our European partners.

Regarding aviation security, over the past several years we have been upgrading security at airports not only in the United States but overseas as well. We are improving systems to identify passengers who should not be allowed to board and tightening procedures for screening checked luggage.

Diplomatically, we have been working persistently to bring all the key players to the table and achieve recognition that there is a viable, moderate Syrian opposition that can anchor a transitional government. That involves outreach not just to our allies, but also to Russia and Iran, who back the other side, to make clear that ISIL ultimately poses a grave threat to them as well as to us.

So you will see a ramping up of the strategy we have set out, but the core strategy itself is the one that will ultimately succeed. I have been clear from the beginning that this will not be quick.

In the discussions since the Paris attacks, when I hear people insist we must do something different, almost all of the measures they call for are things we are already implementing. The main exception is the call from some quarters to deploy large numbers of U.S. ground troops.

We field the best military on earth, with world-class commanders, and I have spent years in intensive discussions with them about options. It is not just my judgment, but also the shared judgment of my closest civilian and military advisers, that a large-scale ground invasion would be the wrong move. It’s not that our forces could not seize places like Mosul, Raqqa, or Ramadi and drive ISIL out for a time; they certainly could. The problem is what happens after. Without local populations committed to inclusive governance and willing to resist extremist ideologies, those groups reemerge as soon as we leave—unless we are prepared for an open-ended occupation.

Consider what it would mean if we decided to send, say, 50,000 American troops into Syria. If the next major plot is directed from Yemen, do we then send tens of thousands there as well? Or into Libya? Or anywhere else in North Africa or Southeast Asia where a new terrorist hub might arise?

Our approach must be sustainable over the long term. The strategy we are pursuing—relentlessly hitting their targets, restricting ISIL’s operational capacity on the ground whenever possible, systematically eliminating their leaders and command structures, weakening their infrastructure, bolstering Syrian, Iraqi, and Kurdish forces ready to fight them, closing their borders, and compressing the territory they can hold until they are ultimately defeated—is the approach we will continue to follow.

We will keep expanding the coalition of partners committed to this course. Some of the initiatives we try will fall short; others will prove effective. When we identify tactics that work, we will reinforce and expand them. That is how we will proceed.

question: Update on vaccine collaboration and progress:
interview_answer:
Yes. On vaccine collaboration, I would first point you to the joint statements and the remarks released to the press today, which describe this in detail. But to restate the core point: the United States and the Republic of Korea have agreed to establish a broad, structured vaccine partnership between our two countries. Both sides have formally reached this agreement.

The United States brings world‑class vaccine research and development, and Korean firms have advanced biopharmaceutical manufacturing capacity. We will integrate those strengths to expand overall vaccine output and speed up global vaccine distribution, with a particular focus on the Indo‑Pacific. I am confident this will significantly help vaccine supply for that region.

Through this framework, Korea will also benefit by gaining greater stability in its own vaccine supply. In parallel, as part of strengthening the ROK‑U.S. alliance, President Biden has decided that, once the United States is logistically prepared, it will provide vaccines for Korean military personnel. My understanding is that the U.S. side will formally announce this, but I will preview it now.

We are talking about roughly 550,000 Korean soldiers, sailors, and airmen who work in close, routine contact with U.S. forces on the peninsula. All 550,000 of those Korean service members who interact regularly with American troops will receive full vaccination, both to protect them and to protect U.S. forces stationed in Korea.

Beyond that, we have discussed producing vaccines in Korea in cooperation with a major U.S. vaccine manufacturer. This is being developed now. Leveraging Korea’s highly advanced production capabilities and the technology and support from that American company, Korea will be able to manufacture a large volume of doses for its own use.

Finally, my goal and expectation—though I cannot state it as an absolute guarantee—is that, over the rest of 2021, we will vaccinate all Americans. At this moment, we possess enough doses to immunize every person in the United States, without exception, and our aim is to complete that by mid‑summer. We are also pushing hard, alongside state governments, with creative efforts to encourage people to actually come forward and get vaccinated. I do not and have never believed that a very high share of Americans will refuse vaccination.

We estimate that, from the latter half of 2021 through 2022, we can manufacture up to an additional one billion vaccine doses. And what I respect about President Moon is that, like me, he is not limiting his concerns to only his own country or only to ours; he is focused on the Indo‑Pacific and on global needs.

Nations with advanced capabilities, like the United States and Korea, have a responsibility to do everything within our power to help protect people worldwide. That is a very ambitious objective, but I believe countries that possess this capacity will continue working together to make it a reality. Thank you.

question: did you bring this up with President Putin?
interview_answer: ——given what we’re hearing from Germany, that appears to be what happened. Jon [Jonathan Karl, ABC News].

question: Questioning Ambassador Woody Johnson about whether he tried to get the British Open moved to the President's Scottish golf resort, Turnberry.
interview_answer: No, I have never talked with Woody Johnson about that, about Turnberry. Turnberry is an extremely well-regarded course, as you know, truly one of the top courses anywhere. I saw a report about this today. But I have never—I have not had any conversation with Woody Johnson about arranging that. No. Regarding shutting down more embassies, that’s always a possibility. You see the situation. We believed there was a fire at the one we already closed. Everyone was saying: There’s a fire, there’s a fire. Apparently they were burning documents or papers, and I do wonder what that was about. All right, one or two more questions. Please.

question: Are you content that the initial $350 billion was used appropriately?
interview_answer:
I back the President-elect in working out, together with Congress, a plan that fits his approach and theirs. That’s the process he’ll have to follow. He’ll need to go to Capitol Hill and clearly lay out why he believes the next $350 billion is required, and he understands that; it’s a standard expectation.

As for the first $350 billion, I am satisfied with this key result of those expenditures: the financial markets are starting to unfreeze. Last fall, I was worried that the credit lockup could push us into a downturn worse than the Great Depression—that’s what the experts said would happen if we failed to act. So we chose to act forcefully.

And it wasn’t just through TARP. If you look at what we did with AIG, with Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, many of the steps my administration took were very forceful, all specifically aimed at stopping the financial system from collapsing.

question: Are you actually planning to attempt to postpone the census?
interview_answer: We’re examining that from a legal standpoint, because they’re requiring us to carry out this census. And the census—it really stunned me. I assumed it wouldn’t cost much to run a census. It’s billions of dollars. You realize that, right? Billions. Billions. They send people to knock on the door of every home in the nation and collect all kinds of information. Yet they’re prohibited from asking if someone is a U.S. citizen? How awful and absurd is that? So yes, we are reviewing it. Yes. Absolutely.

question: Are you planning to implement a nationwide plan to support schools in reopening safely?
interview_answer: Yes, I am in favor of that. We already have a national plan in place, but as you know, the final decisions rest with the State Governors. I believe most, and certainly many, Governors want their schools to reopen. I strongly want schools to reopen, especially when you look at the data we have. The numbers on young people and safety are very encouraging. So we want schools open. We also want the broader economy open. Just as I came in, I got a report that we’ve hit an alltime record in housing price growth—up about 21 percent, they said, which has never happened before. That’s a very positive signal. Our economy is on track to surge. A lot of jobs are being created. The employment figures will be released soon—over roughly the next week—and I think they will continue the trend from the last two months. Those last two months have been remarkable. So I expect a very strong economy and a tremendous year ahead. I also think the third quarter will be very solid when those numbers are released. Interestingly, they’ll come out right before the election, around November 1. So yes, I want the schools fully open, at 100 percent. And we’ll do that in a safe, careful way. When you review the statistics I just mentioned on children and on safety, they’re striking. Young people have very robust immune systems.

question: Must you back the war itself in order to back the soldiers fighting it?
interview_answer: No—what she did was challenge our side, not the men and women in uniform. But let me answer what you’ve asked. No, I absolutely do not think that’s required. You can oppose my decision to go to war and still stand fully behind the troops, without question. The real measure, though, is whether you’re willing to give them the funding they need to carry out their job. Earlier, in my response to Sheryl, I made it clear that someone who disagrees with my policy can be every bit as patriotic as I am. And your question gets to the heart of it: can a person say, “We reject your tactics or your strategy, but we still support the military”? The answer is yes, Ron, absolutely. What will really matter is whether they then refuse to grant the authority and resources our forces need on the ground to implement the strategy that General David Petraeus believes is essential to completing the mission. Michael—Michael Allen from Politico. Michael, who’s your employer?

question: Are you thinking it will happen before year’s end or not until sometime early next year?
interview_answer: Honestly, my best estimate is this: I’m hoping Jill and I can carve out a little time to slip away for about a week somewhere in that stretch between Thanksgiving and Christmas. And my expectation is that we’ll probably reach that decision in the early part of next year.

question: What, in concrete terms, do you anticipate will result from Secretary Rice’s brief several‑hour visit to Kenya on Monday?
interview_answer:
To start, I’ve been very active on these kinds of issues: whenever I sit down with a foreign leader who can influence a problem area, I raise the issue directly and push for attention and real action. For instance, I’ve spent a significant amount of time with Hu Jintao discussing Darfur, emphasizing that we need to work together there, and I’ve done the same regarding Burma. These meetings are my chance to press for practical paths forward in crisis regions.

Regarding Condi’s trip, the central objective is for the Kenyan leaders to hear directly from her what the United States expects: that there must be an end to violence and that a power‑sharing arrangement is needed to help the country work through its current crisis. That is what diplomacy is about, and we’ve been very engaged on multiple fronts.

On Darfur, for example, we were the first, and probably still the only, country to formally call it genocide. Early on, I faced a difficult choice about whether to deploy U.S. forces there. As I’ve said before, I ultimately chose not to send American troops, based on the advice of many organizations working on Darfur and other experts. I listened carefully to their arguments for and against. After deciding not to use our own military, the remaining viable routes were through the United Nations and international peacekeeping. So we have invested a lot of time and effort in trying to secure an effective peacekeeping presence.

The African Union initially took the lead, and one reason I’m visiting Rwanda is to personally thank President Kagame for his strong role in sending peacekeepers to Darfur and being a key leader there.

We have been very active on these matters, and we will stay engaged, because they directly affect U.S. security and our national interests.

Well, Mr. President, thank you. That’s it for the press conference.

question: Is Senator Lindsey Graham correct in saying that Benghazi and the Boston bombing both show America is slipping in national security?
interview_answer:
No, Senator Graham is not correct on that point, even though I recognize it probably attracted some media attention.

In Boston, what we actually witnessed was local, state, and federal authorities — every relevant agency — coming together quickly in response to an attack on a major city. They processed the crime scene, identified the suspects within hours, and we now have one suspect dead and the other in custody, with charges filed.

Our law enforcement agencies behaved in an outstanding manner after the bombings. Their actions should make us proud, just as we are proud of the people of Boston and all the first responders and medical teams who helped save lives.

We also know that Russian intelligence services had warned U.S. authorities about the older brother and his mother, suggesting they might have extremist sympathies. The FBI did, in fact, look into the older brother. It is not the case that the FBI ignored that tip. They opened an inquiry, they interviewed him, and they determined at the time that there was no evidence he was engaged in extremist activities. Those are the facts as we have them.

The issue we now have to examine is whether something changed later — whether there was a turning point that led to his radicalization and to the decision to carry out the terrible attack we saw in Boston — and whether anything more could have been done during that period to stop it.

What Director Clapper is doing now is the standard process in situations like this: after an incident, we systematically review every step taken. We make sure nothing is overlooked. We assess whether there are additional procedures or safeguards we can adopt that would strengthen our ability to detect and disrupt potential attacks. We will not have those answers until that review is finished, and until the criminal investigation itself is fully complete. That work is still underway.

What I can say, based on what I’ve been shown so far, is that the FBI carried out its responsibilities, and the Department of Homeland Security fulfilled its role as well.

This work is inherently difficult. I’ve said for some time that because we have applied sustained pressure on al‑Qaeda’s core leadership and on more organized, well‑funded networks capable of mounting large, cross‑border operations, a major emerging risk is individuals who radicalize on their own here in the United States. Some of these individuals may not belong to any formal group, but driven by distorted, violent beliefs, they may decide to launch an attack. Those kinds of threats can be even harder to detect and prevent.

For months, I’ve been directing our entire counterterrorism team to focus on that evolving danger and to identify additional steps we can take. That includes looking at whether we can do more outreach in communities where this kind of self‑radicalization might occur, and whether we can improve detection of these lone‑actor threats. All of this must be done within the framework of our laws and respect for due process.

Director Clapper’s review is therefore aimed at determining what lessons we can draw from Boston and how we can further strengthen our defenses going forward.

question: On which specific issues do President Macron and President Trump hold differing views?
interview_answer:
Yes, absolutely. I think the reason our relationship works so well is rooted in the long-standing ties between our two nations. We come from different backgrounds, but perhaps that helps; neither of us is a classic career politician, and neither of us is inclined to change his mind easily. And if you’ve listened to this press conference and watched our interaction, you have already seen the answer to your question.

On Iran, we do disagree over the JCPOA. However, I believe we are moving past that disagreement by choosing to work together on a broader, comprehensive agreement—one that addresses the nuclear dimension, but also includes three additional issues that have not previously been part of the framework.

So if, after spending over an hour together, just the two of us, we had concluded that the United States would simply withdraw from the JCPOA while France refused to modify its stance, our friendship would have been squandered. The point is that each of us must factor in the positions and interests of the other’s country.

What we are doing is without precedent. We have never before taken such a unified approach on Syria as we just did, nor on Iran, in support of an agreement that would encompass all four pillars. There is very intensive work going on between us and our teams; otherwise, we would not be capable of accomplishing what we have.

In the past, France sometimes said it was time to respond to the use of chemical weapons, and our allies—including the United States then—did not follow. That is not what happened this time. We jointly decided what could and could not be done, what was legitimate under international law, as two permanent members of the Security Council, and we carried out a military operation that was unprecedented in both scale and level of cooperation.

And I want to pay tribute to our service members, our armed forces, and those of the United Kingdom, because together we conducted a unique, proportionate operation, made possible by the quality of our relationship.

So on Syria, on Iran, and on upholding the credibility of the international community against the use of chemical weapons, you have seen concrete proof that the partnership and friendship between our two countries allow us to obtain real results. This is a clear step forward from where we were only a few weeks ago.

For my part, I believe we share a great deal. On many, indeed most, issues, we have been in agreement, and where we do not, we can still adapt and be flexible. In life, you must show flexibility, and as heads of state, that is essential. I think we are very much aligned on many of the topics we covered today. And I want to add: France is a great nation, and I am convinced it will reach new heights under this President. He will be an exceptional leader, one of France’s great Presidents. I am honored to call him my friend. Thank you. Thank you very much.

question: Would you agree to approve an immigration agreement that ultimately leaves out any money for constructing the border wall?
interview_answer: No. The wall has to be part of it. We need a wall for national security. We need a wall to protect people. We need a wall to stop the flow of drugs coming across. I expect that the lawmakers involved, from both parties, I truly think they’re going to find an answer to the DACA issue—which has dragged on for many years—and perhaps even address the broader immigration system. But whatever deal they reach must include the wall, because if there’s no wall, the whole plan fails. You can see this in other places. Look at Israel. They built a wall and resolved a very serious problem. We need a wall. We must have a wall to protect our security. Security comes first. So my answer is, the wall is absolutely required. Thank you. And that’s that.

question: Have you been personally communicating with him about the coronavirus situation?
interview_answer: No, not directly. We are, however, in communication at the national level about it. We did talk about the reports that he might be infected. It was said he could have it, but he does not, thankfully. Yes.

question: If you choose to run again, will Vice President Harris remain your running mate?
interview_answer: I fully anticipate that she will. She’s doing an excellent job. She’s an outstanding teammate. She’s an outstanding teammate.

question: View on whether the Prime Minister ought to scrap the agreement entirely.
interview_answer: No. At this moment, all I can state is: it’s not under consideration. I’m not going to speculate about what might happen down the road; that would be a major claim. But as of now, it’s not on the table. Ambassador, is that accurate? Yes. The term “suspend” was very deliberately agreed upon by everyone. Suspend, by definition, means a temporary stop. It’s not on the table right now, but it hasn’t been ruled out forever. Next question.

question: Is it possible to impose tougher sanctions on Venezuela?
interview_answer: Much harsher sanctions are indeed possible.

question: What led you to choose Africa as a destination so near the end of your term?
interview_answer:
Let me start by saying this isn’t my first time in Africa as President; I came here early in my administration as well. This is my second visit to the continent, and I’m here to underscore for Americans that it serves our own interests to help African nations tackle preventable diseases like malaria and tougher challenges like HIV/AIDS; that it benefits us to deepen trade ties between African countries and the United States; and that it’s in our interest to fund education so governments can get children into school.

And the most effective way to make that case is to stand here in Ghana, where people can see with their own eyes what I’m referring to. It’s one thing to talk about these ideas back in the U.S.; it’s another to come to Ghana and meet the men and women who are working to make this country stronger.

Also, Africa has changed for the better during my time in office. That’s not because of me; it’s because of African leadership, and I want to be clear about that. When I first took office, there were six regional conflicts. Take Liberia: it was a serious crisis. Working with Nigeria and with John’s counsel, I joined other leaders in taking steps that helped pave the way for the first democratically elected woman President in the continent’s history. I’m going there tomorrow to highlight what she has achieved and to restate our commitment to support that progress.

In other words, we’ve seen real movement on conflict resolution. The United States hasn’t come in trying to dictate outcomes; we’ve aimed to be a constructive partner, as we’ve done in eastern Congo by working with the Presidents of Rwanda, Congo, and Burundi.

At the same time, democracy is advancing across Africa. One reason is that there are role models like John Kufuor. He’s widely respected. People can look at his example and say, that’s the kind of leadership we value.

Third, we’ve overhauled our aid strategy. We moved away from a model that essentially just handed over funds and wished people well, to one that says: in exchange for our generosity, we expect certain standards, such as fighting corruption and investing in children’s schooling. I don’t think that’s an unreasonable expectation when we’re talking about U.S. taxpayers’ dollars. Those expectations weren’t always there before. This approach is relatively new. But I’m comfortable insisting on sound principles because I believe in holding African leaders to high standards.

I’m impressed by the capabilities of the leaders I’ve met— not every leader, but on this trip, the people I’m visiting are clearly committed to serving their citizens, running honest administrations, and investing in their populations. They care more about leaving a legacy of education and opportunity than about building a government that serves their own narrow interests. And again, the best way to convey that message is to come here in person. That’s why I’m here, and I’m pleased to be here. It’s been an excellent visit, and I appreciate my friend’s welcome, as does Laura.

All right, let’s go to John McKinnon from The Wall Street Journal—that’s a very sophisticated newspaper, no doubt about it.

question: Are you seriously claiming that Joe Biden will be using any sort of performance-enhancing drugs before the debates?
interview_answer: No, I’m not kidding at all. I’m ready to take a drug test myself, and I think he ought to do the same, because his performances have been very inconsistent. I watched him when he was debating—when he went up against Pocahontas and against Harris, who was very rough on him. I saw him then, and he was completely out of it. Right? Then I saw him in the debate with Bernie Sanders, and he was all right. Not impressive, but not awful. Just all right. Very different from how he’d been before—you know exactly what I mean. If you go back and watch some of those earlier debates, I said to myself, There’s no way he can keep going. He just can’t. Then suddenly he’s debating Crazy Bernie Sanders, and, honestly, he’s all right. And I thought, How did he go from those terrible showings to being passable? I joke about it, but it’s true: he wasn’t Winston Churchill up there, but he was decent. And people say he was on performance-enhancing drugs. Lots of people have said it. Many people have written it.

question: Will the President actually be in Charlotte in person to deliver his acceptance speech?
interview_answer: The speech is on Thursday—that’s the big speech, the main address. In Charlotte, they’ll handle the nomination on Monday. That’s a separate time, a separate event, but the nominations happen Monday. My speech is on Thursday. All right?

question: What sort of time limit?
interview_answer:
I’d be very happy to host the Prime Minister. We just have to coordinate our calendars, but he’s welcome here at any point. Italy is a close partner. Millions of Americans feel a strong bond with Italy because of their roots there. My own Ambassador’s grandfather came from Italy. As you know, Italian heritage is a powerful influence in our nation and its culture. So the Prime Minister is always invited.

On the question of the deadline, there must be one. This process has to conclude; it has to move forward. In our view, the moment has come to implement the Ahtisaari plan. There have already been a number of postponements. You may recall that at one stage, progress was underway, and then we were told more time was needed to pursue a U.N. Security Council resolution. Our position now is that the extra time has run out.

We’re therefore coordinating with our partners and allies. I spent a long time discussing this directly with Vladimir Putin. To be specific about what I said to him at the table: I told him it is time—time to force a resolution of this question. I recognize there are serious worries about the consequences, including whether he will accept the outcome. I’m aware of those concerns. But the people of Kosovo are waiting impatiently for an international decision.

At the same time, Romano is correct that we must ensure the Serbs can see a constructive path ahead. One strong path forward is the prospect of accession to the European Union. I don’t control that process, but I can engage with Serbia on economic growth and on improving ties with the United States—and we will do that.

Looking back at the summit, I can say it was genuinely successful—we made tangible progress. Remember, many observers were predicting splits, clashes, and major disagreements; instead, we reached some key understandings. Take climate change: we agreed on a central idea, namely that global challenges have to be addressed through global action. We discussed the Kyoto Protocol as an initial step toward international cooperation in this area.

There are still some technical details to settle—some smaller elements we still need to fill in—but the intention to move ahead is unmistakable. There is clear determination. India, China, Brazil, Mexico—everyone showed the same determination. Other non–G‑8 countries were also involved, working on worldwide approaches, because only that kind of cooperation can produce a stable balance in the years to come.

On other topics—Kosovo itself, and our dealings with Russia—I would describe the atmosphere at our meeting as one of mutual recognition of interdependence. That’s really the right word. We did not sense hostility; instead, all of us were looking for ways to reinforce that interdependence. That should guide how we relate to Russia and how we create a stable and peaceful future framework.

Those are the aims of the G‑8. In fact, G‑8 summits are not limited to rigid, pre-set technical agendas. That’s not their nature. We need the chance to sit down and talk freely about long-range issues. And we did that—frankly and openly. We came away agreeing on how to proceed, and I don’t think we could reasonably have asked for more.

Regarding Kosovo again, after our discussions there, we now have a clear understanding of the way forward. And on that front as well, I believe we achieved as much as we realistically could have hoped for.

question: Did you already know when it would be released?
interview_answer: No, I was completely unaware of it.

question: So you don’t believe the market drop was really caused by the coronavirus—[inaudible]?
interview_answer: No, I do think it was. I absolutely think it was. But I also think you can add a lot of selling pressure based on what investors see politically. They see what could happen. Once again, I believe we’re going to win; I’m very confident about that. We’ve delivered everything I promised and more. Look at our record: major tax reductions, cutting burdensome regulations, rebuilding the armed forces, taking care of veterans with choice and accountability. All of that, plus defending the Second Amendment. Voters know the other side would go after the Second Amendment and effectively eliminate it. When people see that agenda, they say, This is bad for the country. So you have to factor that in. I really do think that’s part of it. But, no, the main driver of what we’re discussing here is the virus. That’s the central issue. Still, in terms of what you hear on CNBC and Fox Business, I do believe election concerns are also playing a role. And after I win, I think the stock market will take off like never before—just as it did right after I won the last election, when it shot up the very next day like a rocket.

question: Do you view a possible traumatic brain injury as serious?
interview_answer: I was informed about that several days afterward. You’d need to check with the Department of Defense on the details. No, I do not regard those as very serious wounds, compared with other kinds of injuries I’ve seen. I’ve seen what Iran’s roadside bombs have done to our service members. I’ve seen people lose both legs and both arms. I’ve seen people terribly, terribly wounded in that theater, in that war—often by bombs planted by Soleimani, who is no longer alive. I see those as truly terrible injuries. No, I do not see this as a terrible injury. No.

question: Will you make the donations public as they are received?
interview_answer: No. Yes. No. Yes. [] Let’s move on. [] I haven’t—wow. You clearly haven’t posed a question in quite a while. It was like—look, first, I haven’t reviewed the final budget. Second, as Donnie Evans, the head of the foundation, already said, we’ll examine what the disclosure rules are and then decide. And here’s the thing—there are a number of people—or at least some; I shouldn’t exaggerate—who want to donate but don’t want their identities revealed, whether for this foundation or any other. We’re going to factor that in. Third—and what was the last part?

question: Is the $200 million amount correct?
interview_answer: No, yes, no, yes—take your pick. [] Let’s move on. [] I haven’t—whoo, boy. You clearly haven’t tried asking anything in a while with the way you framed that. Look, first, I haven’t reviewed the finalized budget. Second, as Donnie Evans, the foundation chair, has already said, we’ll examine what has to be disclosed and then decide. The point is—well, I—there are plenty of folks—or at least some; I shouldn’t exaggerate—who prefer to donate without having their names made public, whether to this foundation or any other. We’ll factor that in. Third—and what was the third thing again?

question: Would you be willing to authorize the release of at least two of them before 2020 so the nation can better address the current crisis?
interview_answer: You know, I’ve always wanted to come to Nigeria. I really do want to visit Nigeria. It’s a remarkable place. And from what I’m told, in terms of natural beauty, there may be no country more stunning. So yes, I’d like to visit. As for the first part of what you asked, I honestly didn’t catch it. Please repeat it.

question: Did that statement leave you feeling let down?
interview_answer: Well, as I’ve said, if those really are his remarks, I’ll wait to sit down with him so he can explain exactly what he meant, because when I spoke with him, he recognized that it’s in the international community’s interest to ensure that that country cannot build a nuclear weapon. That’s why, in the first U.N. round, he stood with us, and in the second round we again worked together to send a strong signal. If he didn’t share those concerns, Bret, then why did we see such solid movement at the U.N. in both phases? So I intend to talk with him directly about it. At this point, I haven’t even been fully debriefed by Condi or Bob Gates on their discussions with Vladimir Putin, so I’m not going to characterize my reaction right now.

question: Will you require banks that receive these funds to actually expand credit and issue additional loans with that money?
interview_answer: Helene, let me say this again—I’m not going to get ahead of what my Treasury Secretary is going to announce, because I’d like all of you to attend his briefing; he’s going to do an excellent job. And to connect this to Jake’s point a moment ago, one of my core tests is whether credit is actually reaching those who need it. Is money available to companies, big and small? Is it reaching ordinary borrowers? Can they function in ways that create jobs and boost growth in local communities? The plan we’ve assembled is aimed at accomplishing exactly that. But beyond outlining those goals, I’m going to leave the specific details for Tim to unveil tomorrow so he can have the spotlight. Okay, Major Garrett from FOX News—Major, where are you?

question: Will you answer what I asked you?
interview_answer: Listen, I’m not going to answer you. I’m speaking with this man right now. Kindly take your seat.

question: How do you respond to that?
interview_answer: No, I reject that completely. I just don’t accept it—I really don’t—actually, I can’t even say I know what you’re getting at. If that were true, why are my approval ratings with Black Americans at record highs? Why are my numbers with African Americans among the strongest? Why are my poll numbers overall so high? The question itself is racist. Frankly, I realize you’ve scripted it and plan to read it, but let me be clear: that is a racist question.

question: Do you still believe Hillary Clinton is going to be the nominee?
interview_answer: I’m refusing to discuss politics.

question: Do we need to engage with Iran in direct talks?
interview_answer: So you’re asking whether I’ll personally sit down with Iranian officials?

question: Shouldn’t the Iranian government be made aware there will be repercussions?
interview_answer: I’ve already responded to that, Chuck, and my answer is that we still don’t know how this situation will unfold. All right? Jake Tapper, ABC News.

question: What should people in the United States anticipate Congress will do on abortion rights after the midterms?
interview_answer: I don’t think they should really count on anything beyond us sticking to our current stances. I’m not going to address any further questions on this. I probably shouldn’t have responded to your question in the first place.

question: Are you going to sit down with Rouhani?
interview_answer: Pardon me?

question: Is there any chance Huawei will be taken off the entity list?
interview_answer: I'm not going to address that at this time. We’re examining the issue very closely. Huawei is a major factor for our country and for our intelligence and national security agencies. We’re extremely familiar with Huawei. But I’m not going to get into that right now; I think it would be the wrong moment to do so. Beyond what I’ve already said, we’re not treating it as a central topic publicly. We’ll come back to it at a later point, alright?

question: Evaluating how reliable Donald Trump is in controlling U.S. nuclear arms.
interview_answer: On that follow‑up—and this really goes for any further Trump inquiries—I’m not going to give you an assessment. I’d simply urge each of you to reach your own conclusion. I’ve said this several times already. Just pay attention to Mr. Trump’s statements and decide for yourselves how secure you feel about his capacity to oversee responsibilities such as our nuclear forces.

question: So there’s no chance of a shutdown during the lame duck?
interview_answer: I really can’t say. I’m not in a position to answer that. No, I won’t promise that, although it could happen.

question: Has your administration reached a conclusion on whether President Putin intends to attack or send forces into Ukraine, as you indicated?
interview_answer:
Look, the only thing I can say with certainty is that this choice rests entirely, completely, and exclusively with Putin. No one else will take that decision for him; no one else is really going to determine it. It’s his call and his alone.

Frankly, I suspect his precise course of action may depend on something as arbitrary as how he feels when he wakes up on a given day. And I think it wouldn’t be unreasonable, if he chose, for him to frame this in terms of revisiting strategic doctrine and force posture in Europe and in Russia’s European regions.

But I do not know whether he’s actually resolved to do that. Up to now, the three rounds of talks we’ve had—through the OSCE and other channels—have led nowhere, and what I’m hearing from my Secretary of State, my National Security Advisor, and other senior aides handling these discussions is that even the people on the Russian side may not know what he plans.

So the honest answer is that, while we can outline various scenarios for what he might attempt—for instance, if he were to push in and try to seize the entire country, especially from the north via Belarus, he’d probably have to hold off until the ground is frozen enough for his forces to move across—none of that tells us what he’s actually decided.

At the same time, if he’s instead aiming to pressure us while talking about broader regional security, we are still reinforcing Ukraine’s defensive capabilities. We’re also addressing the situation in the Baltic and Black Sea regions, and so on. There are many factors I’m sure he’s weighing: how quickly he could execute any plan, and even what that plan truly is.

He is not uninformed. I believe he is carefully considering the short-, medium-, and long-term consequences for Russia. And I do not believe he has reached a final decision yet, so I cannot tell you that he has chosen to invade or move in.

question: Mr. President, on what grounds are they being granted an exemption?
interview_answer: That decision was put forward to me by a team of experts, and we’re currently reviewing it in light of the latest figures we’re receiving. It’s possible we’ll end up putting them on the roster of nations that we’ll, in effect, restrict—or whatever term you want to use—for this period. Their case numbers have risen quite sharply in the last day, so we might add them, and maybe a few more countries as well. And we might, to be honest, also consider taking some off that list. I’m not going to spell out the specific justification right now.

question: How do you square your decision to reduce Manning’s sentence with WikiLeaks’ alleged role in Russia’s election hacking last year?
interview_answer:
Let me start by emphasizing something: Chelsea Manning has already endured a very harsh prison term. Anyone out there who might be contemplating leaking critical, classified information is not going to walk away thinking there are no consequences after seeing the time she has spent behind bars.
