So I’m not entirely convinced—David, I’m really not convinced—that he himself fully knows what he’s going to do. My guess is he’ll take some kind of action; he pretty much has to do something.

And I’ve made a few points clear to him. He raised two demands: first, that Ukraine must never enter NATO; second, that NATO—or anyone else—must not place strategic weapons in Ukraine. On that second issue, we might be able to reach some arrangement, depending on what he does along Russia’s border in the European part of Russia.

On the first issue, we have a series of international and European agreements that affirm each country’s right to choose its own alliances. In any event, Ukraine’s accession to NATO anytime soon is quite unlikely, given the democratic reforms and other steps still required, and the question of whether key Western allies would even back Ukraine joining at this moment.

So there’s room for negotiation, if he chooses that path. But as for spelling out what the United States would or would not do in terms of a specific containment policy in response to a hypothetical invasion, I’m not going to lay that out here. I’ll just say that, if he proceeds, it’s going to damage him severely—and I really should not go beyond that in this forum.

question: Was he carrying a weapon, Mr. President?
interview_answer: As far as I’m aware, yes, but I’m not certain. You’ll need to put that question to them instead.

question: Mr. President, Tom Bradby from ITV News. Many people in Britain believe that your country’s insistence on extraditing Gary McKinnon, a computer hacker with Asperger’s, is excessive and rather severe. Do you believe the moment has come to show some mercy in his situation?
interview_answer:
Please, go ahead. This is an issue that came up in our talks. As you know, there is an ongoing dialogue between the U.K. and the U.S. about this case, and I’m not going to say anything that might interfere with those discussions. We fully recognize that Gary McKinnon is charged with a very serious offense, namely breaking into critical government computer systems. No one is disputing that this is a grave matter that must be addressed. I’ve spoken with our Ambassador in London about it, and I raised it with the Prime Minister today as well, and my hope is that a constructive path forward will be found.

Now, one of the broader topics David and I covered was the growing set of problems we face because of the Internet and the importance of close cooperation on cybersecurity. We touched on the reality that, although there may still be attempts to use old-fashioned espionage methods to steal state secrets, today our greatest exposure is much more likely to come through intrusions into our digital networks. So we view this challenge as extremely serious, and I know the British Government shares that view.

Beyond that, it is a longstanding principle here that the President does not intervene in individual prosecution or extradition decisions. So my expectation is that my administration will simply follow the law, while also working in close coordination with what we have just described as an exceptionally close ally. And I am confident this matter will be settled in a way that reflects both the seriousness of the offense and the strength of our cooperation, and that allows us to reach an appropriate outcome. Thank you all very much. Thank you.

question: At your first press briefing 10 months ago, I asked whether you had tools other than legislation to safeguard voting rights. Now that the legislative route seems completely blocked, can you spell out your current plan for protecting voting rights?
interview_answer:
I'm not going to lay that out in detail right now, especially regarding any executive orders I might issue or other specific steps I could take. What I can say is this: we've already substantially increased the number of enforcement officials in the Justice Department who are tasked with challenging what we view as unconstitutional Republican attempts to rig elections and undermine the results.

We've also started organizing in new ways, reaching into communities beyond the traditional civil rights groups, to explain to the broader public what is underway and what could occur if these measures advance.

If I had spoken—again, I don’t mean you personally; I mean the public in general—about election subversion in terms of picking electors after the fact, people would have thought I was exaggerating. I taught constitutional law for 20 years—a three-credit separation-of-powers course on Saturday mornings while I was a Senator—and even I never imagined we’d be seriously debating schemes like what we saw last time, where there was an attempt to send alternate slates of electors to state legislatures claiming I lost and a Republican actually won.

Most people probably never believed something like that could occur in 21st-century America, but it has. So what I’m getting at, Nancy, is that I believe there are several avenues available to us, but I’m still not going to detail them here. I also believe we’ll ultimately secure important portions of the legislation—even if not all of it immediately—so that we can enact a substantial part of the John Lewis bill and the broader fair elections measures.

question: Did he receive an invitation?
interview_answer: I don't know anything about that. I'm not the one who organizes the press briefings. What I can say is they coordinate extremely closely and maintain a very strong partnership, which is what really matters.

question: Can you tell us about the earlier phone conversation you had with Kim Jong Un?
interview_answer: We did speak, but I’m not going to go into that. What I can say is that we’ve had many talks overall, and we’ve built very strong lines of communication at Mike’s level and at other levels as well. In fact, as you know, we actually have several representatives from North Korea here with us today, in this room, and there are a few more in the back. So when we moved toward this final accord, the key point is that we weren’t starting from scratch. We went in with a deep relationship and a great deal of understanding, and I believe that’s how we were able to reach this outcome. Now I’m going to wrap up. I don’t know about all of you, but it’s been a long stretch since I’ve had any downtime. Now we can relax just a bit—and then we get back to work. I’m grateful you all came. I hope we’ve covered what you wanted. Thank you very much, and really, I think everyone deserves credit, because this is, in my view, a very significant moment in world history. And to be honest, I have to say: I want to see this through to the finish. So Mike and our entire team now have to buckle down and make sure we complete it. Otherwise, we’ve done well—but if you don’t carry the ball over the goal line, it doesn’t count for enough. So thank you again, and congratulations to everyone here. Thank you, I appreciate it. Thank you.

question: Does the fact that two Muslim women, including one who wears a veil, were elected to the House reject a particular message?
interview_answer: I have no idea what you’re asking me. Huh?

question: Deliberations and plans about issuing advance pardons
interview_answer: I’m not going to talk about pardons during this briefing.

question: Mr. President, when exactly will that happen?
interview_answer: Honestly, I have no idea.

question: Could you clarify exactly what the President said to French President Nicolas Sarkozy about Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu?
interview_answer:
Let me start by pointing out that Chuck is the only one who’s managed to sneak in two questions so far. So when I wrap up here, whoever thought they were next in line—[]—I’m just giving you a hard time, Chuck.

On the supercommittee, in August we agreed to put in place about a trillion dollars in deficit reduction over the next decade, largely from discretionary spending. But we also made clear that to put our finances on a sounder footing, we needed at least another $1.2 trillion in savings. I actually pushed for a higher figure. And the entire purpose of the sequester was to force both parties to move off their entrenched positions and do what’s necessary for the country.

Since then, they’ve met a lot, but it appears folks are still clinging to their fixed positions instead of coming together to fix the problem.

I’ve laid out a very detailed plan that totals more than $3 trillion in deficit reduction. It’s a balanced framework, which is what the American people say they want. It puts everything on the table. It includes discretionary cuts like those we’ve already enacted, reductions in non‑defense spending, reductions in defense spending, a willingness to address entitlement programs, a focus on lowering health care costs, and the need for additional revenue.

And when we talk about revenue, if we have to raise more money, it’s only reasonable to begin by asking the wealthiest Americans to contribute a bit more before we start asking, say, seniors to shoulder much higher Medicare costs.

This is the same basic proposal I walked through with Speaker Boehner in August. It’s the same balanced mix that every independent panel that has studied this issue says is required. Yet it seems like people keep trying to fiddle with the numbers so they can avoid that outcome.

But the math works out the same no matter how you slice it. If you want a balanced solution that doesn’t eviscerate Medicare and Medicaid and doesn’t block needed investments in education, basic research, and innovation—the very things we’ve been discussing here at APEC that every leader recognizes as critical to long‑term growth—then responsible cuts have to be paired with new revenue.

My expectation is that over the coming days, the supercommittee’s leaders will finally bite the bullet and do what’s needed, because the arithmetic isn’t going to change. There’s no secret formula, no magic beans you can throw on the ground to make money sprout from trees. We simply have to act responsibly. And I’m ready to sign a balanced bill that genuinely addresses this challenge.

Let me add this: When I sit down with foreign leaders, what’s striking—whether in Europe or here in Asia—is that the structural changes they’re enacting, both in terms of taxes and public pensions, are far more sweeping than what we’d need to do to get our own books in order.

We are not talking about transforming America or our basic way of life. We’re talking about spreading the sacrifice in a fair way so that no one feels, yet again, that the well‑connected, or those with lobbyists and special interests, get off easy while the burden lands on middle‑class families who are already under strain. If other nations can make tough but fair choices, so can we, and we can do it prudently.

I’m not going to say in advance whether I would veto some hypothetical bill before I actually see the details, because I still think there’s a chance for that light‑bulb moment when everyone acknowledges: This is what we have to do.

On the hot‑mic episode in France, I am not going to discuss private conversations I’ve had with individual foreign leaders. What I can tell you is this: My main exchange with President Sarkozy in that meeting was to express my deep disappointment that France supported the Palestinian bid to join UNESCO, fully aware that under U.S. law that meant we would have to cut off funding to UNESCO, and after I had consistently argued that the only viable path to resolving the Middle East conflict is for Israelis and Palestinians to sit down together and negotiate—that trying to go around that process through the United Nations is not going to succeed.

So I spoke to President Sarkozy in very direct and firm terms on that matter, entirely in line with what I’ve been saying, both in private and in public, to all the parties over the past several months.

Ed Henry, FOX News.

question: Are you actually able to cooperate with them?
interview_answer: Look, I’m going to have to tell you that I’ll give you a real answer to that in about seven years from now, all right? Think of it as one plus seven. I’d like to—I really would like to be able to, because I do believe in the idea of working across the aisle. But at the moment, they’re only blocking everything. And honestly, when you look at it, they oppose major tax reductions that will make us stronger and more competitive as a nation. That’s a tough position to run on, and I’m convinced some Democrats will ultimately back us when we vote on those tax cuts.

question: When speaking with President Castro, did the speaker bring up Fidel Castro or talk about him?
interview_answer: Lesley, I’m going to stop you right there. I’m not going to get into that; it’s using up too much time.

question: What kind of weapon was used?
interview_answer: I’m not aware of that, no.

question: Have you been notified of any confirmed coronavirus infections in the White House today?
interview_answer: You mean today?

question: When is Kosovo’s independence due?
interview_answer: I must decline.

question: Does the Speaker actually have command over his caucus?
interview_answer: You’d have to put that question to the Speaker himself; that isn’t something I’m going to answer. What I can say is that my dealings with John Boehner have been positive. I believe he’s a decent person who’s trying to do what’s best for the country. As Chip mentioned, the political forces that helped elevate him to the Speakership were effective in a midterm campaign, but they make the job of governing harder. More broadly, the Republican conference as a whole needs to understand that our system functions when people hear one another out, extend good faith, presume the other side’s patriotism, and agree to reasonable compromises to tackle major challenges. And there are still some in that caucus who, in my view, haven’t fully embraced that yet.

question: Will you identify which five Republican Senators you’re referring to?
interview_answer: Of course not. Absolutely no way. Are you serious? I have to keep that private.

question: Is the United States prepared to pledge more vaccine shipments abroad?
interview_answer:
It’s possible this will take a bit more time globally. The United States is going to keep at this effort, and I think there’s a chance that, over the course of 2022 into 2023, we’ll be in a place where we can send up to another billion doses. But that isn’t finalized yet, and I can’t stand here today and say that’s a done deal.

I’ve tried to be very disciplined throughout this pandemic: I only state what I actually know and what I’m confident we can carry out, and whenever I’ve announced something, I’ve followed through. What I’m not going to do is get out ahead of the facts or imply that I—or the United States—can guarantee actions that we haven’t locked in. So I’m not going to make a firm promise on additional specific numbers right now.

There was clear agreement among the G‑7 partners that this effort doesn’t stop here—we’ll keep working until we can meet global needs. That’s not just the morally right thing to do; it’s also necessary for our own health and security. You can’t build a wall high enough to keep out new variants. That simply isn’t possible.

So this is going to remain an ongoing mission for a long time, and we have to be ready for future outbreaks as well. That means building systems to detect a new virus early, before it spirals out of control. As long as there are countries that still need access to vaccines, we’re going to stay engaged. And it’s not only about donating doses; this is a massive logistical challenge. It’s one thing to send a country a certain shipment of vaccines; it’s another to ensure there are people and infrastructure to get shots into arms.

That’s why we’re also helping other nations ramp up their own manufacturing capacity. We’ve all agreed on that. India, for example, has the know‑how but hasn’t yet had all the material resources it needs for large‑scale production, and there’s a lot of work underway to fix that. So we’re focused not just on supplying vaccines, but on enabling countries to produce them themselves.

All right, last one. Last one. I’m not going to respond to anything beyond that on this. No, I’m kidding—last question. Peter Alexander, NBC News.

question: How can you frame this as some kind of worldwide contest when Americans are continuing to die every day and the number of new daily cases keeps rising?
interview_answer: People are dying all over the globe. And that’s something you ought to take up with China. Don’t direct that question at me—put it to China instead, alright? Once you ask them, you might hear a very unexpected response. Yes, the person in the back there.

question: What actions, in your view, should be taken against Russia in response to Alexei Navalny’s poisoning?
interview_answer:
I’d say this is something we have to treat with a lot of seriousness, if that’s in fact what happened, and I believe we will. Nobody has been firmer with Russia than I have. And yet we have a working relationship. I have good relations with almost every country. I have a good relationship with North Korea—recall, we were supposedly on the brink of war with them. If Hillary had won, you’d be in a war with North Korea right now. It would be a disastrous war, an awful war, with millions and millions of casualties. Under me, we’ve avoided that. We didn’t go to war. People claimed if I were elected we’d be in a war within a week; what happened was exactly the reverse.

We’re doing very well in Afghanistan, drawing troop levels down—to very small numbers—and other positive developments are underway. We also have a tremendous peace initiative in the Middle East with the UAE and Israel. That’s been received in a way almost nothing else has been; it’s gotten incredible reviews. More countries will be coming on board; they’ll be announcing soon that they’re joining. Many of them are saying, “Why weren’t we included from day one?” They would have loved to be there at the start, but now they’re eager to participate.

So we’re accomplishing a lot. I was the one who really warned the United States and the rest of the world about Nord Stream 2. I asked: Why is Germany paying Russia billions and billions of dollars for energy, while we’re expected to defend Germany from Russia? How does that make sense? Along with that, we’ve imposed sanctions and taken other steps.

At the same time, with Russia, we’re currently in talks on a nuclear nonproliferation agreement, which is extremely important—far more important, in my view, than what some people put first, like global warming. We’re the largest nuclear power; they’re number two. China is third—a distant third—but they’re expanding quickly. At some point we’ll bring China into those discussions, but for now we’re making strong progress with Russia on nuclear nonproliferation.

So, I do not know precisely what occurred here. I think it’s awful, it’s horrible, and it should never take place. But we don’t yet have concrete evidence, and I’m not going to spell out what should be done to Russia until we do. I’ll review the situation further.

question: Who exactly are U.S. oil producers?
interview_answer: Sorry, could you remind me what you just asked?

question: In what ways does reforming the criminal justice system help resolve the outstanding racial inequities within both state and federal institutions?
interview_answer: That’s a pretty overloaded question, April. [] You know what? I’m just not going to get into that.

question: Should you have done more?
interview_answer: More about what?

question: If no fresh deal is reached, would you be willing to resort to military action to curb Iran’s nuclear program?
interview_answer: I’m not going to say whether I would or wouldn’t use military force. It’s not something I should be discussing publicly. What I will say is this: Iran will not end up with nuclear weapons. That much I can assure you. They will not get nuclear weapons—you can count on that. Alright. Thank you.

question: Does it have to be signed?
interview_answer: Well, that actually raises a good point about whether, in a strict sense, any signature is even involved here, because, as I’ve mentioned, this isn’t a legally enforceable pact. I’m not familiar with the exact formalities. What I can say is that it represents a serious commitment on our part as the United States, and we view it as highly significant. Okay, I’ll leave it there. Thanks, everyone.

question: Is the upcoming meeting going to be soon?
interview_answer: Honestly, I’m not able to say. It could happen in the near future, or it might not take place for quite a while. I’m not going to tell you. I’d like it to be soon, but it could also end up being much later.

question: Are you planning a trip to Charlotte?
interview_answer: Anyone want to take a guess? We’ll make it public shortly.

question: Did you make that public back in February?
interview_answer: You should look at the documents yourself. China publicly said it was transmitted through the air, and I was told it was airborne. I believed that very early on. The point is, you need steady leadership. You don’t want me running around shouting that there will be massive casualties and, in the process, creating extremely serious turmoil for the country. And if Bob Woodward really thought what I said was so terrible, then right after hearing it he should have gone straight to officials so they could get ready and be informed.

question: Do you now regard Kim Jong Un as your peer?
interview_answer: What exactly do you mean?

question: Must President Zelenskyy surrender any part of Ukraine’s territory in order to secure a ceasefire with Russia?
interview_answer: On that second issue, my response is yes. It really hinges on what the G-20 decides. I—that came up in our discussions today. And I suggested that if that’s not workable—if Indonesia and others won’t support it—then we ought, in my opinion, to request that both parties be allowed to participate in the sessions and—well, essentially, to be present at and observe the G-20 gathering. Now, on your earlier point—could you remind me what that first question was?

question: Do you categorically oppose allowing John Bolton to testify?
interview_answer: Look, you’re really throwing a lot at me here.

question: National sovereignty as a fundamental pillar of the global system.
interview_answer: I’m not going to address that directly; how am I supposed to square it with responsibility to protect, which once meant what, exactly?

question: What military response would North Korea face if it failed to honor these commitments?
interview_answer: I’m not going to get into that—yes, I understand the question. It’s a very difficult subject to address, because I don’t want to issue threats. I don’t want to come across as threatening. They got that message. And you’ve seen what many believed was on the verge of happening. Now, Seoul has about 28 million residents. We think we have huge cities—look at New York, with 8 million people, and we call that a big city. Seoul has 28 million. Just think about that. And it sits right on the frontier, right up against the DMZ. It’s right there. If this had gone wrong, I’ve heard estimates of a hundred thousand casualties, but I think you could have seen 20 or 30 million lives lost. I feel it’s a real honor to be in this position, because I believe we might have been looking at 30, 40, even 50 million people killed. Seoul, one of the largest cities on earth, is directly on the border.

question: Leaning toward withdrawing the military forces?
interview_answer: You want me to say that?

question: Does the President intend to reverse the Defense Department’s rule that bars the press from showing images of returning flag-draped military coffins?
interview_answer:
This is a very timely issue. We’ve just received word that four American service members were killed in Iraq today, and of course our deepest sympathies and prayers are with their families. I’ve mentioned before that people sometimes ask me, “When did it really sink in that you’re President?” And I’ve told them that the most sobering part of this job is signing letters to the families of those we’ve lost in uniform. It’s a stark reminder of the weight of this office and of the real human consequences that flow from the decisions I make.

Now, regarding your specific question about allowing media coverage when the remains of our loved ones are brought home, we are currently reviewing those guidelines in consultation with the Department of Defense. Because that review is still underway, I am not going to give you a definitive position at this time, before I’ve seen the results and fully understood all the ramifications.

On Afghanistan, we face an enormous challenge. Thanks to the exceptional efforts of our troops, and some very effective diplomacy by Ambassador Crocker in Iraq, we’ve just witnessed an election there that proceeded with relatively little violence, and there is a sense that their political institutions are starting to function in a real way.

That is not yet the case in Afghanistan. Elections are upcoming, but the central government appears quite removed from the daily realities in much of the country. On top of that, the Taliban and Al Qaida are operating in the FATA and along the Afghanistan‑Pakistan border, and we have not yet seen the sustained, coordinated action needed to eliminate those safe havens that is essential for our long‑term success.

So we are conducting a comprehensive review. General Petraeus, now leading CENTCOM, is carrying out his own assessment and is coordinating closely with my special envoy, Richard Holbrooke, one of our most experienced diplomats, to develop a broader regional strategy. We will have to better align our military operations with our diplomatic and development initiatives, and improve coordination with our allies, if we’re going to achieve our objectives.

The key point I want the American people to bear in mind—because this will be hard—is that this region was the launching pad for an attack that killed 3,000 Americans. Just this past week, I met with families who lost loved ones on 9/11, which underscores the cost of permitting such safe havens to endure. My bottom line is that we simply cannot allow Al Qaida to maintain those sanctuaries. We must act intelligently and effectively, and we must persist, to ensure those safe havens are dismantled. I cannot yet give you a precise timeline for how long that will require. What I can say is that I will not permit Al Qaida or bin Laden to operate freely, plotting attacks against the United States.

All right, Helene Cooper from the New York Times. Helene, where are you? There you are.

question: Your stance on imprisoning political rivals for political reasons
interview_answer:
All right. Turning first to the refugee question: the Paris attacks were only a week ago. The news cycle moves at such a rapid clip that it’s easy to forget how recent that was. The events have been so present in the media, and the images of a major Western city under attack are so familiar to Americans, that I understand why the House moved as quickly as it did on refugee-related legislation.

My expectation, though, is that, now that there’s been a moment to step back and review the situation carefully, people recognize that refugees admitted to the United States go through the most intensive, exhaustive background checks of anyone who comes to this country. The system we have, built up across several administrations from both parties, is extremely rigorous, and it currently takes about 18 to 24 months for an applicant to be cleared.

So even though the House bill, on the surface, just adds certification requirements—and is not as extreme as some of the other ideas floated earlier in the week by certain presidential contenders—the reality is that if you pile on enough procedural hurdles, you end up effectively shutting the program down. When you add layer upon layer of bureaucracy that doesn’t meaningfully improve security or screening, but simply grinds the process to a halt, you are, in practice, cutting off refugee admissions for people in dire need.

When I spoke of betraying our values, I was referring very specifically to remarks suggesting, for instance, that we might admit only Christians and bar Muslims, or that we are so frightened that we would refuse entry even to a 4‑year‑old orphan. Those of you who accompanied me to the refugee center yesterday and saw those children up close—that’s who we’re discussing. If you’re a parent, and you saw what they’ve endured, the idea that we couldn’t find a place for them anywhere in the United States runs counter to who we are.

The encouraging thing is that most Americans, once they understand how extensive our vetting already is and once they actually see these kids, would say, yes, we must do right by them.

Now, as for the matter involving Prime Minister Najib, I have a standard practice: I do not offer public commentary on investigations under way in the United States, and I’m certainly not going to weigh in on any probes or legal cases that may be occurring in another country while I’m there as a guest. I am therefore not going to address that specific issue. What I did do in my discussions with Prime Minister Najib—as I have with many leaders in Southeast Asia, in Africa, in Latin America, and elsewhere—is underscore the critical importance of openness in government, real accountability, and a serious commitment to fighting corruption. These are all barriers to development; they keep nations from advancing, they entrench poverty, and they hold back growth.

If governments tackle those challenges head‑on, even nations that are landlocked or lack natural resources can succeed. If they ignore them, then no amount of oil, minerals, or favorable geography will prevent eventual failure.

I also made a point of stressing to Prime Minister Najib how vital civil society and an independent press are to a country’s long‑term success. Every nation represented here is at its own point on the curve of social and economic development, and we don’t insist that everyone duplicate the precise route taken by the United States. But there are some core principles that, as friends and partners, we believe should be upheld.

Look at a country like Myanmar, which just held a landmark election after four decades of military rule. No one expects them to transform overnight into a model Scandinavian democracy, but the shift we’re seeing there is profound, and we want to engage and reinforce that progress.

Much of our work—the Open Government Partnership I launched through the U.N., our bilateral efforts to strengthen transparency, and the standards built into TPP—is aimed at elevating expectations, pushing countries to aim higher. Some will advance faster than others; some will backslide. The pace will differ, but the direction we’re trying to encourage is the same: a world where ordinary people are treated justly, where the rule of law prevails, where governments are transparent and answerable to their citizens, where people’s voices count, where women have equal standing, and where minorities are not marginalized.

Those are deeply American ideals, but I believe they also belong to humanity as a whole.

Thank you, everyone. Let’s call it a day.

question: What is your position on the topic of federally required busing?
interview_answer: Look, before I touch that subject, let me say this: I think she was overrated. He didn’t perform well, for sure, and the facts may not have backed him up, but she still got more praise than she deserved. What she did wasn’t that remarkable. And frankly, I think he—Biden—took more of a beating than he really should have. In terms of my own view on that issue, I’m not going to lay it out today. In about a month we’ll roll out some policies that a lot of people will find very interesting and probably unexpected. Jennifer, from Bloomberg, do you have something you’d like to ask?

question: By what means can you guarantee that North Korea will actually give up its nuclear weapons?
interview_answer:
Look, can anyone truly guarantee anything? Can I guarantee that you’ll sit in that chair the right way when you sit down? You just can’t absolutely guarantee things. What I can tell you is that they’re interested in reaching an agreement. That’s my business. My entire career has been about making deals. I’ve been very successful at it, that’s what I do. And I can tell when someone is ready to negotiate and when they’re not. Many politicians can’t do that—that’s not their skill set, but it is mine.

Honestly, this probably could have been handled more easily years ago. But I know—I feel very strongly, based on my instincts, my skill, my talent—that they want an agreement. And getting an agreement would be terrific for the world. It’s also very good for China, because I can’t see China being comfortable with nuclear weapons sitting right on their doorstep. They were very cooperative on this. So I believe he wants to reach a deal. Can anyone be totally sure? No. But we’ll find out soon enough as the talks move forward. Okay? Thanks a lot. Next question.

question: So, are the sanctions failing?
interview_answer:
At this stage, we simply cannot say definitively whether the sanctions are achieving their ultimate objective. What we can say is that they are clearly having the intended impact of placing very heavy pressure on the Russian economy. That is not just my personal view; if you look at market reactions, at the scale of capital flight, and at current growth forecasts for Russia, you can see that their economy has essentially stalled. Estimates suggest somewhere between $100 billion and $200 billion has left the country. We’re not seeing a surge of new foreign investors setting up shop in Russia.

Sanctions have forced President Putin to confront a real choice: either move toward settling the situation in eastern Ukraine through negotiation and peaceful means—accepting that Ukraine is a sovereign nation and that the Ukrainian people must decide their own future—or stay on his present course and inflict long‑term damage on Russia’s economy and his own citizens’ wellbeing.

From that perspective, we are following exactly the course we should, and we’re glad our European partners and other countries around the globe have joined us in this effort. But despite that, the fundamental issue is still unresolved. Fighting continues in eastern Ukraine, civilians are still being killed, and we’ve already witnessed the tragic consequences of this conflict in the downing of the Malaysian airliner.

Only when we see real progress—a serious dialogue that ensures all Ukrainians have a voice, that political processes function, that the reforms promised by the government in Kyiv are implemented to safeguard Russian speakers and decentralize power, and when President Putin fully accepts Ukraine’s independence—only then can we honestly say the underlying problem has been settled. Until that point, I cannot tell you that the sanctions have fully ‘worked’ in the sense of resolving the crisis. What I can say is that, in the meantime, they are operating exactly as they were designed to, by exerting substantial economic strain on Russia.

question: Schedule for ultimately pulling U.S. forces out of Afghanistan
interview_answer:
Your question comes at a very sobering moment. We’ve just received word that four American service members were killed in Iraq today, and of course my thoughts and prayers go first and foremost to their loved ones. I’ve said before, when people ask me, “When did it really sink in that you’re President?” that the most sobering moment is signing the letters to families of our fallen. It drives home the weight of this office and the real-world consequences of every decision I make.

On the question of allowing media coverage of the return of the fallen, as you know, that policy is currently under review in close consultation with the Department of Defense. So I’m not going to give you a definitive response on that today before I’ve seen the results of that review and carefully considered all the ramifications.

Turning to Afghanistan, this is going to be an enormous test. Thanks to the remarkable efforts of our troops and some very skillful diplomacy by Ambassador Crocker, we’ve just witnessed an election in Iraq that, by and large, was conducted peacefully. There’s a sense now that Iraqi politics are starting to function in a real, substantive way.

We don’t yet see that same kind of functioning system in Afghanistan. They do have elections scheduled, but the central government still appears largely disconnected from the daily realities in many parts of the country.

On top of that, the Taliban and Al Qaeda are operating out of the FATA and other border areas between Afghanistan and Pakistan. We have not yet seen the sustained, coordinated push to dismantle those sanctuaries that will be necessary for our overall mission to succeed.

So we are engaged in a comprehensive reassessment. General Petraeus, now leading CENTCOM, is conducting his own review, and he’s working closely with my special representative, Richard Holbrooke, one of our most experienced diplomats, to craft a broader regional strategy. Success will require much tighter integration of our military operations with our diplomatic efforts and development work, as well as much better coordination with our allies.

The core point I want the American people to keep in mind—because this is not going to be easy—is that this region once served as the launching pad for an attack that killed 3,000 of our fellow citizens. Just this past week, I met with families who lost loved ones on 9/11, a stark reminder of the price we pay if those safe havens are allowed to endure. My fundamental position is that we cannot permit Al Qaeda to maintain those bases of operations. We cannot accept terrorist sanctuaries in that part of the world. We have to proceed intelligently and effectively, but also persistently, to eliminate them.

I do not, at this point, have a timetable for how long that will take. What I can tell you is that I am not going to set an end date in a way that would let Al Qaeda or bin Laden act freely, plotting attacks against the United States. So I’m not going to give you a specific withdrawal schedule today.

All right. Helene Cooper from the New York Times—Helene, where are you? There you are.

question: How did you view the clash between White House Counsel Pat Cipollone and Jerrold Nadler that took place yesterday evening?
interview_answer: Look, you're hitting me with too many questions.

question: Can you verify reports of drone operations taking place in Yemen?
interview_answer: I’m not going to engage in any discussion of operational matters, Ed Henry.

question: Do you expect the markets to hold out for two years?
interview_answer: I really should’ve taken notes on all this, Jackie. I’ve got no more space left in my head right now.

question: What steps should we take next on the Merida Initiative?
interview_answer: I actually didn’t get that one interpreted. [] It really sounded like an excellent question, though. [] I think the translation is coming through now. Please, go ahead.

question: The QAnon movement has been expanding during the pandemic.
interview_answer: You know, I really don’t know a lot about that group. What I’ve been told is they’re very supportive of me, which I’m thankful for, but beyond that I don’t know much about them. I’ve heard they’re getting more and more popular. And from what people tell me, these are folks who, when they see what’s happening on the streets in Portland, when they see what’s been going on in New York City over the last 6 or 7 months—and, honestly, this kind of thing was starting up 4 years ago when I got here, almost 4 years, hard to believe—these are people who don’t like what they’re seeing in places like Portland, Chicago, New York, and other cities and States. I’m told they’re people who really love our country, and they just hate watching this happen. So I don’t, in truth, really know anything about them except that they supposedly like me, and they want the trouble in these cities—especially the ones we’re talking about—to stop. There’s no reason Democrats can’t manage a city, and if they can’t, we’ll send in whatever Federal resources—troops, law enforcement, whatever they want—and we’ll fix it in 24 hours or less. All right?

question: Is this a hard Brexit?
interview_answer: Is it heart‑wrenching?

question: Could you share any details or views about David Cameron?
interview_answer:
I've never had any dealings with him. And really, it's not my place to be offering guidance to the head of the Conservative Party, or to comment on the internal affairs of another political party. What he and his colleagues decide to do, including whether he comes here or not, is entirely a matter for them.

Let me instead point out something broader: we should set aside, for a moment, personalities and individual party leaders, and look at the relationship between our two nations. Even people who vehemently oppose the Iraq conflict, for example, generally recognize that we are engaged in a global struggle today. And we should also recognize that this is, at its core, a struggle over the values that will shape the world in the early 21st century.

You don’t prevail in that kind of struggle by being a fair‑weather ally. You don’t win it by wavering or backing away from each other when circumstances become difficult. You don’t win it by losing your resolve while your adversary’s determination remains intense and unshaken.

The principles our two countries stand for are demonstrated by what you’ve seen here today. The President faces searching questions from the American press; I face, I like to claim, equally demanding questions from the British press. And as we talk here, I can’t actually hear what’s being shouted over there, but I’d wager it isn’t entirely flattering to either of us—unless, of course, it’s the supporters we brought along, which I rather doubt.

That, however, is the essence of it: democracy. It’s about people being able to voice their opinions freely, and about elected leaders being forced to justify their decisions and accept the consequences if the public disapproves. That shared commitment to democratic values is crucial in the world today.

Now, of course, if you’re advising a politician anywhere in Europe, you know that the easiest way to get applause is to stand up and attack America. Criticize the President, and you’ll get a standing ovation. And if people want to do that, that’s up to them. But once all the applause and posturing are stripped away, you are left with something stark, basic, and unmistakable: the contest of values is still underway.

We can argue about errors and specific decisions, and argue over Iraq—whether we should have done one thing or another. But what is happening in Iraq now is that our enemy is directly confronting us. And if, whenever our enemy confronts us, our response is to edge away from our friends, to make small accommodations to spare ourselves some of the burden, criticism, and unpopularity that comes with hard choices, then our enemy draws strength from that. They watch us constantly. They ask whether we will stand firm for what we believe in, or whether, if they keep up the pressure, our will fades and we walk away because it is too hard—because public opinion is tough, because the polls look grim.

That is what leadership is about. And it’s not just about me and the President; it’s about anyone in public life. People often deride politics as nothing more than posturing, slogans, and, on occasion, worse. But when politics is done properly—when people genuinely defend what they believe in—it is about serving the public, and there is no dishonor in that. The choices are hard; of course they are.

We made a choice we knew was very difficult. I believed then, and I still believe, that it was the right call. History will deliver its verdict in due course. But I am certain of this: what we are embodying today—standing here together in the Rose Garden, taking your questions, and subjecting ourselves to your scrutiny—represents a way of life that is far better than either a brutal secular dictatorship or fanatical religious rule. It is a better way of life, and whenever people are genuinely given the option, it is the way of life they choose.

Our task, as two nations, is to extend that choice to as many people as we can, and to do so with confidence. The world demands courage. And I can say that this good man beside me is a brave leader.

So, to return to your original point, it really isn’t for me to pass judgment on Mr. Cameron personally or to advise him, and I’m not going to do that. Thank you all for coming.

question: Did they make any remarks directed at you personally, Mr. President?
interview_answer: I have no idea. I never inquired about that. It may have had nothing to do with me at all. It could have been about something completely different. What we do know is that it was outside the grounds—on the wall and, as you’re aware, the fencing there, especially the newer sections, is extremely strong. But this was beyond the White House perimeter. All right? The Secret Service will issue a full report on it shortly.

question: Does a congressional vote criticizing the president weaken his ability to implement his Iraq strategy?
interview_answer:
Yes, thank you. Let me make a few points: First, I recognize that Congress is going to voice its views, and it’s obvious where the Democrats stand, and some Republicans as well; I’m well aware of that. They didn’t approve of the choice I made. That doesn’t mean I question their integrity or patriotism—they’re decent citizens who simply disagree. I listened to some of their arguments and concluded that following their approach would not produce an Iraq that can govern and sustain itself and stand with us in the fight against terror—that’s my judgment.

Second, my concern is that this symbolic resolution not be turned into binding legislation that blocks our troops from carrying out the mission I’ve assigned them. That’s why I keep pointing out that, on one hand, Senators unanimously confirmed David Petraeus, yet on the other, some now say they won’t finance the strategy he said he needed to fulfill his responsibilities, a strategy he laid out under oath in the Senate. I will be absolutely clear with Members of Congress, starting right away, that they must fund our forces and preserve the flexibility required to complete the mission.

I also think it’s noteworthy that they’re passing judgment on a plan they have not allowed time to take effect. I get that; I do. Now, what was the other part of your question?

question: Have you obtained any additional information about how Sergeant Bergdahl was taken prisoner?
interview_answer:
From the very beginning of our nation’s history, the United States has operated under a deeply held principle: we do not abandon our servicemen and servicewomen. That commitment goes all the way back to the Revolutionary War.

For quite some time now, we have been in consultation with Congress about the likelihood that we might need to carry out a prisoner swap to secure Sergeant Bergdahl’s release. When an opening presented itself, we were worried about his physical condition, we had Qatar prepared to help facilitate the exchange, and we moved quickly to act. The timeline was compressed precisely because we did not want to lose that chance.

On the specific question of how Sergeant Bergdahl ended up in Taliban hands, we have plainly not been debriefing him. He is coming out of five years in captivity. Right now he is undergoing extensive medical and psychological evaluations, and he faces a major adjustment returning to everyday life. He has not even been reunited with his family yet, which should underscore how seriously we are treating this reintegration phase, informed in part by the lessons we took from the Vietnam era. So I am not going to delve into, or speculate about, the details of his capture at this time.

But let me underscore a straightforward principle here: no matter what the facts ultimately show about those circumstances, we bring home an American soldier who is being held by the enemy. That’s it. That’s non-negotiable. We do not make that duty contingent on the particular situation. That is what every parent sending a son or daughter into harm’s way deserves to count on from their Commander in Chief and from the United States of America.

As for any potential risks, the transfer of the Taliban detainees from Guantánamo came with conditions: the Qataris have agreed to maintain oversight and set up a framework that enables us to track their activities. We will also be monitoring them. Could some of them attempt to go back to behavior that threatens us? Yes, that possibility exists, just as it has for every detainee released from Guantánamo; there is always some rate of return to the battlefield. I would not have authorized this if I believed it ran counter to American national security interests, and we are confident we will be able to respond if they engage in actions that endanger us.

This is the kind of arrangement that has marked the close of wars throughout our history. It was the case for George Washington, for Abraham Lincoln, for Franklin Roosevelt, and in every major conflict: at a certain point, you work to bring your people home. That is the appropriate course of action.

question: The journalist asks President Biden to clarify why travelers from Germany and other EU countries are still barred from entering the United States, while people from Turkey—where the current infection rate is seven times higher—are nevertheless allowed to come in.
interview_answer:
We invited the head of our COVID response team precisely because the Chancellor brought this up. The issue of when we can remove that restriction is under active review right now. I don’t have a definitive answer for you today, but I expect I’ll be able to tell you within the coming days what we’re likely to do. I’m waiting for guidance from our experts and the COVID team on the appropriate timing. And, yes, the Chancellor did, in fact, put this on the table.

I did indeed raise the matter, and I received essentially the same response you just heard from the President. The COVID task force is currently examining the question. We discussed it in both directions. The spread of the Delta variant is increasing, and that presents a fresh challenge for both of us. Obviously, before taking a step like this, one must think it through very carefully and reach a decision that can last. It would make no sense to lift measures only to reverse them a few days later. I therefore have full trust in the American COVID experts.

Having visited many times, I also know that if we don’t leave now, we’ll be late for dinner. The President and I are expected shortly by a number of guests. So we have to end here. Thank you for your attention and for your questions. Thank you very much.

question: Do you believe these three women are all lying? Please answer yes or no.
interview_answer: I’m not going to say that right now. I need to wait until tomorrow, listen to what’s said, and read what comes out. I only heard about one of them not long ago. What I can tell you is that her attorney is a real bottom-feeder, all right? So I’m not going to declare whether they’re lying or not until I’ve actually heard their statements. I don’t know what happened today because I’ve been tied up with Japan, with South Korea, with , and about seven other countries, as you know, and I presided over the Security Council. So I’m not familiar with what today’s accuser said. I do know the lawyer, though, and you really don’t find many worse—terrible reputation. Check his record. As for the other women, I’m going to wait and see what comes out tomorrow. I’ll be watching—believe it or not. I’ll listen to what’s said. It’s possible they turn out to be persuasive. Now, with all that, Judge Brett Kavanaugh has long been considered one of the most respected figures in Washington. He’s served on what you’d probably call the second-highest court, and everyone there knows him; many know him very well. Those people don’t buy what’s being alleged. I can always be persuaded, but I have to hear it first, so I’m not answering that now.

question: Do you believe Ghislaine Maxwell is going to expose influential men involved?
interview_answer: I really can’t say. I haven’t been paying much attention to the case. Honestly, I simply hope things go well for her. I’ve run into her quite a few times over the years, particularly when I was in Palm Beach and, as I understand it, they were in Palm Beach too. But I do wish her well, whatever the circumstances. I’m not familiar with what’s going on with Prince Andrew. I just don’t know. I’m not up to speed on it. Yes, next question, please.

question: Do you believe you bear any responsibility for Tony Blair not remaining in office throughout your time as president?
interview_answer: I haven’t taken a straw poll of the Labour Party, so maybe. [] But as for whether I caused his departure, I really can’t say.

question: Looking back on that difficult year in office, what does the President personally view as the greatest error they made in their choices and conduct?
interview_answer: Are you asking about mistakes just on health care, or about everything?

question: If, from the President’s viewpoint, there is any cause for worry.
interview_answer: To be honest, I can’t really say. I wasn’t part of negotiating that agreement. In principle the agreement seems fine, but I’ll have to get back to you. We’ll take a closer look at it and then we’ll know more.

question: Can you say where that is?
interview_answer: Look, I’m not going to reveal that right now. I need to wait and see. I’ve kept myself out of the middle of this on purpose. As, essentially, the nation’s top law enforcement official, I could step in if I chose to, but I decided it was wiser not to. I think it’s far better for our excellent Attorney General to take the lead. He has Durham, who’s extremely well regarded, handling it. We’ll find out in due time. I’m not going to tell you where it is.

question: So you're saying relations aren't that warm?
interview_answer: At this point, I really can’t say. What I can tell you is that I’m waiting to hear directly from President Putin about how he viewed the meeting. What matters most to me is whether he still shares the same concerns I have. I say “still” because when we met in Australia, he again made clear that he understands it’s not in the global interest for Iran to gain the ability to build a nuclear weapon. Russia has backed key measures at the United Nations, and we’re cooperating with them on a possible third resolution. That’s where my focus is—I’m not concerned about the optics. I understand why these meetings happen. I will keep working with Russia and other partners to maintain steady pressure and make clear: if you push ahead with nuclear weapons ambitions, you will remain cut off from the international community.

question: Do you have even an approximate timeline for Asad’s departure?
interview_answer:
We would have liked to see Asad leave two years ago, a year ago, six months ago, even a couple of months ago. My administration has been entirely consistent: Asad forfeited his legitimacy the moment he turned his guns on his own people and began killing citizens who at first were peacefully demanding a greater say in how their country is run. That violence has only intensified over time. So in terms of timing, our position is that the earlier he goes, the better.

Now, as to the question of how that happens, I think we’ve already touched on that. There is no simple, hidden solution for a situation as brutally complicated as the one we’re talking about. If there were, the Prime Minister and I would already have implemented it and this would be behind us.

Instead, what we can do is keep up steady international pressure and continue to bolster the opposition. I believe that the possibility of negotiations in Geneva, bringing in the Russians and representatives to work seriously on a political transition that all sides can accept, could bear fruit. In the meantime, we’ll keep assisting the opposition and addressing the humanitarian crisis, doing all of this in very close coordination with Turkey, which is deeply engaged and with whom we have an excellent partnership.

Thank you. As you know, we’ll be meeting again this evening, and we’ll have an opportunity then to go into more detail. As I’ve already said, our perspectives are very similar, and in our talks tonight we’ll keep examining what steps we can take jointly, and what elements might form a roadmap with Geneva as a reference point and beyond.

Bringing Russia and China into this effort is crucial, especially given their roles as permanent members of the U.N. Security Council. Their engagement in this process can help create additional momentum. Ongoing international pressure remains key. When we review the humanitarian aid we’ve provided up to now, it already totals more than $1.5 billion.

We are also maintaining an open-door policy and will go on doing so, because we share a 910-kilometer border with that country, and there are families on both sides. We will therefore continue these efforts.

All of this is vital for regional stability. On one side, there are initiatives underway to normalize relations between Israelis and Palestinians; we don’t need new crises in the region. We had previously taken steps to bring that country and Israel together to address their disputes. We held five rounds of talks, but unfortunately they collapsed. Still, I hope that future measures we take for regional peace will be successful and that we can work with the United States with determination to achieve peace.

We are actively discussing all of these matters. The actions to be taken by the U.N. Security Council and the Geneva framework are important, and we will keep evaluating them together.

Thank you. Thank you all. Thanks, everyone. Thank you.

question: Have you settled on a nominee to lead the Federal Reserve Board yet? And if you haven’t, can you walk us through what factors you’re weighing as you decide on the next Fed Chair and candidates for the other vacant positions?
interview_answer: No, no, and still no. I’m not going to get into that with you, because that process is underway right now. We’ll be rolling out those decisions fairly soon. This has been moving forward for quite a while, first of all. Second, I want to stress that we’ll have ample time to ensure all the key nominees can be confirmed before the current terms end. And third, I’ve spent a great deal of time on this and have been consulting with my economic team about the strongest options, and we have many. But I’m not going to offer any predictions today. Nancy—Nancy Cordes from CBS—I think you had your hand up. Did you want to jump in?

question: Do you trust Secretary Azar?
interview_answer: I’ll wish him the best. Trust in whom?

question: What timeframe have you been given for how long this is expected to last?
interview_answer: I haven’t been given any exact timetable. They’ve been in place for quite a while now, under him and the person before him. At some stage, I assume, circumstances will shift. But we actually haven’t implemented the really severe sanctions yet. We’re able to apply much tougher measures, and every option is still on the table, so we might go in that direction. As you know, we’ve imposed sanctions that are more or less in the middle range so far, not the harshest ones. We could escalate significantly if that becomes necessary. What’s happening is extremely tragic, and our only objective is to help the many people who are hungry and dying out on the streets. The situation there is shameful. This used to be among the richest nations on earth, and suddenly it’s devastated—overwhelmed by misery and poverty, with no food, no clean water, no air conditioning, with nothing. Last week they went a long time without power. No electricity. And that’s going to fail again, because the system is barely holding together. It’s heartbreaking to witness, Roberta. Truly heartbreaking. All right?

question: Meaning of the term torture
interview_answer: In what context?

question: Should the delivery of heavy weapons to Ukraine by NATO members require a unanimous decision, and should Germany, as Europe’s leading NATO power, itself provide such heavy arms to Ukraine?
interview_answer:
There is no issue of having to restore confidence here. He enjoys the full confidence of the United States. Germany is—without question—one of our most crucial partners globally. The strength of the German‑U.S. relationship is not in doubt. None at all.

When it comes to supporting Ukraine, Germany has been among the biggest financial backers. Berlin has taken a leading role in ensuring that Ukraine receives economic help.

You also raised another point—you’ve mentioned so many things that I can’t keep track of each one. But regarding claims in the U.S. press that Germany is not dependable, Germany is entirely dependable—wholly, completely, absolutely dependable. I have zero doubts about Germany. We stand together. The transatlantic bond between Germany and the United States is a lasting cornerstone of German policy, and it will remain just as vital going forward. This will always rank among our highest priorities.

As for NATO, we are the continental European state making the largest overall contribution—financially as well as in terms of military capabilities. And we shoulder a very significant share of the burden—we actually do not accept your premise about who is paying the largest share of financial aid to Ukraine. Since 2014, we have provided roughly 2 billion U.S. dollars in direct bilateral assistance, plus another 3.8 billion through EU mechanisms. These are major resources devoted to stabilizing Ukraine’s economy, and we intend to keep up that level of engagement.

That underlines the deep and unshakeable friendship between our two nations. As part of this, in response to the tense situation on Ukraine’s border caused by Russian forces, we have made it absolutely clear that we will act in full unity on sanctions. I am not going to go beyond that and discuss specific weapons deliveries.

question: Do you have worries about how easy or difficult it is for people to vote?
interview_answer: From what I’ve been told, things ran very smoothly in Georgia—really very smoothly. But once again, that’s something you’d need to take up with the state officials, because I’m not the one who’s going to address that for you. All right, next question. Yes, you there, go ahead.

question: Your thoughts on Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg’s hospitalization due to an infection.
interview_answer: I’m not going to address that directly; I just hope she recovers quickly. I actually wasn’t aware of it. She’s in the hospital now? That’s unfortunate. I simply wish her a full recovery.

question: Were you briefed on any confirmed coronavirus infections at the White House today?
interview_answer: You mean, as of today?

question: Did this constitute a threat directed at the President?
interview_answer: We’re not sure at this point. We simply don’t know. Investigators will determine that.

question: How are you going to help them, sir, beyond Syria?
interview_answer: Look, what I can say is that we’re going to be talking through that issue, and those conversations are already underway. What I will emphasize is that the Kurds gave us extraordinary backing in defeating ISIS. All right?

question: What will you do to bring Democrats on board with that plan?
interview_answer: That’s something you’ll need to ask them.

question: Will they continue receiving an extra $600 each week?
interview_answer: I'm not going to confirm that right now. You'll find out when it takes effect, but the funds are there.

question: Possible effects on peace talks
interview_answer: Let me start by saying I find it notable that this move came only after the election, even though we had long expected it. It clearly underscores that Russia’s forces are facing significant difficulties. That’s the first point. Second, will this change the dynamics of peace efforts? At the very least, it’s going to create a pause over the winter for all sides to reassess their positions. Beyond that, we simply don’t yet know whether Ukraine will decide it’s ready to make any concessions to Russia, and I’m not going to venture a prediction on that. I’ll be heading to the G‑20, where I’m told President Putin probably won’t attend, but other world leaders will be in Indonesia, and we’ll use that setting to determine what the subsequent steps might be. Nancy from CBS. Nancy Cordes.

question: Can you provide us with the Red Cross report?
interview_answer: I’m not familiar with that document. Our policy is that we do not torture. Next question, Jim.

question: Do you personally regard homosexual behavior as morally wrong, sir?
interview_answer: I am not going to pass moral judgment on anyone’s sexual orientation. What I will say is that I support the ‘don’t ask, don’t tell’ policy as sound policy. Sammon, from the Washington Times, go ahead.

question: Do you have concerns about this?
interview_answer: Look, I’m not going to say anything more on that. Mary Bruce.

question: What was the reason Prime Minister Maliki did not appear at yesterday’s press briefing with General Casey and Ambassador Khalilzad?
interview_answer: Elaine, I honestly don’t know why he chose not to attend.

question: In your view, how much real influence does he wield while Putin remains involved?
interview_answer:
I don't really have much detailed knowledge about Medvedev either. What will be worth watching is who actually shows up—who formally speaks for Russia—at forums like the G‑8, for instance. That kind of thing will be revealing; it should, I think, give us some sense of how Russia plans to handle its foreign policy once Vladimir Putin is no longer President. And at this point, I just can't give you an answer to that question.

What I can say is that it remains in our national interest to maintain a working relationship with Russia. Take proliferation, for example: it's crucial for us to cooperate in ensuring that dangerous materials with the potential to cause massive harm do not spread. It's also in our interest to coordinate with Russia on Iran. As I mentioned, I believe, the last time I spoke here, I welcomed President Putin’s statement to the Iranians that Russia would supply the enriched uranium needed to fuel the Bushehr reactor, which removes any justification for Iran to enrich on its own. I regarded that as a helpful move, and we need to be able to partner with Russia on the Iran issue.

There are many other areas as well—yesterday, for example, I discussed a European missile defense system with the Prime Minister of the Czech Republic. I think it’s important, from our standpoint, to find a way to make clear to the Russians that this system is not directed at them but at the real 21st‑century dangers, like the possibility that a rogue regime might launch a weapon of mass destruction.

So there are a number of fronts, David, where cooperation is necessary—and let me finish—and my goal is to leave things in a state where my successor can have a constructive relationship with whoever is managing Russia’s foreign policy. That’s simply in America’s interest. That doesn’t imply we’ll always see eye to eye. We clearly disagreed about Kosovo, and there will be other points of disagreement. Yet it still serves our country to maintain a relationship, leader to leader and, ideally, at deeper levels than that.

question: Question asking what the speaker thinks about Hillary Clinton being the nominee.
interview_answer: Excuse me?

question: How do you interpret the unidentified aerial phenomena that President Obama talked about?
interview_answer: You should put that question to him again. Thanks. [] Alright, chief, we’re done here. []

question: Do you think the Government’s move to obtain Associated Press reporters’ phone records this week—or in the episode that just came to light—went too far?
interview_answer:
I’ll leave it to all of you to make those kinds of comparisons, look back at the record, and reach your own judgments. My focus is on making sure that when something goes wrong inside the Government, we correct it. That’s my job, and that’s what we’re going to do. That applies to the IRS, where we have to ensure the law is being enforced as Congress intended. It also applies to the safety of our diplomats, which is why we’ll have to work with Congress so they provide sufficient resources for what’s needed in the field.

On the Justice Department specifically, I’m not going to weigh in on an individual, ongoing matter. I can, however, speak in general terms about the balance we have to maintain. Disclosures involving national security can endanger people. They can endanger the men and women in uniform whom I’ve ordered into harm’s way. They can endanger some of our intelligence personnel, who are deployed in sensitive, high‑risk situations that could easily be compromised.

Our national security depends on those individuals being able to do their jobs with the assurance that people back here are supporting them, so they’re not left exposed or put in even greater danger than they already face. I won’t apologize for that, and I don’t think the American people expect their Commander in Chief to be indifferent to information that could jeopardize missions or cost lives.

At the same time, we are a democracy, and a free press, free speech, and the unrestricted flow of information are essential to holding me and the rest of the Government accountable and to keeping our democracy healthy. I entered public life because I believe very strongly in that democratic process.

The purpose of the media shield legislation—which was developed and broadly backed by groups like the Washington Post editorial board and by prosecutors—was to find an appropriate way to balance those interests. And insofar as this matter—about which we still don’t have every fact—has renewed the debate about how best to set that balance, I think this is exactly the moment to revisit that bill. That’s a useful and important discussion to have.

But I also think we have to remember that when we raise alarms about leaks at a time when more than 60,000 of our troops are still in Afghanistan and many intelligence officers are operating in dangerous posts—some as hazardous as the one in Benghazi—part of my responsibility is to safeguard what they do, while still making room for the flow of information the public needs in order to stay informed and hold my office accountable. So I am not going to discuss the particulars of this case, but I am going to stay focused on getting that balance right.

question: Do you believe that imposing a price cap on Russian oil exports will actually reduce gasoline costs for Americans?
interview_answer: Let me go back for a moment—the latter part of what you asked was, would that lower the price?

question: Is the administration weighing any measures to prohibit or limit Chinese reporters working in the U.S.?
interview_answer: I haven’t been fully briefed on that issue yet, so I can’t really speak to it right now. We’re going to examine it, we will review it carefully. It’s not something I like to see happening. I don’t think it’s right, because we allow very broad access here—some might even argue we allow too much, which I don’t agree with. I think our access is very strong, so it seems unfair. But we’ll reach a decision on this in the near future. All right? Thank you, it’s a good question. Next, please. Yes, go ahead.

question: What information does the President have about the accusations made against nominee Dr. Ronny Jackson?
interview_answer:
To start with, I’m not familiar with the specific accusations you’re referring to, but I can say this: he is one of the best people I’ve ever come across, and I think I can safely include Melania in that as well. He’s served as physician to President Obama, I believe also to President Bush, and to me. I’ve gotten to know him quite well. He’s an excellent doctor.

His nomination came as a recommendation. Now, I’m aware people are raising concerns about his lack of experience, and experience is an issue because the Veterans Affairs Department is extremely important to me. We’ve done really strong work there, as you know, with the Accountability Act and a number of other initiatives. Now we’re moving forward on choice. That’s going to get done. We’re going to look after our veterans incredibly well. That’s a top priority, and we’ve already made major progress.

But I just spoke with Admiral Jackson and I asked him, honestly, Why put yourself through this? This is a ruthless crowd that smears people—and they do exactly that. I’ve been through it myself; we’ve all been through it. You in the media are getting record viewership because of it, so congratulations. But I said to him, Why do you need this? He’s already an admiral. He’s a strong leader. And they’re nitpicking him over every tiny thing.

As you saw with what will, hopefully very soon, be Secretary of State Pompeo, everyone was shocked by how that turned out. Ten minutes before the committee vote yesterday I was being told, He’s not getting out of committee—which would have been the first time in many, many years that a Secretary of State nominee failed there—except I spoke with Rand Paul. Rand has almost never disappointed me. He’s a good man. And I knew some things others didn’t. Rand told me he was changing his vote, and he did, and everyone was stunned. And in fact, it ended up 11–9 because, as you know, Johnny Isakson’s vote is counted so long as it isn’t the deciding vote. So really it was 11–9 with, I think, one member—how would you describe that, John?

So I have not heard the particular allegations you’re asking about, and I’m not going to get into them. What I can do is stand by his character and his service.

question: Have you obtained any concrete intelligence indicating that President Putin is deploying these weapons, positioning them for action, or actively contemplating their use?
interview_answer: On that first point, I’m not going to answer. I’m not prepared to share intelligence information, that’s the first thing. Second, we would take action. If he were to use such a weapon, we would respond, and what we did would depend on how it was used. Next question, Josh from Bloomberg.

question: Is the United States prepared to toughen existing sanctions and potentially step up pressure on North Korea to make progress?
interview_answer: I'm not going to address that directly. What I can say is this: we already have very powerful sanctions in place. I'm not going to get into whether we’d ramp them up. They’re tough. And there are a lot of really good, ordinary people living in North Korea too. That matters to me. And let me add: my overall outlook has changed a great deal because, as you know, I’ve gotten to know Chairman Kim very well. They have their own perspective on things as well. So I really don’t want to go into that topic. I’ll just say that, hopefully, for the sake of , for the sake of Japan, and honestly for the sake of —I was just speaking with President Xi, who is someone many people respect—I told him, you can’t be thrilled having a nuclear-armed neighbor right on your border. And he isn’t. He truly is not. I can tell you he also wants that issue resolved. And I’ll leave it there. Now, ladies and gentlemen, I’ve got a plane to catch back to a great place called Washington, DC. So thank you all very much. Thank you. Thank you, everyone. Thanks a lot.

question: Can the United States call off its attempt to have Meng Wanzhou extradited?
interview_answer: Under which circumstances? Based on what, exactly?

question: Setting everything else aside, do you believe destroying those tapes was a justified and prudent action?
interview_answer: I think it’s already been made quite plain that my earliest memory of this matter is when Mike Hayden briefed me, and I’ll stick with that. Beyond that, I trust that the initial review by the Attorney General and the CIA’s Inspector General, together with congressional oversight, will ultimately clarify exactly what took place. Because of that, as these reviews and hearings move forward, I’m going to hold off on making any judgment until I have the complete picture. I recognize this is a question I’ll be facing repeatedly in the weeks ahead, so let me be upfront: until those investigations conclude and the oversight process is finished, I will not be offering any opinion on this subject from this podium.

question: Why has Vice President Pence not adhered to the CDC’s self-quarantine recommendations?
interview_answer: On that point, I’ll have to leave it to the Vice President to explain his own actions. However he chooses to handle this, he’ll provide you with those details himself. I’ll see that an official statement is released. John, your question, please.

question: Does the election of two Muslim women—one of whom wears a veil—to the House, a historic milestone, contradict this message?
interview_answer: I have no idea what you’re asking me. Excuse me?

question: Do you think Nancy Pelosi looks anything like Bob Bullock?
interview_answer: That’s more of an in-group joke, and I’m just not going to weigh in on it. What I will say is that I’m fundamentally an optimist. I expected we’d lose some seats; I just had no idea what the final number would be.

question: What led you to avoid merging agencies and instead expand their number? Aren’t we already dealing with an excess of agencies, which is part of the issue?
interview_answer:
You’ve put your finger on something important, but I’m not going to preempt the formal rollout I’m making tomorrow. So I’ll stick to general principles right now, and then tomorrow you’ll be able to press the administration on the specific structural choices we’ve laid out.

In broad terms, what happened is that inadequate supervision and multiple holes in the regulatory framework allowed financial firms—not only traditional banks but a range of non‑bank actors—to take on reckless levels of risk. That didn’t just endanger those firms; it put the entire U.S. economy at risk and helped trigger a severe global downturn. We need a modernized regulatory architecture—one that hasn’t really been overhauled since the 1930s—to cope with huge international capital flows and complex new financial products and risk‑taking that have proven extremely harmful to ordinary Americans.

We’re going to unveil a robust package of regulatory reforms that, in our view, will greatly reduce the chances of this kind of crisis happening again. We’re counting on Congress to move quickly so these measures can be enacted, implemented, and fully operational.

Once you see the full framework, I think it’ll be clear that we’re not simply piling on a long list of new regulators.
