There will, in fact, be efforts to streamline, to bring functions together, and to introduce deliberate overlap where necessary so that no area falls into a blind spot—whether that’s consumer protection, investor safeguards, or the oversight of systemic risk. Taken as a whole, it will be a more coherent and integrated regime than what we had before.

That said, this is going to be, as usual, a tough battle. There are folks who want to continue making risky bets while relying on taxpayers to rescue them if those bets collapse. You’re going to hear the familiar complaints that we don’t need more oversight and that government should just get out of the way. People’s memories can be short, and some lobbyists and special interests are banking on the public forgetting the damage caused by their irresponsible practices. I intend to keep calling that out so we put protections in place that stop this kind of disaster from recurring. But as for the precise institutional details you’re asking about, I’m not going to get ahead of tomorrow’s announcement.

question: Would you say the upcoming meeting is likely to happen soon, or could it be a while?
interview_answer: Look, I’m not able to say. It could happen in the near future, or it could be quite a long time from now. I can’t give you an answer. I’d like it to be soon, but it might also be a long way off.

question: Your view on whether this could support or undermine a two-state outcome.
interview_answer:
All right. To begin with, I’d like to commend the Senate for taking steps toward granting me the authority not only to negotiate a smart, forward-looking, pro‑growth trade agreement with a range of Asia‑Pacific nations—and potentially some in Europe as well—but also to equip me with the enforcement mechanisms necessary to make sure those agreements actually have teeth, which has not always been the case in the past. So I want to express my appreciation to all the Senators who voted to move this authority ahead, or at least to open the floor to debating and advancing the process. For those who opposed it, I’ll keep working to lay out the facts and give them the assurances they need so they can be confident that, although there have been very real shortcomings in previous trade pacts, the strategy we’re pursuing here is, in my judgment, the right one—not only for large American corporations, but also for small and mid‑sized firms and, most critically, for American workers. I would never champion any agreement if I didn’t believe that, in the end, it would help generate jobs in the United States and expand the ladders of opportunity, higher incomes, and better wages for Americans. That has been my central priority since the day I took office.

As for the supposed rift between me and Elizabeth, it has never been personal. I realize it’s entertaining for the press to probe that, because when you see two close allies split on a policy matter, it can seem like there’s drama to uncover. But many of my closest friends in both the Senate and the House, some of my earliest backers, disagree with me on this issue. I get where they’re coming from. Like me, they’ve witnessed factories shutting down and jobs moving overseas. Like me, they worry about whether labor and environmental commitments abroad are actually honored. Like me, they’re concerned about whether trade ends up being fair, and not just free.

And, like me, they’re deeply troubled by global trends, and developments here at home, involving widening inequality and what appears to be the combined effect of automation and globalization: people at the very top doing extraordinarily well while wages and incomes remain flat for middle‑class families and for those striving to get into the middle class.

So these are people whose values line up entirely with mine. I saw that some of these friends issued a progressive manifesto laying out what it means to be progressive, and when I read it, I noticed it essentially mirrored my agenda—except when it came to trade. That’s the one major point of divergence. And that’s really where this rests: a policy disagreement and a different assessment of what best serves our constituents and the American people.

I firmly believe that, even acknowledging the flaws of past trade agreements, we are in a stronger position if we help set robust, high‑standard, enforceable rules on issues like child labor, illegal logging, environmental damage, wildlife trafficking, and intellectual property for what will be the world’s largest and fastest‑growing markets. If we don’t define those rules, China and others will, and then our companies will be at a disadvantage and American workers will pay the price.

On the concern about shipping jobs overseas, my view—supported by the data—is that any U.S. company focused solely on chasing the lowest wages already has ample ways to relocate production under the status quo. What we have a chance to do now is bring more manufacturing back to America.

We’re already seeing some signs of that. That’s why I went out to Nike. I know Nike has long produced shoes in low‑wage parts of the Asia‑Pacific region, which hurt American footwear jobs over the last three decades. But now, with new technologies, Nike is talking about creating 10,000 jobs here at home because we’ve moved up the value chain and changed how we manufacture. That’s an opening for us—but to fully benefit from it, we still need to be able to sell to those markets overseas.

That’s why I’ve been telling my progressive friends that, if we truly want to achieve our shared goals, the real focus should be on the domestic measures we all agree on: a strong minimum wage; robust job‑training and skills programs; major infrastructure projects that put people to work; tougher protections for collective bargaining and workers’ voices; vigorous enforcement of rules on things like overtime; guaranteeing paid sick leave; and having an honest conversation about our budget so we’re not hacking away at investments in our future just to keep tax loopholes in place for corporations that don’t produce real economic value.

Those are the tools that will help us address the very challenges they’re raising. Blocking this trade agreement won’t address those concerns—especially when they themselves are the first to say that today’s trade rules are a raw deal for American workers. If the current regime is failing us, how does freezing it in place help working Americans? It doesn’t add up.

I support tough enforcement and the compliance provisions that have been negotiated. I have raised specific worries about how the current currency language in the bill is written. I still need to talk further with Senator Schumer, Senator Brown, and others about crafting language that doesn’t boomerang back and constrain our own ability to manage U.S. monetary policy.

I honestly can’t recall what your other question was, so I’m not going to try to answer it.

question: Can President Lee clarify where things currently stand in the talks on lengthening South Korea’s missile range?
interview_answer:
Thank you. You referred to the North’s upcoming launch of a long-range missile, and let me again emphasize, ladies and gentlemen: if North Korea proceeds with its long-range missile test, that will constitute a blatant breach of U.N. Security Council Resolution 1874. Should Pyongyang carry out this launch, it will be directly reneging on the commitments it has made to the international community.

The international community is calling on the North Korean authorities to withdraw their declaration that they will fire this long-range missile. Now, your question was how we would respond. I want to address that by stating that, if North Korea goes ahead, it and it alone will have to shoulder full responsibility. As I have just said, if it follows through on this plan, it will only increase its own isolation, and Pyongyang will have to bear all of the resulting consequences.

From the viewpoint of the North Korean people, they will see their leadership spending hundreds of millions of dollars merely to launch a long-range missile. Watching their leaders do this, they will not feel genuine pride that their country has fired a long-range missile; instead, they will fully grasp the true character of their rulers and understand more clearly why they must endure such severe hardship today.

So, domestically, I’m sure the North Korean leadership expects to achieve some internal objective by conducting this missile test. But they must recognize that, if they carry out this plan, they will be placing themselves in an extremely difficult situation. I call on North Korea to act as a responsible member of the international community; that is the surest path to improving the lives of the North Korean people.

On this point, there is no divergence between the United States and South Korea. We will remain calm and level-headed, and we will act prudently in dealing with North Korea if it indeed follows through on its announcement.

You also asked about extending South Korea’s missile range. We did not address that matter in our discussions. Naturally, this topic is being handled by working-level officials. But it needs to be viewed within a broader context, since any extension of our missile range is only one element of strengthening and upgrading our overall defense posture vis-à-vis North Korea. Ben Feller, Associated Press.

question: If the administration intends to restore the Houthi rebels or militias to the terrorism designation list.
interview_answer: What I can say is that this is still being reviewed. Bringing this conflict to a close requires both sides to engage, and achieving that will be extremely challenging. That’s all I’m going to say on it.

question: Ask whether the Attorney General and the Deputy Attorney General can count on keeping their positions long term.
interview_answer: I’m not going to get into that right now; that’s something I’d prefer to address at another point. We’re reviewing a number of things, including Cabinet roles. Overall, I’m very pleased with the bulk of my Cabinet. We’re considering various people for various posts—it’s something that routinely happens after the midterms. I chose not to make any changes before the midterms. But I can say that, generally, I’m very satisfied with my Cabinet. Mike Pompeo, for example, has really settled in remarkably well and done a fantastic job——

question: When is the timeline for changing vaccine availability for younger kids?
interview_answer: Right now, researchers don’t yet have enough evidence to be fully satisfied that it’s safe, and that’s exactly what they’re working on. I was asked a very similar question a few months back about kids, say, 7 to 12 years old. Only after they had run enough trials and seen enough data did they feel confident moving ahead for that age group. The same process will happen here; the opportunity will come. But I’m not a scientist, so I honestly can’t give you a date or a specific timeframe. What I can say is that getting to that next stage is extremely important.

question: How do you personally assess North Korea’s leader, Kim Jong Un?
interview_answer:
On the matter of North Korea, our priority—shared by South Korea, the United States, and the broader international community—must be to achieve a complete end to North Korea’s nuclear weapons program and to encourage it to act as a responsible state on the world stage. That outcome would enhance stability on the Korean Peninsula and globally, and it would ultimately benefit North Korea’s own development as well. That is the perspective from which I approach this issue.

To move North Korea toward that course and help shift its strategic thinking, we have to act in close coordination. In this context, China’s leverage and influence are significant, so China’s active participation in these efforts is essential. We have common understandings on that point.

Regarding the positions of China and Russia, my view is that both of them—along with the wider international community—recognize the necessity of a denuclearized Korean Peninsula and are working together to press North Korea to choose a more constructive direction. China, for example, has taken an engaged role in the passage of U.N. Security Council sanctions related to North Korea’s missile launches and nuclear tests and has been diligent in carrying out those resolutions.

Russia likewise supports the goal of denuclearization on the Korean Peninsula. It has backed the adoption of U.N. Security Council resolutions on North Korea and has pushed to ensure that the G‑8 foreign ministers’ joint statement included a clear, firm message directed at Pyongyang. These constructive contributions from China and Russia are crucial to delivering a unified signal that North Korea’s nuclear ambitions will not be accepted, and to urging its leadership to make a different choice.

Now, as for Kim Jong Un himself, I do not know him on a personal level. I have never spoken with him, so I am not in a position to offer a judgment about his individual traits or personality, and I am not going to do so. What we can evaluate are the steps his regime has taken, which have been confrontational and appear to lead North Korea down a blind alley.

I also want to stress that we will sustain a robust deterrent and that we are not going to provide benefits in response to provocations. At the same time, we continue to leave open a peaceful route for North Korea: to give up its nuclear weapons, honor its international obligations, return to engagement with the rest of the world, and move gradually toward both greater security and prosperity for its people.

If the course North Korea has followed so far has failed to deliver strength and prosperity, this is an opportune moment for Kim Jong Un to review that record and choose another way. If he does, that decision would be welcomed not only by us, but by the international community at large.

China, Russia, Japan, and the other key stakeholders in the Six‑Party framework have made that expectation clear. But such a shift will require concrete changes in behavior. As we say in English, “Don’t focus on what I say; pay attention to what I do.” Up to now, we have not seen actions from North Korea that show it is ready to head in a new direction. So I cannot and will not offer a personal character assessment of Kim Jong Un beyond noting the consequences of his government’s choices.

question: If marriage should be treated as a basic civil right
interview_answer:
That was a bit of a grab bag there. [] Chuck, we ought to get you a seat on the Supreme Court. [] I’m not a Justice, and I’m not going to pretend to be your constitutional law professor this afternoon. I’m going to leave that to the courts and skip the doctrinal debate. Instead, let me zero in on the core issue at hand, because there’s been a lot of chatter about procedure and consultation with Congress and all the rest. I’d rather walk through, concretely, what we’ve actually done.

Muammar Qadhafi—who, before Usama bin Laden, was responsible for more American deaths than almost anyone else—was threatening mass slaughter of his own citizens. Working with a broad international coalition, under a U.N. authorization that’s nearly without precedent, we moved in and knocked out air defenses so that our partners could impose a no‑fly zone and provide real humanitarian protection for people on the ground.

I went before the American public and laid out exactly what our role would be. I said there would be no ground troops. I said we would not bear the bulk of the burden; that as NATO allies we would support the mission because it served our national security and because it was simply the right thing to do.

And we have done precisely that. We have not put American boots on the ground. Our allies—who we often criticize for not pulling their weight in NATO—have shouldered a major share of this operation. As a result, thousands of civilians in have been shielded from violence; we haven’t lost a single American service member; and there is no risk of this spiraling into a larger conflict. The mission is tightly limited in both duration and scope.

So I told Americans we had a narrow objective, and we’ve carried out that narrow objective effectively. All along, we’ve been in touch with Congress. There have been about ten hearings. We’ve provided volumes of material on what we’re doing. I’ve brought Members down here to discuss it face‑to‑face. Frankly, a lot of the outrage you’re hearing now is political.

If you look at the substance, we have done exactly what we said we would do, under U.N. authority, and in doing so we have saved thousands of lives. And now a man who long backed terrorist attacks against the United States is cornered, and the pressure on him is steadily increasing.

Remember, the War Powers Resolution grew out of Vietnam—half a million troops deployed, tens of thousands of Americans killed, hundreds of billions of dollars spent. Congress said, in effect, ‘We don’t want that kind of open‑ended commitment again, so if you’re going to take us down that road, you need to come to us first.’ I think that principle of congressional consultation is absolutely right.

But do I believe what we’ve done here violates the War Powers Resolution in any way? No, I don’t. So I’m not going to wade into the broader constitutional question you’re trying to get me to answer.

question: Is he definitively denying that she might have been dismissed from her job?
interview_answer:
Look, Jackie, I honestly can’t give you that. I’m being straight with you here — I just heard about this earlier today, and I don’t have the details yet, and Secretary Salazar has been tied up testifying on the Hill.

On your first point about MMS, Secretary Salazar was already in the middle of trying to overhaul that agency’s practices. But the larger point I’m making is that those changes clearly weren’t happening quickly enough. If they’d moved faster, maybe this problem would have been caught. Then again, maybe it still would have slipped through. You could imagine us doing a brand‑new environmental review, bringing in a team of technical experts to scrutinize BP’s proposal, and they might still have concluded that it met industry standards, that we hadn’t seen an incident like this in a decade and a half, and that it was acceptable to proceed.

That’s exactly what this Commission has to get to the bottom of: Was there a fundamental system failure? Is this a one‑in‑a‑million event, or something that could happen once every thousand or every five thousand times? What are the real risks we’re dealing with?

Let me step back and talk more broadly about energy. The very fact that oil companies now have to operate a mile below the surface and then drill another three miles down to reach oil tells us a lot about where this industry is headed. Getting that oil is more costly, and it’s inherently more dangerous.

That’s why you’ve never heard me chanting “Drill, baby, drill,” because we cannot simply drill our way out of our energy challenges. Offshore drilling may play a role as a temporary bridge while we move toward new technologies and new energy sources, but we need to be realistic: the easy‑to‑reach oil has already been largely pumped out.

As we push ahead, the technology becomes more complex, the reserves are in more remote locations, and that inevitably raises the level of risk. As a country, we will have to make some very tough choices about how much risk we’re prepared to tolerate. That’s one of the core issues the Commission is going to have to examine.

At the same time, we have to recognize that, for the foreseeable future, our economy and our way of life are still going to rely on oil, even as we start shifting away from it. That’s why, when I met with the Republican caucus this week, I urged them to partner on this. Senators Lieberman and Kerry had been working with Senator Graham, and even though Senator Graham isn’t currently on the bill, they’ve developed a framework that could draw support from both parties. It acknowledges that we’re still going to need oil production, but it also recognizes what’s coming down the road — and that we’re going to have a serious problem if we don’t begin changing how we operate.

Macarena Vidal, EFE News. She’s not here? Oh, there you are.

question: Inquiry regarding the intelligence dossier on Al Qaida activities.
interview_answer: Come again?

question: Did you dispatch your Chief of Staff at the time, along with the White House Counsel, to John Ashcroft’s hospital room while he was ill to secure his approval for that program?
interview_answer: Kelly, there’s been a great deal of conjecture about what did or did not occur, and I’m not going to address that. This is an extremely sensitive program. What I can say is, first, the program is vital for safeguarding the American people, and it remains vital because there are still adversaries intent on harming us. As president, I have a responsibility to establish measures that respect Americans’ civil liberties—measures which, in this instance, were regularly reviewed and reported to the United States Congress. This program, as I’ve said, is a critical tool in defending our nation. People will continue to debate and discuss it. But, as I’ve made clear, I am not going to advance that debate by commenting on such a highly sensitive, highly classified matter. What I can reiterate is that the program is essential.

question: To be precise, have you observed any tangible results from your diplomatic approach up to this point?
interview_answer: Look, I think by now you know that I’m not someone who likes to sit here and label something a success or a failure in a situation like this. We prefer to keep our intentions and our evaluations a bit under wraps. What I can say is this: I’m convinced that pursuing this course is the right thing to do—not just for our country, but for people everywhere. There are very strong reasons behind it, extremely strong reasons. Now, with that in mind, yes, I think we are moving forward in a significant way. We’re demonstrating tremendous power. They can see that our capabilities are unmatched. There has never been power like what we’re showing. We deployed three of the world’s largest aircraft carriers, and they’re in position right now. We have a nuclear submarine in place as well. There are a lot of assets in motion that we sincerely hope—and I’ll even say we pray—we never actually need to use. Given all of this, I truly think it’s in their interest to come to the negotiating table and strike an agreement that benefits their people and the world. I do notice some shifts, yes. But I’m not going to get into calling that ‘success’—we’ll have to wait and see how it turns out.

question: Could you clarify whether making federal contractors follow rules on advancing gay rights or on contraception coverage would be the first step you’d focus on?
interview_answer: Margaret, I’m not going to roll out specific decisions piecemeal right now. Once we’ve completed a comprehensive review and are clear on what powers we have, I’ll share that information with you. Chris Jansing, NBC News.

question: Second, regarding Operation Fast and Furious: lawmakers in Congress and officials in Mexico say they still lack clear explanations. Do you intend to change the leadership at ATF, and when should we expect the findings of the current investigation?
interview_answer:
On your second point, as you’re aware, my Attorney General has already stated plainly that he never would have authorized allowing guns to be trafficked into Mexico. That inquiry is still ongoing. I’m not going to discuss an active investigation. I’ve already said very clearly that such an approach by the ATF would be inappropriate, and we need to determine how it came about. Once the investigation concludes, I’m confident the necessary steps will be taken.

On E‑Verify, what we really need is comprehensive immigration reform. I’ve said this before, I’ll repeat it now, I’ll repeat it next week, and I’ll be saying it 6 months from now. We must put in place a system that both preserves our identity as a nation governed by laws and honors our heritage as a nation of immigrants. That requires strong border enforcement, serious penalties for employers who illegally hire and exploit workers, and also a process for people currently living in the shadows to obtain legal status.

We may not get every element I’d ideally want in a single bill, but the package has to be balanced. E‑Verify can serve as a useful enforcement mechanism if it’s accurate and if American citizens are protected, because we cannot force businesses to rely on a system they can’t trust. And we don’t want employers exposed to legal risk because they turn away a qualified applicant the database wrongly labels as undocumented when that person is, in fact, here legally. That would be unfair to the worker and would likely create problems for the employer too.

So our objective right now should be to keep improving the E‑Verify program, build in safeguards to avoid the kinds of errors I just described, and at the same time remember that verification is only one piece of immigration reform. Take, for example, DREAMers—young people raised in the United States, who see themselves as Americans, who lack legal status through no fault of their own, and who are eager to contribute, attend college, serve in our Armed Forces, and start businesses here. We need to ensure they can remain in this country. We need a broader, more balanced approach than relying solely on a verification system. All right?

question: Now that Mr. Trump has been elected, wouldn't basic political courtesy require you to go ahead and announce that you’ll be running for office again?
interview_answer:
I generally make it a point not to insert myself into the domestic politics of other countries. What I can say is that Chancellor Merkel has been an exceptional counterpart. Among our closest partners, she is perhaps the only head of government who was already in office when I first took mine, so in many respects we are the old hands who have weathered numerous crises together over the last eight years. And while we haven’t agreed on every single question, when it comes to fundamental principles—her honesty, her reliability, her careful, fact‑based way of working, and her commitment to serving the German people while also understanding that real leadership includes engaging the wider world and working effectively through multilateral institutions—I think she has been remarkable.

Whether she chooses to run again is ultimately her decision, and in the end it will be for the German electorate to decide what comes next. If I were living here as a German citizen and had a ballot, I might well cast it for her. Whether that endorsement helps her or not, I really can’t say.

On Russia, my basic stance has not changed since I came into office. Russia is a major power, with significant military capabilities and considerable influence both in its neighborhood and globally. For that reason, it’s in our interest to engage Russia and seek its cooperation if we want to resolve some of the world’s most difficult problems.

We should all want a Russia that succeeds—a Russia where people are working, the economy is expanding, relations with neighbors are constructive, and the country is playing a positive role on global challenges like climate change. That’s why I’ve worked to build a constructive relationship with Moscow. At the same time, I’ve tried to be clear‑eyed about the fact that Russia often sees the world differently than we and many of our allies do.

The values I’ve spoken about—democracy, free expression, adherence to international rules, the rule of law, and respect for other nations’ right to chart their own course and safeguard their sovereignty and borders—are not things we can just put aside. So on matters such as Ukraine and Syria, we’ve had very serious disagreements. My hope is that the President‑elect will pursue a similarly constructive course: finding areas of cooperation where our interests and values align, but also being prepared to push back when Russia strays from those values and from accepted international norms.

I do not anticipate that the incoming administration will duplicate our policies point for point. What I hope, though, is that it avoids a narrowly transactional “deal‑making” approach that accepts arrangements with Russia even if they harm others, undermine international rules, leave smaller countries exposed, or create enduring instability in places like Syria simply because such deals might seem convenient in the short term. We will see more clearly how this develops as the President‑elect assembles and empowers his team.

I am, however, encouraged by his clear statement that NATO remains a firm and unshakable commitment. His expressed full support for NATO as the bedrock of our collective security is, in my view, extremely significant.

As for my own discussions with President Putin, those took place before the U.S. election. As I’ve said already, there is ample evidence that Russian actors have carried out cyberattacks. This phenomenon is not brand‑new, and it’s not confined to Russia; a number of countries have engaged in low‑level intrusions, industrial espionage, and other activities in cyberspace that we consider unacceptable. I made it very clear that, while we understand that all intelligence services collect information—and we may object but still recognize that reality—there is a difference between espionage and attempts to manipulate elections or target private organizations and commercial entities. I also made clear that we are watching these activities closely and will react in an appropriate way if and when we see them occurring.

More broadly, I think cyberspace is an area where we urgently need international rules and understandings so that we don’t drift into a cyber arms race. Many states now possess sophisticated cyber capabilities, and because our economies and infrastructures are so dependent on digital systems, we must work to ensure that the cyber domain does not become a kind of lawless, constant battlefield.

We’ve started laying down some basic principles at the G‑20, G‑7, and U.N. levels, but there is still a great deal of work ahead of us in that regard.

Allow me also to emphasize how impressed I am that, despite a very contentious campaign, this period of transition in the United States is proceeding in an orderly way, in keeping with democratic norms. This process concerns the American people and their future, and the outgoing administration is responsibly sharing its experience and expertise with the incoming team. For us, that is an encouraging signal that the strong cooperation between the United States and the Federal Republic of Germany—which serves both of our countries—will continue.

I intend to carry that cooperation forward in a spirit of openness and deep conviction in my dealings with President‑elect Donald Trump.

On the matter of Russia, I can only reiterate what the President has said. From a European—and specifically a German—perspective, this is fundamentally about safeguarding key principles. The fact that Europe has enjoyed more than seventy years of peace is closely connected to the protection of every European state’s territorial integrity and sovereignty. Given our history, abandoning those principles would put us on a very dangerous path, and we must firmly resist any such tendencies from the outset.

Our focus remains on political solutions. That is why we initiated the Normandy format, in close cooperation with the United States. And from where we stand in Europe, Russia is, after all, our neighbor—just look at Poland and others in the region. We therefore have a strong interest in a stable and constructive relationship with Russia. We share many historical links, some of them difficult, but that cannot prevent us from speaking plainly where there are sharp disagreements—always by political means and always with the goal of finding negotiated outcomes.

That is what I will continue to devote myself to wholeheartedly. As for your specific question about whether I will be a candidate again, I will address that when the time is right, and that time is not today.

question: Do you still plan to move forward with that birthright citizenship Executive Order?
interview_answer: I’m not going to address that right now—bring it up again later. Next question.

question: Is President Putin going to order an attack on Ukraine before this winter is over?
interview_answer:
I’ve spoken many times with Russian officials, and with President Putin personally. I honestly don’t believe he’s even settled on a final decision yet—I’m not sure he himself knows what he’ll ultimately choose to do. What I am certain of is that launching an assault on Ukraine would be an enormous error on his part. It would have dire consequences for Europe and far beyond, and he would face very serious repercussions as a result.

I’ve been extremely direct with President Putin, both in our calls and in face‑to‑face meetings. If he goes ahead, we will enact the toughest economic measures ever imposed—unprecedented sanctions—with significant costs over time. Those consequences won’t be limited to Russia; they will touch us and our European allies as well. But they would strike his economy in a very deep way.

That said, I cannot tell you what he is going to do. He is now militarily capable of invading, particularly if the ground around Kyiv remains frozen. What choice he makes, I do not know—and I don’t think anyone other than him does.

question: Advance pardons for members of your administration
interview_answer: I am not going to talk about pardons during this briefing.

question: Is this title going to make your list?
interview_answer: I don't make my complete movie list public. But let’s focus on what we’ve actually learned here. The FBI announced today, and we can now verify, that North Korea carried out this attack. It says something revealing about that regime that it chose to unleash a full-scale, state-backed operation against a film studio over a comedy featuring Seth Rogen and James Flacco [Franco]—over a comedy featuring Seth Rogen and James Flacco [Franco]—over a comedy featuring Seth Rogen and James Flacco [Franco]. They inflicted serious damage, and we will answer that. Our response will be proportional, and it will come at a time, in a manner, and at a place of our choosing. That’s not the sort of thing I’m going to spell out here in a press conference. More broadly, this underscores the urgency of working with the international community to establish clear norms and rules for behavior in cyberspace. At the moment, it’s essentially a Wild West environment. You have vulnerable states able to launch these kinds of operations, and nonstate actors capable of tremendous harm. That’s why cybersecurity is such a pressing challenge. It’s also why Congress needs to partner with us to get a real bill passed that enables the level of information sharing that’s required. If we fail to build the defensive architecture that can stop these intrusions before they happen, the consequences won’t be limited to the entertainment industry; they will hit our entire economy in extremely serious ways. And, by the way, I’ve heard you’re heading to Europe. Where are you planning to live over there?

question: How do you respond to commentators like Novak who claim you’re now even more politically alone than Richard Nixon was during Watergate?
interview_answer: What exactly does he mean by ‘alone’?

question: How does the President view this movement, and what is his message to its supporters?
interview_answer: Look, I really don’t know a lot about this movement, other than I’m told they’re very fond of me, which I’m thankful for, but beyond that, I don’t know much about them. I’ve heard they’re becoming more popular. And from what I’m told, these are people who, when they see what’s happening on the streets of Portland, when they look at what went on in New York City over the last 6 or 7 months—really, this all started about 4 years ago when I got here, almost 4 years, hard to believe—they’re people who don’t like what they’re seeing in places like Portland, Chicago, New York, and other cities and States. I’ve heard they’re people who love our country, and they just don’t like watching this happen. So I honestly don’t really know anything about them except that they supposedly support me. And they want the problems in these places—especially the ones we’re talking about—to be fixed. Because there’s no excuse for Democrats being unable to run a city. And if they can’t, we’ll send in whatever Federal help is needed—troops, law enforcement, whatever they want—we’ll go in and solve the problem in 24 hours or less. All right?

question: Will you address what I just asked?
interview_answer: Listen, I’m not going to answer you. I’m currently speaking with this man. Could you sit down now?

question: Your view on Michael Gove?
interview_answer: Well, I know Boris, and I’m fond of him. I’ve liked him for many years, and I think he’d perform excellently. I also know Jeremy, and I believe he’d do an excellent job too. Michael, I don’t know. So would Jeremy do well—what do you think? You tell me. All right? All right. Thanks very much, everyone. Thanks. Really, thank you all.

question: President Zelenskyy’s statements about the major counteroffensive and whether it has actually begun yet.
interview_answer: Sure, I’ll lay out the exact moment they’ll launch and the precise locations they’ll hit. That’s what he told me—[]—oh, please. Bless your heart. Even if I had that information down to the last detail, do you seriously think I’d reveal their plans for this offensive to you?

question: Have you received any phone calls about that?
interview_answer:
I have, but I'm not going to go into that. What I will say is that we've held many talks and built very strong channels at Mike’s level and at other levels as well. In fact, as you know, we even have a couple of representatives here from North Korea today—some are seated here, and a few others are in the back of the room. So when we reached this final agreement, the key point is we weren’t starting from scratch. We went in with a very solid rapport and a great deal of insight, and that, in my view, is why we were able to reach this outcome.

Now I’m going to wrap up. I don’t know about all of you, but it’s been a long stretch without much rest. So we’ll take a brief breather, and then the hard work starts up again. I’m grateful to all of you for coming. I trust we’ve addressed your concerns. Thank you very much. And, in a way, congratulations to everyone, because this is, to me, a very significant moment in global affairs. And to be honest with myself, I have to add that I want to see it fully carried through. So Mike and our entire team need to buckle down and finish the job. Otherwise we’ve done well, but if you don’t push the ball over the goal line, it doesn’t count for enough. All right? So thank you, and, again, congratulations to everyone here. Thank you very much. I appreciate it. Thank you.

question: Worries about what President‑elect Obama might do in office
interview_answer:
Look, Michael, I'm not going to sit here and guess about what choices he's going to make. Once he's in the Oval Office, he'll review each issue, weigh the circumstances, and then decide what he believes has to be done.

And I'll tell you something else: when I walk out of here, I'm stepping off the public stage. I think there should really be only one person under the bright lights at a time, and I've already had my turn up there. Sure, I'm sure you'll hear me sounding off now and then, but I genuinely want him to succeed.

Some people say, "Oh, you just say that, it's just a line." It really isn't. The stakes are serious. We still face enemies. Folks might try to spin that as me laying some kind of groundwork, but I'm telling you plainly: there are people out there who would like to strike America and Americans again. That's just how the world is. And so I sincerely hope he does very well.

He’s also going to be dealing with a very tough economy. I get that. A lot of working people are under strain and worried about their financial future. One of the hardest calls I had to make in the financial crisis was using taxpayers’ money to head off a collapse, even though some of that support went to Wall Street outfits that helped create the mess. I wasn’t joking when I said Wall Street got drunk and we ended up with the hangover.

But even so, President‑elect Obama will discover that the way problems present themselves sometimes forces leaders into choices they weren’t initially comfortable with, and that’s what happened with Wall Street. When I went back to Midland, plenty of folks said, "What on earth are you up to, son? Those people back East caused this." And I told them, I know that, but if we hadn’t moved to fix it, you’d be worse off yourselves.

In any case, I truly do wish him nothing but success. Sheryl [Sheryl Gay Stolberg, New York Times].

question: President Obama, can you state plainly whether the blind Chinese activist Chen Guangcheng is currently under U.S. protection in Beijing? And how do you expect this case to be settled? Would the United States offer him asylum if he were to request it?
interview_answer:
I have, of course, seen the media coverage about the situation in China, but I am not going to offer any comment on that specific matter. What I do want to underscore is that whenever we sit down with the Chinese leadership, we raise human rights. We believe that doing so is not only consistent with our core values—our commitment to freedom and human dignity—but that, in the long run, China itself will be more stable and successful as it opens up and reforms its own system. 

We want a strong, thriving China, and we welcome the many areas in which we’ve been able to cooperate. At the same time, we’re convinced that this partnership will be deeper, more resilient, and more prosperous as we see ongoing improvements in China’s human rights record.

I realize this question wasn’t specifically aimed at me, but let me briefly address North Korea as well. That was a major topic in my talks with our allies. Our close coordination during and after North Korea’s failed missile launch shows how vital our alliance is, not only for our two nations but for the broader region. Since taking office, I’ve tried to make it clear to Pyongyang that the old cycle—provocations designed to grab attention, followed by the international community essentially buying better behavior—has to end.

We have said, and will continue to say, that the more North Korea engages in provocative actions, the more isolated it will become; sanctions will be tightened, and its diplomatic, political, and economic isolation will only grow. While we can’t predict—and I won’t speculate about—what they may do in the months ahead, I think it’s evident to Pyongyang that the United States, Japan, South Korea, and others in the region are united in insisting that it meet its obligations, respect international norms, and understand that further provocations will not yield any rewards.

Regarding North Korea’s recent activity, President Obama and I earlier reaffirmed that what they call a satellite launch—in reality, a missile test—undermines the efforts of all concerned parties to resolve issues through dialogue. With this missile launch, there is now a serious risk they may proceed to another nuclear test. I believe the international community, speaking with one voice, must urge the DPRK to show restraint. More concretely, the steps laid out in the latest UN Security Council presidential statement have to be fully implemented. Among Japan, the United States, South Korea, as well as China and Russia, we need robust communication and a shared message that China’s role remains crucial, even as we coordinate closely with the United States. That is the understanding we reached. And now I will invite Mr. Imaichi from TBS in Japan to pose the next question.

question: Conditions for private, one-on-one talks.
interview_answer: I’m not going to get into the specifics of how those talks are conducted. What I can say is that our aim is to reach a resolution, and we won’t let formal diplomatic customs or procedures box us in. If Iran genuinely wants a solution, they’ll be able to help bring one about.

question: If Congress fails to approve the USMCA, does that effectively end NAFTA?
interview_answer:
It would really be unfortunate. Look, I’m not going to respond directly to that question, but you already know my views on NAFTA. In my opinion, NAFTA is the worst trade agreement ever signed, and I also think the World Trade Organization hasn’t exactly been a great success either. Not one of the great ones. That deal basically launched China, and from the moment they joined, their economy shot up like a rocket. It was—it was a disaster.

But anyway, we’ll see what happens. That’s going to be a very interesting thing to watch, especially with Nancy and Chuck and the rest of them obsessed with this witch hunt because they know they can’t defeat us at the ballot box. They can’t beat us at the ballot. And they’re not going to win the Presidency. Our polling is terrific. Our internal numbers in places like Ohio and Iowa are strong. Pennsylvania looks good. North Carolina, too.

We just won two races that a lot of people thought we were going to lose. In one of them, we were down 17 points three weeks before Election Day, and the candidate ended up winning by a big margin—a very big margin. That was Dan Bishop. And then we had another race, as you know, where our candidate was up by just a point or two and ended up winning by—what was it? Twenty-five points or something huge—I’ll let you reporters say the exact number so I’m not accused of exaggerating and getting a big front-page headline out of it.

But he won by a lot. He was ahead by maybe two or three, and he ended up winning by something in the twenties. So we’re doing great in North Carolina, doing great in Florida.

And you’ve had one or two Democratic Members of Congress admit, basically, “We can’t beat him in an election, so let’s just impeach him.” You heard that—Al Green, real piece of work. He said it very clearly, it was everywhere. Now they’re probably trying to bury that tape. But he essentially said: We can’t beat him, so we’ll impeach him. That’s very dangerous. 

Steve, go ahead.

question: What are Americans hoping for, and what would count as a successful result from this meeting?
interview_answer:
I’ll walk you through all of that after the meeting is finished. Let me be clear: I’ve been at this for many years, and no serious leader lays out his negotiating plan in front of the global press before sitting down with an opponent or potential opponent. I’m not going to start doing that now. 

What I can say is this: I intend to convey to President Putin that there are potential areas for constructive work together, if he opts to pursue them. If instead he continues behaving as he has in the past on cyber issues and other matters, we will answer accordingly—we will answer in a way that matches those actions. We ought to identify where cooperation serves both our countries and the broader international community, and test whether that’s possible. Where cooperation isn’t possible, we will spell out our red lines. 

I’ve met him before; he’s sharp, he’s tough, and, as we used to say in sports, he’s a formidable opponent. But I am only going to discuss the content and outcome of our talks once they are concluded—not beforehand. I’ll be glad to go into detail with you after it’s done, but not now. Nancy Cordes, CBS. The President’s Meeting With / Detention of Russian Opposition Leader Alexei Navalny.

question: What action will the President take if Iran approaches breaching the uranium enrichment limits set under the JCPOA?
interview_answer: You’ll find out.

question: What stance would Brazil take if the United States carried out a military operation in Venezuela?
interview_answer: There are certain matters which, once discussed openly, cease to function as strategic considerations. For that reason, these confidential questions—whether they have been addressed internally or not—will obviously not be disclosed in public. I recall a debate here in the United States when one candidate asked another how he would confront the Islamic State, and the reply was, “If I spell it out, I won’t be able to defeat them.” It’s the same logic of strategy. Everything we have agreed upon here will be respected, but some information, if it exists, simply cannot be part of an open public debate.

question: Do you regard Russia as a partner or a foe?
interview_answer: Is that question meant for me?

question: Do the Attorney General and Deputy Attorney General currently enjoy stable, long-term positions?
interview_answer: I’m not going to get into that right now; I’ll address it another time. We’re reviewing a number of things, including our Cabinet lineup. Overall, I’m very pleased with the bulk of my Cabinet. We’re considering different people for various roles, which is totally typical after the midterm elections. I chose not to make any moves before the midterms. But I can say that, generally speaking, I’m tremendously satisfied with my Cabinet. Mike Pompeo, for example, has blended in wonderfully and has done a fantastic job——

question: Do you support the way Israel is acting?
interview_answer: What I want to see is a lasting cease-fire. For that to happen, Hamas has to stop launching rockets into Israel—there is no way to have a durable cease-fire if those rocket attacks go on. In my view, the real decision lies with Hamas. Our assessment is that the most effective route to a lasting cease-fire is close cooperation with Egypt to block the flow of weapons into Gaza that allows Hamas to keep firing. So the states that are arming Hamas must end those transfers, and the international community must maintain strong pressure on them to cut off the weapons supply. If Hamas truly wants a stable cease-fire, it must stop rearming. Neighboring countries around Gaza also have to step up efforts to shut down smuggling routes. That’s a very hard job; there are tunnels and many ways for actors who want to undermine democracy to move weapons. Now, could you repeat the second part of your question, please?

question: Did the United States use nuclear bombs on the cities of Hiroshima and Nagasaki?
interview_answer: No, see, you actually raised three different questions here. Which one did you mean?

question: If there is no meaningful political progress in Iraq by September, will you continue the troop surge regardless of General Petraeus’s recommendations?
interview_answer: I appreciate the question, but you’re asking me to guess now about what my thinking will be in September, and I’m not going to do that. I’d prefer you wait until General Petraeus has returned and briefed me, and after that I’ll be willing to respond to questions in that area.

question: By what means and on what timetable will Asad leave power?
interview_answer:
From our perspective, it would have been far better if Asad had left 2 years ago, or last year, or 6 months ago, or even 2 months ago. My administration has been entirely consistent in its view that Asad forfeited his legitimacy the moment he turned his guns on his own citizens and began killing people who, at the outset, were demonstrating peacefully for a stronger voice in how their country is run. That violence has only intensified over time. So in terms of timing, the answer is: the earlier he goes, the better.

Now, as for the question of how, I’ve essentially addressed that already. There is no simple, secret recipe for resolving an exceptionally brutal and complex conflict like the one in Syria. If such a straightforward solution existed, the Prime Minister and I would have already put it into practice and the crisis would be over.

Instead, what we can do is maintain steady, coordinated international pressure and continue to bolster the opposition. I believe that the potential talks in Geneva — involving Russia and Syrian representatives and focused on a serious political transition that all sides can accept — may produce progress. In the meantime, we will keep assisting the opposition on the ground and responding to the humanitarian catastrophe. We will do this in close coordination with Turkey, which has deep stakes in this situation and with whom we enjoy an excellent partnership. 

Thank you. As you are aware, we will be meeting again later this evening, and that will give us a chance to delve into these matters in more detail. As I have noted, our positions are largely aligned, and in our discussions tonight we will continue to examine what we can jointly undertake and what elements might form a roadmap linked to Geneva and beyond.

The involvement of Russia and China in this framework is crucial, especially given their roles as permanent members of the U.N. Security Council. Their engagement can give the process additional momentum. Continued pressure from the international community remains a key factor, and if we look at the humanitarian assistance we have already delivered, it now exceeds $1.5 billion.

We in Turkey will also maintain our open‑door policy, because we share a 910‑kilometer border with Syria, and families are divided on both sides. So we will sustain these efforts.

All of this matters for wider regional stability. On one side, there are ongoing initiatives to normalize relations between Palestinians and Israelis; we do not need fresh crises elsewhere in the region. We previously took initiatives to bring Syria and Israel together to address their differences, and we held five rounds of talks, but those unfortunately stalled. Still, I remain hopeful that the regional peace efforts we pursue going forward will bear fruit, and that we can act with determination alongside the United States to advance peace in the region. Thank you.

We are in active consultation on all these files. The measures to be considered by the U.N. Security Council and the Geneva track are central, and we will keep reviewing them together.

Thank you. Thank you all. Thank you. Thank you, everyone.

question: Where does the President stand right now, post-midterms, regarding the Attorney General?
interview_answer: I’m not going to get into that right now; that’s something I’d prefer to address at another point. We’re reviewing a range of matters, including the Cabinet as a whole. I’m very pleased with the vast majority of my Cabinet members. We’re considering different individuals for various roles, which is entirely typical after midterm elections. I chose not to make any moves beforehand. Overall, I’m extremely satisfied with my Cabinet. Mike Pompeo, in particular, has really settled in remarkably well and done a fantastic job——

question: What unfinished areas will still be left at the close of the President’s term?
interview_answer: You crammed a lot into that, April. Look, I’m not going to get into that.

question: Why are Republicans being excluded from today’s budget talks instead of bringing them in to begin negotiations?
interview_answer: I really should’ve jotted all of that down, Jackie. I’m honestly tapped out mentally and can’t go into it.

question: Is the NHS something you’d consider, sir?
interview_answer: I can’t make out what he said. Pardon?

question: Your conversation with Barbara Lagoa
interview_answer: Sorry, I can’t make out what you’re saying with that mask on.

question: Is NATO the main organization you’d like to assume that command role?
interview_answer:
Regarding Libya, the facts on the ground are still shifting. The speed and nature of any handoff will depend on when our commanders in the field judge that this opening phase of the operation has run its course.

As I’ve already noted, our first priority has been to neutralize Libya’s air defenses so that the no‑fly zone can function properly and coalition pilots and aircraft aren’t endangered while enforcing it. A second core objective is ensuring we can effectively address the humanitarian side of this effort.

I do want to stress that we foresee this transition happening in days rather than stretching into weeks. So in the coming days, as the situation develops, you’ll get more concrete information, and the Pentagon will be providing detailed briefings to both the American public and the media.

NATO will have a role in helping to coordinate because of the alliance’s exceptional capabilities. But I am not going to spell out here who should be the “preferred” lead or precisely how that transfer of authority will look; I’m going to leave that to Admiral Mullen and the officers directly managing the mission to explain in due course.

On the question of this new partnership, I’m not going to pre‑empt the remarks I’ll be giving later today—if I give away everything now, there’s not much reason for anyone to attend. What I can say is that what excites me most about our relationship with a country like Chile is that it’s not limited to what the United States can provide; it’s equally about what Chile brings to the table.

Chile, for instance, has been pursuing innovative approaches to clean energy, and that’s why we’ve created a clean energy partnership. We’re making significant strides in renewable and alternative energy, but there may be projects underway here that we can adapt and learn from.

In education, we have a strong tradition of public schooling, and our universities are, I would argue, world‑class. But in a world that’s more connected every day, we don’t want American students to look inward only. That’s why a broad, hemisphere‑wide exchange program could be transformative.

On security, the scourge of drug trafficking is something all of us in the region recognize. Colombia offers a powerful example of how a nation can reclaim security after years of drug‑related violence. We need to ask what aspects of Colombia’s experience might be tailored to smaller Central American countries now confronting similar threats.

In that context, Chile, the United States, Colombia, and others working together to help train capable security forces in Central America to counter narcotraffickers will be far more effective than if the United States tried to act solo.

So across the issues that matter deeply both to us and to Chile, this new partnership is, above all, reciprocal. It’s not a one‑way relationship where a wealthy nation simply assists a poorer one; it’s a collaboration between a rising regional power with an influential international voice and the United States, working together to promote peace and prosperity in the hemisphere and beyond.

It’s also true that, when it comes to integration of the Americas, we are behind where we should be. A useful comparison is with Europe. In the last century, Europe twice descended into catastrophic war, costing more than 70 million lives. Yet, at a certain point, European leaders had the foresight to move beyond the old logic of the Maginot or Siegfried Lines and to embrace agreements like the Maastricht Treaty. Under the guidance of visionary figures such as Adenauer, De Gasperi, Hausmann, and Schuman, they began building what is now the European Union.

In the Americas, we haven’t gone as far. Some two decades ago, President George H. W. Bush floated the idea of a free trade area stretching from Alaska to Tierra del Fuego, which generated enthusiasm but ultimately didn’t come to fruition.

Now is the right moment to change that. Latin America has long been seen as the continent of the future, but no region can remain just a promise indefinitely. We’re ready to shoulder our responsibilities and realize our full potential. Latin America must make up for lost time and fully use its strengths.

We share much with the United States: extensive territories, relatively cohesive societies, hard‑working populations. We have not experienced the same racial conflicts as some African countries, nor the devastating wars Europe endured, nor its deep‑rooted religious strife. Latin America is therefore called to take charge of its destiny.

We’re eager to hear the President’s vision. We have many parallels—we both studied at Harvard; we’re both sports enthusiasts; he still plays basketball, and I did in my day. We agree that both First Ladies are remarkable. But the truly important common ground will be in what we forge together this afternoon.

If I may, I’d suggest that what we need is a Partnership 2.0 with the United States—a relationship of shared responsibilities, not just a symbolic or purely rhetorical alliance, because that sort of existential connection has never been enough to meet major challenges. We want a practical, cooperative partnership between Latin America and the United States based on shared values, shared principles, and a shared outlook.

Such an alliance must be broad. It should encompass democracy, freedom, the rule of law, and protection of human rights, and we should strengthen the democratic charter of the OAS. It should also promote freer trade in goods and services, at a faster pace than we’ve managed so far. Beyond that, it must focus on the pillars of 21st‑century development: high‑quality education, science, technology, innovation, and entrepreneurship. Those are the keys for Latin America to overcome poverty and underdevelopment.

We have much to learn from a country like the United States which, in just over two centuries of independence, has shown extraordinary innovative capacity and made enormous contributions to human progress.

Latin America and the United States both stand to gain from such a partnership. It must also tackle two of this century’s defining issues: energy—clean, safe, renewable sources—and water, which, if climate change continues unchecked, may become one of our scarcest resources. And it must address challenges no nation can handle alone: organized crime, terrorism, drug trafficking, climate change, and global security. These problems cannot be managed unilaterally; they demand coordinated action.

From our perspective, that implies a new international order, replacing the framework born at Bretton Woods after World War II with one that fits the realities and demands of the 21st century, an era in which the only constant is change.

The time has come to reclaim lost opportunities. The time has come to move beyond a relationship marked by alternating closeness and estrangement, by handshakes followed by distance. We should launch a new era of frank, effective, and concrete cooperation, one that truly addresses our biggest problems and opens the way to new opportunities.

The knowledge and information revolution is upon us. Latin America arrived late to the Industrial Revolution; we cannot afford to be late to this deeper revolution. It is generous to those who embrace it and unforgiving to those who don’t.

In the United States, the phrase is ‘no child left behind.’ Here in Latin America, we say: no country should be left behind.

Thank you.

question: Did you back Israel’s 1981 attack on the Iraqi nuclear facility near Baghdad?
interview_answer: Well, Dave, I honestly can’t recall what I thought back in 1980. I remember I was down in Midland, Texas, but I just don’t remember how I reacted that long ago.

question: Does that arrangement also cover Syria?
interview_answer: Elaine, with all due respect to you and to Gregory, this is hardly my first time dealing with questions like this. I see exactly what you’re aiming for here, trying to draw me into taking a position. I’m not going to weigh in on that at all, in any direction, Elaine.

question: Would you say this election represents a rejection of that message?
interview_answer: I have no idea what you're talking about. Excuse me?

question: Dispute with P.J. Crowley
interview_answer: As I said, I’ve already addressed the core point at stake.

question: Would you be willing to approve a spending bill that keeps the government running at the reduced sequester levels, even if that approach is not what you favor?
interview_answer: Zach, I'm not going to—and I really don't intend to spell things out completely for you all. But I think it's reasonable to say this: I already agreed to a budget framework, with specific figures. There's no cause to go back and renegotiate that. That agreement was made with Speaker Boehner and the rest of the leadership. So if a funding bill reaches my desk that matches the commitments we've already made, then of course I'd sign it, because I want to ensure we continue doing what we must for the American people. Okay? Jessica [Jessica Yellin, CNN].

question: How is the President’s sudden choice to pull out of Syria perceived across Europe—what is the European view of that withdrawal decision?
interview_answer: I’ve already responded to a question about Syria. I’m not here to assess the actions of other nations; my role is to state my own country’s stance. And our stance is exactly what I outlined earlier on Syria: we unequivocally denounce what Turkey has done in recent days, without any room for doubt. All right?

question: How do you expect the Canada–U.S. relationship to look four years from now?
interview_answer: Look, you’ve crammed a whole bunch of different questions into that. Were you even asking me directly? I’m not getting into that.

question: As President, what steps are you taking to guarantee that the election is free and fair?
interview_answer: I’m supposed to be doing what, exactly?

question: The appeal presented to President Zelenskiy concerning Vice President Biden and his son Hunter.
interview_answer: Is that question meant for me?

question: Did you raise this issue directly with King Abdallah?
interview_answer: I discussed Middle East peace with King Abdallah. I can’t say whether that particular issue was mentioned.

question: Would you back legislation that would grant congressional voting representation to the District of Columbia and add one more House seat for Utah?
interview_answer: Well, I really can’t say—I’m only learning about it now. I had no idea that was going to be introduced in the lame duck.

question: Is the $200 million total actually correct?
interview_answer: No, yes, no, yes—take your pick. [] Let’s move on. [] I haven’t—wow. You clearly haven’t posed one of these in a while. It was, like, first, I haven’t reviewed the finalized budget. Second, as Donnie Evans, the foundation’s chair, has said, we’ll examine what has to be disclosed and then decide. Now, look—I—there are many people—or at least some; I shouldn’t exaggerate—who want to donate without having their identities made public, whether to this foundation or any other. We’ll factor that in. And third—and what was the last part again?

question: Would you be willing to authorize the release of at least two of them before 2020 so the nation can better address the current crisis?
interview_answer: You know, I’ve always wanted to travel to Nigeria. I truly do hope to go there; it’s a remarkable nation. From what I’m told, in terms of natural beauty, it has few rivals anywhere in the world. So yes, I’d like to visit. But as for the first thing you asked, I honestly didn’t catch it. Please repeat it.

question: Did that statement leave you feeling let down?
interview_answer: As I’ve indicated, if those really are his remarks, I’m not going to characterize my reaction right now. Instead, I’m eager to have him explain exactly what he meant, because when I met with him, he recognized that it’s in the global community’s interest to ensure that that nation cannot develop a nuclear weapon. That’s why, in the first round at the U.N., he stood with us, and in the second round, we again worked together to send a strong signal. If he didn’t share that concern, Bret, why did we achieve such meaningful movement at the U.N. in both rounds? So I’ll talk with him directly about what he said. At this point I haven’t been fully briefed by Condi or Bob Gates on their discussions with Vladimir Putin.

question: Will you mandate that banks and other financial firms spend these funds specifically to ease tight credit and issue additional loans?
interview_answer: Helene, I’m not going to spell that out right now because I don’t want to step on what my Treasury Secretary will announce; I’d like you all to attend his briefing too—he’ll do an excellent job. To link this back to Jake’s point, one of my key tests is simply whether credit is actually reaching those who require it. Is it getting to companies, big and small? Is it reaching individual borrowers? Can they function in ways that create jobs and real growth in local communities? The plan we’ve crafted is aimed at making that happen. Beyond saying that, I’m going to leave the detailed explanation for Tim’s big day tomorrow. Okay, Major Garrett from FOX News—where’s Major?

question: Would you please address what I asked?
interview_answer: Look, I’m not answering you. I’m addressing this man here. Now, would you sit down, please?

question: Do you still believe Hillary Clinton is going to be the nominee?
interview_answer: I’m not discussing political matters.

question: Do you believe it’s essential to engage with Iran face‑to‑face?
interview_answer: So what you’re really asking is whether I’ll sit down with the Iranians personally?

question: Does the Iranian government not need to understand that there will be repercussions?
interview_answer: I’ve already responded to that, Chuck, and my answer is that we still don’t know yet how this situation will unfold. All right?

question: After the midterm elections, what should Americans anticipate Congress will do on abortion rights?
interview_answer: I don’t think they should really expect anything beyond us sticking to our current positions. I’m not going to address this any further. I shouldn’t even have responded to your question.

question: Are you going to sit down with Rouhani?
interview_answer: Come again?

question: Chance that Huawei could be taken off the entity list
interview_answer: I’m not going to address that at this time. We’re examining the situation very closely. Huawei is a major factor for our country and for our intelligence services. We have extensive information on Huawei. But I’m not going to get into that right now. I just don’t think it’s appropriate. Beyond what I’ve already said, we’re not treating it as a main topic of discussion. We’ll come back to it at a later point. All right?

question: How reliable do you believe Donald Trump is in controlling America's nuclear arsenal?
interview_answer: Regarding your second question—and let me extend this to cover any further questions about Mr. Trump—I’m not going to give you an assessment. I would simply urge all of you to reach your own conclusions. I’ve said this repeatedly before: pay attention to what Mr. Trump himself says, and then decide for yourselves how secure you feel about his capacity to handle responsibilities like our nuclear triad.

question: So there’s no chance of a shutdown during the lame-duck session?
interview_answer: I’m not sure. I’m not able to say that. No, I won’t give you that assurance, although it could happen that way.

question: Has your administration reached any conclusion about whether President Putin intends to invade or advance into Ukraine, as you have suggested?
interview_answer:
Look, the only thing I can say with certainty is that this choice belongs entirely and exclusively to Putin. No one else is going to make that call; no one else is really going to determine it. It’s his decision and his alone.

And frankly, I think his course of action may depend on something as simple as what kind of mood he wakes up in on a given day. It’s not unreasonable, if he wants to, for him to raise issues about strategic doctrine and about military deployments in Europe and in the European parts of Russia.

But I do not know whether he has actually decided to take that route or not. Up to now, the three sets of talks we’ve had—through the OSCE and so on—haven’t yielded concrete results, because, from what I’m hearing from my Secretary of State, my National Security Adviser, and other senior aides who are in those discussions, there’s real doubt about whether the Russian representatives themselves know what he intends.

So the fact is—and this is based on various scenarios of what he might do—for instance, if he were to try to move in and seize the entire country, especially from the north, via Belarus, he’d likely have to wait until the ground is fully frozen to move his forces across.

At the same time, if he’s considering a different approach, we are still strengthening Ukraine’s defensive capabilities, and we’re engaged on what’s happening in the Baltic and Black Sea regions, and so forth. There’s a wide array of factors he’s probably weighing: how fast he could act, how far he wants to go, and what exactly his objectives are.

But he’s not uninformed; he’s a calculating actor. I believe he is weighing the immediate, short-term, medium-term, and long-term implications for Russia. And I do not believe he has reached a final decision yet—so I cannot tell you that he plans to invade or move in, because I don’t know that he has decided.

question: On what basis, Mr. President, are they being granted an exception?
interview_answer: That decision was put forward to me by experts, and we’re reassessing it now in light of the latest figures we’re seeing. It’s possible we’ll end up putting them on the roster of countries we will, for lack of a better term, restrict during this timeframe. Their case numbers have risen quite sharply in just the past day, so we might add them, and we might add one or two more countries as well. And we might also, to be candid, consider removing some nations from that list. But I’m not going to spell out right now why they have an exemption at this moment.

question: How do you square your decision to commute Manning’s sentence with WikiLeaks’ role in Russia’s hack during last year’s election?
interview_answer:
Let me start by emphasizing that Chelsea Manning has already endured a very difficult period of incarceration. So I don’t think anyone who might be contemplating exposing critical, classified material could reasonably conclude, based on her experience, that such actions carry no consequences.

From my perspective, she was tried in a court of law, afforded due process, and ultimately accepted responsibility for her actions. The punishment imposed on her was, in my view, vastly out of line—far harsher than penalties imposed on comparable leakers—and she had already spent many years behind bars. Under those circumstances, I determined that reducing her sentence, rather than granting a full pardon, was the right course.

I’m entirely confident that justice has been done here and that we have still made clear that protecting our national security is paramount. People inside government who have legitimate qualms about what their agencies or superiors are doing should, wherever possible, use established internal mechanisms and the whistleblower protections that exist to raise those concerns.

I’m aware that some people argue those protections don’t go far enough, and I understand that anyone working in a large organization may sometimes disagree with the policies being carried out. But when it comes to national security matters, we’re often dealing with individuals deployed in harm’s way, or with the safety, effectiveness, and operational capacity of our military, intelligence services, and diplomatic posts. Those stakes have to stay front and center.

On the specific issue of WikiLeaks, I don’t perceive any inconsistency. To begin with, I have not been offering general commentary on WikiLeaks. The intelligence community’s findings regarding Russian cyber operations did not definitively establish whether WikiLeaks knowingly served as a vehicle for the release of the hacked DNC emails. I don’t follow Mr. Assange’s social media statements, so his public comments played no role in this decision. And I am not going to get into any potential criminal cases, indictments, or extradition questions involving him; for that, you’ll need to speak with the Justice Department.

What I can address in broader terms is that, in this digital era, we must constantly refine how we strike the proper balance between accountability, openness, and transparency—values at the core of our democracy—and the need to protect ourselves from adversaries and malicious actors. Those actors may try to exploit that very openness to commit financial fraud, carry out terrorist plots, or interfere in our elections.

We have to keep strengthening a framework that safeguards our democratic ideals while also enabling our national security and intelligence professionals to execute policy without broadcasting our plans to those who wish us harm, and still keeping the public reasonably informed about what their government is doing.

As for Chelsea Manning specifically, I evaluated the details of her case using the same standards I’ve applied in all other commutation and pardon decisions, and I concluded that, given all the facts, commuting her sentence was fully justified. I’m not going to go beyond that to draw connections to WikiLeaks or the Russian hacking issue.

Margaret Brennan [CBS News].

question: Is it accurate to say that the White House will be pushing the NIH and FDA to move this forward as fast as they possibly can?
interview_answer: Look, if the figures are as strong as I’ve been hearing—I’ve been told it’s above 50 percent, which is very strong. And we’ve authorized—some things are at about 31 percent, and that’s still decent; that’s not terrible. It’s made a really big difference. But as for what you’re saying now—I wasn’t aware of that, it actually surprises me, and I can’t really address it right now. We’ll look into it right after this.

question: Cite or bring up this individual's name.
interview_answer: We still have no idea.

question: Should we understand this declaration as marking the conclusion of the conflict, or as Moscow merely stalling for time while it reorganizes and prepares a renewed military offensive?
interview_answer: We’ll find out in due course. I’m not going to ascribe any meaning to it until we can judge them by their behavior. We’ll see whether they actually implement what they’re now indicating. Talks are underway again today—continuing, really—including one round in Turkey and others elsewhere. Earlier, I sat down with the leaders of four NATO partners—France, Germany, the United States, and the United Kingdom—and there was broad agreement that we should hold off on drawing conclusions and simply wait to see what they actually put on the table and how they act. In the interim, we will maintain robust sanctions, we will go on equipping Ukrainian forces so they can defend their country, and we will keep monitoring developments very closely. Thank you. Let me turn now to Dawn Tan from Channel NewsAsia.

question: 2) Will the renewed commitment to aid Iraq involve sending more American military troops?
interview_answer:
