On that second point, I'm not going to speculate about failing to achieve our goal. My expectation is that we will achieve it. And I’m hopeful we’ll have a vote soon, because I believe that’s the correct course of action.

On ISIL, we’ve made substantial headway in driving them out of areas they once controlled or disrupted, but we’ve also seen situations like Ramadi, where they’re pushed out of one location only to resurface somewhere else. They’re adaptive, they’re aggressive, and they seize opportunities. So we need to get faster at training Iraqi forces. In places where we have directly trained and equipped Iraqi units and maintained a train-and-assist presence, they perform well. Where that support is lacking, low morale, inadequate gear, and similar problems can weaken Iraqi security forces.

So our aim is to increase the number of Iraqi troops who are newly trained, properly armed, and clearly focused. Prime Minister Abadi shares that objective. We are therefore examining various options to speed up the process of getting Iraqi forces effectively trained and equipped, under a coherent strategy and solid leadership. Once the Pentagon presents me with a finalized plan, I will then lay it out for the American people. Right now, we do not have a fully developed strategy, because it also depends on commitments from the Iraqi side regarding recruitment and how the training will be conducted. Those specifics have not yet been fully resolved, so I’m not in a position to go into those details at this time.

question: Did Fidel Castro play any part in the negotiations when the United States made its approach to Cuba?
interview_answer: Okay, Lesley, I’m going to stop you right there. We’re not going into that; it’s using up too much time.

question: Is President Karzai right to hold Pakistan’s intelligence agencies responsible for the recent terror strike in Afghanistan?
interview_answer: To begin with, we’re going to review his accusation thoroughly and coordinate with his intelligence service so we can fully understand and assess what he’s alleging. What is clear already, though, is that certain extremist elements are operating from areas inside Pakistan and crossing into Afghanistan. That’s a source of concern for us, for Afghanistan, and it ought to concern Pakistan as well. We all face the same foe: violent extremists who seek to derail democracy or stop it from taking root.Al Qaida is present in the region. We have struck Al Qaida very hard and significantly weakened them around the world, including inside Pakistan. And we will keep up that pressure on Al Qaida in partnership with our Pakistani allies.I sincerely trust that Pakistan’s leadership recognizes how dangerous it is to allow extremists to move freely within their borders. I believe they do. In fact, I’ll be meeting with the Pakistani Prime Minister on Monday, and we will have a chance to talk more about these issues then. Pakistan is our partner; Pakistan is our friend. And, again, all three nations—the United States, Pakistan, and Afghanistan—are united against the same adversary.I vividly recall hosting President Musharraf and President Karzai at the White House, where we discussed the importance of cross-border cooperation to stop dangerous groups from training, crossing into Afghanistan, and then going back home with skills that could be turned against their own government.We saw some encouraging steps forward, though the struggle remains very difficult. We took heart from the provincial elections in that region. We’re going to continue helping the government there both to confront violent extremists and to pursue an effective counterinsurgency approach that uses assistance to spur economic growth. It’s a tough problem. But the three of us working together can handle it far better than if we act alone.All right, I’ve said what I’m going to say. Thanks a lot for your time. I appreciate it.

question: Doubts remain about whether Afghan security forces can effectively handle major attacks in Kabul. How do you respond to that concern?
interview_answer: First, remember that our troop drawdown hasn’t actually started yet. We’ve always recognized that this remains a dangerous environment, that the Taliban is still operating, and that incidents like this will continue to happen from time to time. The issue we look at is whether, over the long run, Afghan capabilities are growing. Take Kabul: it’s home to a very large share of Afghanistan’s people, and Afghan forces have been primarily responsible for security there for quite a while now. They’ve performed fairly well. Kabul is considerably safer than it used to be, and the Afghan units there are far more competent than in the past. That does not imply these kinds of attacks won’t occur; they likely will for some period. Our mission isn’t finished. But as I noted in my remarks, the tide of war is going out, and we have moved into a transition period. Much as in Iraq—where we drew down our presence, are completing the withdrawal this year, and the Iraqis have sustained a democratic system while reducing violence—we believe a comparable strategy is appropriate in Afghanistan. Yet even in Iraq, you still see periodic bombings or assaults. These societies are emerging from years of intense conflict. They’re inherently risky places and will not become completely secure, even if we remained there indefinitely. So I’m not going to give a simple yes‑or‑no judgment on that specific attack scenario; what I can say is that we are focused on steadily improving Afghans’ capacity to protect themselves.

question: What was the distance between the shooting scene and the White House?
interview_answer: The officials will be providing you with a comprehensive briefing on this shortly.

question: Do you approve of Postmaster General DeJoy having allegedly urged ex-staff at his private firm to contribute to Republicans and then paying them back afterward?
interview_answer: Well, I really can’t speak to that right now because I barely know anything about it. I glanced at a report this morning, but beyond that, I’d need to review the facts. What I can say is he’s widely regarded as a credible person. My understanding is that both parties, in effect, signed off on him. I don’t yet know the full situation. I expect I’ll be briefed soon. I only saw the story for the first time this morning, same as you.

question: How do you explain that the share of people who have died is significantly higher in the United States than in other nations?
interview_answer: Look, many of our figures are driven by what happened in—well, New York really went through an extremely rough period, as everyone knows. New York, New Jersey—that whole region. And if you set those aside—just for illustration, compare Florida to New York. I’m not criticizing New York; it was simply a very difficult situation. The population is packed together, it’s dense, it’s a hard environment for this. But if you exclude that, our statistics are among the very best. And even including it—I’ll have to come back to you with the exact explanation, but our numbers are still among the lowest. They’ve handled it incredibly well. Next question.

question: It appears you’re edging toward scrapping the filibuster altogether. Is that right?
interview_answer: I’ve already responded to that./Tax Policy Overhaul/Federal Spending Levels/The Administration’s Priorities

question: How do you respond to Russia’s claim that it will reduce military activity around Kyiv and another northern Ukrainian city?
interview_answer: We’ll have to wait and see. I’m not going to draw any conclusions until I can judge them by what they actually do on the ground. We’ll see whether they really implement what they’re talking about. There are talks underway today — continuing talks — including one in Turkey and others elsewhere. I also just met with the leaders of four NATO partners: France, Germany, the United States, and the United Kingdom. And the general view is that we should hold off on any judgments and see what Russia actually puts on the table and how it behaves. In the meantime, I’m not going to comment beyond that; we will keep sanctions firmly in place, we will keep supplying Ukraine with what it needs to defend itself, and we will keep watching developments very closely. Thank you. Next, Dawn Tan from Channel NewsAsia.

question: Do you believe Saudi Arabia bears responsibility for having him killed?
interview_answer: I’ll be able to give you a much firmer view on that in the coming days, and I’m in very close consultation with Congress right now. We’re coordinating together, with some extremely capable people. We’re engaging with Congress, with Turkey, and with Saudi Arabia. I’m in the process of reaching a very definitive conclusion. Next question, Jon. Go ahead, Jon.

question: If, before assuming the presidency, you had owned hotels, real estate holdings, and various other enterprises across multiple countries, would you have considered it proper to liquidate those assets and place the funds in a blind trust?
interview_answer: That initial question doesn’t call for an answer; it’s purely rhetorical.

question: For what period will Israel put its West Bank annexation plan on hold?
interview_answer: What’s your own estimate? Go ahead, you tell me. Look, we’re investing everything in advancing peace. We’ve reached an understanding with the Emirates, and now we’ll work through all the specifics—embassies, flight routes, trade—and then try to expand that framework across the broader region. I can’t say how long that entire process will last. What I can say is that we’ve chosen to focus on peace rather than on the sovereignty initiative for now, but we haven’t abandoned it. It’s simply being pushed back while we exhaust every possible path to peace.

question: By arranging your own talks in Washington, haven’t you effectively undercut his effort to set high-level goals here?
interview_answer:
Let me take the second part first. I’m getting on in years, so you might need to restate the opening of your question. Now, Kyoto might be suitable for some nations. It may well have been appropriate for certain countries that signed onto it; it simply wasn’t right for the United States. So I was straightforward about that. And remember, before I ever got to Washington, the U.S. Senate had an opportunity to say whether Kyoto was right for America or not. The vote was 95–0 against Kyoto. So this isn’t just my personal view; a lot of people concluded Kyoto was not the correct path.

Instead, we adopted a different strategy: tightening energy-efficiency standards and driving innovation in new technologies. As I mentioned earlier, last year we cut greenhouse gas emissions in the United States while our economy expanded by 3 percent. I’m not sure many other countries can point to that combination, but we can, because our technology-driven approach is delivering results.

I also believe we can significantly reduce our reliance on foreign oil. As an energy-exporting region, that might sound unsettling to some producers, but we have to lessen that dependence. That’s why we put forward what we call the 20-in-10 plan. Over the next decade, we will cut U.S. gasoline usage by 20 percent through ethanol and a range of emerging technologies. I’m convinced advances in battery technology are coming that will allow, say, drivers in Sydney to travel the first 40 miles on battery power without their vehicles looking like golf carts.

In other words, we’re on the verge of major technological changes. The key issue is how fast we can move those technologies into everyday use. From our perspective, Kyoto didn’t achieve that. In fact, if you look carefully at how many signatories are actually on track to meet their Kyoto commitments, you may conclude that another method is warranted. We rejected Kyoto precisely because it would have constrained our ability to do three things at once: grow our economy, cut our dependence on imported oil, and act responsibly toward the environment.

John and I have discussed his determination to make climate change a centerpiece of APEC, and I strongly backed him on that. I also reminded him that at the G‑8 I delivered a message to our partners: if you truly want to address global climate change, everyone has to be included. That means countries like China and India must sit at the same table as we chart a path forward. If they feel that other nations are going to impose a solution on them without giving them a meaningful voice, they’re likely to walk away. And if a major emitter like China isn’t involved, you cannot have an effective global climate regime.

I think the Prime Minister made a wise move in this framework. He called for cutting tariffs that block the export of technologies that would let countries like China burn coal more cleanly. At present, those nations shield parts of their environmental industries. He wants to lower those trade barriers, and we firmly support that, so that cleaner technologies can flow more easily from countries that possess them to those that need them.

The core questions are these: How rapidly can we bring effective technologies—such as carbon capture for coal and advanced nuclear fuel recycling—to market? And, once those technologies exist at scale, can we help developing countries gain access to them? Without that, the whole exercise will fall short.

I am convinced the approach we have set out is the most realistic and effective way to handle this problem in a serious manner. I also value the fact that the Prime Minister has put this issue squarely on the agenda and is focusing on energy security and efficiency standards, which are central pieces of the climate challenge. He’s showing leadership on this matter, and I’m glad to be here discussing it with him. All right, thanks.

question: Do you back Congressman Chaka Fattah’s proposal, the Debt Free America Act? And are you throwing your support behind the comparable Republican legislation modeled on Congressman Fattah’s bill?
interview_answer:
I’m not going to take a position on any specific piece of legislation right now. Instead, let me address the larger issue you’re really raising, April.

This downturn has hit everyone, but it’s been especially brutal for people who started out with the least. What has driven me, from the moment I took office, is all those families out there doing everything right—caring for their kids, working hard day in and day out—who still feel like they’re slipping behind no matter how much effort they put in.

Just this past week I got a letter from a woman whose husband lost his job. He went out, knocked on doors, searched and searched until he finally landed new work. For a few months, they thought they were back on solid ground. Six months later, he was laid off again. Now they’re back out searching, wondering how they’re going to pay the bills. And there are hundreds of thousands of Americans who’ve gone through something just as tough in what is, for many of us, the hardest economy we’ve ever seen.

When I came into office, we moved very aggressively to pull the economy back from the brink of another Great Depression and to stabilize the situation. On that front, we largely succeeded. But the reality is, we stabilized at a point where unemployment is still unacceptably high and growth is too slow to make up for the massive job losses that occurred before I arrived and in the early months of my administration.

So we’re dealing with a very stubborn unemployment problem. There are several ways to tackle it. First, we need the broader economy to grow. That’s why we’ve taken repeated steps to put more money directly into people’s pockets so they can spend and support demand. The payroll tax reductions were aimed squarely at that.

Second, we’ve acted to encourage businesses to invest. That’s the rationale behind small-business tax cuts and incentives for companies that put money into plants and equipment, and measures like eliminating capital gains taxes for many small businesses—these are designed to give firms more reasons to expand.

We’ve also focused on improving job training for workers whose old jobs may never come back, so they can gain the skills needed for positions that are available now and in the future. At the same time, we’ve proposed a series of efforts to review regulations that might be unnecessarily burdening investment. We’re looking carefully at the costs and benefits of those rules, and where a regulation isn’t delivering on promised benefits—whether for public health, clean air and water, or worker safety—we should be willing to revise or remove it.

So we’ve been examining the full range of tools available to us. But we’re now facing a situation where, because growth has been slower than we’d hoped and because the recession and financial crisis blew a big hole in our finances, pursuing a strategy that would cost trillions of dollars simply isn’t realistic. We don’t have that kind of fiscal space right now.

What we can do is put in place a credible, long-term solution to our debt and deficit challenges. If we do that, we can then return to a conversation about, once that cloud is lifted—once deficits are no longer dragging on growth or fueling uncertainty, and businesses and families feel the ground is steady—whether there are targeted steps we can take to spur job creation. Infrastructure is a prime candidate there.

For example, the infrastructure bank we’ve proposed is fairly modest. But we could envision a much broader initiative to rebuild roads, bridges, ports, schools, broadband networks, and smart grids—putting construction workers and related industries back to work right away. I can easily see a robust infrastructure program that the American people would strongly support, and that would strengthen our economy not just in the next year or two, but for decades.

The problem is, we can’t even credibly entertain those kinds of big ideas if there’s still widespread concern that we don’t have our fiscal house in order. So the concept here is: act now to address the long-term debt and deficit problem, get that off the table, and then, with more solid footing and a sound fiscal position, we’ll be able to make the kinds of strategic investments that are essential if we want to stay competitive in the future.

This shouldn’t be framed as a simple left-versus-right, liberal-versus-conservative debate. It’s about governing wisely, recognizing that we’ve got immediate problems to manage and deeper, structural ones to fix. If we can make headway on the long-term issues, it frees us up to devote more energy and resources to dealing with the short-term pain as well.

So, to return to your question, I’m not going to endorse or oppose that particular bill—or the similar Republican measure—right now. My focus is on the broader approach I’ve just laid out. Thank you all very much.

question: At what point should we anticipate that announcement?
interview_answer: Look, as you’re aware, some are moving forward and others aren’t. But there are definitely places that are advancing. As they hit the targets that everyone agrees are appropriate—the figures they recognize are correct and we recognize are correct—we’ll start opening things up. And a few are advancing very quickly. Our aim is to reopen just as soon as we can. Next question, please. Yes, go ahead.

question: What do you say to people who argue that your administration is ignoring the persecution of religious minorities and the suppression of dissent in Prime Minister Modi’s country?
interview_answer: My response to your first question is: No. Look, when we engage with allies and partners around the globe, India included, the notion that I’m going to start picking and choosing my words or stop saying what I think are the facts about our relationship with India—or with China—is just not something I’m going to do differently. I’ve said for quite a while that all the overheated rhetoric about the U.S.–China relationship collapsing, shifting, and so on—well, we had an episode that created some, let’s say, confusion. But the Secretary of State just had a very successful visit to China, and I anticipate meeting with President Xi before long. I don’t see that episode having any real impact. Now, what was your second question again?

question: Update us on the event that occurred near California’s shoreline
interview_answer: I’m actually set to receive a briefing shortly; it’s already planned to take place a little later today.

question: What steps is your administration weighing as a response to the Chinese government’s expulsion of Wall Street Journal journalists?
interview_answer: At this point I haven't received a complete briefing on that issue. We'll review it, we’re going to review it. It’s not something I like to see happen. I don’t think it’s right, because we allow very broad media access here—some might say we allow too much, and I don’t agree with that critique. I think our access is very generous, so I see this as unfair. But we’ll reach a decision on how to handle it fairly soon. All right, thanks. Good question. Next, please. You, go ahead.

question: Requesting details about the members of the President's Cabinet.
interview_answer: You tell it. Go on.

question: Would you be willing to back the WHO again if Tedros were removed right away?
interview_answer: Look, we’ve got a review under way right now. I don’t personally know this individual, but I do know there have been serious issues. The United States has been treated very badly, just like with the World Trade Organization, which has also been extremely unfair—though they’re finally starting to come around. When they label China a “developing country,” China uses that to massively exploit the United States. Why didn’t previous Presidents put a stop to that? I’ve raised this from the moment I took office, and we’re examining it very, very closely. World Trade, World Health—I’ve got concerns with both. I honestly can’t tell you which one is worse at this point, and I’m not going to say more than that until we sort it out.

question: Is the Deputy National Security Adviser right in saying there’s no intent to launch strikes if Congress withholds authorization?
interview_answer: I’m not sure that’s precisely how he put it. In any case, I’ve already responded to that question. Major Garrett, CBS News.

question: What specific reassurance can you offer employees who are being told to resume work but feel afraid to return?
interview_answer:
Ultimately, that’s a decision each governor will have to handle. They’re the ones who will want to be certain conditions are safe. Now, I believe businesses can set up testing, maybe once a week or so; you don’t need to test the same person every single day, but they can run tests. They can use thermometers. They can implement a whole range of measures.

And remember, this is only for a limited period. Our goal is to get back to where we used to be. We want fans actually side by side at sporting events again. We’re not going to permanently remove every other seat in baseball and football stadiums. We want to return to the old setup.

That’s what I want people to grasp. We’re not staying like this. Right now, we have hundreds of journalists in the back who would rather be seated normally. Just look at this arrangement, look at how it appears. I don’t even like how it looks, even though having fewer reporters isn’t exactly a problem for me.

But just look at it. I’ve never really seen anything like this before. There are many people who are unhappy with it—we don’t want this. I don’t want this at all.

In time, restaurants that had 150 seats will be back to 150 seats, not 50, because at 50 they can’t survive. And beyond that, the environment is much better when they’re full. We’ll adapt to some changes. Maybe people won’t be as quick to shake hands. Some will; some won’t.

I even asked some hospital staff today, “So, are people going to go back to shaking hands?” And I have to say, I was a bit surprised most of them said, “Probably,” because there’s something about it people like. I was never very big on handshakes myself, but when I went into politics, I said, “I’d better start shaking hands.”

Next question, John.

question: What is the President currently thinking, after the midterms, regarding the Deputy Attorney General?
interview_answer: I’m not going to get into that right now; that’s something I’ll address at another time. We’re reviewing a number of matters, including the Cabinet overall. I’m very satisfied with most of the people serving in my Cabinet. We’re considering various individuals for various roles, which is very typical after midterm elections. I didn’t want to make any moves before the midterms. But I can say that, on the whole, I’m very pleased with my Cabinet—Mike Pompeo, for example, has really settled in remarkably well and done a fantastic job—

question: Possible U.S. role in this particular issue
interview_answer: I’m not willing to speak about that topic. Shall we go to another question?

question: Could we negotiate a plan that includes a ban on assault weapons?
interview_answer: I’ll go over those specifics later this week. Next question, Chuck Todd.

question: Will the border end up tighter or looser?
interview_answer: Look, you’ve crammed about half a dozen questions into that. Were you even asking me or Jonathan? What is this supposed to be?

question: Will Kimberly, Donald Trump Jr., and Ivanka Trump, who are very well liked among Indian Americans, actively campaign for you within that community, given your stance on relations between India and the United States?
interview_answer:
I have to say, I didn’t quite catch your entire question. I do know India very well, and I know the young people you referred to. They’re terrific, very impressive young people. I also know they have a very strong connection to India, and I share that—I have a very strong connection to India as well. And Prime Minister Modi is a friend of mine; he’s doing an excellent job. It’s a tough job—very tough—but he’s doing it very well.

Since you raised it, we’re also dealing with the situation between China and India. As you know, things on their border have been very tense—very difficult, very serious. We stand ready to assist with that situation between China and India. If there’s anything we can do, we’d be very happy to step in and help, and we are in communication with both sides about it.

So I do appreciate your kind remarks. They care deeply about India, and I do as well. And I have great respect for your Prime Minister. Thank you.

question: Is this that sort of deal?
interview_answer: Well, it actually brings up an intriguing point about whether, in a formal sense, there even is a signature involved, because, as I’ve mentioned, this isn’t a legally enforceable pact. I’m not familiar with the precise procedures. What I can say is that this reflects a commitment by the United States that we view as very significant. Okay, I’ll leave it there. Thanks, everyone.

question: Deadline for Prime Minister Maliki to achieve his objectives
interview_answer: Could you restate the first section of that three-part question?

question: Are you ruling out any chance of a shutdown during the lame-duck session?
interview_answer: I really can’t say. I’m not in a position to answer that. No, I won’t make that promise, although it could happen.

question: Both of you?
interview_answer: So, the two of you then? You can proceed.

question: What do you think is going to happen with that?
interview_answer: I really can’t say. I haven’t been keeping up with it in any detail. I simply hope things turn out well for her, to be honest. I’ve run into her many times over the years, especially when I was living in Palm Beach and, as I understand it, they were in Palm Beach too. But I do wish her the best, whatever the circumstances are. I’m not familiar with what’s going on with Prince Andrew. I truly don’t know. I’m not informed about it. All right, next question, please.

question: Did someone within your team ever urge him not to go through with it?
interview_answer:
I never had a conversation with him about it, so I can’t say. I’m not aware of what was said in any of those discussions, and I don’t see Hamas altering its behavior. It’s up to Hamas to make a change. You sit down with these folks, they promise one thing and then act completely differently. That’s how it’s been for the last seven and a half years of this administration, watching Hamas play a destabilizing role.

I did back the elections, incidentally. And interestingly, they beat Fatah by running on an anti‑corruption message. The unfortunate reality is that now that they’ve held power, they’ve failed to deliver for the people in Gaza.

My job, when I travel to the Middle East, is to keep working with both the Israelis and President Abbas and his administration on multiple tracks: helping them shape a shared vision, helping them find common ground on that vision, and also coordinating with Israel to give Palestinians in the West Bank more control over their own security, fewer daily obstacles, and better prospects for economic activity and growth. There are some promising initiatives taking shape there.

I still believe we can reach an agreement before my term is over. Condi is going back out there. I’ve been speaking with President Abbas here in the Oval Office, and I’m in regular contact with Prime Minister Olmert, and the outlook is positive. There’s a real recognition of how crucial it is to define a Palestinian state.

But Hamas—look, if you’re Israel and rockets are being fired into your territory, you’re going to respond. The question is, why is Hamas launching those rockets? One major reason is that they’re trying to undercut stability and sow turmoil and uncertainty. And as for whether anyone’s conversations with them made any difference, the only real way to judge that is to watch how Hamas acts going forward.

question: Have you seen the New Yorker article reporting that the Red Cross has judged the CIA’s detention and interrogation practices to be equivalent to torture?
interview_answer:
What I’ve already said is that this country ought to have, as a national objective, the closure of Guantanamo. I’ve also been clear that one major reason this hasn’t happened yet is that many governments have been unwilling to accept back some of the individuals held there. Put simply, to make closure feasible, there have to be destinations for these detainees. You may have noticed the recent Senate resolution where all but three Senators essentially said they do not want these prisoners brought into the United States. I don’t recall if it was 97 to 3, but it was something like that. So, a central part of the challenge, Peter, is a very practical one: what are we supposed to do with these people?

You’ve suggested that no progress has been made, and I completely reject that. First, we are actively working with other countries to repatriate detainees. That is no small task. Many nations do not want to receive dangerous individuals, and many of these detainees are, in fact, extremely dangerous. Second, we are committed to ensuring that when we do repatriate people, they are treated as humanely as we can reasonably ensure.

Another key question is whether and how we can bring these individuals to trial. One of my priorities is to see real trials conducted. The courts have intervened and are helping determine the appropriate framework, and the Defense Department is trying to design the procedures so that these trials can move forward. From my perspective, the sooner those proceedings begin, the better. I am not going to offer any prediction about whether Guantanamo will ultimately be closed. What I am saying is that this is an extremely complex matter.

I have outlined a goal. Whether we ultimately reach it, we will certainly make the effort. But this is far from the simple issue some people imagine. Again, I would point you to that Senate vote. When Senators were effectively asked if they wanted Guantanamo closed and thus to accept some of these detainees in their own states, there was very little enthusiasm. Only about three of them essentially said, ‘That’s fine by me.’

As for the specific point you raised, I’m not going to get into that. Next question, sir?

question: President Uribe’s view on whether he believes Chavez’s impact across the region is exaggerated
interview_answer: And what exactly is it I’m supposed to think about?

question: Given Vice President Biden’s claim that the race remains wide open, do you agree it still is?
interview_answer:
First, I’ve already run my final campaign, and second, forecasting upcoming elections is simply not something I do. That’s your line of work. And there’s certainly no lack of commentators eager to offer predictions. I am not going to join them.

On Cuba, our objective is not to topple the government. Our focus is on helping ensure that the Cuban people enjoy freedom, can take part in shaping their own futures and their own lives, and on strengthening civil society.

Changes in Cuba are going to occur over time regardless of what we choose to do. Some of that will be generational. If you listened to President Castro’s remarks this morning, many of his arguments referred back to events that took place before I was even born, and part of what I am saying here is that the Cold War is finished. We still confront many serious challenges and lots of global issues, and we will continue to have major disagreements with Cuba, not only about how its government treats its citizens but also about broader regional concerns. There will also be areas where we can work together. Cuban medical teams helped during the Ebola outbreak; Cuban efforts in Haiti after the earthquake also had an impact. So there may be other opportunities for cooperation.

What I told President Castro is what I tell leaders across the hemisphere: we have clear views, and we are not going to hesitate to voice them. But I believe the best way to advance the values we care about is through persuasion. That will be our main method on many of these questions, in large part because they do not directly threaten our national security.

And we should be absolutely straightforward about this: Cuba does not pose a threat to the United States. That doesn’t mean we don’t have real differences. But if you look at the threats that keep me up at night, I think it’s reasonable to say that, between ISIL, the danger of Iran obtaining a nuclear weapon, instability in Yemen and Libya, Boko Haram, Russian aggression in Ukraine and its effects on our allies—I could extend that list—plus climate change, Cuba is not at the top.

So our strategy has to involve working with countries in the region and beyond, and being explicit about our principles, our priorities, and what we think is effective or ineffective. Too often, when we intervene in ways that go beyond persuasion, it ends up hurting rather than helping. It backfires. That’s been part of our history, and it’s why some governments keep trying to blame us for their own governance failures. We should remove that excuse. And we should make clear that we’re ready to cooperate and engage with everyone who is serious about expanding opportunity, prosperity, and security for people throughout the region.

Major Garrett, CBS News.

question: Were you made aware of any new COVID-19 cases at the White House today?
interview_answer: I’m not able to hear what you said, I’m afraid.

question: Can you confirm whether the White House has asked that the Republican coronavirus legislation include specific language about the FBI headquarters in downtown Washington, DC?
interview_answer:
I’m not aware that it’s actually being written into this particular bill, but I do know there’s a push to get a new FBI headquarters. The current facility is extremely old and, as you’ve likely heard, it wasn’t constructed to a very high standard. It’s become hazardous—parts of the exterior are coming loose, bits of concrete are falling off the structure. And they’re interested in rebuilding on the same site they currently occupy. They had proposals for locations much farther from downtown Washington, and I told them very directly: you really need to stay close to the Justice Department. There’s no location better than where they are now. They were reviewing sites in Maryland and Virginia, different areas, but those would have been too remote.

So I’ve been urging them to go ahead and rebuild. And when you look at it, there are two choices: you can try to renovate the existing building—which isn’t a good building—or you can demolish it and put up a first‑rate headquarters that will serve the FBI for the next hundred years and be truly outstanding. They’re even discussing putting running tracks on the roof, because FBI personnel like to exercise a lot, and the footprint is big enough for quarter‑mile tracks on top; it’s a very large, very wide parcel.

So in my view, the best course is to construct a brand‑new facility so it will last for generations. Renovation in this case can’t match what you’d get with new construction. I know there are ongoing discussions about it—whether it shows up in this bill or in some other measure—but I can’t tell you if it’s being inserted here. What I can say is that the FBI needs a new building, and we’re going to see that it happens. Thank you very much. Thanks. Thank you.

question: Is it completely ruled out that Ukraine might give up any land?
interview_answer: On that second issue, I’ll say yes. But that really hinges on what happens with the G-20. That matter came up today, and I brought up the option that, if that doesn’t work out—if Indonesia and some others don’t go along—then we should, in my opinion, push to have both parties able to take part in the sessions, essentially to attend the G-20 and sit in as observers. Now, as for—remind me, what was your first question again?

question: Will you grant certification to Mexico?
interview_answer: I didn’t catch the interpreter on that question. [] It really seemed like a great question, though. [] Now I believe the translation’s on its way—please, go ahead.

question: Is the upcoming meeting going to be held soon?
interview_answer: I’m not able to say. It could happen in the near future, or it might be quite a while from now. I’m not going to tell you. I’d like it to happen soon, but it could just as easily be delayed for a long time.

question: How do you gauge how willing troops are to accept this?
interview_answer: That’s not something we have any say over. It’s a matter for the United States to determine. Headcounts won’t alter the realities on the ground in Afghanistan; what truly matters is the wider strategic relationship that will shape outcomes in Afghanistan and across the region. Decisions about exact troop figures are strictly for the military authorities, and Afghanistan has no particular stake in the precise numbers or their deployment patterns. Afghan media here? English-language media? [He then spoke briefly in Dari to reporters, with no transcript provided.]

question: Would the President consider taking back Bill Cosby’s Presidential Medal of Freedom?
interview_answer: You’ve packed quite a lot into that, April. You know what?

question: What is the suspect's sex?
interview_answer: I’m not sure about that. You’ll need to check with the team—they can give you a full briefing on it afterward.

question: Does misusing one’s authority qualify as grounds for impeachment?
interview_answer:
You’d really need to ask the attorneys about that. What I can say is this: There’s absolutely nothing there. I had a totally straightforward call with a very good man from Ukraine, and that’s what this is all about. People don’t even want to focus on the call itself. I only managed to catch a few snippets in between all the meetings I just mentioned, and what I saw was Washington wasting time. And when I watched, I noticed they’re not actually talking about my call. They’re not talking about the written record of it.

Keep in mind: When Schiff fabricated that bogus version and read it to Congress and to everyone watching—a completely made‑up account—I then put out the transcript. They claimed there was going to be a second whistleblower. Where is that person? And—hold on, hold on, or I’m not staying on your program. There was supposed to be some inside source. Where did that person go? All these people vanished. Once they saw the actual transcript, they realized they had a problem. But they pushed ahead anyway because they had already committed to a fake, manufactured narrative.

So here’s the bottom line: I did nothing wrong. The call was flawless. Entirely proper. Top legal experts have reviewed it. The Justice Department reviewed it and cleared it. There was no wrongdoing.

They never expected me to release the call. They probably didn’t think we had a proper record or that we’d make it public. When we did release it, Democrats went into a panic, because they realized: This is nothing like what Shifty Schiff described.

So things are going very well for us. I watched enough to see that our side presented the case very effectively. And frankly, we control all the key documents; they don’t have them.

question: If the North Korean leader refuses to negotiate or take the necessary steps, would the United States be prepared to tighten sanctions and increase pressure on North Korea to push the process forward?
interview_answer: I’m not going to give an answer on that. What I can say is this: we already have very tough sanctions in place. I’m not going to get into whether we’d ramp them up. They’re tough. And there are a lot of good, ordinary people in North Korea who still have to live their lives, and I care about that. I’ll also say this: my whole perspective has shifted quite a bit since I got to know Chairman Kim, as you’re aware, very well. They have their own viewpoint too. So I really don’t want to address that specific issue. I just believe that, hopefully, for the good of , for the good of Japan, and honestly for the good of —I was just speaking with President Xi, who’s a leader respected by many—and I told him, you can’t be happy having a nuclear-armed neighbor right on your border. And he’s not. He truly isn’t. I can tell you, he also wants that problem resolved. And I’ll leave it there. Now, ladies and gentlemen, I’ve got a plane to catch back to a fantastic place called Washington, DC. Thank you all very much. Thank you. Thanks, everybody. Thanks a lot.

question: Is backing same-sex marriage what you personally believe?
interview_answer: I’m not about to wade into that issue today. Nice attempt, though.

question: In what way did your dialogue with Prime Minister Reinfeldt help address the climate emergency?
interview_answer:
I’d really point you back to the remarks I delivered when I accepted the Nobel Peace Prize. I opened that address by noting that, compared with earlier laureates, I hardly felt I deserved the honor. I also laid out there the broader dilemma we all face when we cherish peace but live in a world saturated with violence and, at times, outright evil. That forces us to ask: what obligations do we have in such a world?

I’ve tried, throughout my presidency, to bring the Iraq war to a close, to draw down our forces in Afghanistan, to deepen our engagement in multilateral institutions, and to put diplomacy at the center of how we solve conflicts. But the fundamental question that confronts everyone—not only me, but ordinary citizens as well as their leaders—is: when do we decide we must directly challenge actions that offend our shared sense of humanity?

When I witness 400 children attacked with poison gas, see more than 1,400 innocent people killed for no reason in a context where tens of thousands have already died, and recognize that we have a chance to take limited yet significant steps that, while they won’t fix everything, could at least reduce the suffering in that particular situation, then I believe the morally responsible choice is not to remain passive.

These are agonizing choices. This is the side of the presidency I struggle with every day. I would far prefer to devote my time to figuring out how every 3- and 4-year-old can have access to high‑quality early education than to be forced to think about how to keep 3- and 4-year-olds from being hit with nerve agents and toxic gas.

But these are the kinds of decisions that land on my desk as President of the United States. And the reality is, in this job I cannot simply turn away from them, because no matter how often we’re criticized, the immediate reaction when something terrible happens anywhere on the globe is to ask what the United States is going to do. That’s the expectation on every front—Libya, Rwanda, Sierra Leone, and now Syria. That’s part of the responsibility that comes with this office.

Now, remind me—what was your second question again? Ah, yes: climate change. I’m not going to get into that here.

question: Could relations between North Korea and the U.S. someday resemble the U.S.–Vietnam relationship?
interview_answer: You’ll need to ask something else.

question: What is your response to the idea that you are secretly rescuing the world from a satanic cabal of child abusers and cannibals?
interview_answer:
I haven’t come across that. But are you saying that would be a negative thing or a positive thing? Because, look, if I can play a role in fixing the world’s problems, I’m more than happy to do it. I’m prepared to step up and take that on. And in fact, that’s what we’re doing: we’re protecting the world from an extreme left-wing agenda that would ruin this nation. And if this nation collapses, the rest of the globe goes right behind it. The rest of the globe goes right behind it. That’s how crucial this country is.

And when you listen to what some of these people are pushing—cutting off police funding, having no borders, totally open borders where anyone can just flood into the United States, no screening, nothing at all. You keep bringing up testing—there’s no testing. Mexico, as you know, has a very high infection rate.

By next week, the wall will be 300 miles long. Our border numbers are phenomenal. If that—well, that’s due to luck maybe more than skill, though the skill is being built up while one party fights it every step of the way. You don’t hear them bringing up the wall anymore.

What I will say is this: our country needs toughness, not softness. We’ve got way too much softness. Yes, John. Go ahead.

question: Do the dismissal of the general and Prigozhin’s mutiny suggest anything about what Putin might do going forward?
interview_answer: To start with, they’ve already meddled in U.S. elections, so that kind of interference wouldn’t be something new. They did it before—they attempted to do it the last time around. Now, on the question of Putin using nuclear weapons, I don’t really see that as a likely possibility—you can never be completely certain—but the West, China, and the broader international community have all made it very clear: Don’t cross that line. Don’t even think about it. Second. Third, as for what ultimately happens to Prigozhin and what comes out of our talks in Vilnius—well, honestly, only God knows what he might end up doing. We don’t even truly know his current whereabouts or what his status is. If I were him, I’d be very cautious about my food. I’d be studying my plate closely. Jokes aside, who can say? I can’t. I don’t think any of us can confidently predict Prigozhin’s fate in Russia. So I really can’t give you an answer to that question beyond what I’ve already said.

question: In what ways does the Israel–U.A.E. agreement benefit Christian communities facing hardship and persecution across the Middle East?
interview_answer: Help whom?

question: So are you, in practice, authorizing Putin to carry out a limited invasion of that nation?
interview_answer: I’m not going to answer that.

question: Is Kamala Harris constitutionally eligible to serve as Vice President or President, given that she was born in Oakland, California?
interview_answer: I really have no involvement in that matter. I’ve seen a few things written about it, and I understand a very smart attorney put out a piece on the subject. But I’m not familiar with the details, and frankly, it’s not an issue I’m going to get into.

question: Can you specify what is supposedly un-American about federal training initiatives designed to boost diversity or highlight existing prejudices?
interview_answer: Look, we’re going to put out a full report on that. Yes, I let those people go; they’re all out. And honestly, the whole thing was a shameful situation. We’ll release a very extensive report that you’re really going to like. Okay, next, go ahead.

question: How does the Pope’s recognition relate to achieving a two-state solution?
interview_answer:
All right, let me start here. First, I want to applaud the Senate for taking steps toward granting me the authority not only to negotiate a smart, modern, pro-growth trade agreement with key partners in the Asia-Pacific region, and possibly in Europe as well, but also to equip me with the means to actually enforce those agreements—something that has too often been neglected in the past. So I’m grateful to all the Senators who voted to advance that authority, or at least to open the debate so we can move that process along. For those who voted against it, I intend to keep talking with them, laying out the facts they need to feel assured that, even though serious and legitimate concerns exist about how some prior trade agreements were handled, what we are pursuing now is, in my judgment, the best path forward—not just for large U.S. corporations, but for small and mid-sized firms, and, at the core, for American workers themselves.

I would never advocate any trade deal that I did not firmly believe would, when all is said and done, result in more jobs being created here at home and more pathways into the middle class—better wages and higher incomes for Americans—because that has been my central priority from the day I took office.

Now, when it comes to the disagreement between Senator Warren and me, it’s important to be clear: this has never been about personalities. I realize the press enjoys speculating about tensions when two close allies clash over a specific policy, but some of my closest friends in the Senate and the House—people who backed me early on—disagree with me on this issue. And I get why. Many of them, like I did, came of age watching factories close and jobs move overseas. They are, like me, wary about whether labor and environmental commitments in trade pacts are truly enforced. They share my worries about whether trade is actually fair, not just free.

And they share my deep anxiety over wider trends—global and domestic—where technological change and globalization help those at the very top do exceptionally well, while middle-class wages stagnate and people working hard to climb into the middle class find it tougher and tougher.

These are people whose values mirror mine. I noticed the release of a progressive platform defining what it means to be progressive, drafted by some of these same friends. And when I looked at it, it was essentially my agenda—except on trade. That’s the one place we part ways. At bottom, that’s a straightforward policy difference based on how we each evaluate what will best serve our constituents and the country.

My strong conviction is that, despite real problems with earlier trade agreements, the United States is better off taking the lead in setting high-standard, enforceable rules on issues like child labor, deforestation, environmental harm, wildlife trafficking, and intellectual property for what will be the largest and fastest-growing markets in the world. If we don’t set those standards, China and others will. That would undercut our companies and ultimately hurt our workers.

On the concern about job flight, it’s my view, grounded in the data, that any U.S. company currently intent on chasing low wages abroad already has ample ability to do so under the existing system. What we have a chance to do instead is encourage companies to build and expand here in America.

We’re already seeing some of that. That’s part of why I went to visit Nike. I know Nike has a long record of producing footwear using low-cost labor in parts of the Asia-Pacific, and that has taken a toll on American manufacturing jobs in that sector over the last three decades. But now, with advances in technology and production methods, Nike is saying it could bring 10,000 jobs back to the United States as we move up the value chain and produce in new ways. That’s a real opportunity. Still, to fully capitalize on it, we need to be able to sell our goods in those overseas markets.

So when I disagree with some of my progressive allies, what I argue is that if we really want to tackle the problems we all care about, our attention should be on the domestic policies we already agree on: a strong minimum wage; robust worker training and apprenticeship programs; major infrastructure projects that put people to work; stronger protections for collective bargaining and workers’ voices; tougher enforcement on overtime rules; guaranteeing paid sick leave; and an honest budget debate that doesn’t gut future-oriented investments just to preserve tax loopholes for corporations that aren’t delivering real economic value.

Those are the tools that will address the inequality and wage stagnation they’re rightly worried about. Blocking a trade agreement will not, especially when they’re the first to admit that the current trade framework is already stacked against U.S. workers. If today’s rules are bad for us, I don’t see how freezing them in place helps American workers. That logic doesn’t hold up.

I fully support strong enforcement mechanisms in the trade package under discussion. I’ve also voiced concerns about the way the currency provisions are currently drafted. I need to sit down with Senator Schumer, Senator Brown, and others to find language that doesn’t accidentally undercut our own ability to manage U.S. monetary policy.

And as for the rest of your question, I have to be candid: I honestly don’t recall what the other part was, so I’m not going to try to address it here.

question: If chemical weapons were deployed in Ukraine, would that automatically lead NATO to launch a military response?
interview_answer: There would be a corresponding reaction, but as for whether NATO would go that far militarily, that’s a choice we’d only make at that moment.

question: How does the President respond to the claim by Mitt Romney’s economic adviser that his proposals to Europe show a misunderstanding of the roots of the crisis and suffer from the same defects as his own economic program at home?
interview_answer:
To begin with, when it comes to Mr. Romney’s advisers, I’m going to refer you to Mr. Romney for any discussion of what his advisers are saying. Let me just underline that we have only one sitting President and one governing administration at any given time, and, historically, the idea has been that partisan fights in the United States should not spill over into our conduct abroad. I’d also note that he may not actually know what we’ve been advising the German government to do. Often there’s a tendency back in Washington to project domestic ideological debates onto what is, in reality, a very complex European situation.

What’s happening in Europe stems from multiple sources. In some countries you’ve seen irresponsible fiscal behavior and high levels of public debt. In others, like Spain, the difficulties grew out of a housing bubble and private‑sector imbalances that weren’t primarily about government borrowing. At this stage, every European government understands that they need pro‑growth policies that fit alongside their fiscal consolidation efforts. And, for that matter, that’s precisely the balance I believe the United States has to strike as well. The core of the proposal I laid out back in September was about how we boost growth and employment in the near term while making sure we have a credible path to bringing down our deficits and debt over the medium and long haul.

I think that’s the right overall formula, not just for us but more broadly. You mentioned you had another question—could you remind me what it was?

question: What specific form of boycott are you proposing?
interview_answer: Well, I really can’t say. That’s for people themselves to decide. I’m not going to spell it out. I don’t think it’s something I should be pushing. If folks want to express their political views, if they want the freedom not to go along with whatever everyone else is doing—if they want to wear a MAGA cap or a Blue Lives Matter hat—you understand Blue Lives Matter refers to our police, men and women—that kind of thing should never be punished. It’s a disgrace. They’re throwing their weight around against ordinary people who just want to wear what they believe in. So if, because of that, people choose not to shop there, I’d absolutely support their right to do that. And remember, they’ll find work; we’ve just had record job growth, the best numbers our country’s ever seen, so they can get other great jobs. What happened here is shameful. Okay, next question, back there.

question: How do you foresee future ties between the U.S. and North Korea compared with those between the U.S. and Vietnam?
interview_answer: You’ll need to ask that again.

question: Do you believe that home prices are going to keep dropping?
interview_answer:
David, let me start by saying I’m smart enough to recognize that I’m not a professional economist, and I’m going to leave precise predictions and forecasts about the economy to the people whose job it is to make those forecasts. If you ask them, you’ll probably hear a lot of “on the one hand, on the other hand” in their answers.

What I concentrate on instead are the basic strengths of our economy. I’m confident that people ultimately act rationally when they have solid information in front of them. And the underlying fundamentals of our economy remain strong. I’ve already listed some of them. We’re seeing solid job growth; real, after‑tax incomes are increasing; inflation is staying low. On top of that, the world economy is performing well, which has boosted our exports and contributed to a healthy second‑quarter growth rate of 3.4 percent.

You also have to consider how much liquidity is out there in the financial system. In plain English, is there enough available capital so that markets can adjust and correct themselves? And from what I’m being told, there is sufficient liquidity in the system to allow those corrections to take place.

Now, in terms of helping homeowners—because obviously, when someone loses their house, that’s a situation that deserves a great deal of compassion—let me be clear on one point. I’m not exactly sure how you’re defining the word “bailout.” If you’re talking about direct cash payments to homeowners, then no, I do not favor that approach. If instead you mean giving institutions like the FHA more flexibility so they can help families refinance and stay in their homes, then yes, that’s something I do support.

One thing that absolutely has to improve is transparency in financial documents. Too many people have signed loan papers without really understanding what they were agreeing to. We need more financial literacy—that’s probably the best way to describe it. A lot of very hard‑working Americans entered into mortgages without fully grasping the terms. So I do think the Government has a legitimate role in expanding financial education efforts. We are pursuing that, and we’ve allocated funds in the budget for those initiatives.

Okay, let’s move on—Mike [Michael Emanuel, FOX News].

question: If Israel hadn’t agreed to pause annexation, could this agreement still have happened?
interview_answer: I’m sorry, what was that? Could you repeat it more clearly?

question: Are you, personally, going to step in and talk with Republican members of Congress who oppose easing the embargo and funding a new Embassy to try to stop that money?
interview_answer: Lesley, I’m going to stop you right there. We’re spending too much time on this and I’m not going to get into it.

question: Are you open to cutting off their federal funds?
interview_answer: I'm not going to say that. They'll cooperate. Things will work out. I expect we'll have a strong working relationship. They rely on the Federal Government not just for money—and I'm not saying we're going to pull that—but also for guidance. They may need equipment that only we can provide. We’ve built up a massive reserve that we’re now finalizing. We’re in excellent shape. When I came in, there was basically nothing. And to be fair to earlier administrations, no one imagined something like this would occur, but it did. No, the Governors will show a great deal of respect for the office of the President. This isn’t about me personally; it’s about the institution. The Presidency carries enormous weight in what we’re doing. You can discuss the Constitution, federalism, all of that, but from a management perspective, the best approach is to let Governors handle their own States and come to us if they run into problems, and we’ll support them. Yes, John.

question: In what specific situations would you contemplate offering Edward Snowden a plea bargain or granting him amnesty?
interview_answer:
I need to tread carefully here, Major, because Mr. Snowden is currently under indictment; he’s facing criminal charges. That puts this squarely in the hands of the Attorney General and, ultimately, a judge and jury. So I am not in a position to opine directly on his individual case right now. Let me—let me see if I can speak to the broader intent behind your question, even though I can’t address the particulars.

I’ve previously said—and I still think—that this is a conversation the country needed to have. I’ve also consistently said that the way these revelations were made public has harmed the United States and undermined our intelligence capabilities. And I believe there was a way to hold this debate without inflicting that kind of damage.

To give just one concrete illustration: despite our flaws, the United States is a nation governed by the rule of law, with a strong commitment to privacy, civil liberties, and our Constitution. Yet because of these leaks, governments that actually engage in the very abuses Mr. Snowden claims to be concerned about—spying on their own people, going after political opponents, intimidating and muzzling the press—can now stand off to the side and pretend that the U.S. is the main offender on surveillance and intelligence practices. That’s a highly misleading picture of reality.

So while I think this debate has been both important and overdue, we also have to recognize that it has caused needless harm to U.S. intelligence operations and to our diplomacy. But as for the concrete details of Mr. Snowden’s legal situation, I am going to leave any public judgments to the courts and to the Attorney General, and I’m not going to address those specifics myself.

question: Roughly what share of those individuals do you think actually merit asylum in the United States?
interview_answer:
I’m not going to start assigning specific percentages here. What I will say is that we are a country built on laws, and that means we must have enforceable borders. Without borders, we simply don’t have a nation. I’ve been watching this caravan for weeks as it moved north, and Mexico, by the way, has very strict immigration rules—extremely strict. It began with well over a thousand people, and now it’s reportedly down to around a hundred, traveling the length of Mexico. People forget how large Mexico is, but the group shrank a lot on the way.

Now at our own border we’re dealing with some of the weakest immigration laws anywhere. You can look around the globe—other countries are amazed by how bad our laws are. We’re doing everything we can within those limits, but we need Congress to act, and we need them to act fast. First, we need a wall. You can see that clearly right now. Even though the current barrier in that area isn’t the best design and a small number of people in excellent physical condition can scale it, we have other sections that are much harder to get over.

Without that barrier, you’d see thousands of people streaming into the United States. A wall is essential. We also need strong border security, and we’re moving in that direction. As you know, we’re sending a large number of National Guard troops to the border—most are already deployed—and that’s making a real difference. But the laws themselves have to change. This catch‑and‑release system is absurd: if they step onto our territory, you detain them and then release them into the country. That’s unacceptable. So what we need is a change in the law, not a discussion of percentages.

question: Are you suggesting that a limited Russian move into parts of Ukraine would not trigger the sanctions you have warned about?
interview_answer: That’s a fair question.

question: Do you feel personally betrayed by Scott McClellan’s critique of the Iraq war?
interview_answer: I haven’t commented on Scott’s book, and I’m not going to comment on it now. What I will say is that people should pay close attention to what Ambassador Crocker and General Petraeus are saying. It’s very easy to let the political battles back home override the sober assessments of the commanders on the ground. That’s exactly why I firmly opposed an imposed timetable for withdrawal. That kind of schedule is arbitrary—it says, in effect, that decisions about how to fight a war will be made in Congress based on polling and partisan maneuvering, and then forced on our forces, rather than relying on our generals, our diplomats, and the Iraqi government itself. The Iraqis have asked us to be there, and they share our objective of bringing our combat forces home when conditions allow. In fact, that’s what has been happening. Our policy has been ‘return on success,’ and our troops are coming back according to the progress we’ve seen. So I would urge any member of Congress who goes over there to really listen to what’s happening on the ground and recognize that the right path forward is to heed those who are actually there. I know that’s difficult for some in Washington, because there’s enormous political pressure—groups like MoveOn.org are constantly hammering these candidates, and it’s tough to separate yourself from that. That’s why I’m pleased so many elected officials are visiting Iraq; they’ll gain a perspective they cannot get just from reading your fine newspapers or watching television news. John, go ahead. [John McKinnon, Wall Street Journal].

question: Potential talks on allowing inspections of North Korea’s nuclear facilities during negotiations.
interview_answer: You’ll need to raise your voice, I can’t hear you. And which outlet are you with? Which outlet are you with?

question: What additional steps are currently on the table?
interview_answer: So I'm not going to get into that specifically, but I will tell you this: I believe the sanctions are effective. And of course the military option would be effective too, but that’s a very tough way to prevail. Still, we win. No one can defeat us on the battlefield. No country is even in the same league. What we’ve done for our armed forces over the last three years is remarkable. All built here in the United States, by the way. It’s really remarkable. Our nuclear arsenal had become very outdated. They hadn’t invested in it. Now we’ve brought it back, put it, as we like to say, in absolutely top‑top condition. Really top‑top. We have brand‑new systems and fully overhauled systems, and it’s extraordinary. And we should all hope we never have to use any of it. We should never need to use it. Our overall military is in outstanding condition. And we have a terrific leader, as you know, coming in as Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff. Joe Dunford has been outstanding, a truly great man and a friend of mine. But General Milley is coming in next. We’ll be marking that with a bit of a celebration for Joe and for Mark—for everyone involved. And as you know, our Secretary of Defense, Mark Esper, has just stepped in. He hasn’t been in the job long, but he’s got tremendous drive. He understands this. It’s what he’s done his whole life, from the day he graduated—or really from the day he entered—West Point, where he was a top student and so on. So we’ve got extraordinary people. And Steve Mnuchin is here as well. We rolled out the sanctions today, and I think, Steve, they’re probably the toughest ever imposed on any nation. We will certainly never do anything like that to Australia, I can promise you. [] Glad to hear it.

question: Do you believe Israel’s decision in this case was justified?
interview_answer: David, I’m not going to weigh in on that issue or give the kind of comment you’re asking for.

question: How is that relevant?
interview_answer: People are dying all over the globe. And frankly, that's something you should take up with China. Don't direct that at me; put that question to China, all right? When you pose it to them, you might hear a very surprising response. Yes, you there in the back.

question: Will Meng Wanzhou’s case have to be dealt with last?
interview_answer: Wait, in relation to what? According to which timeline?

question: What is your reaction to the fact that some opponents are likening the controversies this week to the scandals that took place during the Nixon era?
interview_answer:
I’m going to leave it to you and the historians to make those kinds of comparisons, and people can look back at the record and reach their own judgments. What I’m focused on is that if something in the Federal Government isn’t working correctly, we fix it. That’s the duty I bear, and that’s what we’re going to carry out. That applies to the IRS, where we have to be sure the law is enforced exactly as Congress meant it to be. It also applies to the safety of our diplomats, which is why we’ll have to partner with Congress to ensure they have the resources they need in some very dangerous places.

Now, when it comes to the Department of Justice, I am not going to discuss a particular, active matter. I will not comment on a specific ongoing case. What I can address is the broader balance we have to maintain. Unauthorized disclosures involving national security can endanger people. They can endanger the men and women in uniform whom I have ordered into combat. They can endanger intelligence professionals operating in highly sensitive, easily compromised environments.

Our national security depends on those individuals being able to do their jobs with the assurance that their government stands behind them, that they’re not being abandoned and placed in even greater peril than they already face. I make no apologies for being concerned, as Commander in Chief, about information that could jeopardize their missions or cost them their lives, and I don’t think the American people would expect me to be indifferent to that.

At the same time, we are a democracy in which a free press, free speech, and the open circulation of information are essential to holding me and the rest of the Government accountable and to making our system work. The reason I entered public life in the first place is that I have a deep commitment to that democratic process.

So the purpose of the media shield legislation—which was crafted and largely supported by, for example, the Washington Post editorial board and by many prosecutors—was to find an appropriate way to strike that balance. And if this particular matter—which, again, we don’t yet have every fact about—has renewed interest in how best to maintain that balance, then this is an appropriate moment to revisit that bill. That’s a useful debate to have, and it matters.

But it’s also crucial to remember that when we raise concerns about leaks at a time when we still have more than 60,000 troops in Afghanistan and numerous intelligence officers deployed worldwide in hazardous posts—some of them as dangerous as the facility in Benghazi—part of my obligation is to protect their work, even as we continue to provide the public with the information it needs to stay informed and to hold my office to account. I am not, however, going to get into the details of that particular case or the Nixon comparison you mentioned.

question: Hello, sir, Emily Goodwin from DailyMail.com. I’d like to ask: in your view, was justice done in the Harvey Weinstein trial?
interview_answer:
I was never a supporter of Harvey Weinstein, as everyone knows. In fact, he publicly vowed to do everything he could to stop me from winning the election. We all saw how that turned out, didn’t we? I’ve always disliked him, never cared for him at all. I really don’t know the specifics of the trial because, as you’re aware, I’ve been overseas. I’ve been on the road and in back‑to‑back meetings almost nonstop, so I really haven’t had the chance to follow it in any real detail.

I just was not his fan. I only knew him slightly, not well. I knew who he was because he was a New York figure. Definitely not someone I felt any fondness for.

What I can say is this: the people who embraced him were the Democrats. Michelle Obama thought he was wonderful. Wonderful. Hillary Clinton was a big fan too. And he poured huge sums of money into Democratic campaigns. So my question is: are Democrats going to return that money? Because he gave them a fortune. It’s unfortunate, but that’s how it all happened. Yes, Peter, go ahead.

question: How do you align national sovereignty with the doctrine of responsibility to protect?
interview_answer: Look, I’m not going to explain how to square that with the responsibility to protect—what does that even mean now?

question: If Putin proceeds with an invasion of Ukraine, will the United States return to a Cold War–style containment strategy?
interview_answer:
My view is, first, that he still does not actually want an all-out war. Second, do I believe he’s going to probe the West, push the United States and NATO as hard as he thinks he can? Yes, I do. But I also believe he’ll face a very high and painful cost for that, a cost he’s underestimating right now, and I think he will come to regret choosing that path.

Now, as to whether—or, let me put it this way—how do I talk about this in an open forum? I believe he sees himself wrestling with what he considers the greatest calamity to befall Mother Russia: the fall of the Berlin Wall, the collapse of the Soviet empire, the loss of the near abroad, and so on. The USSR was torn apart.

Yet look at what he still controls: a country spanning eight time zones, a warming permafrost that likely won’t refreeze on its own, considerable oil and gas resources, and a leadership trying to situate Russia somewhere between China and the West.

So I’m not convinced—David, I’m really not convinced—that even he is fully decided on his next move. My hunch is he will take some action; he’s under pressure to do something.

And I’ve made it clear to him that there are two main assurances he’s seeking. First, that Ukraine will never be admitted to NATO. Second, that NATO—or anyone, really—won’t deploy strategic weapons in Ukraine. On that second point, there might be some room for an arrangement, depending on what he does along Russia’s borders in Europe.

On the first point, we’re bound by a series of international and European agreements that affirm each nation’s right to choose its own alliances. In any event, Ukraine’s near-term prospects for NATO membership are quite low; there’s still substantial work to do on democratic reforms and other issues, and it’s unclear whether key Western allies would currently support bringing Ukraine in.

So there is space to negotiate if he wants it. But as for how far I’m prepared to go in describing our response or future posture, I’m not going to spell that out here. As usual, I expect his actions will end up doing him significant harm—and I’ll stop there rather than go into more detail about our specific strategy.

question: Was he carrying a weapon, Mr. President?
interview_answer: As far as I’m aware, yes, but I’m not sure. You’ll need to put that question to them.

question: Mr. President, Tom Bradby from ITV News. Many people in Britain believe that your country’s insistence on seeking the extradition of computer hacker Gary McKinnon, who has Asperger’s, is excessive and unduly severe. Do you believe the moment has come to show some mercy in his case and ease your stance?
interview_answer:
Please, go ahead. This is an issue that came up in our talks. As you know, there is an active dialogue between the United States and the United Kingdom on this matter, and I am not going to say anything that might interfere with or bias those ongoing discussions. We fully recognize that Gary McKinnon is alleged to have committed a very serious offense by breaching sensitive government systems, and no one is suggesting that such conduct should be treated as trivial. I’ve spoken with our Ambassador here in the U.K., and I raised the case with the Prime Minister today, and my hope is that a constructive path forward will be identified.

One of the broader points David and I covered is the growing difficulty all our countries face because of the Internet, and the need for us to work very closely together on cybersecurity. We touched on the fact that, while old‑fashioned espionage and cold‑war style intelligence gathering may still occur, the real and expanding vulnerability now lies in these kinds of intrusions into our information infrastructure. So we regard this as a grave concern, and I know the British government does as well.

Beyond that, we have a longstanding principle in the United States that the President does not personally intervene in individual prosecutorial or extradition decisions. So I will not be directing an outcome here. What I expect is that my administration will adhere to the law, and at the same time coordinate closely with a partner that, as we’ve said, is unmatched in the depth of its cooperation with us. I am confident this will be settled in a manner that reflects how serious these issues are, while also reflecting the strength of our partnership and our ability to arrive at an appropriate resolution.

All right, thank you all very much. Thanks again.

question: At your first press briefing 10 months back, I asked whether you had any tools other than legislation to safeguard voting rights. Now that those bills seem thoroughly blocked, can you now spell out the concrete strategy you’ll use to defend voting rights?
interview_answer:
I’m not ready to walk through that in a detailed way, particularly regarding any executive actions I might take or other specific steps I could pursue. What I can say is this: we’ve already substantially increased the number of Justice Department officials tasked with pushing back against what we view as unconstitutional attempts by Republicans to rig elections and undermine the results.

We’ve also started organizing in new ways, reaching beyond the traditional civil rights networks to broader communities, to explain to Americans what’s unfolding and what it would mean if these measures actually move ahead.
