If, a while ago—speaking to the broader public, not just you personally—I had warned that elections could be subverted by trying to pick alternate slates of electors after the fact, most people would have thought that sounded far-fetched. I taught constitutional law for years—a three‑credit course on separation of powers on Saturday mornings while I was in the Senate—and I still never imagined we’d be seriously discussing efforts like the last one, where they tried to send different electors to state legislatures to claim I lost and a Republican actually won. I doubt many people believed something like that could occur in 21st‑century America, but it did.

So, Nancy, what I’m really saying is that there are several avenues available to us, but I’m not going to get into the detailed game plan right now. I also believe we’ll still manage to enact major portions of the legislation—maybe not the entire package immediately—but enough to secure a substantial part of the John Lewis bill and the broader fair elections measures.

question: Did he receive an invitation?
interview_answer: I honestly don’t know. I’m not the one who handles news conference invitations. What I can say is they coordinate very closely and have a strong working relationship, and that’s what really matters.

question: Can you tell us about the earlier phone conversation you had with Kim Jong Un?
interview_answer: We did speak, yes, but I'm not going to go into that. What I can say is that we've had many talks overall, and we've built very significant lines of communication at Mike’s level and at other levels as well. In fact, a couple of representatives from North Korea are here with us right now, as you know; there are also a few more of them toward the back of the room. So when we moved toward this final arrangement, the key point is that we weren’t starting from scratch. We came in with a very strong rapport and a great deal of understanding, and I believe that’s why we were able to reach this outcome. Now I’m going to wrap up. I don’t know about you all, but it’s been a long stretch without much rest for me. So we can relax briefly, and then the hard work starts again. I’m grateful to everyone for being here. I trust we’ve addressed your questions. Thank you very much, and really, congratulations to all of you, because this is, in my view, a truly major moment in world history. And to be honest with myself, I have to add that I want to see it fully carried through. So Mike and the entire team need to get back to work and finish the job, because if you don’t push the ball over the goal line, it doesn’t count enough. All right, thank you, and again, congratulations to everyone here. Thank you very much; I appreciate it. Thank you.

question: Does the election of two Muslim women to the House, including one who wears a veil, represent a rejection of a particular message?
interview_answer: I have no idea what you’re asking me. Come again?

question: Deliberations and plans regarding advance-use pardons
interview_answer: I’m not going to talk about pardons during this briefing.

question: When exactly will that happen, Mr. President?
interview_answer: Honestly, I have no idea.

question: Explanation of the President’s remarks with French President Nicolas Sarkozy about Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu
interview_answer:
Let me start by noting that Chuck is the only one who’s managed to squeeze in two questions so far. So when I wrap up here, whoever thought they were next in line—[]—I’m just giving you a hard time, Chuck.

On the supercommittee: In August, we agreed to lock in a trillion dollars in deficit reduction over the next decade, largely from discretionary spending. But we also made clear that to put our finances on a sounder footing, we needed at least another $1.2 trillion in savings—I actually argued we should go beyond that. The point of the sequester was to ensure both parties felt real pressure to move off of entrenched positions and do what was necessary for the country.

Since then, they’ve talked a lot, but it still seems like folks are clinging to rigid stances instead of actually fixing the problem.

I’ve put forward a very detailed plan that delivers more than $3 trillion in deficit reduction. It’s the kind of balanced approach the American people favor. It says everything is on the table. We need more discretionary cuts, like those we’ve already enacted. We need reductions in nondefense spending. We need responsible trims in defense. We have to examine entitlement programs. We must lower health care costs. And we will require additional revenues.

And when we talk about revenue, if we have to raise more money, it’s just common sense to begin by asking the wealthiest Americans to contribute a bit more, instead of first demanding that seniors, for example, shoulder big new costs for Medicare.

This is essentially the same framework I presented to Speaker Boehner back in August. It’s the same type of balanced solution that every bipartisan or independent commission that’s studied this has recommended. Yet it feels like people keep trying to fiddle with the arithmetic to get a different result.

But the basic math doesn’t change. If you want a balanced plan that doesn’t eviscerate Medicare and Medicaid, that doesn’t block us from investing in education, basic science, research—all the things we’ve been talking about here at APEC that every leader recognizes as critical to long-term growth—then sensible spending cuts must be paired with new revenue.

My expectation is that, over the coming days, the supercommittee’s leadership will finally accept that and do what’s required, because the numbers won’t magically rearrange themselves. There’s no secret formula here. There are no magic beans you can scatter that suddenly cause dollars to grow on trees. We just have to act responsibly. And I’m ready to sign a bill that is balanced and that actually resolves this.

Let me add one more point: When I sit down with other heads of government, what’s striking—whether in Europe or here in Asia—is that the structural reforms they’re undertaking, both in terms of revenues and public pensions, go far beyond what we need to do to get our books in order.

We are not talking about drastic changes to American life. We’re talking about spreading sacrifice more evenly so it’s fair, so people don’t feel that once again the well-connected, those with lobbyists and special interests, get to duck the hard choices while middle-class families already under strain carry the load. If other countries can make these adjustments, we can too, and we can do it in a measured, responsible way.

I’m not going to say whether I would veto some hypothetical piece of legislation until an actual bill is in front of me, because I still think there’s a chance for that light-bulb moment when everyone realizes: OK, here’s what we have to do.

As for the live microphone incident in France, I am not going to discuss the private conversations I have with individual foreign leaders, but I will say this: The main issue I raised with President Sarkozy in that meeting was my deep disappointment that France voted to admit the Palestinians to UNESCO, fully aware that under U.S. law that meant we’d have to cut off our contributions to UNESCO, and after I had repeatedly made the case that the only way to resolve the Israeli-Palestinian conflict is through direct negotiations between the two sides—that trying to go around that process via the United Nations is not going to succeed.

So I spoke to President Sarkozy in very direct and firm terms about that matter. And that’s entirely in line with what I’ve been saying, in public and in private, to everyone over the past several months.

Ed Henry, FOX News.

question: Are you actually able to cooperate with them?
interview_answer: Look, I’m going to have to ask you to wait on that one—I’d prefer to give you my answer in about seven years, all right? As in, not now. I do want to be able to, because I like the idea of working across party lines. But at the moment, all they’re doing is blocking everything. And remember, they’re opposing major tax reductions that will strengthen our economy and our competitiveness. That’s not exactly a winning campaign message, and I think some Democrats will end up supporting us when it’s time to vote on those tax cuts.

question: When you spoke with President Castro, did you raise Fidel Castro or bring him up in your conversation?
interview_answer: Okay, Lesley, I’m going to stop you right there. I’m not getting into that; it’s using up too much time.

question: What kind of weapon was it?
interview_answer: I’m not aware of that, no.

question: Were you briefed on any confirmed coronavirus infections at the White House today?
interview_answer: You mean today?

question: When is Kosovo set to become independent?
interview_answer: I’ll pass.

question: Does the Speaker actually have a firm grip on his caucus?
interview_answer: That’s something you really need to ask the Speaker himself; it’s not something I’m going to answer for him. In my dealings with John Boehner, I’ve had positive experiences. I believe he’s a decent person who’s trying to serve the country well. But, as Chip suggested, the political forces that elevated him to the Speakership may have been advantageous in a midterm race, yet they make the work of governing very difficult. More broadly, the Republican conference needs to come to terms with the fact that our democracy functions best when we actually listen to one another, extend good faith, assume the other side’s patriotism and sincerity, and are prepared to strike reasonable compromises to tackle major challenges. And there are still some members of that caucus who haven’t fully embraced that idea.

question: Will you identify which five Republican Senators you’re referring to?
interview_answer: Of course not. Absolutely no. Are you serious? I have to keep that confidential.

question: Is the United States willing to pledge more vaccine shipments abroad?
interview_answer:
It could take a bit longer than it has elsewhere around the globe, but the United States is going to keep at this. I think it’s possible that over the course of 2022 and into 2023 we may be in a place where we can supply another billion or so doses. But that isn’t finalized yet.

I’ve tried to be very disciplined throughout this pandemic about only telling you what I actually know and what I’m confident can be carried out. When I’ve made a specific announcement, I’ve followed through on it. What I’m not going to do is get out in front of the facts by claiming that I, or the United States, can commit to things that we haven’t actually secured or completed yet. So I’m not going to make that kind of promise here.

There was, however, a strong agreement among the G‑7 leaders that this effort doesn’t stop now; we’re going to keep working until we can meet global needs. That’s not only the morally right thing to do, it’s also essential for our own health and security. You simply cannot build a wall high enough to block new variants from coming in.

So this is going to be an ongoing effort for quite some time, and we have to be ready for future pandemics as well. That’s why we’re focused on early detection systems so we can spot a potential outbreak before it’s out of control.

We will continue as long as there are countries that still need help getting their people vaccinated. And it’s not just about handing over doses. As I’ve underscored, and as my counterparts have learned from their own efforts, this is a massive logistical undertaking. Sending a country a certain number of doses is one thing; having the personnel and infrastructure to put shots in arms is another.

That’s why we’re also helping other nations build the capacity to manufacture vaccines themselves. We’ve reached an understanding on that. India, for example, has the technical ability but has lacked enough material resources for large‑scale production, and we’re working on that front too.

So again, we’re committed to supporting vaccines worldwide and supporting countries’ own production, but I’m not going to state a specific new U.S. commitment that hasn’t been nailed down yet. Next question—Peter Alexander, NBC News.

question: How can you frame this as a worldwide contest when Americans are still dying every day and the number of new cases here keeps rising daily?
interview_answer: People are dying all over the globe. Maybe that’s something you should be taking up with China. Don’t put that question to me; put it to China, all right? When you pose it to them, you might hear a very unexpected response. Yes, you there in the back.

question: In your view, what measures ought to be taken against Russia over Alexei Navalny’s poisoning?
interview_answer:
I think, first of all, we’d have to treat the matter very seriously, assuming it happened the way it’s being reported, and I’m sure we will. No one has been more firm with Russia than I have. At the same time, I’ve built good relationships all over the world. I have a good relationship with North Korea, for example. Remember, everyone said we were headed to war with North Korea? If Hillary had won, you’d be in a war with North Korea right now, and it would be a disastrous, extremely deadly conflict—millions of lives lost. Instead, under my leadership, we’ve avoided that war. People said that if I won the election, we’d be at war within a week; the reality has been exactly the opposite.

We’re making strong progress in Afghanistan, reducing troop levels to very small numbers, and we’re seeing other important developments. We’ve also launched a historic peace initiative in the Middle East with the U.A.E. and Israel. It’s been received in an incredibly positive way—one of the most well-received efforts that many people can remember. And more nations are going to come on board soon; they’re already saying they want to join. Many of them are asking why they weren’t included from day one, because they were eager to participate.

We have a lot underway. I was the one who brought major attention to Nord Stream 2. I asked why Germany is paying Russia massive sums for energy while we’re expected to defend Germany from that same country. It doesn’t make sense. And we’ve imposed sanctions and taken other steps.

At the same time, with Russia, we’re currently in talks on a nuclear nonproliferation agreement, which is extremely significant. To me, that’s the most critical issue. Some people point to climate change; I don’t. I believe this is far more important. We’re the largest nuclear power; Russia is number two. China is third—still well behind but expanding quickly. Eventually, we’ll bring China into these discussions, but for now we’re making strong headway with Russia on nuclear nonproliferation.

So, I can’t tell you precisely what happened there. It’s a horrible situation, very sad, and it should never occur. We don’t yet have conclusive evidence, and I’m not going to lay out any specific actions until we do, but I will review it.

question: Who exactly are U.S. oil producers?
interview_answer: Sorry, could you remind me what you just asked?

question: In what ways should criminal justice reform be part of addressing the remaining racial disparities in both state and federal governance?
interview_answer: That’s a lot to unpack in one question, April. [] Tell you what—

question: Should you have done more?
interview_answer: More regarding what?

question: If no new deal is reached, are you willing to resort to military action to curb Iran’s nuclear program?
interview_answer: I’m not going to say whether I would or wouldn’t use military force; that’s not something I’m going to get into. But I will say this: Iran will not be allowed to obtain nuclear weapons. That much I guarantee you. They are not going to have nuclear weapons. You can count on that. Alright. Next.

question: Does it have to be signed?
interview_answer: Well, that actually brings up an interesting point about whether there even is a formal signature in this case, because, as I mentioned, it’s not a legally enforceable document. I’m not sure what the formal procedures are. What I can say is that this represents a serious pledge on our part as the United States, and we view it as very significant. Okay, thank you all.

question: Is the upcoming meeting going to be soon?
interview_answer: Honestly, I’m not able to say. It could happen in the near future, or it could be quite a while from now. I’m not in a position to tell you. I’d like it to be soon, but it might also be a long time before it happens.

question: Are you planning to head to Charlotte?
interview_answer: Anyone want to take a guess? We’ll make it public shortly.

question: Did you release that information back in February?
interview_answer: Look, take a look at the documents. China publicly said it was spreading through the air, and I heard the same thing and figured that out pretty early myself. What mattered was keeping people steady. You don’t want your president running around yelling, ‘Huge numbers are going to die,’ and throwing the whole country into chaos. And if Bob Woodward truly believed what I told him was so terrible, then he should have gone straight to officials right then so they could get ready and be warned.

question: Do you now regard Kim Jong Un as your peer?
interview_answer: What exactly do you mean?

question: Must President Zelenskyy surrender any part of Ukraine’s territory in order to secure a ceasefire with Russia?
interview_answer: On that second issue, my response is yes. That will be up to the G‑20. It—uh—that came up in our discussions today. And I brought up the idea that if that can’t happen—if Indonesia and some others don’t go along—then we ought, in my opinion, to request that both parties be permitted to take part in the sessions, and, well—essentially, to attend the G‑20 gathering as observers. Now, on the—remind me, what was your first question again?

question: Do you completely oppose having John Bolton testify?
interview_answer: Look, you're really throwing a lot at me.

question: National sovereignty as the fundamental basis of global order.
interview_answer: I’m not going to address that directly—how am I supposed to square it with the responsibility to protect, which we once emphasized so strongly?

question: What military actions would North Korea face if it fails to honor its promises?
interview_answer: I’m not going to get into that—yes, I understand what you’re asking. It’s a very difficult subject to address, and I’m not going to spell that out because I don’t want to issue threats. I don’t want to be threatening. They were made well aware of that reality. And you’ve seen what was, potentially, on the verge of happening. Remember, the Seoul area has about 28 million people. We think of New York, with 8 million, as a huge city. Seoul has 28 million—just think about that. And it sits right on the border, right next to the DMZ. It’s right there. If things had broken down, I’ve heard people talk about maybe a hundred thousand casualties. I believe you could have seen 20 or 30 million deaths. For me, taking this on is truly an honor, because I think we might have been looking at 30, 40, even 50 million lives at risk. Seoul, one of the world’s largest cities, is essentially on the front line.

question: Leaning toward withdrawing the military forces?
interview_answer: You expect me to answer that?

question: Will the President reverse the Defense Department rule that bars press coverage of returning flag-draped military coffins?
interview_answer:
Your question comes at a poignant moment. We’ve just received word that four American service members were killed in Iraq today, and, of course, our hearts and prayers are with their families. I’ve said before that when people ask me, “When did it really strike you that you’re the President?” I tell them the most sobering moment is sitting down to sign letters to the families of our fallen service members. It drives home the weight of this office and the very real human consequences of the choices I make.

Now, on the specific issue of whether to permit media coverage when the remains of our loved ones return home, we are currently examining that policy in consultation with the Department of Defense. Because that review is underway, I’m not going to give you a definitive position right now before I’ve seen the results and fully considered all the ramifications.

On Afghanistan, this is going to be an enormous test for us. Thanks to the extraordinary work of our troops and very strong diplomacy from Ambassador Crocker, we’ve just seen an election in Iraq that was conducted with relatively little violence, and you can sense that their political institutions are beginning to function in a real, substantive way.

We don’t yet have that in Afghanistan. Elections are scheduled there, but the central government is, in many respects, quite removed from the realities in outlying areas. On top of that, we see the Taliban and Al Qaida operating in the FATA and in those border regions straddling Afghanistan and Pakistan, and what’s been missing is a sustained, coordinated push to eliminate those safe havens that must be dismantled if our mission is to succeed.

So we’re conducting a comprehensive strategic review. General Petraeus, now leading CENTCOM, is carrying out his own assessment and working closely with the special envoy I’ve appointed, Richard Holbrooke, one of our most experienced diplomats, to design a truly regional strategy. Success will require much tighter integration of our military operations with diplomacy and development, and closer, more effective cooperation with our allies.

The essential point—and I want the American people to keep this in mind, because this will be hard—is that this part of the world served as the launching pad for an attack that killed 3,000 of our citizens. Just this past week, I met with relatives of those lost on 9/11, which is a powerful reminder of the price we pay when such safe havens are allowed to exist. My bottom line is that we cannot permit Al Qaida to maintain those sanctuaries. We have to act intelligently and effectively, and we have to stay at it, to ensure that those safe havens are eliminated. I don’t yet have a precise timetable for how long that will take. What I can tell you is that I will not allow Al Qaida or bin Laden to operate freely, plotting attacks on the United States.

Okay. Helene Cooper from the New York Times. Helene, where are you? There you are.

question: Your position on imprisoning political rivals and critics
interview_answer:
All right. On the refugee issue first: the attacks in Paris were just a week ago. The news cycle moves at such a rapid pace now that it can feel like events from a few days earlier are already distant. The horror in Paris has been so prominent in the coverage, and the similarities between Paris and many American cities are so obvious, that I fully understand why people are anxious and why the House moved a bill so quickly.

My expectation, however, is that now that we have a little breathing room to review this more carefully, people will recognize that individuals admitted to the United States as refugees go through the most extensive checks of anyone who comes to our shores. The system, built and refined by multiple administrations from both parties, is extremely rigorous; right now, it generally takes 18 to 24 months for a refugee to clear all the hurdles and be given permission to enter.

So even though, at first glance, the House bill might look modest, simply asking for certain certifications and not going as far as some of the more extreme ideas floated by a few Presidential candidates earlier in the week, the reality is that if you clog the system enough, you effectively stop refugee admissions altogether. If you just pile on more bureaucracy that doesn’t add real security or improve screening, but instead makes it nearly impossible to process applications, you are, in practice, shutting down the program for people who are in dire need.

When I talked about this being a betrayal of our values, I was speaking directly to statements suggesting, for instance, that Christian refugees could be admitted but Muslims excluded, or that we should be so afraid that even a 4-year-old orphan might be barred. Those of you who joined me at the refugee center yesterday and saw those children know exactly who we’re talking about. As a parent, if you looked at those kids and considered what they’ve endured, the idea that we couldn’t find a place for them anywhere in the United States runs counter to who we are.

And the encouraging thing is that most Americans—once they understand the level of vetting and the safeguards already in place—if they met those children, would say, yes, we have an obligation to help.

Now, turning to your question about Prime Minister Najib or any similar situation: I do not make public comments on investigations that are underway in the United States, and I am certainly not going to offer any remarks on investigations or legal cases that may be unfolding in another country while I am there as a guest. I am explicitly declining to weigh in on that kind of legal action.

What I did do in my discussions with Prime Minister Najib—and with many leaders across Southeast Asia, as well as in Africa, Latin America, and elsewhere—is stress the need for transparency and accountability and the necessity of tackling corruption. These are all obstacles to development; they drag nations down, they deepen poverty, and they limit growth.

Countries that deal seriously with those challenges can prosper even if they lack natural resources or easy access to trade routes. Countries that ignore them will struggle, no matter how rich they are in oil, minerals, or favorable geography.

I also underscored for Prime Minister Najib how vital a vibrant civil society and an independent press are for a country’s success. Every nation represented here is at a different point in its social and economic evolution, and we don’t insist that anyone copy the U.S. model step for step. But there are some fundamental norms that we think it’s important to promote as friends and partners.

If you look, for example, at Myanmar, which just held a landmark election after roughly four decades of military rule, it’s obvious they’re not going to transform overnight into Sweden in terms of democratic standards. Still, there’s a major shift underway, and we want to support and engage with that process.

Much of our work—the Open Government Partnership we launched through the United Nations, our bilateral initiatives to strengthen transparency, the rules and principles we’ve written into the TPP—aims to raise expectations and encourage higher standards. Some countries will advance faster, others will fall back at times, but the overall direction we’re pushing for is the same: a world where ordinary people are treated justly, where the rule of law prevails, where governments are open and answerable to their citizens, where people can speak out, and where women and minorities are not treated as second-class.

Those are deeply rooted American ideals, but I believe they are also shared human values.

Thank you all very much. Let’s wrap it up for today.

question: What is your position regarding compulsory busing mandated by the federal government?
interview_answer: Let me start by saying, before I touch that topic, I really think she was overrated. He clearly didn’t perform well, and maybe the facts weren’t working in his favor, but she still got more praise than she deserved for her role there. It just wasn’t that remarkable. And frankly, I believe he—Biden—took more criticism than was warranted. In my view he was treated more harshly than he should have been. Now, on the specific question you asked, I’m not going to lay out my stance today. In roughly a month we’ll roll out some policies that will address this in a way many people will find very interesting and unexpected. All right, Jennifer from Bloomberg, what’s your question?

question: What concrete steps do you take to guarantee North Korea gives up its nukes?
interview_answer:
Look, can anyone truly guarantee anything? Can I guarantee you’ll manage to sit down properly the next time you take a seat? You simply can’t guarantee outcomes like that. What I can tell you is, they’re interested in reaching an agreement. That’s my field. My entire career has been about making deals. I’ve been very successful at it, that’s what I do. And I can tell when someone is serious about negotiating and when they’re not. Many politicians can’t do that; it’s not their strength, but it is mine.

Honestly, this probably could have been handled more easily years ago. But I know—very strongly, based on my instincts, my skill, my talent—that they’re looking to strike a deal. And getting a deal done would be terrific for the world. It’s also very good for China, because I can’t see China being thrilled about having nuclear weapons right on its doorstep. They were very cooperative here. So I believe he wants an agreement. Can anyone be absolutely sure? No. But we’ll find out soon enough as the talks keep moving forward. All right? Thanks a lot. Next question.

question: So, are these sanctions failing?
interview_answer:
At this stage, we simply can’t say definitively whether the sanctions are succeeding. What we can say is that they are having the intended effect of putting massive stress on Russia’s economy. That’s not just my view; if you look at the markets, at the scale of capital outflows, at the forecasts for Russia’s economic growth, you see an economy that has essentially stalled. Estimates suggest capital flight on the order of $100 to $200 billion. Foreign investors are largely staying away; you’re not seeing new investment pouring into Russia.

These measures have forced President Putin to confront a choice: either he pursues a diplomatic, peaceful settlement in eastern Ukraine—accepting that Ukraine is a sovereign state and that the Ukrainian people have the right to decide their own future—or he stays on his current path, in which case he will continue to damage Russia’s economy and, ultimately, his own citizens’ welfare over time.

From that perspective, we are doing exactly what our policy set out to do, and we’re glad that our European partners and other countries around the world have joined us in this effort.

But having said all of that, the core conflict is not yet resolved. There is still active combat in eastern Ukraine. Civilians are still being killed. We’ve already seen the tragic consequences in the downing of the Malaysian airliner.

The faster we return to serious negotiations that ensure all Ukrainians have a voice, can work through political channels, are properly represented, and that the reforms already proposed by the government in Kyiv—protections for Russian speakers, decentralization of power—are fully implemented, and the faster President Putin accepts that Ukraine is an independent nation, only then will we be able to say the underlying problem has really been settled. Until that happens, I can’t give you a final judgment on whether sanctions have ‘worked’ in the sense you’re asking, but they are operating exactly as they were designed to in the meantime.

question: Schedule for ultimately pulling troops out of Afghanistan
interview_answer:
This question comes at a very poignant moment. We’ve just received word that four American service members were killed today in Iraq, and of course our hearts and prayers are with their loved ones. I’ve mentioned before that people often ask me when it really sank in that I had become President. I tell them the most sobering moment is signing condolence letters to the families of our fallen men and women in uniform. It drives home the weight of this office and the real‑world impact of the choices I make.

On the issue of whether to allow broader media coverage of the return of fallen soldiers, we are actively reviewing that policy in close consultation with the Department of Defense. Because that process is ongoing, I am not going to give you a definitive response on that today, before I’ve thoroughly considered the review and grasped all of its implications.

Turning to Afghanistan, this will be a significant and difficult undertaking. Thanks to the tremendous efforts of our troops and some very skillful diplomacy by Ambassador Crocker in Iraq, we’ve just witnessed elections there that were carried out with relatively little violence, and there is a sense that Iraq’s political institutions are beginning to operate in a substantive way.

We don’t yet see that kind of situation in Afghanistan. Elections are on the horizon, but in practice the central government appears largely disconnected from conditions in many of the outlying areas.

On top of that, the Taliban and Al Qaeda continue to function out of the FATA and other border areas between Afghanistan and Pakistan. We have not yet mounted the kind of sustained, coordinated push against those sanctuaries that will be necessary if we are to achieve our objectives.

So we’re conducting a comprehensive review. General Petraeus, now leading CENTCOM, is carrying out his own assessment, and he’s working closely with my special envoy, Richard Holbrooke, one of our most experienced diplomats, to craft a broader regional framework. Success is going to require much tighter coordination between our military operations, our diplomatic efforts, and our development work, as well as better cooperation with our allies.

What I want the American people to keep in mind—because this is going to be tough—is that this is the part of the world from which an attack was launched that killed 3,000 of our citizens. I met just last week with some of the families who lost loved ones on 9/11, which is a powerful reminder of the price we pay when those safe havens are allowed to persist. My fundamental position is that we cannot permit Al Qaeda to maintain operational bases there. We cannot tolerate those safe havens. We will have to act intelligently, effectively, and with persistence to ensure they are dismantled.

I do not, at this point, have a timeline for how long that will require, and I am not going to put one out there now. What I can tell you is that I will not allow Al Qaeda or bin Laden to operate freely, planning strikes against the United States.

All right, Helene Cooper from the New York Times. Helene, where are you? There you are.

question: How did you view the confrontation between White House Counsel Pat Cipollone and Jerrold Nadler that took place last night?
interview_answer: You’re really throwing a lot of questions at me.

question: Can you verify whether drone attacks have taken place in Yemen?
interview_answer: I am not going to comment in any way on operational matters.

question: What steps should we take to advance the Merida Initiative?
interview_answer: I didn’t get that one interpreted. [] It really sounded like an excellent question, though. [] I think the translation is on its way now—please proceed.

question: The QAnon movement has been expanding during COVID. Your thoughts?
interview_answer: Look, I really don’t know a lot about that group, other than I’m told they’re very supportive of me, which I’m grateful for, but beyond that I don’t know much about them. I’ve heard they’re becoming more common. And from what I’m told, these are people who see what’s happening in Portland, who see what’s gone on in New York City over the last 6 or 7 months—really, it started back when I first got here almost 4 years ago, hard to believe—and they don’t like what they’re seeing in places like Portland, Chicago, New York, and other cities and States. I’m told these are people who love our country and don’t like watching this happen. So I really don’t actually know anything about the movement itself except that, supposedly, they’re fans of mine. And they also want the problems in these cities—especially the ones we’ve been talking about—to be fixed. There’s no reason Democrats can’t manage a city. And if they won’t, we’ll send in all the Federal help—troops, law enforcement, whatever is needed. We’ll fix it in 24 hours or less. That’s what we’ll do.

question: Is this a tough Brexit?
interview_answer: Is this a rough break-up?

question: Can you share any insights about David Cameron?
interview_answer:
I've never actually met him. And, look, I'm not—it's really not my role to be handing out guidance to the head of the Conservative Party or to the leader of any other party. It's entirely for them to decide what they do, and entirely for him to decide whether he comes here or not.

But let me make this broader point to you—that what we're trying to focus on goes beyond these two particular party leaders and concerns our two nations as a whole. Let's, for a moment, accept that—even if people intensely oppose what happened in Iraq, for instance—they at least recognize that there is a struggle we are engaged in across the globe today.

And let’s also accept that this struggle is about the values that will shape the world in the early 21st century. You don't prevail in such struggles by being a part‑time ally. You don't prevail by wavering or backing away from each other when circumstances become difficult. You don't prevail by losing your resolve when your opponent’s determination remains fierce and unrelenting.

In fact, the values our two countries stand for are on display in what you've seen here today. The President faces tough questioning from the American press; I like to claim I get even tougher—or at least equally tough—questions from the British press. And as we stand here at this press conference, I can't quite make out what some of the people over there are shouting, but I very much doubt they're offering unqualified praise to either of us. It might be the supporters we brought along, but I strongly suspect it isn't.

And that, really, is the essence of it. It's about democracy. It's about people having the freedom to voice their opinions, and about elected leaders having to justify their choices, and, if the public disapproves, to face the consequences. That shared commitment to democratic values is profoundly important in the world today.

Now, of course, any adviser to a politician anywhere in Europe knows that if you want the easiest applause line, you stand up and criticize America. You'll get applause if you attack the President. You might even get a standing ovation. And if people want to play that game, that's their choice. But once all of that posturing is stripped away, you’re left with something very basic and very clear: this contest over values is still being fought.

You can argue about missteps, about particular decisions, about whether this or that should have been done in Iraq. But the reality is that, in Iraq today, our enemy is actively confronting us. So if, when our enemy confronts us, our reaction is to pull away from our friends, to make small compromises so we can dodge some of the burdens and backlash that come with hard decisions, our enemy draws encouragement from that. They watch us constantly. They ask themselves, are these people really prepared to stand by what they say they believe, or, if we keep up the pressure, will their resolve weaken and they’ll walk away because the situation is too hard, because public opinion is hostile, because the polls are unfavorable?

That is what leadership is about. And it's not just a question for me or for him; it's a question for anyone in public life. People sometimes disparage politics as nothing more than stances, gestures, sound bites, and, at times, even falsehoods. But when politics is conducted properly, when people genuinely defend what they believe in, it is fundamentally about serving the public, and there is nothing disreputable in that. Naturally, the choices are hard; they are bound to be hard.

We took a course of action that was, in my view, extremely difficult. I believed then, and I believe now, that it was the right course. History will reach its verdict in due time. What I do know is that what we stand for by being here today, in the Rose Garden, answering your questions and submitting to your scrutiny, represents a richer, better way of life than either a ruthless secular dictatorship or fanatical religious rule. It is a better way of life, and, whenever people are given a genuine choice, it is the way of life they choose.

Our two nations should be committed to extending that choice to as many people around the world as we can and to taking pride in that. I know the world needs courage. And I know this man beside me is a brave leader.

So, to come back to your original point, it really isn’t for me to pass judgment on David Cameron personally or to offer him advice, and I’m not going to do that.

question: Did they make any remarks directed at you personally, Mr. President?
interview_answer: I’m not sure. I didn’t inquire about that. It may have had nothing to do with me at all; it could have concerned something entirely different. In any case, it was outside the grounds. The barrier—the, as you know, the fencing, especially the newer sections, is extremely strong. But this was beyond the White House perimeter, all right? And there will be a complete review—Secret Service will issue a full report before long.

question: Would a congressional vote expressing opposition weaken the president’s capacity to implement their Iraq strategy?
interview_answer:
Yes, thanks. Let me make a couple of things clear: First, I recognize that Congress is going to voice its views, and it’s obvious where the Democrats stand, along with some Republicans; I’m aware of that. They didn’t support the course of action I chose. That does not mean I think they’re anything less than decent, patriotic Americans—they just disagree. I weighed some of their arguments and concluded they would not produce an Iraq that can stand on its own, sustain itself, and stand with us in the fight against terror—first point.

Second, I strongly hope this symbolic resolution does not morph into binding legislation that blocks our forces from carrying out the mission I’ve assigned them. That’s why I keep pointing out that, on the one hand, you unanimously confirmed David Petraeus, and on the other hand, you say you won’t finance the approach he himself said he needs to fulfill his responsibilities, the approach he described in sworn testimony before the Senate. I’m going to be absolutely plain with Members of Congress, starting right now: they must provide funding for our troops and preserve the flexibility required to complete the mission.

Also, I think it’s noteworthy that they’re issuing a verdict on a plan they haven’t allowed time to take effect. Again, I get it; I get it. Now, what was the other part of what you asked?

question: Should officers be permitted to use non-deadly force to bring urban violence under control?
interview_answer: In which locations are you referring? And by ‘non-deadly force,’ what exactly do you mean?

question: Have you gained any further information about how Sergeant Bergdahl was captured?
interview_answer:
Our country has long upheld a deeply held principle: we do not abandon men and women who serve in our armed forces. That commitment goes back to the very founding of the Republic.

For quite some time, we had been in touch with Congress about the likelihood that, to bring Sergeant Bergdahl home, we might have to pursue a prisoner swap. When a chance presented itself, we were worried about his physical condition, we had assurances from the government of Qatar that they would help facilitate an exchange, and we acted on that chance. The timeline was compressed precisely because we did not want to lose that narrow opening.

Now, regarding the specific details of how Sergeant Bergdahl fell into Taliban hands, we have not been questioning him about that. He is emerging from five years as a prisoner of the Taliban. He is undergoing extensive medical and psychological evaluations, and he faces a major adjustment period as he reenters normal life. He has not even reunited with his family yet—and that, I think, shows how seriously we take this reintegration phase, a lesson we carried forward from the experience of Vietnam. So I am not going to get into, or speculate about, the particulars of his capture at this time.

What I do want to stress—very plainly—is that, no matter what the facts of those circumstances ultimately prove to be, our obligation is the same: if an American service member is being held, we bring that person home. That’s it. We don’t attach conditions to that. And that is exactly what every parent sending a son or daughter into a combat zone should be able to count on from their Commander in Chief and from this nation.

On the question of security risks, the transfer of the Taliban detainees from Guantánamo was carried out with clear understandings that Qatar would keep them under close observation and put in place mechanisms that allow us to track their behavior. We will also be monitoring them. Could some attempt to go back to activities that threaten us? Yes. That risk has existed with every detainee who’s been released from Guantánamo; there is always some rate of return to the battlefield. I would not have approved this if I believed it ran counter to our national security interests, and we are confident that we can respond if they engage in actions that endanger our country.

These are the kinds of difficult choices that accompany the winding down of wars. That was the case under George Washington, under Abraham Lincoln, under Franklin Roosevelt—indeed, in every major conflict. At some point, you work to bring your people back. And that remains the right course of action.

question: A journalist asks President Biden to clarify why travelers from Germany and other EU countries are still barred from entering the U.S., while visitors from Turkey—where infection rates are roughly seven times higher—are nevertheless allowed to come in.
interview_answer: We asked our top COVID advisers to join us precisely because the Chancellor brought this issue up. Right now, it’s under active review—there’s an ongoing process to determine how quickly we can remove those restrictions. I’ll be in a position to give you a substantive answer in the next few days, once I hear back from our team about what they recommend. I’m waiting for their guidance on the timing, and, yes, as the Chancellor mentioned, she and I did discuss it. I received essentially the same response you just heard: the COVID experts are still assessing the situation. We talked about both areas of concern. The rise of the Delta variant presents a fresh challenge to both our countries. Before any change is made, it has to be very carefully considered and designed to last—we don’t want to reverse course days later. I have full trust in the American COVID team’s judgment. Now, on a more practical note, I’ve been here many times, and if we don’t head out now, we’ll miss our dinner engagement. The Chancellor and I are due to meet with several guests shortly. So let me stop here and thank you for your attention and for your questions. Thank you. Thank you.

question: Do you believe these three women are all lying, yes or no?
interview_answer: I’m not in a position to tell you that right now. I need to watch the proceedings tomorrow and look over everything. I only learned about one of them very recently. What I can say at this point is that her attorney is a real bottom-feeder, all right? So I cannot say whether they’re lying or not until I’ve actually heard their testimony. I don’t know what went on today because I’ve been tied up with Japan, with South Korea, with other nations, as you’re aware, and I presided over the Security Council. So I’m not familiar with the details of the latest woman who came forward. I do know plenty about her lawyer, and you won’t find many worse—terrible reputation, just look at his record. Regarding the other women, I’m going to wait and see how things unfold tomorrow. I’ll be paying attention, believe it or not. I’ll listen to what’s said. They might come across as persuasive. Still, with all that, Judge Brett Kavanaugh has long been regarded as one of the most respected figures in Washington. He’s served on what you’d call the second-highest court, and everyone knows him; many know him very well, and they don’t buy these accusations. I’m always open to being persuaded, but I need to hear it first before I can say.

question: Do you think Ghislaine Maxwell is likely to expose influential men involved?
interview_answer: I really can’t say. I haven’t been paying much attention to it. I honestly just wish her the best. I’ve run into her many times over the years, particularly when I was in Palm Beach and, as I understand it, they were in Palm Beach too. But I do wish her well, whatever the circumstances are. I’m not familiar with what’s going on with Prince Andrew. I just don’t know. I’m not informed about it. Yes, next question, please.

question: Do you believe you bear any responsibility for Tony Blair not remaining in office throughout your time as president?
interview_answer: I haven’t exactly surveyed the Labour delegates, so maybe. The real issue is whether I caused his departure, and I honestly can’t say.

question: Looking back over that difficult year in office, what does the President personally view as the single greatest error in their choices and behavior?
interview_answer: Are you asking about that solely in terms of health care, or in a broader sense?

question: Are there, from the President’s point of view, any reasons to be worried?
interview_answer: At this point, I really can’t say. I had no part in negotiating that agreement. In theory, it seems like a positive arrangement, but I’m not going to give you an answer right now. We’ll review it more closely, and only then will we know.

question: What place is that?
interview_answer: Look, I’m not able to tell you that right now. I’ll have to wait and find out myself. I’m not taking part in that process. I’ve intentionally kept my distance. As, essentially, the nation’s top law enforcement official, I could step in if I chose to, but I decided it was wiser not to. I think it’s best to let our outstanding Attorney General manage it. He’s appointed Durham, who is extremely well regarded, and we’ll learn the outcome in due time. I’m not going to tell you where it is.

question: Are you suggesting the situation isn’t that heated?
interview_answer: At this point, I really can’t say. What I can tell you is that I’m waiting to hear President Putin’s account of the meeting. What I’m mainly focused on is whether he still shares the same worries that I have. I say “still” because when we were in Australia, he again made it clear to me that he understands it’s not in the global community’s interest for Iran to be able to develop a nuclear weapon. Russia has backed that up at the United Nations, and we’re cooperating with them on the possibility of a third resolution. That’s where my attention is—I’m not concerned about the photos. I understand why they sit down together. I will keep working with Russia and other countries to maintain a clear, united message: if you push ahead with nuclear weapons plans, you’re going to find yourself isolated.

question: Do you have any approximate schedule for when Asad should leave power?
interview_answer:
We would have liked to see Asad step down two years ago, a year ago, six months ago, even two months ago. My administration has been consistent in saying that Asad forfeited his legitimacy the moment he turned his guns on his own citizens and began slaughtering people who at first were peacefully demanding a greater say in how their country is run. That violence has only intensified over time. So in terms of when he ought to go, our position is simple: the earlier, the better.

Now, as to the question of timing in a more precise sense, that really comes back to the issue of how this happens, which we’ve already talked about. There is no simple blueprint for resolving an extremely brutal and complex crisis like the one we’re dealing with. If there were, the Prime Minister and I would have implemented it already and this would be behind us.

Instead, what we can do is maintain firm international pressure and continue to bolster the opposition. I believe that the planned talks in Geneva, which would involve the Russians and representatives from the regime in a serious discussion about a political transition acceptable to all sides, may bear fruit. But while that unfolds, we will keep supporting the opposition and addressing the humanitarian catastrophe, and we will coordinate closely with Turkey, which has deep stakes in this situation and with whom we have an exceptionally strong partnership.

We’ll be meeting again this evening, as you know, so we’ll have another opportunity to delve into all these matters at greater length. As I indicated earlier, our perspectives are largely aligned, and in our discussions tonight we’ll continue to work through what we can undertake jointly, and what elements we might include in a broader roadmap tied to Geneva and what comes after.

It is especially important that Russia and China be engaged in this effort, given their role as permanent members of the U.N. Security Council. Their involvement can significantly increase the momentum behind this process. Ongoing pressure from the international community remains crucial. When we examine the humanitarian aid we’ve provided so far, it now exceeds $1.5 billion.

Turkey will maintain its open‑door policy; we will keep doing so because we share a 910‑kilometer border with Syria, and families are split across that border. These efforts are vital for regional stability. On one side, we see measures underway to improve relations between Palestinians and Israelis; we do not need additional crises in the region. We had previously worked to bring Syria and Israel together to address their differences through five rounds of talks, which, unfortunately, were not concluded. Still, I hope that our future steps on regional peace will produce tangible outcomes, and that, working resolutely with the United States, we can advance peace throughout the region.

All of these issues remain under discussion. The role of the U.N. Security Council and the Geneva framework will be essential, and we will go on evaluating those tracks between us. Thank you all very much.

So, to be absolutely clear: beyond stating that we want Asad out as soon as possible, I am not going to give any specific timetable for his departure.

question: Have you settled on your pick to lead the Federal Reserve Board yet? And if you haven’t, can you walk us through what factors you’re weighing as you choose the next Fed Chair and fill the remaining vacant seats?
interview_answer: No, no, and still no. I’m not going to get into that with you, because that process is already underway. We’ll be rolling out those decisions fairly soon; this has been moving forward for quite a while, first of all. Second, I want to stress that we’ll have ample time to ensure all the key nominees are confirmed before any current terms run out. Third, I’ve spent a great deal of time on this, consulting closely with my economic team about the strongest candidates, and we have many solid options. But I’m not going to go into hypotheticals right now. Nancy—Nancy Cordes from CBS—you had your hand up, I think. Did you want to jump in?

question: Do you still trust Secretary Azar?
interview_answer: I’ll wish him well—trust in whom?

question: What timeline have you been given for how long this is expected to last?
interview_answer: I haven’t been given any exact timetable. They’ve been in place for a very long time, this leader and the one before him. I assume that at some stage the situation will be different. But we actually haven’t imposed the really severe sanctions yet. We’re capable of much tougher measures, and every option is still on the table, so we may move in that direction. So far, as you know, our sanctions have been more in the middle range. We could ratchet them up significantly if we decide that’s necessary. What’s happening there is terribly tragic, and our only aim is to help the many people who are literally starving and dying in the streets. The situation is a shameful one. This used to be among the richest nations on earth, and now suddenly it’s overwhelmed by grief and poverty, with no food, no water, no air conditioning, nothing. There was no electricity for a long stretch last week. No power. And that’s likely to fail again, because everything is hanging by a thread. It’s heartbreaking to witness, Roberta. Truly heartbreaking. Alright?

question: Should NATO require unanimous agreement before sending heavy weaponry to Ukraine, and should Germany, as Europe’s strongest NATO member, itself supply such heavy weapons to Ukraine?
interview_answer:
There is no deficit of confidence to repair. He enjoys full confidence from the United States. Germany is—among our most crucial partners anywhere in the world. There is absolutely no question about the strength of Germany’s relationship with the United States. None at all.

On the issue of supporting Ukraine, Germany is one of the largest financial backers Ukraine has. Germany has been at the forefront when it comes to ensuring economic aid is provided. You also raised another point—you posed so many that I can’t keep track of each one precisely. But as for this notion you mention—that U.S. media claim Germany is an unreliable partner—Germany is entirely dependable: fully, completely, unequivocally dependable. I have zero doubts about Germany. We stand together. The transatlantic relationship between Germany and the United States is a lasting foundation of German policy, and it will remain just as significant going forward. It will always rank among our top priorities.

Within NATO, we are the continental European country making the largest overall contribution—financially and militarily. And regarding who contributes the most funding for Ukraine, we don’t fully share your assessment. Since 2014, we have provided roughly 2 billion U.S. dollars in direct bilateral support, plus an additional 3.8 billion through the EU framework—substantial sums to help stabilize Ukraine’s economy—and we intend to keep up that level of engagement.

That is what underpins the very strong, unshakeable friendship between our two nations. As part of that, given the tense situation on Ukraine’s border stemming from Russian forces, we have made it crystal clear that our response will be united when it comes to sanctions. I am not going to get into the specific question of delivering heavy weapons.

question: Your response to Ruth Bader Ginsburg’s hospitalization due to an infection.
interview_answer: I’m not going to comment on that beyond saying I wish her well. I hope she recovers quickly. I hadn’t even heard she’d been admitted—if so, that’s unfortunate, and again, I simply wish her the very best.

question: Were you briefed on any confirmed coronavirus infections inside the White House today?
interview_answer: You mean, as of today?

question: Did this constitute a threat directed at the President?
interview_answer: We’re unsure at this point. We simply don’t know. Investigators will determine that later.

question: What actions will you take to assist them beyond Syria, sir?
interview_answer: Look, what I can say is that we’re going to talk about that situation. We’ve already begun talking about that situation. But the Kurds have given us incredible help in fighting ISIS, all right?

question: How do you plan to win Democratic backing for that proposal?
interview_answer: You’ll need to put that question to them.

question: Will they continue receiving an extra $600 each week?
interview_answer: I’m not going to confirm that now. You’ll find out when it’s in place, but we do have the funds.

question: How might this affect peace talks?
interview_answer: To start, what stands out to me is that they chose to make that call only after the election, even though we’d long known they were planning to do it. That, in itself, shows you they’re facing some serious issues—specifically the Russian military. That’s the first point. Second, in terms of where that takes us, at the very least it gives everyone a winter window to reassess their stances. And we simply don’t yet know whether, during that time, any decision will be made about Ukraine being willing to make concessions to Russia. I’ll soon be heading to the G‑20. I’m hearing President Putin probably won’t attend, but a number of other global leaders will be in Indonesia. That’s where we’ll be able to gauge what might come next. I’m not going to go further into your question right now. Let me bring in Nancy from CBS—Nancy Cordes.

question: Could you provide us with the Red Cross’s report?
interview_answer: I’m not familiar with it. We do not engage in torture. Next question, Jim.

question: Do you personally regard homosexuality as morally wrong, sir?
interview_answer: I am not going to pass moral judgment on anyone’s sexual orientation. I do, however, think the ‘don’t ask, don’t tell’ approach is sound policy. Sammon, from the Washington Times, go ahead.

question: Is it beneficial for companies to be concerned about climate issues?
interview_answer: I’m afraid I’m not following what you’re asking.

question: Does that concern you at all?
interview_answer: Look, I’m not going to add anything more on that. Mary Bruce, ABC News.

question: What was the reason Prime Minister Maliki did not appear at yesterday's press briefing with General Casey and Ambassador Khalilzad?
interview_answer: Elaine, I honestly don't know why he chose not to attend.

question: How influential do you believe he really is now that Putin remains on the scene?
interview_answer:
I don’t have a deep understanding of Medvedev either. What will be telling is who actually shows up and speaks for Russia at forums like the G‑8, for instance. That will be revealing—it should offer some clues about how Russia plans to approach its foreign policy after Vladimir Putin’s term as President. At this point, I’m not in a position to answer that question.

What I can say is that it clearly serves our interests to maintain a working relationship with Russia. On proliferation, for example, it’s vital that we cooperate to ensure that dangerous materials capable of causing massive harm don’t spread. It’s in our interest to work together on Iran. As I mentioned, I believe in this very room during my last visit, I welcomed Vladimir Putin’s statement to the Iranians that Russia would supply the enriched uranium needed to fuel the Bushehr plant, removing any justification for Iran to enrich on its own. That struck me as a positive step. And we need to be able to engage Russia on the Iran issue.

There are many areas—yesterday, for instance, I discussed a European missile defense system with the Czech Prime Minister. I believe it’s important for us to find a way to convince the Russians that this system is not directed at them but at real 21st‑century threats, like a possible launch of a weapon of mass destruction by a rogue regime. So there are domains, David, where cooperation is essential—and let me finish—and my goal is to leave things so that whoever succeeds me can maintain a workable relationship with whoever is in charge of Russian foreign policy. That’s simply in our national interest. That doesn’t imply constant agreement. As you know, we disagreed over Kosovo, and there will be other points of disagreement. But it remains vital for the country that there be a relationship at the leadership level and, ideally, more broadly than that. And again, on your specific question, I’m not going to give you an assessment right now.

question: How would you describe the unidentified flying objects that President Obama referred to earlier?
interview_answer: You’ll have to put that to him again. Thanks. [] Alright, chief, we’re done here. []

question: Do you think the government’s seizure of Associated Press reporters’ phone records this week—or in the earlier episode just disclosed—amounted to an abuse of authority?
interview_answer:
I’ll leave it to you and your colleagues to sort through those comparisons, look back at the record, and reach whatever judgments you think are appropriate. My focus is that when something in the Government isn’t working the way it should, we identify it and correct it. That’s the obligation I bear, and that’s what we’re going to keep doing. That applies to the IRS, where we have to ensure the law is carried out as intended. It also applies to the safety of our diplomats, which is why we have to partner with Congress to see that there are sufficient resources for what’s required in the field.

Now, on the Justice Department matter, I am not going to weigh in on a particular, active case. What I can address is the broader balance we have to achieve. Disclosures involving national security can endanger people. They can put at risk the men and women in uniform whom I’ve ordered into harm’s way. They can jeopardize some of our intelligence personnel who are operating in sensitive, precarious environments where they can be easily exposed.

Our national security depends on those individuals being able to do their jobs confident that the people back home are protecting them, that they’re not being abandoned and put in even greater danger than they already face. So I’m not apologetic about being deeply concerned—as the American people would expect their Commander in Chief to be—about information that could compromise their missions or cost them their lives.

At the same time, we’re a democracy, and a free press, free speech, and the unrestricted exchange of information are essential for holding me accountable, holding our Government accountable, and making our democracy work. That commitment to democratic accountability is why I entered public life in the first place.

The intent behind the media shield legislation—which had broad backing from, for example, the Washington Post editorial board and from prosecutors—was precisely to get that balance right. And insofar as this particular matter—about which we still don’t have a complete public picture—has renewed interest in how we should appropriately strike that balance, then this is an appropriate moment to revisit that bill. That’s a valuable debate to have, and I think it’s necessary.

But it’s equally important to remember that when we’re raising alarms about leaks at a time when more than 60,000 of our troops are still in Afghanistan and numerous intelligence officers are deployed worldwide in dangerous posts—some as hazardous as the facility in Benghazi—part of my responsibility is to safeguard their work. I have to do that while still making room for the public’s right to know and its ability to hold my office to account. So I’m going to speak to that broader balance, but I am not going to comment on that specific ongoing case.

question: Do you believe that imposing a cap on Russian oil exports will actually reduce gasoline costs for U.S. motorists?
interview_answer: Let me just make sure I understand you—the second thing you asked was whether it would lower the cost, right?

question: Is there any plan to prohibit or limit Chinese reporters’ ability to work in the United States?
interview_answer: I actually haven’t been fully briefed on that issue yet. It’s something we’re going to review. We’ll take a look at it, absolutely. It’s not a situation I’m happy about. I don’t think it’s right, because we allow very broad media access here. Some might argue we allow too much access, but I don’t agree with that. I think our access is very generous, so it doesn’t seem fair to me. But we’ll reach a decision on that in the near future. All right? Thanks. Very good question. Next, please. Yes, you—go ahead.

question: What information does the President have about the accusations made against nominee Dr. Ronny Jackson?
interview_answer:
I’m not familiar with the specific accusations you’re referring to, and I’m not going to get into them, but I can say that he’s one of the best people I’ve ever met, and I think my wife would say the same. He served as physician to President Obama, I believe also to President Bush, and now to me. I’ve come to know him quite well. He’s an excellent doctor.

His nomination came as a recommendation. I understand some people are raising concerns about his experience, that he supposedly doesn’t have enough of it. But the VA is extremely important to me. We’ve done terrific work there already, as you know, with the Accountability Act and a lot of other reforms. Now we’re pushing hard on choice. It’s going to get done. Our veterans are going to be cared for incredibly well. That matters a great deal to me, and we’ve delivered.

I just spoke with Admiral Jackson and said, Why put yourself through this? These are very nasty opponents who attack people—and that’s what they do. I’ve gone through it myself; we’ve all seen it. And you in the press are seeing record viewership because of it, so good for you. But I asked him, Why do you need this? He’s an admiral, a strong leader. They’re going after him over every little thing.

And as you saw with what I expect will soon be Secretary of State Pompeo, everyone was predicting disaster. I was told ten minutes before the committee vote that he was going to fail in committee—which hasn’t happened to a Secretary of State nominee in many decades. But I spoke to Rand Paul—he’s almost never let me down, he’s a good man—and I knew some things others didn’t know. He decided to change his vote, and it shocked everyone. Pompeo ended up with an 11–9 vote, because, as you know, Johnny Isakson’s vote is counted when it’s not the tie-breaker. So that made it 11–9 with, I think, one vote listed as—what’s the term for that, John?

question: Have you obtained concrete intelligence indicating that President Putin is actively deploying these weapons, positioning them for action, or seriously contemplating using them?
interview_answer: On that first point, I’m not going to answer. I will not disclose intelligence information, that’s the first thing. Second, we would take action. If he were to employ such a weapon, we would respond, and the form of that response would be determined by how it was used. Josh from Bloomberg.

question: Is the United States prepared to toughen its sanctions and potentially step up pressure on North Korea to make progress?
interview_answer:
I’m not going to address that directly. What I can say is this: we already have very powerful sanctions in place. I’m not going to get into whether we’d ramp them up. They’re tough. And there are many decent, ordinary people in North Korea who have to get by under those measures too, and that matters to me.

I’ll also say this: my overall perspective has shifted a great deal since I came to know Chairman Kim, as you’re aware, quite well. They have their own perspective on all of this.

So I really don’t want to discuss that specific point. I just believe that, with any luck, for the good of South Korea, for the good of Japan, and frankly for the good of everyone in the region—I was just speaking with President Xi, who, as you know, commands enormous respect—I told him, you can’t be happy having a nuclear-armed neighbor right on your border. And he isn’t. He truly isn’t. I can tell you he also wants that issue resolved. So I’ll leave it there.

Now, ladies and gentlemen, I have a plane to catch back to a wonderful place called Washington, D.C. Thank you all very much. Thank you. Thanks, everyone. Thank you.

question: Can the U.S. decide to abandon its attempt to have Meng Wanzhou extradited?
interview_answer: Drop what? Based on what?

question: In any case, do you believe that destroying those tapes was an appropriate course of action?
interview_answer: I think I’ve already made it pretty plain that my earliest memory of this is when Mike Hayden briefed me, and I want to underscore that. Beyond that, I trust that the initial review by the Attorney General and the CIA Inspector General, together with congressional oversight, will ultimately clarify precisely what took place. Because of that, as those reviews and oversight hearings move forward, I am withholding any judgment until I have a complete picture of the facts. I realize this is an issue I will be pressed on repeatedly. Let me be very clear: until those reviews are concluded and the oversight process is finished, I will not be offering any opinion on this matter from this podium.

question: Why has Vice President Pence not complied with the CDC’s self-quarantine recommendations?
interview_answer: On that point, I’m going to have to leave it to the Vice President to explain his own actions. Whatever choices he’s making and steps he’s taking, he’ll address those himself. I’ll see that they release a statement. John, your question, please.

question: Do you think Nancy Pelosi looks like Bob Bullock?
interview_answer: That’s more of a private joke, and I’m not going to weigh in on it. What I will say is that I’m fundamentally an optimist. I expected we’d lose some seats; I just wasn’t sure how many.

question: What led you to choose not to merge existing agencies, but instead to increase their number? Doesn’t having so many agencies contribute to the problem?
interview_answer:
You’ve put your finger on exactly what I’m not going to get ahead of before tomorrow’s announcement. So I’m not going to walk through the specifics right now; instead, let me stick to general principles, and tomorrow you’ll be able to press the administration on the precise details of what we’re putting forward.

Broadly speaking, we ended up in this mess because weak oversight and numerous regulatory loopholes let financial players—not only traditional banks, but a range of non‑bank institutions—take reckless risks that didn’t just endanger their own firms, but put the entire U.S. economy at risk and triggered a deep global downturn. We need to update a regulatory framework that, in many respects, hasn’t been overhauled since the 1930s, so it can handle huge cross‑border capital flows and complex new financial products and risk‑taking that have proven extremely harmful to ordinary Americans.

We will be unveiling a robust package of regulatory reforms that we believe can help prevent a repeat of this kind of crisis. We’re going to urge Congress to move quickly so these measures can be enacted, implemented, and made fully operational, and I’m eager to sign them into law.

Once you see the overall design of our plan, you’ll recognize that we are not simply piling on a lot of new agencies. In fact, the approach involves rationalization, some consolidation, and tighter coordination so that gaps are closed—whether those gaps concern consumer protections, investor safeguards, or the monitoring of systemic risks we absolutely must avoid. Across all of those dimensions, the system will be more coherent and better integrated than what we had before.

Now, getting this done is going to be difficult, as always, because there are powerful interests that want to keep placing risky bets while assuming taxpayers will rescue them if things go south. You’ll hear the usual complaints that we don’t need tougher rules and that government should back off. People forget quickly, and some lobbyists and special interests are counting on that amnesia to erase the damage caused by their irresponsible behavior. I intend to keep reminding the country of what happened so we can put strong safeguards in place and stop this kind of crisis from recurring—but I’m still not going to get into tomorrow’s specific announcement today.

question: Your view on whether this could advance or obstruct a two-state solution.
interview_answer:
All right. Let me begin by applauding the Senate for taking steps toward granting me the authority not only to negotiate a modern, fair, and growth-oriented trade agreement with key partners across the Asia-Pacific region, and possibly in Europe as well, but also to equip this office with the means to effectively enforce those agreements—something that has too often been neglected in the past. I’m grateful to all the Senators who supported moving this authority forward, or at least agreed to open a serious debate on it. For those who opposed it, I intend to keep engaging with them, laying out the facts and analysis they need so they can be confident that, while previous trade deals have had real and serious shortcomings, the framework we’re pursuing now is different—designed not just for large American corporations, but for small and mid-sized firms and, above all, for American workers.

I would not champion any agreement if I didn’t believe that, when all is said and done, it would generate jobs here in the United States and expand opportunities for people to climb the economic ladder, earn higher incomes, and secure better wages. That has been my overriding priority from the day I took office, and it remains my focus now.

On the matter of disagreements between myself and Senator Warren, this has never been about personal animosity. I know it can be tempting for the press to frame it as a personal rift when you see two long‑time allies diverging on a particular policy question. But several of my closest friends and earliest supporters in both the Senate and the House are on the other side of this trade debate. I understand why, because, like me, they watched factories close and jobs shipped overseas. Like me, they worry whether labor and environmental commitments in trade deals are truly honored. Like me, they’re concerned that trade must be fair, not only free.

And they share my deep concern about broader economic trends, both globally and at home: widening inequality, the impact of technology and globalization in allowing those at the very top to do extremely well, while middle‑class wages stagnate and families struggle just to hold on or break into the middle class. So we are united in values. I noticed not long ago that some of these colleagues released a progressive statement of core principles. When I read through it, I saw my own agenda reflected almost point for point—except on trade. That’s the one major area where we part company.

So what we’re dealing with is a genuine policy dispute, rooted in different readings of how best to serve our people and our constituencies. I remain convinced that, despite past mistakes, we are better off being the ones to set high standards—with binding, enforceable rules on issues like child labor, illegal logging, environmental destruction, wildlife trafficking, and intellectual property—for what will be the largest and fastest‑growing markets in the world. If we don’t shape those rules, China and others will, and American businesses and workers will be put at a disadvantage.

On the concern about jobs moving overseas, the analysis shows that, under the current system, any U.S. company that is solely chasing cheap labor already has plenty of ways to relocate production. What we have a chance to do now is to create incentives for companies to bring production back to the United States.

We’re already seeing signs of that. That’s one reason I went to Nike.
