I’m fully aware that Nike has long produced footwear in low‑wage countries in the Asia‑Pacific, and that this has had real consequences for American shoe‑manufacturing jobs. But that shift has unfolded over the last three decades. Now Nike is saying that, because of new technologies, it can potentially create 10,000 jobs here at home as production moves up the value chain and relies on different kinds of manufacturing. That’s a real opportunity. But to fully capitalize on it, we still have to be able to sell our products in those overseas markets.

Which is why, when I talk with my progressive friends, I emphasize that if we’re serious about addressing the concerns we all share, we should be zeroing in on the domestic measures we already agree on: raising the minimum wage, strengthening job‑training programs, investing in infrastructure that puts people back to work, reinforcing collective bargaining and workers’ ability to have a real voice, strictly enforcing overtime rules, ensuring paid sick leave, and having an honest debate about our budget priorities so we’re not gutting forward‑looking investments merely to safeguard corporate loopholes that don’t actually strengthen our economy.

Those are the tools that will help us directly confront the problems they’re worried about. Simply blocking a trade deal will not do that—especially when many of these same critics acknowledge that the current trade rules are already a poor bargain for American workers. If the existing framework isn’t serving us, it’s hard to see how freezing that status quo in place is supposed to help.

I support tough enforcement, including provisions that were negotiated. I have, however, raised questions about the way the currency provisions in the bill are drafted. I need to talk further with Senator Schumer, Senator Brown, and others about how we can refine that language so it doesn’t unintentionally undermine our own monetary policy.

And as for your other question—I honestly don’t even recall what it was, so I’m not going to try to answer it.

question: Could President Lee clarify where things currently stand in talks on lengthening South Korea’s missile range?
interview_answer:
Thank you. You have raised the issue of an imminent long-range missile launch by the North, and allow me to underscore once again, ladies and gentlemen: should the North press ahead with a test of a long-range missile, that would constitute a blatant breach of U.N. Security Council Resolution 1874. If Pyongyang moves forward with this launch, it will be acting in direct contradiction to the commitments it has made before the international community.

The global community is calling on the North Korean authorities to retract their declaration that they will proceed with a long-range missile test. Now, your question was how we intend to react. Let me respond by saying that, if the North carries out this launch, it alone will bear full responsibility. As I have just indicated, if they proceed, they will only deepen their own isolation and will have to face all of the resulting consequences themselves.

From the standpoint of ordinary North Koreans, they will see their leadership spending hundreds of millions of dollars on a long-range missile. In witnessing this, they will not feel a sense of pride that their country managed to fire such a missile; rather, they will come to clearly recognize the true character of their leaders and understand why they themselves must endure such severe hardship today.

Domestically, I am sure the North Korean leadership is seeking some internal objective by staging a long-range missile test. But they must clearly recognize that, if they go forward, they will be placing themselves in an extremely difficult situation. I urge the North to act as a responsible member of the international community, because that is the surest path to improving the lives of its own people.

On this point, there is no divergence between the United States and the Republic of Korea. We will remain calm and level-headed, and we will respond prudently if the North in fact follows through on its announcement.

As for your specific question about extending South Korea’s missile range: we did not take up that subject in our talks. I am not going to go into that matter here. Working-level officials, of course, are handling discussions on that issue, but it has to be considered within a broader, comprehensive framework of strengthening our defensive posture vis-à-vis the North. Ben Feller, Associated Press.

question: Whether the administration plans to restore the Houthi movement or allied militias to the terrorism list.
interview_answer: What I can say is that this is still being reviewed. And bringing this conflict to an end requires both sides to fully engage. Achieving that will be extremely challenging. That’s all I can say on it.

question: Ask whether the Attorney General and the Deputy Attorney General can expect to remain in their posts long-term.
interview_answer: I’m not going to get into that right now. We’re reviewing a number of matters, including our Cabinet. Overall, I’m very pleased with most of the people serving in my Cabinet. We’re considering various individuals for various roles. As you know, post‑midterm adjustments are very typical. I chose not to make any moves before the elections. But I can say that, generally, I’m extremely satisfied with my Cabinet. Mike Pompeo, in particular, has really settled in remarkably well and is doing a fantastic job—

question: When is the timeline expected to shift for young kids to get the vaccine?
interview_answer: Right now, the research isn’t yet at a stage where experts are fully satisfied that it’s safe for that age group, and that’s what they’re still working through. A few months back, I was asked a similar thing about kids roughly 7 to 12, and only after they had enough trials and solid data did they feel comfortable saying it was safe for them. The same process is happening here, and it will happen in due course. But I can’t give you a date—I’m not a scientist, and I simply don’t know when. What I can say is that reaching that next step is extremely important.

question: How would you characterize your opinion of North Korea’s ruler, Kim Jong Un?
interview_answer:
On the matter of North Korea, I believe that South Korea, the United States, and the broader international community must all focus on a single overarching goal: persuading North Korea to give up its nuclear arsenal and to act as a responsible member of the international community. That outcome would advance peace on the Korean Peninsula and globally, and it would also ultimately benefit North Korea’s own economic and social development. That is the framework through which I view this issue.

To nudge North Korea toward that course and help change its strategic calculations, we need closely coordinated efforts. In that context, China’s influence is substantial, so Beijing’s active engagement is crucial. We’ve reaffirmed our common understanding on that point.

Regarding the positions of China and Russia, my assessment is that both of them—alongside the wider international community—recognize the necessity of a nuclear-free Korean Peninsula and are working together to encourage North Korea to choose a more constructive direction. China, for instance, has taken a leading role in supporting and implementing U.N. Security Council resolutions responding to North Korea’s missile launches and nuclear tests, and has been diligent in carrying out those measures.

As for Russia, it also strongly backs the objective of denuclearizing the Korean Peninsula. Moscow has been very supportive in the passage of U.N. Security Council resolutions on North Korea and has pushed to ensure that strong language directed at Pyongyang was included in the G‑8 foreign ministers’ joint statement. Such efforts by China and Russia are essential to present a united front to North Korea, making clear that its nuclear program is unacceptable and urging its leadership to choose a different course.

Now, with respect specifically to Kim Jong Un as an individual, I have to be candid: I do not know him, have never met or spoken with him, and therefore I am not in a position to offer any judgment about his personal traits. What we can evaluate are the steps his regime has taken—steps that have been confrontational and are leading, in our view, to a dead end.

I want to stress that we share the determination to maintain a robust deterrent posture and not to compensate or reward provocative acts. At the same time, we remain willing to engage if North Korea chooses a peaceful route of denuclearization, complies with its international obligations, and reintegrates into the community of nations, allowing for a gradual process that could deliver both greater security and prosperity for its people.

If North Korea’s past and current behavior has not created a secure and thriving country, then this would be an appropriate moment for Kim Jong Un to look at that record and chart a new direction. Should he decide to alter course, that decision would be welcomed not only by us but by the international community more broadly.

China, Russia, Japan, and the other participants in the Six‑Party Talks have all signaled that point. But any shift will have to be demonstrated through concrete changes in conduct. As we say in English, “Don’t judge me by what I say; judge me by what I do.” Up to now, we have not seen actions from Pyongyang that suggest it is ready to move onto a different path. So I cannot and will not offer a personal appraisal of Kim Jong Un beyond noting the pattern of actions his government has taken.

question: If marriage should be treated as a basic civil right
interview_answer:
That was quite a grab bag of issues you just tossed out. [] Chuck, we're going to have to get you sworn in on the high court. [] Look, I'm not sitting on the Supreme Court, and I'm not about to start pretending I'm your resident constitutional law scholar on television. So I'm not going to weigh in on that specific legal classification. What I am going to do is concentrate first on the underlying policy question at hand, because there has been a lot of chatter about legal theories and procedural maneuvering and congressional involvement. I want to set that aside and walk through, in concrete terms, what has actually taken place.

We had a situation where Muammar Qadhafi — who, before Usama bin Laden, was behind more American deaths than almost anyone else — was openly threatening mass slaughter of his own citizens. In response, under a virtually unparalleled U.N. authorization and as part of a broad international coalition, we moved to neutralize his air defenses so a coalition could enforce a no‑fly zone and offer humanitarian protection to people on the ground.

I addressed the nation and made clear exactly what we would and would not do. I said there would be no American combat troops on Libyan soil. I said the United States would not shoulder the bulk of the operation; rather, as a NATO partner, we would support our allies because doing so serves our national security interests and, frankly, because it is the morally right choice.

We have followed through precisely on those commitments. We have not deployed ground forces. Our NATO partners — whom we have often criticized in the past for not carrying enough of the burden — have, in this case, taken on a substantial share of it. As a result, thousands of Libyans have been shielded from potential atrocities; we have not lost a single American service member; and we have avoided a spiral of escalation. The mission remains circumscribed in both duration and objectives.

From the outset, I laid out for the American public a clearly defined, limited mission, and we have executed that mission effectively. Throughout, we kept Congress informed. There have been numerous hearings. We have delivered extensive documentation on the scope and conduct of our operations. I have personally met with Members of Congress to discuss it. A good deal of the current outcry is, frankly, political.

Substantively, when you examine what we have undertaken, we have done exactly what we said we would do, operating under a U.N. mandate and saving many lives in the process. Meanwhile, a man long known as a state sponsor of terrorism against the United States is now cornered, and the pressure on him continues to intensify.

Now, if you go back to the origins of the War Powers Resolution, it was enacted after Vietnam, when we had hundreds of thousands of troops deployed, tens of thousands of Americans killed, and hundreds of billions of dollars expended. Congress decided it never wanted to see that kind of open‑ended commitment again and insisted that, in cases like that, a president must consult with Congress in advance.

I fully agree that this kind of consultation is appropriate and important. But in my judgment, nothing we have done in Libya runs afoul of the War Powers Resolution. And because I do not believe we are in violation of that statute, I do not need to reach, or opine on, the larger constitutional question you are asking about.

question: Is the individual completely rejecting the idea that she was dismissed from her job?
interview_answer:
Look, Jackie, honestly, I just don’t know. I’m being straight with you here — I only learned about this earlier today, so I’m not yet aware of all the details, and Ken Salazar has been tied up testifying on the Hill.

On your first point about MMS, Ken Salazar was in the middle of trying to overhaul that agency. But the broader point I’m making is that clearly those changes weren’t moving quickly enough. If they had been fully in place in time, this situation might have been caught. It’s also true that it might not have been caught. We could have had a brand‑new environmental review process, a whole team of technical experts combing through BP’s plans, and they still might have said: this complies with industry norms, there hasn’t been an accident like this in 15 years, and it’s acceptable to proceed.

That’s exactly what this Commission has to figure out: Was there a fundamental system failure? Is this the kind of thing that happens once in a million times? Once in a thousand? Once every 5,000? What are the real, quantifiable risks here?

Let me step back and make a larger point about energy. The fact that oil companies are now going down a mile under the ocean and then drilling another three miles below that to reach oil should tell us a lot about where this industry is headed. Getting that oil out is more costly, and it is, by its nature, more dangerous.

That’s one of the reasons you never heard me chanting, “Drill, baby, drill,” because we’re not going to solve this problem simply by drilling more. Sure, domestic production can be one piece of the picture as we move toward new technologies and new energy sources, but we need some humility in recognizing that the easy‑to‑reach oil has largely already been taken out of the ground.

As we go forward, the technology will keep getting more complex, the fields more remote, and that inevitably means higher risks. As a country, we’ll have to make some very tough choices about what level of risk we’re prepared to accept. And that’s one of the central questions this Commission will need to examine.

At the same time, we have to be realistic: our economy and our daily lives are still going to depend on oil for some time. That’s why I’ve been saying we have to accelerate this transition. When I met with the Republican caucus this week, I urged them to work with us. Senators Lieberman and Kerry have been developing a framework — originally with Senator Lindsey Graham, even though he’s stepped back from the bill at the moment — that could actually draw bipartisan support. It acknowledges that yes, we’re still going to rely on oil production, but it also recognizes that we can already see the problems on the horizon if we don’t start changing course.

Macarena Vidal, EFE News — you’re here? There you are.

question: Inquiry regarding details of the Al Qaida intel briefing.
interview_answer: Come again?

question: Did you dispatch your Chief of Staff at the time, along with the White House Counsel, to John Ashcroft’s hospital room while he was ill to secure his approval for that program?
interview_answer: Kelly, there are all sorts of rumors about what did or did not occur, and I’m not going to address them. This is an extremely sensitive program. What I can say is, first, the program is vital for safeguarding the American people, and it remains vital because we still face enemies who want to harm us. As president, I have a responsibility to implement measures that both protect the country and respect Americans’ civil liberties, and in this instance the program was continually examined and reported to the United States Congress. As I’ve said, it is a critical tool in our national defense. People will continue to speculate about it, but I’m not going to advance that debate by discussing such a highly sensitive, highly classified matter. What I will repeat is that the program itself is necessary.

question: To be precise, have you observed any concrete results from your diplomatic approach so far?
interview_answer: Look, you’ve known me long enough to understand that I’m not someone who likes to sit here and declare whether something is a success or not in a situation like this. That’s just not how I operate. We prefer to keep our intentions and evaluations very close to the chest. What I can say is this: I’m convinced that pursuing this course is the right thing to do, not only for our country, but for people everywhere. There are many solid reasons behind what we’re doing. Now, within that context, yes, I think we’re moving forward in a strong way. We’re demonstrating tremendous power. I believe they recognize that our capabilities are unmatched—no one has ever had this kind of strength. We’ve deployed three of the world’s largest aircraft carriers, and they’re in position right now. We also have a nuclear submarine in place. There are a lot of things underway that we hope—and I’ll say we pray—we never have to actually use. Given all of that, I genuinely think it’s in their interest to come to the table and strike an agreement that benefits their people and the world. I do notice some shifts, yes. But as for labeling it ‘success,’ I’m not going to do that—let’s just see how it plays out.

question: Can you clarify whether making federal contractors follow policies that advance gay rights or govern contraception would be the first action that comes to your mind?
interview_answer: Margaret, I'm not going to lay out particular steps in bits and pieces. After we've fully reviewed the situation and know exactly what powers we have, I’ll brief you then.

question: And second, regarding Fast and Furious, lawmakers in Congress and officials in Mexico still say they’ve received no explanations. Do you intend to change the leadership at ATF, and when will the ongoing inquiry produce its findings?
interview_answer:
On your second point, as you know, my Attorney General has already stated unequivocally that he never would have authorized trafficking guns into Mexico. That review is still underway. I’m not going to discuss an active investigation. I’ve been very clear that such an approach by ATF would have been unacceptable, and we need to determine exactly how it occurred. Once the investigation is finished, I expect that whatever steps are warranted will be taken.

On E‑Verify, what we really need is broad immigration reform. I’ve said that before, I’m saying it now, and I’ll be saying it again months from now. We need a framework that both honors our tradition as a country of laws and our history as a country of immigrants. That means strong border enforcement, real consequences for employers who unlawfully hire and exploit workers, and also a path to lawful status for people who are currently living in the shadows.

We may not be able to pass every element I’d like to see in a single bill, but it has to be a balanced approach. E‑Verify can play a useful enforcement role, provided it isn’t full of mistakes and that U.S. citizens are adequately protected, because I don’t want a situation where businesses are required to rely on a system they can’t trust. And we don’t want employers in a position where they turn down a qualified applicant because a database wrongly labels that person as undocumented, when in fact they are not. That would be unfair to the worker and could create legal problems for the employer too.

So our objective for now should be to keep working to improve the E‑Verify system and to make sure there are safeguards to avoid the kinds of problems I just mentioned. At the same time, we can’t ignore the other pieces of immigration reform—for instance, ensuring that DREAMers—young people who grew up here, see themselves as Americans, are undocumented through no fault of their own, and are ready to serve, study, and start businesses here—are able to remain in this country. We need a more comprehensive approach than just a verification system. Okay?

question: In light of Mr. Trump’s victory, as a gesture of political fair play, don’t you essentially have to announce now that you’ll be running for office again?
interview_answer:
I generally make it a point not to get involved in another country’s internal political debates. What I can say is that Chancellor Merkel has been an exceptional counterpart. Among our closest partners, she’s virtually the only one who was already in office when I first arrived, which makes us, in a sense, the old hands who’ve weathered a great many tests together over these past 8 years. And while we haven’t agreed on every single issue, when it comes to fundamental values—her integrity, her honesty, her careful deliberation, her command of the details, her dedication to defending the interests of the German people while also understanding that good national leadership includes constructive engagement with the wider world and effective participation in multilateral institutions—I consider her exemplary.

So it is entirely her prerogative to decide whether she wishes to seek another term, and ultimately it is for the German electorate to determine what course the future will take. If I happened to be living here as a German citizen with a vote, I might well cast it for her. But I’m not sure whether saying that is a help or a hindrance.

On the matter of Russia, my basic stance has remained consistent since the start of my administration. Russia is a significant nation, a military power with regional clout and global reach. To resolve many of the major challenges we face worldwide, it serves our interests to engage with Russia and to secure its cooperation where possible.

We should all wish for a Russia that is stable and prosperous, with people in work, a growing economy, constructive ties with its neighbors, and a positive role in tackling major global issues, such as climate change. That’s why I’ve always pursued a constructive relationship with Russia. At the same time, I’ve been clear-eyed about the fact that there are substantial divergences between their worldview and ours.

The principles we’ve discussed—democracy, freedom of expression, adherence to international norms and the rule of law, and respect for the right of other nations to choose their own path and safeguard their sovereignty and territorial integrity—are not negotiable for us. So on matters like Ukraine and Syria, we’ve had deep disagreements. My hope is that the incoming President will adopt a similarly constructive posture: cooperate with Russia wherever our interests and values align, but also be prepared to push back firmly when Russia departs from those values and from accepted international standards.

I don’t anticipate that the President-elect will replicate our strategy point for point, but I do hope he avoids a purely transactional, realpolitik mindset that would treat relations with Russia as a series of deals to be cut even if they harm vulnerable populations, breach international norms, leave smaller nations exposed, or plant the seeds of long-term instability in regions like Syria—simply because such deals seem expedient in the moment. How he approaches this is something we’ll understand better as he assembles his team.

I am encouraged that the President-elect has stressed that the NATO commitment is unwavering. His strong affirmation of NATO as the cornerstone of our shared security architecture is extremely important.

As for my own discussions with President Putin, those took place before the U.S. election. As I’ve made clear, there is ample evidence that Russian actors have carried out cyber intrusions. This pattern is not entirely new, nor unique to Russia; we’ve seen low-level cyber activity, industrial espionage, and other problematic behavior from various states. I delivered a firm and unambiguous message that, although we recognize that intelligence gathering—by Russia or any state—will continue even when we object to it, there is a clear line between that and attempts to interfere in elections or to target private organizations or businesses. We are watching this closely, and we will respond in due course if and when we see such actions.

More broadly, this whole cyber domain is an area where the international community must develop stronger frameworks and norms to avoid sliding into a cyber arms race. Many countries now possess sophisticated capabilities, and because our economies and critical infrastructure rely so heavily on digital systems, we have to guard against turning this space into a lawless, constant low-level conflict. We’ve begun laying out some basic principles in forums like the G‑20, the G‑7, and the United Nations, but there is a great deal more work ahead.

Let me also emphasize that I’m impressed that, despite a very contentious campaign, the transition in the United States is proceeding smoothly and in accordance with democratic procedures. This is ultimately about the American people and their future, and the outgoing administration is sharing its experience and expertise with the incoming one. For us, this is an encouraging signal that we can sustain and deepen the strong cooperation between the United States and the Federal Republic of Germany—cooperation that serves both our nations well. I will carry this forward with President‑elect Trump in a spirit of openness and firm conviction.

On Russia, I can only echo what the President has just outlined. The key is upholding core principles. Speaking now from a European, specifically a German, perspective: the fact that Europe has enjoyed more than 70 years of peace rests heavily on the respect for the sovereignty and territorial integrity of every European state. Given our continent’s history, undermining that principle would send us down a very dangerous path, and we must stop that at the outset and confront any such moves decisively.

Our preference remains political solutions. That’s why we initiated the Normandy format, closely coordinating with the United States. From a German and broader European standpoint, Russia is our neighbor; consider, for instance, the position of Poland and others. We have a stake in a constructive relationship with Russia. Our histories are intertwined in many ways, but those historic links cannot prevent us from raising serious disagreements frankly when they arise—always by political means and always seeking negotiated outcomes.

And that is precisely what I intend to keep working on, with full commitment.

So, as to your specific question about whether I will declare a new candidacy: I will address that matter when the time is right, and that time is not today. I am not going to make such an announcement now.

question: Do you still intend to issue the executive order on birthright citizenship?
interview_answer: You can put that to me again a bit later, not now. Please move on.

question: Is President Putin going to greenlight a military assault on Ukraine before winter ends?
interview_answer: Look, I’ve spoken many times with Russian officials, and with Putin himself. I honestly don’t believe he’s settled on a course of action yet—I’m not even sure he knows what he intends to do. What I can say is that launching an attack on Ukraine would be an enormous error on his part. The fallout for Europe and for the wider world would be severe, and he would face very serious consequences. I’ve made that crystal clear to President Putin, both in person and over the phone. We are prepared to enact the toughest economic sanctions ever used, and there will be a substantial price to pay over time. Those measures will affect others too—our own country and our European partners—but they will hit his economy particularly hard. Still, I can’t tell you what he will ultimately decide. He is now in a military position where he could invade, if conditions on the ground near Kyiv allow it. He has that capability. But what choice he’ll make, I don’t know—and I don’t think anyone other than him does.

question: Advance presidential pardons for members of this administration
interview_answer: I am not going to talk about pardons during this briefing.

question: Is this title going to be on it?
interview_answer: I never make my entire film list public. But let me focus on what we do know right now. The FBI announced today, and we can verify, that North Korea carried out this attack. I think it tells you something revealing about North Korea that the regime chose to unleash a full-scale operation against a movie company over a comedy featuring Seth Rogen and James Flacco [Franco]. They inflicted substantial damage, and we will respond. Our response will be proportional, and we’ll do it at a time, in a way, and in a place of our choosing. That’s not the sort of thing I’m going to spell out here at a press conference. More broadly, this underscores the need for us to work with other nations to establish clear norms and rules for behavior in cyberspace. Right now, it’s essentially a Wild West. Part of the challenge is that you have fragile states capable of launching these attacks, and you also have nonstate actors who can cause massive harm. That’s why the issue of cybersecurity is so pressing. Again, this is why it’s critical for Congress to partner with us and pass a real bill that enables the kind of information sharing we require. If we don’t build the framework needed to head off these intrusions, the consequences won’t be limited to the entertainment industry; they’ll hit our entire economy in extremely serious ways. And, incidentally, I hear you’re heading to Europe. Where are you moving to?

question: How do you respond to commentators like Novak who claim you’re now even more politically alone than Richard Nixon was during Watergate?
interview_answer: What exactly does he mean by “alone”?

question: What is the President’s view of this movement, and what would he say to its supporters?
interview_answer: To be honest, I really don’t know much about that movement, except that I’m told they’re very supportive of me, which I’m grateful for, but beyond that I don’t know a lot about them. I’ve heard they’re becoming more popular. And from what people tell me, these are folks who look at what’s happening on the streets in Portland, what we’ve seen in New York City over the past 6 or 7 months—and frankly, this started almost 4 years ago when I got here, nearly 4 years, hard to believe—and they don’t like what they’re seeing in places like Portland, Chicago, New York, and other cities and States. I’ve been told they’re people who love our country and are simply upset by what’s going on. So I really don’t actually know anything concrete about them, other than that they supposedly like me. And I’m also told they want the problems in these cities we’re discussing—especially those areas—to be solved. There’s no reason Democrats can’t run a city properly. And if they can’t, we’ll send in whatever Federal resources they want—troops, law enforcement, whatever it takes. We’ll fix the situation in 24 hours or less. All right?

question: What about Michael Gove?
interview_answer: Look, I’ve known Boris for years, and I’m very fond of him. I think he’d make an excellent leader. I know Jeremy as well, and I believe he’d do an excellent job too. Michael, I don’t really know. So would Jeremy perform well? You tell me. All right? All right, great. Thanks so much, everyone. Thank you, thank you all.

question: What has President Zelenskyy said about the major counteroffensive, specifically on whether it has begun?
interview_answer: Sure, I’ll lay out the precise timing and exact locations of their advance for you. He told me—[]—oh, please. With all due respect, even if I had that level of detail, do you really think I’d reveal their offensive plans to you right now?

question: Have you received any phone calls—[inaudible]?
interview_answer:
I have, but I'm not going to get into that. What I can say is that we've had a lot of conversations overall. We've built extremely strong ties at Mike’s level and at other levels as well. In fact, as you know, a couple of representatives from North Korea are actually here with us. They're in this room right now, with a few more in the back as well. So when we moved toward this final agreement, the key point is that we weren’t walking in blindly. We came in with a deep relationship and a great deal of understanding. That’s a big reason we were able to reach this outcome.

Now I’m going to head out. I don’t know about the rest of you, but it’s been a long time since I’ve been able to slow down. So now we can relax just a bit, and then the real work starts up again. I’m grateful to all of you for being here. I hope we’ve addressed your questions. Thank you very much. And, in a way, congratulations to everyone, because this truly is—at least in my view—a very significant moment in world history. And to be honest with myself, I have to add this: I want to see it all the way through. So Mike and our entire team need to roll up their sleeves and finish the job. Otherwise, we’ve done well, but if you don’t carry the ball across the goal line, it doesn’t count for enough, right?

So thank you, and again, a kind of congratulations to everyone here. Thank you very much. I appreciate it. Thanks.

question: Worries about what President-elect Obama might do in office
interview_answer:
Look, Michael, I’m not going to sit here and guess about what choices he’ll make. Once he’s in the Oval Office, he’ll review the circumstances, weigh the facts, and then act in the way he thinks is appropriate.

And let me add this: when I walk out of here, I’m leaving the stage. I think there should only be one person under the bright lights at a time, and I’ve already had my turn. Sure, you may hear me offering an opinion every now and then, but I genuinely want him to succeed.

Some people say, Oh, you’re just saying that, it doesn’t mean anything. It’s not an empty phrase. The stakes are serious. There is still a real enemy out there. Folks can try to spin that as me setting something up, but I’m telling you the truth: there are people who would like to strike America and Americans again. That’s just the world we live in. And I sincerely wish him every success.

He’s also going to be dealing with a very tough economy. I get it—things are hard for a lot of working families. People are worried about their financial future. One of the hardest parts of my decision-making in the financial crisis was using taxpayers’ money to try to stave off a collapse, even though some of that money went to the very Wall Street institutions that helped cause the mess. I meant it when I said Wall Street went on a binge and the rest of us paid the price. But even so, President-elect Obama is going to find that the nature of these problems forces presidents into choices they weren’t initially eager to make. That’s how it was with Wall Street.

When I went back to Midland, a lot of folks said, What on earth are you doing? Those guys back East created this. And I’d say, I know, but if we hadn’t tried to solve it, your situation would be worse. In any case, I truly do want him to do well.

Sheryl [Sheryl Gay Stolberg, New York Times].

question: President Obama, can you clarify whether the blind Chinese activist Chen Guangcheng is currently being sheltered by U.S. authorities in Beijing? And how do you expect this matter to be resolved? Would the United States offer him asylum if he were to request it?
interview_answer:
I have, of course, seen the media coverage about what is happening in China, but I am not going to comment on that specific case. What I do want to stress is that whenever we meet with Chinese leaders, we raise human rights. We believe that doing so is not only consistent with our core values and our commitment to liberty and human dignity, but that China itself will ultimately be more stable and successful as it opens up and reforms its own system.

We want a strong, thriving China, and we welcome the many areas in which we are able to cooperate. At the same time, we are convinced that our partnership will be even more productive and resilient as we see continued progress on human rights inside China.

I realize the question wasn’t aimed at me, but let me briefly address North Korea. This was a major subject in my discussions with our counterparts. Our close coordination during and after the failed missile launch shows how vital our alliance is, not only for our two nations but for the broader region. Since taking office, I’ve worked to make clear to the North Korean leadership that the old pattern—provocations designed to draw attention and then extract concessions for stopping that behavior—is over.

We have made it clear that the more Pyongyang engages in destabilizing actions, the more isolated it will become, the tougher the sanctions it will face, and the deeper its diplomatic, political, and economic isolation will grow. While we cannot predict—and I do not want to speculate about—what they might do in the months ahead, I think it is evident to Pyongyang that the United States, Japan, South Korea, and others in the region are united in insisting that it live up to its obligations, respect international standards, and understand that it cannot gain anything through further provocations.

On the issue of North Korea, President Obama and I agree that the so‑called satellite launch—essentially a missile launch—undermines the efforts of the concerned parties to resolve matters through dialogue. Given the recent missile activity, there is also a significant risk that they may carry out a nuclear test, and I believe the international community as a whole must jointly urge the DPRK to exercise restraint.

More specifically, I think it is essential that the measures laid out in the recent statement by the U.N. Security Council’s president be fully implemented. Among Japan, the United States, and South Korea, as well as China and Russia, we must maintain close communication, underscore that China’s role remains crucial, and work together while preserving close coordination with the United States. That is the understanding we reached. And now I will turn to Mr. Imaichi from TBS in Japan for the next question.

question: When would you hold direct, one-on-one talks?
interview_answer: I’m not going to go into the specifics of our talks. What I can say is that our aim is to bring this to a conclusion, and we’re not going to let formal diplomatic etiquette or procedures hold us back. If Iran genuinely wants a solution, they’ll be able to achieve one.

question: If Congress fails to approve USMCA, does that effectively end NAFTA?
interview_answer:
That would really be unfortunate. But I'm not going to respond directly to that question; you already know exactly what I think about NAFTA. I've always said NAFTA is the worst trade agreement ever put in place, and I also believe the World Trade Organization hasn’t exactly been a great success either. It basically paved the way for China to take off like a rocket the moment they joined, and that was a disaster. Anyway, we’ll see what happens. It’s going to be a fascinating issue, especially with Nancy and Chuck and the rest of them obsessed with this witch hunt, because they know they can’t defeat us at the polls. They can’t beat us at the polls, and they’re not going to win the presidency. Our polling is very strong. Our internal numbers in places like Ohio and Iowa are excellent. Pennsylvania is shaping up well. North Carolina too.

We just had two elections that many people thought we would lose. In one of them, our candidate was trailing by 17 points less than a month before Election Day, and he ended up winning by a big margin—a really big margin—Dan Bishop. And then there was another race, as you all know, where the candidate was only ahead by a point or two, and he finished ahead by, what, 25 points or something huge—I’ll let you reporters confirm the exact number so you don’t claim I exaggerated on your front pages. But he won by a lot. He was up a couple of points and finished winning by something in the twenties. So things look very strong for us in North Carolina, and in Florida as well.

You even had one or two Democratic Members of Congress come out and say openly, “We can’t beat him at the ballot box, so we have to impeach him.” You heard that—Al Green. He’s something else. A real piece of work. But he said it very clearly; it was everywhere. Now, I think they’re trying to make that clip disappear. But what he basically said was: We can’t win, so let’s impeach. That’s extremely dangerous talk. Steve, go ahead.

question: What should Americans anticipate, and what would count as a successful result from this meeting?
interview_answer:
I’ll go into all of that with you after the meeting, not beforehand. Let me be clear: I’ve been at this for many years, and no serious leader lays out his negotiating hand in front of the global press before sitting down with a counterpart, whether that person is an opponent or a potential opponent. I’m not going to break that rule now.

What I can say is this: I intend to convey to President Putin that there are specific spheres where we can work together, if he decides to do so. And if he chooses not to, and instead continues the kind of behavior we’ve seen before on cyber issues and other matters, we will answer that. Our response will be proportionate.

We should be able to identify where cooperation serves our shared interests and the wider world, and try to move forward there, while in areas of disagreement, we’ll spell out our red lines.

I’ve dealt with him personally. He’s intelligent, he’s resilient, and, as we used to say in sports, he’s a formidable opponent.

But I’m not going to preview the substance of the talks now. I’ll gladly discuss what was covered once the meeting is finished, and only then.

question: What actions will the President take if Iran comes close to breaching the JCPOA limit on uranium enrichment?
interview_answer: We’ll find out.

question: How would Brazil respond if the United States carried out a military operation in Venezuela?
interview_answer: Look, there are certain matters that stop being strategic the moment you talk about them openly. So these sensitive issues, which may or may not already be under internal discussion, simply cannot be made public. I recall a debate here in the U.S. when one candidate asked another how he would defeat the Islamic State, and he replied that if he revealed his plan, he could no longer win. That’s exactly the point: it’s a strategic concern. Everything we legitimately can discuss here will be, but, regrettably, some information, if it exists, cannot and will not be debated in public.

question: Do you regard Russia as a partner or as a foe?
interview_answer: Is that question meant for me?

question: Do the Attorney General and Deputy Attorney General currently enjoy stable, long-term positions?
interview_answer: I’m not going to get into that right now; that’s something I’ll address at another time. We’re reviewing a number of matters, including the makeup of the Cabinet. I’m very pleased with most of my Cabinet. We’re considering various individuals for various roles, which is very typical after the midterm elections. I didn’t want to make any moves before the midterms. But overall, I’m extremely satisfied with my Cabinet. Mike Pompeo, for example, has really settled in remarkably well and done a fantastic job as——

question: Do you support the way Israel is acting?
interview_answer: I’m not going to give a yes-or-no judgment on that. What I will say is that my priority is achieving a durable cease-fire. For that to happen, Hamas has to stop launching rockets into Israel—there simply cannot be any lasting cease-fire while those attacks continue. In my view, the real decision lies with Hamas. The most effective path toward a stable halt to the fighting is to cooperate with Egypt to block the flow of weapons into Gaza that allows Hamas to keep firing. The states that are arming Hamas must end those transfers, and the broader international community has to maintain strong pressure on them to cut off the weapons supply. If Hamas genuinely wants a lasting cease-fire, it must stop rearming. Neighboring countries have to make serious efforts to shut down smuggling routes, which is extremely challenging—there are tunnels and many ways for those who want to undermine democracy to keep sending in arms. Now, what was the second part of your question, ma’am?

question: Did the United States use nuclear bombs on the cities of Hiroshima and Nagasaki?
interview_answer: I’m not answering that; I thought you had three separate questions?

question: If Iraqi leaders haven’t made meaningful political headway by September, would you still be prepared to maintain the troop surge regardless of General Petraeus’s recommendations?
interview_answer: I appreciate the question, but you’re asking me to predict what my perspective will be months from now, and I’m not going to do that. I’d prefer that you wait for General Petraeus to return and brief me first. After that happens, I’ll be willing to address questions of that sort.

question: By what means and on what timetable will Asad leave power?
interview_answer:
In an ideal world, Asad would have stepped down two years ago, or last year, or six months ago, or even just a couple of months back. My administration has been absolutely consistent that Asad forfeited his legitimacy the moment he turned his guns on his own citizens and started killing people who at first were peacefully demanding a greater say in how their country is run. Since then, the bloodshed has only grown. So as far as when he should go, our position is: the earlier, the better.

Now, as for the question of how that happens, I think we’ve already gone over that. There isn’t some secret, simple solution to an extraordinarily brutal and complicated crisis like this. If there were, the Prime Minister and I would have already implemented it and this would be behind us.

Instead, what we can do is keep up sustained international pressure and continue to bolster the opposition. I believe that the possibility of negotiations in Geneva, bringing in the Russians and representatives from the parties to work on a credible political transition that everyone can accept, could eventually bear fruit. In the meantime, we’re going to keep assisting the opposition and addressing the humanitarian catastrophe. We’ll do that in very close coordination with Turkey, which has a major stake in this situation and with whom we enjoy an excellent partnership.

Thank you. As you’re aware, we’ll be meeting again later this evening, and we’ll be able to examine these matters in more depth then. As I’ve mentioned, our perspectives are largely aligned, and in our talks tonight we’ll keep looking at what steps we can jointly take and what elements might go into a roadmap as we look toward Geneva and what follows it.

It is crucial that Russia and China be involved in this effort, particularly given their roles as permanent members of the U.N. Security Council. Their engagement can give the process additional momentum. The continued pressure of the broader international community is also vital. When you look at the humanitarian assistance we’ve provided so far, it now totals more than $1.5 billion.

We are maintaining an open-door policy and will go on doing so. We share a 910‑kilometer border with that country, and families live on both sides. So we will sustain these efforts. All of this matters greatly for stability in the region. On one side, we have ongoing initiatives aimed at normalizing relations between Israelis and Palestinians; we do not need new crises in the neighborhood. As you know, we previously worked to bring and Israel together to resolve their differences. We held five rounds of talks, but regrettably they stalled. I still hope that the steps we take in the coming period for regional peace will produce results, and that we can move forward with the United States in a determined way to secure peace in the region.

We are reviewing all of these issues. The actions to be considered by the U.N. Security Council and the Geneva track are key, and we will keep evaluating those among ourselves. Thank you. Thank you all. Thanks, everyone. Thank you.

question: Can you clarify the President’s current thinking, post‑midterms, regarding the Attorney General?
interview_answer: I’m going to address that at some other point, not right now. We’re reviewing a number of issues, including the Cabinet overall. I’m very pleased with the majority of my Cabinet. We’re considering various people for various roles. As you know, that’s very typical after the midterm elections. I didn’t want to make any moves before the midterms. But I can say that, generally, I’m very satisfied with my Cabinet. Mike Pompeo, for example, has really been an excellent fit. He’s done a fantastic job——

question: What unfinished priorities will still be left at the end of this President's term?
interview_answer: You really packed a lot into that, April. [] You know what, I'm not going to get into that.

question: Why aren’t you bringing Republicans into talks today so you can begin working through the details of the budget?
interview_answer: I really should’ve jotted all of this down, Jackie. I’m honestly tapped out mentally and can’t go into that.

question: Is the NHS something you're willing to discuss, sir?
interview_answer: I can’t make out what he said.

question: Appointment with Judge Barbara Lagoa
interview_answer: Sorry, I’m not able to hear you with that mask on.

question: Is NATO the organization you would ideally want to assume that command role?
interview_answer:
When it comes to Libya, the facts on the ground are still changing quickly, and the speed of any handover will depend on when our commanders determine that this initial phase of the operation has run its course. As I’ve indicated, our first priority is disabling Libya’s air defenses so that a no-fly zone can function effectively and coalition aircrews can enforce it without facing unacceptable risks. A second key piece is ensuring that the humanitarian requirements of the mission are adequately addressed.

What I can underscore is that we see this transition happening over days, not stretching into weeks. So in the coming days there will be additional clarity, and the Pentagon will be providing full briefings to the American public and to all of you in the press on how that will unfold.

NATO will be playing a role in coordination, drawing on the alliance’s exceptional capabilities. But I am not going to spell out who should or will take over that command; I’m going to leave it to Admiral Mullen and the officers directly running this operation to explain in detail how, and under whose leadership, any transfer of responsibility will occur.

On this new partnership I’m discussing today, I don’t want to give away the heart of my remarks in advance, or there’ll be no reason for anyone to listen later. What excites me most is that in a country like Chile, the relationship is not just about what the United States can provide, but also about what Chile can contribute to us.

Chile has been doing notable work on clean energy, so we’ve created a clean energy partnership. We believe we’re making strong progress on alternative energy ourselves, but there may be programs here in Chile that could be adapted to the United States.

In education, we have a long tradition of public schooling and, in my view, world-class universities. But in an increasingly interconnected world, we don’t want American students focused only inward; we want them to engage outward as well. That’s why the notion of a broad exchange initiative with the Americas can be so significant.

On security, the scourge of drug trafficking is something this region knows all too well. Colombia offers a powerful example of a society that has dramatically improved its security after years of drug-related conflict. The question becomes: What lessons can we draw from Colombia and apply to smaller Central American nations now confronting similar threats?

If Chile, the United States, Colombia, and others work together to build professional, effective security institutions in Central America to take on traffickers, that collective effort will be far more effective than if the United States tries to act alone.

So across the range of issues that matter deeply to both our nations, this new partnership will be defined by mutuality. It’s not a case of a wealthy country aiding a poor one; it’s a relationship in which a rising regional power with a strong international voice joins with us to promote greater peace and prosperity in the hemisphere and beyond.

There’s no question that, in terms of regional integration, the Americas trail behind. The easiest way to see that is to compare what’s unfolded here with what Europe has accomplished. Last century, Europe endured two world wars with tens of millions of casualties, yet ultimately had the vision to abandon fortified lines and embrace the Maastricht framework, under the leadership of statesmen like Adenauer, De Gasperi, and Schuman, building what we now know as the European Union.

In the Americas, we haven’t kept pace with that. Two decades ago, President George H.W. Bush floated the idea of a free trade area from Alaska to Tierra del Fuego, which generated enormous enthusiasm but never became a reality.

Now is the moment, because Latin America has spent far too long as the “continent of the future.” No region can remain a promise indefinitely. Latin America has come of age and must seize its responsibilities. Our main task is to make up for lost time and unlock our full potential.

We share many traits with the United States: vast lands, industrious and largely cohesive societies. We don’t suffer the same racial conflicts seen in parts of Africa, or the kinds of wars and religious strife Europe experienced. That’s why Latin America must fully commit to shaping its own destiny.

So we’re eager to hear the President’s views. We have a number of personal similarities—left-handers, Harvard backgrounds, a love of sports. He still plays basketball; I did in my day. I think the First Lady of the United States is very elegant, and he’s said similar things about Chile’s First Lady. We have many such points of connection, but the most important common ground will be what we establish later today.

If I may offer a suggestion, what we’re looking for is a partnership 2.0—one based on shared responsibilities, not a vague or purely symbolic alliance. We want a practical partnership between Latin America and the United States, grounded in common values, principles, and a shared outlook.

That partnership must be broad: it should encompass democracy, freedom, the rule of law, and the defense of human rights. We should strengthen the OAS democratic charter. It should also open markets more rapidly for goods and services. And beyond that, it must address the real foundations of 21st-century progress: quality education, science, technology, innovation, and entrepreneurship. Those are the pillars that will allow Latin America to overcome poverty and underdevelopment.

We have a great deal to learn from a country like the United States which, in just over two centuries of independence, has demonstrated extraordinary innovation and contributed so much to human progress.

This alliance must also tackle two of the century’s central challenges: energy—developing clean, safe, renewable sources—and water, which could become extremely scarce if climate change continues unchecked. And we have to address modern threats that no nation can confront alone: organized crime, terrorism, drug trafficking, climate change, and global security. These issues demand joint, not unilateral, action.

In our view, that implies the need for a new international framework to replace the post–World War II Bretton Woods system, one that reflects the realities and demands of the 21st century, where change is the only constant.

So the time has come to recover that lost ground. It’s time to leave behind a history of episodic engagement—of alternating handshakes and cold shoulders—and begin a new phase of consistent, concrete cooperation that addresses major challenges and seizes major opportunities.

The knowledge and information revolution now underway is knocking at our door. Latin America missed the Industrial Revolution; we cannot afford to be late for this transformation, which runs even deeper. It rewards countries that embrace it and leaves behind those that do not.

We say, as you’ve often said about children, that no country in our region should be left behind. Thank you.

And again, regarding who specifically should assume command, I’m not going to go beyond what I’ve already said: that decision and its details will be explained by our military leadership, not by me here today.

question: Did you back Israel’s 1981 attack on the Iraqi nuclear facility near Baghdad?
interview_answer: You know, Dave, I honestly can’t recall what I thought back in 1980. I remember I was living down in Midland, Texas, but I just don’t remember my reaction from that long ago.

question: Does that arrangement also cover Syria?
interview_answer: Elaine, with all due respect to you and to Gregory, this is hardly my first time dealing with questions like this, and I can see exactly that you’re trying to draw me into a statement here. I’m simply not going to weigh in on that at all, in any direction, Elaine.

question: Conflict with P.J. Crowley
interview_answer: As I said, I’ve already addressed the core substance of that.

question: Would you approve a funding bill that keeps the government operating at the existing sequester-reduced levels, even if that's not the outcome you would ideally choose?
interview_answer: Zach, I'm not going to—and I really don’t intend to spell this out completely for you all. But I think it’s reasonable to point out that I already agreed to a particular budget framework, with specific figures. There’s no justification for reopening that agreement. It was an arrangement Speaker Boehner and the rest of the leadership also accepted. And if the legislation that ends up on my desk matches the commitments we’ve already made, then of course I would sign it, because I want to ensure we continue doing what we need to do on behalf of the American people. Okay? Jessica [Jessica Yellin, CNN].

question: How is the President’s sudden choice to pull out of Syria seen across Europe—what is the European perspective on that withdrawal?
interview_answer: I’ve already taken a question about Syria. I’m not here to pass judgment on the decisions of other countries; my role is to explain my own country’s stance. And that stance is exactly what I laid out earlier on Syria: we unequivocally denounce the actions Turkey has taken in recent days, without any room for doubt. All right?

question: How do you foresee the Canada–U.S. relationship changing over the next four years?
interview_answer: Look, you’ve crammed a whole bunch of questions into that. Were you actually directing that to Jonathan?

question: As President, what specific steps are you taking to ensure the election is free and fair?
interview_answer: I’m supposed to do what?

question: The appeal made to President Zelenskiy concerning Vice President Biden and his son Hunter.
interview_answer: Is that question meant for me?

question: Did you bring this up directly with King Abdallah?
interview_answer: I spoke with King Abdallah about peace in the Middle East. I can’t say whether that particular issue was mentioned.

question: Would you back legislation that would grant congressional voting rights to the District of Columbia and add an additional House seat for Utah?
interview_answer: Well, I mean, this is honestly the first I’m hearing about that. I had no idea it was on the lame-duck agenda, so I’m not going to take a position on it.

question: Is the $200 million estimate correct?
interview_answer: Yes, no, maybe, maybe not. Anyway, let’s move on. I mean—whew. You clearly haven’t posed a question in a while. Look, first, I haven’t reviewed the finalized budget. Second, as Donnie Evans, the foundation’s chair, has already said, we’ll examine what has to be disclosed and then decide how to proceed. Now, there are people—well, some people—who prefer to donate without having their identities revealed, whether that’s to this foundation or any other. We’re going to factor that in. And third—what was the last part again?

question: Could you kindly ensure that at least two are made public before 2020 so the nation can start dealing with the situation we face?
interview_answer: I really want to go to Nigeria. I’d very much enjoy a visit there. It’s an incredible nation, and from what I’m told about its scenery, there’s hardly a more beautiful place anywhere. So yes, I’d like to go. As for the first part of what you asked, I honestly didn’t catch it. Please repeat it.

question: Did that statement leave you feeling let down?
interview_answer: As I’ve said, if those are indeed his remarks, I’m going to wait and talk with him so he can explain exactly what he meant, because when I met with him, he clearly recognized that it’s in the international community’s interest to ensure that that country cannot develop a nuclear weapon. That’s why he stood with us in the first vote at the U.N., and why we worked together again in the second vote to send a firm signal. If he didn’t share those worries, Bret, we wouldn’t have seen that kind of strong cooperation in both rounds at the United Nations. So I’m going to speak with him directly about all this. I also haven’t yet received a full briefing from Condi or Bob Gates about their discussions with Vladimir Putin, so I’m not going to characterize my reaction right now.

question: Will you require these financial institutions to actually use these funds to ease credit conditions and expand their lending activities?
interview_answer: Let me say this, Helene—I'm really trying not to get in front of what my Treasury Secretary will lay out, and I want all of you to attend his briefing as well; he’s going to do an outstanding job. Now, tying this back to Jake’s point earlier, a core test for me is whether credit is actually reaching those who need it. Is financing getting to businesses, big and small? Is it reaching ordinary borrowers? Are they able to function in ways that produce jobs and economic growth in communities across the country? The plan we’ve crafted is aimed at making that happen. But aside from that, I’m going to leave the specifics for Tim to unveil tomorrow so he can have the spotlight. Okay. Major Garrett from FOX News—Major, where are you?

question: Do you continue to believe Hillary Clinton will secure the nomination?
interview_answer: I'm not going to discuss politics.

question: Do you believe it’s essential to engage with Iran face‑to‑face?
interview_answer: So the issue you’re raising is whether I’ll personally sit down with Iranian officials?

question: Should the Iranian government be made aware that there will be repercussions?
interview_answer: I’ve already responded to that, Chuck — the fact is we simply don’t yet know how this situation will unfold. All right? Jake Tapper, ABC News.

question: What, if anything, should people in the U.S. anticipate Congress will do on abortion after the midterms?
interview_answer: I don’t think they should really count on anything beyond us sticking to our current stances. I’m not going to address this topic any further. Honestly, I shouldn’t have even responded to your question.

question: Are you going to sit down with Rouhani?
interview_answer: What did you say?

question: Is there any chance Huawei will be taken off the entity list?
interview_answer: I’m not going to get into that right now. We’re examining the issue very closely. Huawei is a major factor for us, both nationally and for our intelligence and security agencies. We’re very well informed about Huawei. But I’m not going to address that at this moment; I don’t think it’s the right time. Beyond what I’ve already said, we’re not treating it as a main topic of discussion right now. We’ll come back to it later, all right?

question: Judging how reliable Donald Trump is in handling U.S. nuclear arms.
interview_answer: Regarding that second question—and really this applies to any further Trump questions—I’m not going to give you an assessment. I’d simply urge all of you to draw your own conclusions. I’ve stressed this repeatedly already. Just pay attention to what Mr. Trump himself says and then decide for yourselves how secure you are with him overseeing our nuclear arsenal.

question: So there’s no chance of a shutdown during the lame-duck session?
interview_answer: I’m not sure. I can’t say that for certain. No, I’m not going to promise that, although it could happen.

question: Has your administration reached any conclusion about whether President Putin intends to attack or move into Ukraine, as you previously suggested?
interview_answer:
Look, the only thing I can say with any certainty is that this choice belongs entirely, exclusively, and completely to Putin. No one else is going to take that decision for him; no one else is really going to determine it. It’s his call, and his alone.

Frankly, I wouldn’t be surprised if it depends on something as arbitrary as what mood he wakes up in on a given day. And it would not be unreasonable, if he chose to, for him to frame all this as a discussion about strategic doctrine and about force deployments in Europe and in the European parts of Russia.

But I do not know whether he’s actually decided to go that route or not. Up to now, across the three sets of talks we’ve had—through the OSCE and—well, in any case—none of that has produced a clear outcome, because, from what my Secretary of State, my National Security Adviser, and other senior officials involved are telling me, even the people they’re meeting with may not know what he’s planning.

So the response is this: based on several possible scenarios of what he might do—for instance, if he wanted to move in and seize the entire country, especially approaching from the north, from Belarus, he would need to wait for harder, frozen ground to move his forces across.

If he’s heading in a different direction—one where he mainly wants to negotiate about what’s happening—we are, for our part, still strengthening Ukraine’s defensive capabilities. We’re in discussions about developments in the Baltic and the Black Sea, and so on. There are many factors he’s probably weighing: how fast he can carry out whatever he decides to do and what exactly that is.

But he is not uninformed. I am convinced he is weighing the short-, medium-, and long-term consequences for Russia. And, to be clear, I do not believe he has reached a final decision yet—so I cannot tell you that he has chosen to invade or not.

question: On what grounds, Mr. President, are they being granted an exemption?
interview_answer: That decision was put forward to me by a team of experts, and we’re currently reviewing it in light of the latest figures we’re seeing. It’s possible we’ll end up moving them into the group of nations that we will, in effect, restrict—or whatever final term we use—during this period. Their case numbers have actually risen quite sharply in the last day, so we might add them, and maybe a few others, and we might also, to be honest, consider removing some countries as well.

question: How do you square your decision to reduce Manning’s sentence with WikiLeaks’ alleged role in Russia’s hacking during last year’s election?
interview_answer:
To begin, let me emphasize that Chelsea Manning has already endured a very severe prison term. So anyone contemplating leaking sensitive, classified material would not reasonably conclude from her case that such actions carry no consequences. From my perspective, she had a full trial, received due process, accepted responsibility for what she did, and the punishment imposed on her was extremely harsh—harsher than what many other leakers have faced. Given the substantial time she has already served, I determined that commuting, rather than pardoning, her sentence was warranted.

I am fully confident that justice has been done here, and that a strong signal still goes out that on matters of national security, people who may have valid concerns about government behavior or the conduct of their superiors or agencies should use the formal channels available and take advantage of the whistleblower protections we have established. I understand there are critics who say those protections don’t go far enough, and all of us who have worked in large organizations know it’s possible to disagree with policies. But when we talk about national security, we are often talking about people in dangerous environments whose lives could be endangered, or about the safety, effectiveness, and operational capability of our military, intelligence services, and diplomatic missions. That reality has to stay front and center.

On the matter of WikiLeaks, I do not view there as being any inconsistency. Let me be explicit: I have not, as a rule, weighed in on WikiLeaks itself. The intelligence community’s findings about Russian cyber activities did not definitively establish whether WikiLeaks knowingly cooperated in serving as the channel for the DNC emails that were released. I don’t devote much attention to Mr. Assange’s social media statements, so they played no role in this decision. And I am not going to discuss any potential criminal cases, charges, or extradition issues involving him—those are questions you’d need to put to the Justice Department.

What I can address in broader terms is that, in this digital era, we constantly have to recalibrate the balance between accountability, openness, and transparency—which are core to our democracy—and the need to protect ourselves against adversaries and malicious actors. Those bad actors may exploit our openness to commit financial crimes, carry out terrorism, or meddle in our elections.

We will have to keep strengthening a framework that both preserves the most important features of our democratic system and allows our national security and intelligence professionals to do their jobs without telegraphing our plans to our enemies, while still ensuring that citizens remain informed about what their government is doing in their name.

But strictly regarding Chelsea Manning, I evaluated the specific facts of her case the same way I have in every commutation or pardon I’ve granted, and I concluded that, considering all the relevant factors, commuting her sentence was the right and justified course of action. I am not going to go beyond that into any further comment on WikiLeaks itself.

question: Is it accurate that the White House will push the NIH and FDA to move this forward and make it available as quickly as possible?
interview_answer: Look, if the figures are as strong as what I’m being told—people are saying above 50 percent, which is excellent. And we’ve signed off on other things that are around 31 percent, and that’s still acceptable; that’s not terrible at all. They’ve really made a big difference. But as for what you’re asking, no—I wasn’t aware of that, and what you’re saying actually catches me off guard. I can’t really address it right now, but we’ll look into it immediately after this.

question: How should we understand this announcement—does it mark the conclusion of the war, or is it Russia’s attempt to gain breathing space and reorganize for a renewed offensive?
interview_answer: Time will tell. I’m not going to draw any conclusions about it until we see what they actually do. We’ll have to wait to see whether they act on what they’re now signaling. There are negotiations under way today—some in Turkey and others elsewhere. I also just met with the leaders of four NATO partners—France, Germany, the United States, and the United Kingdom—and there’s broad agreement that we should hold off on interpreting this and instead wait to see what concrete steps they take. In the meantime, we will maintain strong sanctions, we will keep supplying Ukraine with what it needs to defend itself, and we will continue to monitor developments very closely. Thank you. Let me turn to Dawn Tan from Channel NewsAsia.

question: 2) When you say the U.S. will increase support for Iraq, does that mean sending more American troops?
interview_answer:
On that second point, I’m not going to speculate about a scenario where we don’t succeed. My objective is to succeed, and I expect we’ll get a vote soon, because I believe that’s the right course of action.

Regarding ISIL, we’ve made real headway in driving them out of areas they once held or disrupted, but we’ve also seen places like Ramadi where they’re pushed out of one location and then reappear somewhere else. They’re flexible, they’re ruthless, and they exploit openings. So one key area where we have to get better is how quickly we can train Iraqi forces. In those cases where we’ve directly trained and equipped Iraqi units and maintained a train-and-assist posture, they’ve performed well. Where we haven’t done that, issues like low morale or inadequate equipment have weakened Iraqi security forces.

Our goal is to increase the number of Iraqi units that are newly trained, properly armed, and clearly directed. Prime Minister Abadi shares that goal. We’re therefore looking at a variety of options to speed up the training and equipping of Iraqi forces so they have a coherent strategy and strong leadership.

Once the Pentagon brings me a finalized proposal, I’ll explain it to the American people. But at this stage, I’m not going to get into specifics—I don’t yet have a fully developed strategy, in part because it depends on decisions the Iraqis still need to make about recruitment and training. Until those pieces are settled, I can’t provide the details you’re asking for.

question: Did Fidel Castro personally participate in any way in the negotiations when the United States first approached Cuba?
interview_answer: Lesley, I’m going to stop you right there. I’m not going to get into that; we’re short on time.

question: Is President Karzai right to hold Pakistan's intelligence agencies responsible for the latest terrorist strike in Afghanistan?
interview_answer:
To begin with, we’re going to look carefully into his accusation, and we’ll coordinate with his intelligence service to fully examine what he’s alleged. What is clear, though, is that certain violent extremists are coming from areas inside Pakistan into Afghanistan. That worries us, it worries Afghanistan, and it ought to worry Pakistan as well. We’re all up against the same threat: extremists who resort to violence to derail democracy or stop it from taking root.

Al-Qaida elements are present in that region. We’ve dealt some very tough blows to Al-Qaida—struck them hard and weakened them across the globe, including inside Pakistan. And we will keep up that pressure on Al-Qaida together with our Pakistani partners.

I certainly trust that the Pakistani authorities recognize how dangerous it is to have extremists operating on their soil. My sense is that they do. In fact, I’ll have another chance to discuss this in more depth on Monday with Pakistan’s Prime Minister. Pakistan is a partner; Pakistan is a friend. And let me stress again: the United States, Pakistan, and Afghanistan all face the same adversary.

I vividly recall the session I hosted at the White House with President Musharraf and President Karzai. We agreed then on the importance of cooperation across the border to stop militants from training, slipping into Afghanistan, and then heading back home with combat experience that could be turned against their own government.

We saw some encouraging signs of progress. Of course, it remains a very difficult struggle. We were encouraged by the provincial elections in that region. We’re going to keep supporting the Afghan Government as it confronts extremists, while also pursuing a solid counterinsurgency approach that uses assistance to spur economic growth. This is a hard problem. But the three of us working together can manage it far more effectively than acting alone.

So I’m not going to pass judgment right now on President Karzai’s specific claim. We’ll let our joint investigation run its course. Thanks for your time; I appreciate it.

question: How worried should people be about whether Afghan security forces can handle major attacks in Kabul?
interview_answer:
First, remember that our troop reduction hasn’t even started yet. We’ve always recognized that this is a dangerous environment, that the Taliban still operates there, and that incidents like this will continue to occur from time to time. What really matters is whether, overall, Afghan capabilities are growing. Kabul, for instance, where a very large share of Afghans live, has for quite a while now been primarily under Afghan security control. And they’ve performed fairly well. The city is considerably safer than it used to be, and Afghan units in Kabul are far more competent than they were in the past.

That does not imply that attacks like this won’t still happen; in fact, episodes like this are likely for some time to come. Our mission isn’t finished. But, as I said in my remarks, the broader tide of conflict is moving in the right direction. We have moved into a phase of transition. And similar to what we saw in Iraq—where we reduced our forces, with the rest leaving by the end of the year, yet Iraq has managed to keep a functioning democracy and significantly reduce violence—we believe a comparable strategy is appropriate in Afghanistan.

Even so, in Iraq you still occasionally see bombings and other violence. These are societies emerging from years of war and turmoil. They are inherently risky places. So they aren’t going to be completely secure, even if we kept large numbers of our own troops there. I’m not going to give a direct guarantee on individual incidents; what we can do is enhance the Afghan people’s ability to protect themselves. Jim Sciutto, ABC News.

question: What was the distance between the White House and the site of the shooting?
interview_answer: The full details on that will be provided to you in an upcoming briefing.
