question: Do you approve of Postmaster General DeJoy having allegedly asked staff at his former private firm to contribute to Republicans and then paying them back afterward?
interview_answer: Well, I really can’t speak to that right now because I don’t know much about it. I glanced at a report earlier today, but beyond that, I’d need to review the details. From what I understand, he’s highly regarded. My understanding is that he was confirmed with strong backing from both sides. I’m not clear on the specifics of these claims. I expect I’ll get fully briefed soon. I only came across the story this morning, same as you.

question: How do you explain that the share of people who have died is so much higher in the United States than elsewhere?
interview_answer: Look, a big part of our figures comes from—well, New York went through an extremely difficult period, as everyone knows. New York, New Jersey—that whole region. And if you remove them—just as an illustration—compare Florida to New York. That’s not criticizing New York. It was just a very hard situation there. People are packed together. It’s dense. It’s complicated. But if you set that aside, our figures are among the lowest. And even including it—I’m not going to go into the exact numbers right now; I’ll have to follow up on that, but we’re still among the lowest. They’ve handled it incredibly well. Okay, next question.

question: It appears you're edging toward doing away with the filibuster. Is that right?
interview_answer: I’ve already responded to that./Tax Policy Changes/Government Spending/The Administration’s Priorities

question: Russia says it will reduce its military activity around Kyiv and another northern Ukrainian city. Your response?
interview_answer: We’ll have to wait and see. I’m not going to draw any conclusions about that until I actually see what they do on the ground. We’ll find out whether they back up their words with real action. Talks are taking place today—really continuing—in Turkey and elsewhere. I just met with the leaders of four NATO partners: France, Germany, the U.S., and the U.K. And there was broad agreement that we should hold off on judging this and see what they actually put forward and how they behave. In the meantime, we are not changing course: the sanctions will remain robust, we will go on supplying Ukraine with what it needs to defend itself, and we will keep watching developments very closely. Thank you. I’ll now turn to Dawn Tan from Channel NewsAsia.

question: Do you believe Saudi Arabia bears responsibility for having him killed?
interview_answer: I’ll be in a much better position to speak on that in about a week, as I continue to work very closely with Congress. We’re coordinating together, some extremely capable people. We’re engaging with Congress, with Turkey, and with Saudi Arabia. I’m in the process of reaching a very firm view. Next question, Jon. Go ahead, Jon.

question: Had you owned hotels, properties, and other global business interests before assuming the presidency, would you have considered it proper to sell them and place the money from those sales into a blind trust?
interview_answer: That initial question was meant rhetorically, not for an answer.

question: For what time period will Israel put the West Bank annexation plan on hold?
interview_answer: You tell me—how long do you think it will be? Look, we're fully invested in pursuing a path of peace. We've reached an understanding with the Emirates, and now we'll be working through all the specifics—embassies, air routes, trade—and then seeking to extend that framework across the broader region. I cannot give you a timeline for how long that will take. What I can say is that we've chosen to focus on peace rather than sovereignty for now, but the sovereignty option remains. It’s simply being postponed while we exhaust every opportunity for peace.

question: By arranging your own talks in Washington, haven’t you effectively sabotaged his push to set high-level targets here?
interview_answer:
I’ll tackle the second part of your question first. I’m getting on in years, so you may need to remind me of how you started. The Kyoto framework might work for some nations; it may have been sensible for those that signed on. It simply wasn’t the right fit for the United States. So I was straightforward about that. And remember, even before I got to Washington, the U.S. Senate had the opportunity to signal whether Kyoto was right for America. They voted 95–0 against it. So this isn’t just my personal view—there were plenty of people who believed Kyoto wasn’t the appropriate path forward.

We chose another route: tightening energy-efficiency standards by driving innovation in new technologies. As I’ve already mentioned, last year we cut greenhouse gas emissions in the United States while our economy expanded by 3 percent. I’m not sure many other countries can point to that kind of outcome, but we can, because our emphasis on deploying new technologies is delivering results.

I also think we can make much more progress in cutting our dependence on foreign oil. That might be unsettling to some countries that sell energy, but we have to lessen our reliance on imported oil. That’s why we’ve proposed what we call the 20-in-10 initiative. Over the next decade, we intend to reduce gasoline consumption by 20 percent through ethanol and other advanced technologies. I’m confident battery advances are on the way so that, say, drivers in Sydney can travel the first 40 miles on battery power without their cars looking like golf carts.

Put differently, new technologies are coming. The core challenge is how to speed their introduction into the marketplace. From our perspective, Kyoto didn’t accomplish that. In fact, if you take stock of which countries are actually meeting their Kyoto commitments, you’ll likely see why a different model might be more effective. We turned down Kyoto because it wouldn’t have let us do what we believe we must: grow our economy, reduce our reliance on foreign oil, and still act responsibly as guardians of the environment.

John and I have discussed his intention to highlight climate change at APEC, and I’ve strongly backed that. I also reminded him that at the G‑8 I made the case to our partners that, if we’re serious about addressing global climate change, every major player needs a seat at the table. That includes countries like China and India. If they feel that solutions are being imposed on them without a voice in setting shared goals, they’ll walk away. And you can’t have an effective global climate strategy if a country like China isn’t participating.

I think the Prime Minister showed real foresight in this new initiative. He called for lowering tariffs that block the transfer of technologies that, for example, would help China burn coal more cleanly. Up to now, those environmental industries have been shielded by trade barriers. He wants those walls down, and we’re firmly behind him, so that advanced technologies can move more easily from countries that possess them to those that don’t.

Fundamentally, we have to ask: how quickly can we get key technologies—like carbon capture for coal and new nuclear fuel-cycle technologies—into commercial use? And once they’re viable, can we help developing countries gain access to them? If not, what we’re doing won’t be truly effective.

I’m convinced the approach we’ve outlined is the most realistic and effective way to tackle this problem. I also value the Prime Minister’s role in putting this matter front and center and in emphasizing energy security and efficiency standards as part of the broader climate picture. His leadership on this is evident, and I’m glad to be here focusing on these issues with him. Thank you.

question:
Do you back Congressman Chaka Fattah’s proposal, the Debt Free America Act?
- And are you throwing your support behind the Republican proposal that mirrors Congressman Fattah’s bill?
interview_answer:
At this point, I’m not going to take a position on any specific piece of legislation. Instead, let me address the larger issue you’re raising, April.

This downturn has been painful for everyone, but it has hit people with the fewest resources the hardest. From the moment I entered office, what has driven me is the situation of those families who play by the rules every day, take care of their kids, try to stay afloat, and yet feel like they’re sliding backward no matter how much effort they put in.

I recently got a letter from a woman whose husband lost his job, went out day after day looking for work, finally landed a position, and for a short while they began to feel like things were settling down. Then, six months later, he was laid off again. Now they’re back on the job hunt, trying to figure out how to cover the bills. There are hundreds of thousands of people just like them who are living through the toughest economy they’ve ever known.

When I came into office, we moved quickly and aggressively to pull the economy back from the brink of another Great Depression and stabilize it. By and large, we accomplished that stabilization. But we stabilized the economy at a point where unemployment remained unacceptably high and growth wasn’t strong enough to recover all the jobs that were lost before I took office and in the early months afterward.

So unemployment has been stubbornly high. There are several ways to tackle that. First, we have to ensure the overall economy is expanding. That’s why we’ve consistently taken steps to put more money in people’s pockets so they can spend it—that’s the purpose behind the payroll tax cuts.

We’ve also acted to encourage businesses to invest. That’s what the small‑business tax cuts and incentives for companies to buy new plants and equipment were about—along with eliminating capital gains taxes for many small businesses. Those policies were designed to give firms stronger reasons to expand and hire.

We’ve worked to improve training programs for workers who are forced to move out of industries that may never fully come back, so they can gain the skills needed for the jobs that actually exist now and in the future.

On top of that, we’ve proposed a range of measures to review and streamline regulations that might be unnecessarily holding businesses back from investing. We’re looking carefully at the costs and benefits of these rules, and if some don’t deliver the promised protections for public health, clean air and water, or worker safety, then we should remove or revise them.

So we’ve been considering the full set of tools available. But we’re now confronting a situation where, because growth has been slower than we’d like and because the recession and financial crisis left us with large deficits and debt, pursuing an approach that would cost trillions of dollars simply isn’t on the table. We don’t have that kind of fiscal space.

What we can do is address our long‑term deficit and debt challenges in a serious way so that once that burden is lifted, we can return to a discussion about what additional, targeted steps might help generate jobs—especially in areas like infrastructure.

For instance, the infrastructure bank we’ve proposed is relatively modest. But you can imagine a much larger initiative to rebuild roads, bridges, ports, schools, broadband networks, and a smarter electric grid—putting construction workers back on the job right away. I can envision a bold program like that, one that I believe the American people would support because it would boost the economy not just over the next year or two, but for decades to come.

However, we can’t even begin that conversation if people doubt that our fiscal house is in order. So the idea is: let’s deal with the long‑term debt and deficit problem now, get that concern off the table, and then, on more solid fiscal ground, we’ll be in a much stronger position to make the kinds of investments we need to compete and prosper in the future.

This really isn’t about left versus right, or conservative versus liberal. It’s about governing intelligently, recognizing that we face both immediate and longer‑term challenges. If we can resolve some of the long‑term issues, that frees us up to focus more energy and resources on the short‑term ones as well.

All right? Thanks very much, everyone.

question: At what point should we expect you to make that announcement?
interview_answer: Look, some areas are moving forward, others less so, as everyone can see. But several really are advancing. And as they keep advancing, as they reach the figures that all of us agree are appropriate—that they recognize are appropriate and we recognize are appropriate—we’ll start opening things up. Some are making very fast progress. Our goal is to reopen as soon as we possibly can. Next question, please. Next question.

question: Are you currently associated with the New York Times?
interview_answer: You’re from where now? From where exactly?

question: What do you say to critics who argue that your administration is ignoring the persecution of religious minorities and the suppression of dissent occurring under Prime Minister Modi’s government?
interview_answer: On your first question, the response is: No. When we engage with allies and partners around the globe, India included, I’m not going to start tailoring or avoiding my honest views about the facts of our relationship with India—or with China—that’s simply not something I’m willing to alter. I’ve long said that the overheated rhetoric about our ties with China dramatically unraveling and shifting is overblown—we did have an episode that caused some, let’s say, uncertainty. But the Secretary of State just wrapped up a very productive visit to China, and I anticipate meeting with President Xi relatively soon. I don’t see that situation having any real impact. Now, what was the second part of your question?

question: Update on the event that occurred near California’s shoreline
interview_answer: I’m actually set to receive a briefing shortly. That meeting is planned to take place a bit later today.

question: What possible response is the administration weighing after the Chinese government expelled reporters from The Wall Street Journal?
interview_answer: I haven’t been fully briefed on that situation yet. We’re going to review it. We will review it. It’s something I really don’t like to see. I don’t think it’s right, because we allow very broad access. Some people even say we allow too much. I don’t agree with that. I think we offer very broad access, so I don’t think this is right. But we’ll be reaching a decision on that in the near future. All right? Thanks. That’s a good question. Next, please. Go ahead, go ahead.

question: Requesting details about the members of the President's Cabinet.
interview_answer: You say it, not me.

question: Would you back the WHO again if Tedros were removed right away?
interview_answer: At this point, we're still conducting a review. I don't personally know this individual, though I'm aware there have been issues. And the situation has treated the United States very poorly, much like the World Trade Organization has treated us very, very poorly—though they’re finally starting to change. When they label China a developing country and, because of that label, China takes enormous advantage of the United States—why didn’t previous Presidents put a stop to it? I’ve been raising this from the moment I took office, and we’re examining it extremely closely. The WHO, the WTO—I have serious concerns about both. I honestly couldn’t tell you which one is worse right now, if you want the truth, and I’m not going to give you a specific answer on that today. We’ll sort it out.

question: What specific reassurance can you offer employees who are being told to return to their jobs but are afraid to go back?
interview_answer:
That really is a decision for the governors to handle. They’re going to be the ones who insist that conditions are safe. I do think businesses can put testing in place, maybe every week; you don’t need to test the same employee daily, but they can be tested. They can use temperature checks. They can implement a range of precautions.

And remember, this is just for a temporary phase. Ultimately, we want to get back to how things used to be. We want fans sitting right next to each other at games again. We’re not planning to permanently remove every other seat in baseball parks and football venues. Our goal is to return to the old normal.

I want people to really hear that. We’re not going to stay in this setup. We’ve got hundreds of reporters who’d love to be packed in the briefing room. Look at this arrangement—look how it appears. I don’t even like the look of it, even though having fewer reporters isn’t exactly a problem for me.

But just look at how it all looks. I’ve never seen anything quite like it, John, I really haven’t. But there are a lot of people—we do not want this to be the lasting situation. I don’t want it. I really don’t want this.

At some point, we’ll return to the point where a restaurant with 150 seats has 150 seats again, not 50, because they can’t survive with only 50. And beyond that, the atmosphere is better when they’re full. We’re going to have to adapt in certain ways. I’m not sure people will be as quick to shake hands as before. Some probably will.

I even asked some hospital staff today, “Do you think people will go back to shaking hands?” And I was actually a bit surprised that most of them said, “Probably,” because there’s something about it. I was never a big handshaker myself, but once I got into politics I thought, well, I’d better start shaking hands.

Next question, John.

question: Where does the President currently stand, post‑midterms, on the future of the Deputy Attorney General?
interview_answer: I’m not going to get into that right now; that’s something I’d prefer to address at another point. We’re reviewing a number of matters, including Cabinet roles. Overall, I’m very satisfied with most members of my Cabinet. We’re considering various people for various posts, which is very typical after midterm elections. I chose not to make any changes before the midterms. But I can say that, generally, I’m very pleased with my Cabinet. Mike Pompeo, in particular, has really settled in remarkably well and done a fantastic job—

question: Possible U.S. role in this particular issue
interview_answer: I’m not going to address that topic. Would you like to ask something else?

question: Is there a negotiable deal that could enact a ban on assault weapons?
interview_answer: I’ll lay out those specifics later this week. Next question.

question: Will the border end up more open or tighter?
interview_answer: Look, you’ve crammed a whole bunch of different questions into that. Were you asking Jonathan that? What is this—

question: Given how popular Kimberly, Don Jr., and Ivanka Trump are with Indian Americans, will they actively campaign among Indian American communities to promote your positions on the relationship between India and the United States?
interview_answer:
I didn’t quite catch every part of what you asked, but I did hear the names of those young people. They’re impressive, and I know they have a very strong connection to India—just as I do. Prime Minister Modi is a personal friend, and he’s doing an excellent job under very tough circumstances—none of it is easy, but he’s handling it very well.

Since you raised it, we’re also looking at what’s happening between China and India. As you know, there have been serious clashes along their border. It’s a very rough, very troubling situation, and we are fully prepared to assist both sides. If there’s any way we can help, we’re eager to be involved, and we are in communication with both countries about that.

I do appreciate your kind words. They have great respect for India, I have great respect for India, and I have great respect for your Prime Minister. Thank you.

question: So, is this that sort of deal?
interview_answer: Well, that actually raises an intriguing point about whether there even is a formal signing in the strict sense, because, as I’ve mentioned, this isn’t a legally enforceable pact. I’m not familiar with the exact procedures on that. What I can say is that it represents a pledge by the United States that we consider extremely significant. Okay, thank you all.

question: Deadline for Prime Minister Maliki to achieve his objectives
interview_answer: Could you repeat the first section of what you asked?

question: So are you ruling out any chance of a shutdown in the lame-duck session?
interview_answer: I really don’t know. I can’t give you an answer on that. No, I won’t make that kind of commitment, although it could happen.

question: So, is it both?
interview_answer: Maybe it’s both. Are you two together? Go on.

question: What do you think is going to happen with that?
interview_answer: I really can't say. I haven’t been paying close attention to it. Honestly, I just hope things turn out well for her. I’ve run into her quite a few times over the years, especially when I was living in Palm Beach and, as I understand it, they were in Palm Beach too. But I genuinely wish her the best, whatever’s going on. I’m not familiar with what’s happening with Prince Andrew. I just don’t know. I’m not informed about it. Yes, next question, please.

question: Did anyone on your team in government tell him not to proceed?
interview_answer:
I never spoke with him about it, and I just don’t know. I have no idea what was said in any of those discussions, and I don’t see Hamas altering its behavior. It’s on Hamas to change. You sit down with these folks, they make promises in private and then act differently in public. That’s how it’s gone for 7½ years in my administration, watching Hamas act as a force for instability.

I did back the elections, incidentally. And interestingly, they beat Fatah by campaigning against corruption. Tragically, once they gained authority, they failed to deliver for people living in Gaza.

My focus, when I travel to the Middle East, is to keep working with both the Israelis and President Abbas and his administration on several tracks: helping them shape a shared vision and identify common ground; working with Israel to give Palestinians in the West Bank more responsibility for security; reducing the obstacles they face; and helping spur economic activity and growth. There are some promising projects being put together there.

I still believe we can reach an agreement before I leave office. Condi is going back out to the region. I’ve spoken with President Abbas here in the Oval Office, and I’m in regular contact with Prime Minister Olmert; the overall mood is constructive. There is a real recognition that defining a Palestinian state matters.

But as for Hamas—look, if you’re Israel and rockets are being fired into your territory, you’re going to act to protect yourself. The question is, why is Hamas firing those rockets? One reason is they’re trying to undermine stability and sow turmoil and uncertainty. And as for judging whether anyone’s talks with them made a difference, the only way to know is to watch what Hamas does from here on out. That’s all I can say on those conversations. Richard.

question: Have you seen the New Yorker article stating that the Red Cross has judged the CIA’s detention-site interrogation program to be effectively torture?
interview_answer:
I’ve already said that closing Guantanamo should be a national objective. I’ve also explained that one major reason it hasn’t happened yet is that many countries are hesitant to repatriate some of the individuals currently held there. Put simply, to close the facility we first have to find somewhere for these detainees to go. You may have noticed the recent Senate resolution where all but three Senators opposed bringing these prisoners into the United States. I don’t recall if the exact tally was 97–3, but it was something like that. In short, Peter, a big part of this debate is the very practical question of where these people can be sent.

You’ve suggested that nothing has been done. I emphatically reject that. First, we are actively working with other governments to transfer detainees back to their home countries. That’s not a simple task. Many nations do not want dangerous individuals living among them, and many of these detainees are in fact dangerous. Second, we’re trying to ensure that when we do send them back, they are treated as humanely as possible.

Another challenge involves bringing people to trial. I am very eager to see prosecutions proceed. The courts have been involved in determining how that should work, and the Defense Department is developing procedures to get those trials underway. The faster we can move that process forward, the better, in my view.

I’m not going to speculate on whether Guantanamo will ultimately be closed. What I can say is that the situation is highly complex. I expressed a goal. Whether we can fully realize it, we’re certainly going to try. But it is far more complicated than it might appear at first glance. Again, I’d point you to that Senate vote: when Senators were asked if they wanted to shut down Guantanamo and accept some of those detainees in their own states, there was very little enthusiasm. Only about three members said they were fine with that.

Your next question, sir?

question: Please allow a question from a foreign press outlet.
interview_answer: Which country?

question: President Uribe's view on whether he believes Chavez's clout in the region is exaggerated
interview_answer: And what exactly is my stance supposed to be?

question: Given Vice President Biden’s remark that the contest is still wide open, do you agree it remains unsettled?
interview_answer:
I’ve already run my final campaign, and I’m not going to start predicting the outcomes of future races. That’s what you all get paid to do. There’s certainly no lack of commentators eager to weigh in on that; I’m just not going to be one of them.

On the issue of Cuba, our goal is not to engineer regime change. Our objective is to ensure that the Cuban people have greater freedom, a stronger voice in shaping their own future, and a vibrant civil society.

Cuba is going to change over time no matter what we do. Some of that will come simply from generational shifts. If you listened to President Castro this morning, much of what he raised involved events that happened before I was even born. Part of what I’m saying here is that the Cold War is over. We still face many serious global challenges, and we will continue to have real disagreements with Cuba—not only about how the Cuban Government treats its own citizens, but also about broader regional matters. At the same time, there will be areas where we can work together. Cuban medical teams made a real contribution during the Ebola outbreak; their work in Haiti after the earthquake also mattered. So there are likely to be spheres of cooperation as well.

What I told President Castro is what I tell leaders throughout the hemisphere: We have our perspective, and we will voice it openly. But I believe the most effective way to advance the values we care about is through persuasion. That will be our main approach on many of these questions, in large part because they do not directly implicate our core national security.

We should be very straightforward: Cuba does not pose a threat to the United States. That doesn’t mean there aren’t serious differences. But when I look at the threats that concern me—from ISIL to the possibility of Iran obtaining a nuclear weapon, to the conflicts in Yemen and Libya, to Boko Haram, to Russian aggression in Ukraine and what that means for our allies, and even to climate change—I can list quite a few that rank higher.

Our strategy, then, has to focus on working with partners in the region, making clear what our principles are, what we stand for, and what we believe succeeds or fails. Too often, when we intervene in ways that go beyond persuasion, it has the opposite effect and ends up hurting our own goals. That’s part of our history, and it’s why some governments keep trying to blame us for their own failures at home. We should remove that excuse and demonstrate that we’re ready to cooperate and engage with everyone who’s interested in expanding opportunity, prosperity, and security for people across the region.

Major Garrett, CBS News.

question: Did anyone brief you about new COVID-19 cases at the White House today?
interview_answer: I’m not catching what you’re saying—I really can’t hear, sorry.

question: Can you confirm whether the administration has asked Republicans to insert provisions about the downtown D.C. FBI headquarters into their coronavirus relief legislation?
interview_answer:
I’m not actually sure they’re including it in this particular package, but I do know they’re looking to put up a new FBI headquarters. The current building is very outdated, and, as you’ve probably heard, it was never constructed to a very high standard. It’s got serious issues—safety concerns, exterior panels coming loose, chunks of concrete falling off the structure.

They want to rebuild right where it is now. They had been considering locations much farther from Washington, and I told them, honestly, you have to be close to the Justice Department. There’s really no site better than the one they already have. They were exploring sites in Maryland and Virginia, different areas, but those would have been too far away.

So I’ve been pushing them to move forward with a new facility. And when you look at it, you’ve got two options: you can renovate the existing building—which is a poor building—or you can demolish it and put up a first‑class FBI headquarters that will last a century and be really outstanding. They’re even talking about putting running tracks up on the roof, because FBI personnel like to work out. You could literally have quarter‑mile tracks on top; it’s a huge footprint, very wide.

So, in my view, the best approach is to construct a new building so it serves them well for a long time. In this case, renovation will never match a brand‑new facility. I know there are discussions going on—whether it ends up in this bill or in some other measure, I can’t say. But the FBI does need a new building, and we’ll make it happen. Thanks, everybody. Thank you. Thank you very much.

question: Is completely ruling out giving up any Ukrainian land even an option?
interview_answer: On that second part, my response is yes. But that really hinges on what the G‑20 decides. It actually came up in our discussions today, and I suggested that if that arrangement isn’t possible—if Indonesia and some others don’t consent—then, in my opinion, we should push for both parties to be allowed to take part in the sessions, essentially to attend the G‑20 and sit in as observers. Now, on the earlier point—could you remind me what the first question was?

question: Will you formally approve Mexico’s certification?
interview_answer: I didn’t catch the interpretation for that question. It seemed like an excellent one, though. Now I believe the translation’s coming through—please, you go ahead instead.

question: Is the upcoming meeting going to be held soon?
interview_answer: Honestly, I’m not able to say. It could take place in the near future, or it might not happen for quite a while. I’m not going to give you a specific answer. I’d like it to be soon, but it could just as easily be delayed for a long time.

question: Do you feel there is acceptance of the troops?
interview_answer: That’s not a decision for us to make. That is a matter for the United States. The actual size of the forces will not alter the realities in Afghanistan. What will matter for Afghanistan and for the wider region is the overall nature of the relationship, not the headcount. Determining precise troop figures is the responsibility of the military, and Afghanistan has no particular stake in how many are sent or how they’re positioned. Afghan journalists here? English-language media? [He then spoke briefly in Dari, with no transcript provided.]

question: Is the President willing to take back Bill Cosby’s Medal of Freedom?
interview_answer: You’ve packed quite a bit into that, April. Listen, I’m not going to get into that.

question: What is the suspect’s gender?
interview_answer: I’m not sure about that. You’ll need to check with the team—they can give you a fuller briefing afterward.

question: Did they receive approval without your consent?
interview_answer: Yes, thanks, Pete. I’m not going to address any of that. Thanks.

question: Does misusing presidential authority qualify as grounds for impeachment?
interview_answer: You’d really have to put that to the attorneys. What I can say is this: there is absolutely nothing here. I had a completely straightforward call with a very respectable leader from Ukraine, and that’s all this is about. People don’t even want to focus on the call itself. I caught bits and pieces of what’s going on—total time-wasting—in Washington between the meetings I just mentioned. And when I watched, I saw they’re not actually discussing my call. They’re not talking about the official record. Remember this: when Schiff invented that fake version of the call and performed it for Congress and for everyone watching—a total fabrication—that’s when I released the real transcript. They said there was going to be a second whistleblower. Where is that person now? And—hold on, hold on. I’m not going to get into this if I’m interrupted. There was also supposed to be some insider source. What happened to that so‑called source? They all vanished. Once they saw the transcript, they knew they had a serious problem, but they pushed forward anyway because they’d already built this bogus narrative. So here’s the bottom line: I did nothing wrong. The call was perfect. Entirely proper. Top legal experts have reviewed it. The Justice Department reviewed it and cleared it. There was no misconduct. They never expected me to release the record of the call. They probably didn’t think we had note‑takers or a transcript or a recording we’d put out. When we did release it, Democrats went wild, because it didn’t match the fake version Schiff sold them. So things are going very well for us. From what I saw, our side handled it extremely well. But to be clear, we have all the evidence. They don’t. And as I said, whether that’s an impeachable issue is something you’ll have to ask the lawyers, not me.

question: If the North Korean leader refuses to engage in talks or take the necessary steps, would the United States be prepared to escalate sanctions and increase pressure to push North Korea toward progress?
interview_answer: I’m not going to address that directly. What I can say is this: we already have extremely powerful sanctions in place. I’m not going to get into whether we’ll ramp them up. They’re tough. And there are many decent people in North Korea who still have to get by under those measures. That matters to me. I’ll also say this: my outlook shifted quite a bit after I got to know Chairman Kim, as you’re aware, very well. They have their own perspective too. So I really don’t want to get into that specific issue. I just believe that, hopefully, for the sake of , for Japan, and honestly for —I was speaking with President Xi, who is someone widely respected—and I told him, You can’t be happy having a nuclear-armed neighbor on your doorstep. And he isn’t. He truly isn’t. He’d like that situation resolved as well. So I’ll leave it there. Now, ladies and gentlemen, I’ve got a plane to catch back to that great place we call Washington, DC. Thank you all very much. Thank you. Thanks, everyone. Thank you very much.

question: Is backing same-sex marriage what you personally believe?
interview_answer: I’m not going to wade into that issue today. Nice effort, though.

question: In what way did your talks with Prime Minister Reinfeldt help address the climate emergency?
interview_answer:
I’d really point you back to the remarks I made when I accepted the Nobel Peace Prize. I opened that address by noting that, compared with earlier laureates, I hardly felt deserving. In that speech I also laid out the fundamental dilemma facing anyone who believes in peace yet lives in a world saturated with violence and, at times, real evil. The issue then is: what obligations do we bear in such a world?

That’s why I’ve worked to bring the Iraq war to an end, to reduce our military presence in Afghanistan, to reinvigorate multilateral cooperation, and to put diplomacy at the center of how we tackle international problems. But the underlying question that confronts all of us—not just me, not just elected officials, but our citizens as well—is when we finally say we must respond to deeds that trample on our shared humanity.

So, when I witness 400 children being gassed, over 1,400 innocent civilians killed pointlessly in a conflict where tens of thousands have already perished, and we possess the capacity to take steps that, while they won’t resolve the entire crisis, can at least lessen this particular horror, then I believe the ethical choice is not to simply stand aside.

These are agonizing choices. This is the aspect of the presidency I wrestle with every day. I would far prefer to spend my hours figuring out how every 3- and 4-year-old can get a strong early education than to devote that time to figuring out how to keep 3- and 4-year-olds from being attacked with nerve agents and chemical weapons.

But, as President of the United States, those are the kinds of decisions I cannot escape. Like it or not, when something terrible happens anywhere in the world, people immediately ask what the United States is going to do. That’s the expectation on every front—Libya, Rwanda, Sierra Leone, and now Syria. That’s inherent in the job.

Now, I’m not going to go into the specifics of my conversation with Prime Minister Reinfeldt or claim that it directly ‘solved’ climate change. What was your second question again? Ah, climate change.

question: Chance that future ties between North Korea and the U.S. could resemble current U.S.–Vietnam relations.
interview_answer: You’ll need to ask that again.

question: What do you say about the notion that you are secretly protecting the world from a satanic ring of pedophiles and cannibals?
interview_answer:
I haven’t really come across that claim before. And honestly, are people presenting that as something negative or something positive? Look, if I can play a role in helping to rescue the world from serious problems, I’m happy to step up. I’m prepared to put myself on the line for that. And in many ways we are doing exactly that: we’re defending the world from a far‑left agenda that would wreck this nation. And if this nation collapses, the rest of the world will go right behind it. That’s how critical this country is.

Just listen to what some of these folks are pushing: defunding the police, getting rid of borders, just letting anyone stream into our country with no checks, no screenings—none of it. You mention testing; they don’t want any testing. Mexico, as you know, has a very high infection rate.

By next week, the wall will be at 300 miles. Our border results are phenomenal. That happened partly by luck, maybe, but also by building real capability, even though one party keeps trying to block it. Notice you don’t hear them bringing up the wall anymore.

What I will emphasize is this: our nation needs toughness, not weakness. We’re seeing far too much weakness. Yes, John, go ahead.

question: Do the general’s dismissal and Prigozhin’s mutiny suggest anything about what Putin might do going forward?
interview_answer:
Look, to begin with, they’ve already meddled in U.S. elections, so that kind of behavior wouldn’t be something new. They did it before—they gave it a shot last time. As for—well, I don’t really see any real likelihood—you can never be entirely sure—of Putin actually resorting to nuclear weapons. The West, but also China and pretty much the rest of the world, has made it very clear: Don’t even think about that. Don’t cross that line.

Second, and third, when it comes to what ultimately happens to Prigozhin and how that ties into what we talked about in Vilnius—honestly, only God knows what Putin might decide. We’re not even certain where Prigozhin is right now, or what his current standing is. If I were him, I’d watch what I eat and pay close attention to the menu.

Jokes aside, who can say? I can’t. I don’t think any of us can really predict Prigozhin’s fate in Russia. So I honestly don’t know how to give you a better answer to that question than that.

question: In what ways does the Israel–U.A.E. agreement benefit persecuted and impoverished Christian communities across the Middle East?
interview_answer: Benefit who?

question: Why aren’t you able to do that while subpoenas are pending?
interview_answer: Pardon me? Cooperation.

question: So are you, in practice, authorizing Putin to carry out a limited entry into that nation?
interview_answer: Nice question.

question: Is Kamala Harris legally eligible to serve as Vice President or President because she was born in Oakland, California?
interview_answer: I’m not involved in that at all. I saw a bit about it somewhere. From what I understand, the attorney who wrote about it is very smart, a really excellent lawyer. But I don’t have any real information on it, and frankly, it’s not an issue that concerns me.

question: Can you spell out what is supposedly un-American about federal training initiatives designed to boost inclusivity or highlight implicit bias?
interview_answer: Look, we’ll be releasing a full report on that. Yes, I removed those folks; they’re out. What happened was shameful, honestly. And we’ll put out a very detailed report that you’re really going to like. Okay, next, go ahead.

question: How does the Vatican’s recognition relate to achieving a two-state solution?
interview_answer:
All right. To begin, I’d like to commend the Senate for advancing legislation that grants me the authority not only to negotiate a forward-looking, worker-focused, pro-growth trade agreement with key Asia-Pacific partners, and potentially some in Europe as well, but also to equip my office with meaningful enforcement mechanisms—something that has been lacking in past agreements. I’m grateful to every Senator who supported moving this authority forward, or at least voted to open a full debate so we can keep making progress. For those who opposed it, I intend to keep engaging with them, laying out the facts and analysis they need to be assured that, while earlier trade deals have had serious flaws, the framework we’re putting in place now is fundamentally different—designed to benefit not just major U.S. corporations, but also small and mid-sized businesses and, above all, American workers.

I would never champion any deal unless I believed it would, in the end, help create and sustain jobs here at home, expand pathways into the middle class, and raise wages and incomes for working families. That’s been my central priority from the day I took office.

Now, as far as the disagreement between Senator Warren and myself, that’s been framed as more personal than it really is. I know it makes for compelling headlines when two people who generally see eye-to-eye on many issues have a dispute over one specific policy area. But she’s not alone. A number of my closest friends and earliest backers, in both the House and the Senate, part ways with me on this question. And I get where they’re coming from.

Like me, they watched factories close, saw neighbors lose work, and witnessed jobs being shipped overseas. Like me, they’re wary about whether labor and environmental standards in international agreements are truly followed. Like me, they’re worried about whether trade winds up being genuinely fair, not just nominally free. And like me, they’re deeply troubled by global and domestic trends toward higher inequality, where gains have increasingly accrued at the top while wages and opportunities for middle class families—and for those striving to reach the middle class—have stagnated.

So these are people whose core values track very closely with my own. I noticed a recent progressive statement of principles outlining what it means to be a progressive, put forward by some of these allies. And aside from their position on trade, it pretty much mirrored the agenda I’ve been pursuing. That’s the one key area of divergence. In other words, we’re talking about a disagreement over policy assessments and what best serves our constituents, not any deep split in values.

My conviction is that, notwithstanding the shortcomings of prior agreements, we are far better off if the United States helps set strong, enforceable, high-standard rules for the world’s most dynamic and fastest-growing markets—rules that cover issues like child labor, illegal logging, environmental damage, wildlife trafficking, and intellectual property protections. If we step back, other nations, including China, will step in to write those rules. That would disadvantage American firms and, ultimately, hurt American workers.

On the concern about jobs being outsourced, the evidence suggests that, if a company is solely chasing very low labor costs, it already has multiple avenues to relocate production under the existing system. What we can do with a modern trade arrangement is create conditions that actually draw investment and production back to the United States.

We’re starting to see that in practice. That’s one of the reasons I went to visit Nike. I understand fully that Nike has, for decades, relied on low-wage production in parts of the Asia-Pacific region, which dealt a serious blow to footwear manufacturing jobs here at home. Those shifts occurred over the last 30 years. Now, with new manufacturing technologies and a move up the value chain, Nike is talking about potentially adding around 10,000 jobs back in the United States. That’s a chance we should seize—but to maximize those benefits, our companies still need access to consumers in those growing markets abroad.

That’s why my debate with my progressive friends is really about strategy. If we all care about higher wages, stronger protections, and more security for working families—and we do—then we should be prioritizing the tools we know help: raising the minimum wage, expanding serious job training and apprenticeship programs, investing in modern infrastructure that puts people to work, strengthening collective bargaining rights so workers have a meaningful voice, and enforcing rules on things like overtime. We should ensure paid sick leave is a reality, and we should be honest in our budget choices—avoiding reckless cuts to future-oriented investments just to preserve special-interest loopholes that don’t deliver real economic value.

Those are the measures that directly address the problems my progressive colleagues are worried about. Simply blocking a trade pact won’t solve those issues—particularly when they’re the first to acknowledge that the current trade status quo is already unfavorable to American workers. If the existing framework is failing us, it’s not logical to cling to it and call that a victory for working people.

I fully support robust enforcement, including the provisions that have already been negotiated. I’ve also raised concerns about the way the currency language in the bill is currently written, because I want to be sure that, in trying to address currency manipulation, we don’t inadvertently lock ourselves into constraints that hamper our own monetary policy. I’ll have to sit down with Senator Schumer, Senator Brown, and others to refine that language so it achieves our objectives without causing unintended harm.

As for the rest, I’m going to be candid here: I’ve completely lost track of what your other question was, and I’m not going to get into that specific issue right now.

question: If chemical weapons were deployed in Ukraine, would that automatically lead to a NATO military intervention?
interview_answer: There would definitely be a response, but as for whether NATO would go that far militarily, that’s a choice we’d only make if and when it happens.

question: Do you back providing back pay to furloughed employees?
interview_answer: Come again?

question: How does the President respond to Mitt Romney’s economic adviser saying that his advice to Europe shows he misunderstands the roots of the crisis and suffers from the same weaknesses as his domestic economic agenda?
interview_answer:
To begin with, when it comes to Governor Romney’s team of advisers, that’s really a matter you should raise with Governor Romney himself. I’ll note that we only have one sitting President and one governing administration at any given time, and the long‑standing principle has been that partisan battles in the United States stop at the shoreline. I’d further note that his adviser may not actually know the substance of what we’ve been urging the German government to do.

Often, political actors back home try to lay their own ideological debates over a very complex European reality. What’s happening in Europe has multiple causes. In some cases, you had governments that behaved irresponsibly on the fiscal side and ran up public debts. In other cases, such as Spain, the main difficulties came from a property bubble and broader private‑sector imbalances that weren’t primarily about government borrowing.

What’s clear now is that every European country understands the importance of pursuing growth‑oriented policies that fit alongside credible fiscal consolidation. And that, incidentally, is exactly the kind of approach I believe the United States needs as well. The central idea behind the plan I laid out in September was to boost growth and employment in the short term while laying out a clear path to bring down our deficit and debt over the medium and long term. I think that’s the right general formula, not only for us but more broadly.

You mentioned you had a second question. Could you remind me what it was?

question: What specific form of boycott are you proposing?
interview_answer: Well, I really can’t say. That’s for the public to decide. I’m not going to spell that out. Personally, I wouldn’t urge anything in particular. If a company is going to police people’s political expression—if they won’t let you act differently from the crowd, if they won’t let someone wear a MAGA cap, or a Blue Lives Matter item, which, as you know, supports our police officers—that’s just wrong. It’s awful behavior. They’re throwing their weight around against workers who just want to wear what we’re talking about here, and you understand that. So if folks decide on their own they don’t want to spend money there, I’d certainly support that choice. And remember, people can find good jobs elsewhere; we’ve just had record job growth, the best numbers in our nation’s history, so there are plenty of opportunities. What they did is shameful. Next question, in the back.

question: Looking ahead, how might future North Korea–U.S. ties compare with those between the U.S. and Vietnam?
interview_answer: You’ll need to ask another.

question: Could you at least give a clue about the area or nations involved?
interview_answer: No, I’m not going to do that now—you’ll get the full announcement very soon, all right?

question: Do you believe that home values are going to keep dropping?
interview_answer:
David, let me start by saying plainly that I’m not a housing-market analyst or an economist, so I’m not going to give you a prediction on where prices are headed. If you’re looking for specific forecasts, that’s really a question for the professionals whose job is to issue those kinds of projections, and you’ll probably hear the usual “on the one hand, on the other hand” variety of answers from them.

What I concentrate on instead are the underlying strengths of our economy. I have confidence that people will respond sensibly when they see the real data. And those fundamentals are solid. I’ve outlined a few already: our economy is adding jobs, real after-tax incomes are increasing, and inflation remains low. On top of that, the world economy is performing well, which has boosted our exports and contributed to strong second-quarter growth of 3.4 percent.

It’s also important to consider the overall liquidity in the system—whether there is enough capital available for markets to adjust in an orderly way. I’m advised that there is sufficient liquidity for those adjustments to occur. Now, when it comes to homeowners, anyone who loses a house deserves our deep concern and compassion.

Regarding the idea of a “bailout,” that term can mean different things. If by that you mean sending direct cash payments to individual homeowners, I do not favor that approach. If instead you mean giving institutions like the FHA greater flexibility so they can help borrowers refinance and stay in their homes, then yes, that is something I support.

One thing that is absolutely clear is the need for better clarity in loan documents. Too many families signed agreements without fully grasping the terms. We need stronger financial literacy so people actually understand what they’re committing to. Many hardworking Americans have taken on mortgages they didn’t completely comprehend, and I believe the government has a legitimate role in promoting financial education. We’re acting on that belief and have dedicated funds in the budget to support those efforts.

All right, let’s move on—Mike [Michael Emanuel, FOX News].

question: Could the agreement have been finalized without Israel consenting to pause annexation temporarily?
interview_answer: Come again? You’ll have to speak up for me.

question: Will the Speaker personally step in to talk with Republicans in Congress who oppose easing the embargo and policy changes in order to block funding for the new Embassy?
interview_answer: Okay, Lesley, I'm going to stop you right there. We’re not going to get into that — it’s taking up too much time.

question: Are you willing to revoke their federal financial support?
interview_answer: I'm not going to say that. They'll cooperate. Things will work out. I expect we'll have a solid working relationship. They rely on the Federal Government not just for money—and I'm not saying we're cutting that off—but also for guidance. They may need resources that only we can provide. We’re building up a very large reserve of supplies and are in a strong position now. When I arrived, we had almost nothing. To be fair to those who came before, no one really anticipated something like this, but it happened. No, the Governors will show a great deal of respect for the office of the President. This is about the institution, not just about me personally. The Presidency carries enormous weight in the work we’re doing. You can talk Constitution, you can talk federalism, you can talk theory all day, but from a management perspective, the best system is to let each Governor run their own State and come to us when they run into trouble, and then we’ll step in to assist them. Yes, John []./

question: In what situations, if any, would you be open to a plea deal or pardon for Edward Snowden?
interview_answer:
I need to tread carefully here, Major, because Mr. Snowden is currently under federal indictment; he faces formal criminal charges. Matters like that fall squarely within the responsibilities of the Attorney General and, ultimately, the judge and jury. So I am not in a position to comment directly on his case right now. What I can do, though, is try to address the broader intent behind your question without getting into the particulars.

I’ve said before, and I still believe, that this is a conversation our country needed to have. I’ve also been clear that the manner in which these leaks took place has harmed the United States and undermined our intelligence operations. I think there was a way to have this debate without inflicting that kind of damage.

Let me offer one concrete illustration. The reality is that the United States, for all our imperfections, is a nation governed by the rule of law, with a strong commitment to privacy, civil liberties, and our Constitution. Because of these revelations, governments that actually do many of the things Mr. Snowden says he is concerned about—monitoring their own citizens, targeting political opponents, restricting and intimidating the press—have been able to stand off to the side and pretend that it’s the United States that is the main offender on surveillance and intelligence. That’s a very skewed picture of what’s really happening around the world.

So while this debate has been significant and useful, we should also recognize that it has caused avoidable harm to U.S. intelligence work and our diplomatic standing. On the specific questions of any plea arrangement or clemency for Mr. Snowden, I am going to leave those decisions, and any public discussion of them, to the courts and to the Attorney General, and I’m not going to address them myself.

question: What share of those individuals, in your view, actually qualifies for asylum in the United States?
interview_answer:
I’m not going to talk about specific percentages. What I will say is that this country is built on the rule of law. We must have enforceable borders—without real borders, we don’t have a real nation. For weeks I’ve been watching that caravan move north, and people forget how strict Mexico’s immigration rules are—very tough. It began with well over a thousand people; from what I’m hearing, it’s now closer to a hundred, crossing the length of Mexico, which is a very large country. The numbers have dropped sharply.

Now we’re confronting this at our own border under some of the weakest immigration laws anywhere. Leaders around the world are stunned by how bad our statutes are, and we’re doing everything possible under those constraints. But we need Congress to act, and we need that done fast. Priority one is a wall—you can see that with what’s happening now. Even though the existing barrier there isn’t very strong, and a small fraction of people in excellent physical condition can get over it, only a few can do that. Our newer designs are far harder to scale.

Without any wall, you’d see thousands streaming into the country. A physical barrier is essential, and so is overall border security—and we’re going to secure that border. We’re already deploying large numbers of National Guard troops, most of whom are in place now, and that’s already making a real difference. But we still need statutory reforms. This “catch-and-release” system is absurd: once they set foot on our soil, the process ends up with them being released into the interior. That’s unacceptable. So the laws must be changed.

question: Are you suggesting that if Russia only carried out a limited advance into Ukraine, the sanctions you warned about would not be imposed?
interview_answer: I’m not answering that.

question: Do you feel personally betrayed by Scott McClellan’s critique of the Iraq war?
interview_answer: I’ve given no reaction to Scott’s book, and I’m not going to offer one now. Instead, I’d urge him to pay close attention to what Ambassador Ryan Crocker and General Petraeus are saying. There’s always a temptation to let domestic political squabbles override the careful, professional judgments of our commanders in the field. That’s exactly why I strongly opposed setting any fixed, arbitrary withdrawal dates. It’s like saying: we’ll sit here in Congress, watch the polls, check the politics, and then dictate strategy from Washington—rather than relying on the people actually on the ground, including our diplomats and the Iraqi government. The Iraqis invited us there, and they share our objective of seeing U.S. combat forces leave when conditions allow. And that’s what’s happening. Our approach has been ‘return on success,’ and our forces are coming home based on progress, not political timelines. So I would tell any member of Congress who visits Iraq to really listen to what’s happening there and recognize that the best path forward is to heed those directly involved on the scene. I know that’s not easy for some in Washington—there’s intense pressure from advocacy groups like MoveOn.org constantly hammering these candidates, and it can be tough to rise above that. That’s why I’m pleased so many elected officials are traveling over there; they’ll gain a perspective you simply can’t acquire just by reading your excellent newspapers or watching your TV programs. John [John McKinnon, Wall Street Journal].

question: Were potential arrangements for inspecting North Korea’s nuclear facilities part of the talks?
interview_answer: I can’t really hear you—speak up. Also, which outlet are you with? Tell me your outlet?

question: Which additional steps are currently on the table?
interview_answer: I’m not going to get into that, but I will tell you this: I believe the sanctions are effective. And our armed forces would be effective too, but that’s a very serious way of achieving victory. Still, we win. No one can defeat us militarily. No one is even in the same league. What we’ve done for our military over the past three years is remarkable. All of it built here in the United States, by the way. It’s truly remarkable. Our nuclear arsenal was getting very outdated; they hadn’t invested in it. Now we’ve brought it—if I can put it this way—into absolutely top-tier condition. Absolutely top-tier. We have brand-new systems and upgraded ones, and it’s extraordinary. And we should all hope we never have to use them. We should never have to use them. Our overall military is in outstanding condition. And we have a terrific man, as you know, about to take over as Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff. Joe Dunford has been outstanding. He’s a terrific person and a friend of mine. But General Milley will be taking over, and we’ll have a little ceremony for Joe and for Mark—for everyone. And as you know, our Secretary of Defense, Mark Esper, just came in. He hasn’t been here long, but he has tremendous drive. He understands it. This is what he’s done his whole life, from the time he graduated—or really from when he entered—West Point, where he was an absolutely top student and so on. So we have truly exceptional people. And Steve Mnuchin is here; we rolled out the sanctions today, and I think they’re probably, Steve, the toughest ever imposed on any country. We will definitely never do that to Australia, I can promise you. [] Glad to hear it.

question: Do you believe Israel’s decision in this case was justified?
interview_answer: David, I’m simply not going to give any opinion on the very issue you’re pressing me to address.

question: Will Meng Wanzhou's case have to be dealt with last?
interview_answer: Wait, last in what sense? Last compared to which cases?

question: What is your reaction to critics who are likening this week’s controversies to those that occurred during the Nixon era?
interview_answer:
I’m going to leave it to all of you to make those kinds of historical comparisons, and you can look back at the record and reach your own judgments. My focus is that if there are problems inside the Government, we identify them and fix them. That’s my job, and that’s what we’re going to stay focused on. That applies to the IRS and ensuring that they enforce the law in the manner it was meant to be enforced. It also applies to the safety of our diplomats, which is why we have to partner with Congress to guarantee that there are sufficient resources for what’s needed on the ground.

Now, when it comes to the Department of Justice, I’m not going to weigh in on a particular, ongoing matter. What I can do is speak in general terms about the balance we have to achieve. National security leaks can endanger people. They can endanger the men and women in uniform whom I’ve ordered into harm’s way. They can endanger intelligence professionals working in sensitive and fragile environments, where they can easily be exposed.

Our national security depends on those individuals being able to do their jobs knowing that those of us back home are supporting them and not leaving them exposed or in even greater danger than they already face. I won’t apologize for that, and I don’t believe the American people expect me, as Commander in Chief, to ignore information that could jeopardize their missions or cost them their lives.

On the other hand, we’re also a democracy, and a free press, free speech, and the open movement of information are essential to holding me and the rest of the Government to account and to making our democracy work. The very reason I entered public life is because I have a deep belief in that democratic system and in that process.

The purpose of this media shield legislation—which was developed and broadly supported by, for example, the Washington Post editorial board and by prosecutors—was to find an appropriate way to strike that balance. And if this particular case—about which we still don’t have all the facts—has sparked a renewed debate over how best to strike that balance, then I think this is an appropriate moment to revisit that bill. That’s a valuable discussion to undertake.

But I also think we have to remember that, when we voice concerns about leaks at a time when more than 60,000 of our troops are still in Afghanistan and many intelligence officers are deployed around the world in hazardous posts—some as dangerous as the facility in Benghazi—part of my responsibility is to safeguard their work while still allowing for the flow of information the public needs to stay informed and to hold my Administration accountable. What I’m not going to do is comment directly on the specific case or engage in the historical analogy you mentioned.

question: Hello, sir, Emily Goodwin from DailyMail.com. I’d like to ask: in your view, was justice done in the Harvey Weinstein trial?
interview_answer: I was never a supporter of Harvey Weinstein, as you’re aware. In fact, he once said he’d pour his energy into trying to stop me from winning the election. You can see how that turned out. He’s someone I disliked, always did. I really don’t know many details about the proceedings because, as you know, I’ve been overseas. With constant travel and back‑to‑back meetings nearly every hour, I honestly haven’t had the opportunity to follow it closely. What I can say is, I just wasn’t a fan of his. I knew him a bit, but not well. I knew of him because he was active in New York. Not someone I cared for. I will point out, though, that his real friends were Democrats. Michelle Obama thought very highly of him. Very highly. Hillary Clinton thought very highly of him. And he poured massive amounts of money into Democratic campaigns. So my question is this: are Democrats planning to return all that money? Because he funneled a great deal to them. It’s unfortunate, but that’s how it ended up. Yes, Peter [Peter Alexander, NBC News].

question: How do you balance national sovereignty with the doctrine of responsibility to protect?
interview_answer: Look, I’m not going to explain how to square that with this so‑called responsibility to protect—whatever that even means now.

question: If Putin does launch an invasion of Ukraine, will the United States return to a classic containment strategy?
interview_answer:
My view is, first, that he still wants to avoid an outright, large-scale war. Second, do I believe he’ll push the limits with the West, with the United States and NATO, as far as he thinks he can? Yes, I do. But I also believe he’ll incur a very real and steep cost for any such move, a cost he is currently underestimating, and that he will ultimately regret taking that course.

Now, as to whether—whether—well, I need to think about how to put this in a public setting. I think he is wrestling with what he sees as the greatest calamity to befall Mother Russia: the fall of the Berlin Wall, the loss of the empire, the disappearance of the near abroad, the breakup of the Soviet Union.

Yet look at what he still controls: a country spanning eight time zones, a thawing permafrost that won’t naturally refreeze, significant oil and gas reserves, and a need to define Russia’s position between China and the Western world.

So I’m not entirely convinced—David, I’m not convinced he even fully knows what course he’ll ultimately choose. My hunch is that he will take some form of action. He has to act in some way.

I’ve also made it clear to him that he’s raised two main demands: First, that Ukraine must never enter NATO; second, that NATO—or anyone else—will not place strategic weapons in Ukraine. On that second point, we might be able to find an arrangement, depending on what he does along Russia’s border in Europe. On the first point, there are long-standing international and European agreements that say nations are free to choose their own alliances. And in any case, Ukraine joining NATO anytime soon is not very probable, given the additional democratic reforms they need and the question of whether key Western allies would back Ukrainian membership right now.

So there is space for negotiation if he wants to pursue it. But I think, consistent with his usual behavior, he’s going to—well, I really shouldn’t elaborate further. I’ll just say I believe it will backfire on him in a serious way.

question: Was he carrying a weapon, Mr. President?
interview_answer: As far as I’m aware, yes, but I really don’t know. You’ll need to put that question to them.

question: Mr. President, Tom Bradby from ITV News. Many people in Britain feel that your government’s insistence on pursuing the extradition of computer hacker Gary McKinnon, who has Asperger’s, is excessive and overly severe. Do you believe the moment has come to show some mercy and ease your position in his case?
interview_answer:
Please, go ahead. This was one of the matters we covered in our talks. As you know, there is an active dialogue between the United States and the United Kingdom on this case, and I’m not going to say anything that might interfere with or bias that process. We fully recognize that Gary McKinnon has been charged with a serious and significant offense involving the penetration of critical government systems, and no one is suggesting that such behavior should be treated as anything other than a major crime that needs to be addressed. I’ve spoken with our Ambassador in London about it, and I raised it again with the Prime Minister today, and my hope is that a constructive way forward will be identified. 

Now, one of the broader points David and I talked about is the growing challenge posed by the Internet and the imperative for close cooperation on cybersecurity. We touched on the reality that, while some may still resort to old‑style espionage to steal secrets, the real vulnerabilities we face now are the kinds of intrusions that undermine our information networks. So we view these threats with great seriousness, and I know the British government does as well. 

Beyond that, though, we have a long‑standing practice that the President does not step into decisions about whom to prosecute or how to handle extradition cases. So I am not going to involve myself in directing the outcome here. What I expect is that my administration will follow the law and work in close coordination with what we have just described as an exceptionally strong and unique ally. And I am confident this will be settled in a manner that reflects the gravity of the conduct at issue, but also reflects the depth of our partnership and our ability to reach a fair, mutually acceptable outcome. 

All right. Thank you all very much. Thank you.

question: At your initial press briefing 10 months back, I asked whether you could do anything besides pursue legislation to safeguard voting rights. Now that those bills seem thoroughly bogged down, can you now clearly spell out what your plan is to protect voting rights?
interview_answer:
I'm not going to spell that out in detail right now, particularly regarding any executive actions I might take or other specific steps available to me. What I can say is this: We've already substantially increased the number of Justice Department officials tasked with challenging what we view as unconstitutional Republican efforts to rig elections and undermine legitimate results.

We’ve also started organizing in new ways, reaching beyond traditional civil rights organizations to broader communities, to warn the wider public about what is underway and what could happen if these measures advance.

If I had tried to explain to the public earlier—not you personally, but people generally—that elections could be subverted by changing who the electors are after the fact, most would have reacted like, “Come on.” I taught constitutional law for 20 years—a three‑credit separation‑of‑powers course on Saturday mornings while I was in the Senate—and even I never imagined we’d be debating a scenario where, as happened last time, people tried to send alternate slates of electors to state legislatures claiming I lost and a Republican actually won. I don’t think many Americans believed something like that could occur in this century, but it is happening.

So, Nancy, what I’m really saying is this: I believe there are several avenues still open to us, but I’m not going to lay them all out here. And I also believe we can still secure major portions of the voting rights package—maybe not the entire thing immediately, but enough to move us toward enacting a substantial part of the John Lewis Voting Rights Advancement Act and the broader fair elections measures.

question: Did he receive an invitation?
interview_answer: I honestly don't know. I'm not the person who handles who gets asked to come to press events. What I can say is they coordinate very closely and have a strong working partnership, and that’s what really matters.

question: Can you tell us about the earlier phone conversation you had with Kim Jong Un?
interview_answer:
We did speak, but I'm not going to get into that. What I can say is that we've held many talks overall. We've built very significant lines of communication at Mike’s level and at other levels as well. In fact, a couple of representatives from North Korea are actually here with us today, as you know. They’re in this room, and there are also a few others in the back.

So when we moved toward this final arrangement, we were absolutely not going in blind. We approached it with a very strong relationship and a great deal of understanding. And that, in my view, is a big part of why we were able to reach this outcome.

Now I’m going to wrap up. I don’t know about all of you, but it’s been a long time since I’ve had any sort of break. So now we can take a brief breather, and then we get back to the real work. I appreciate all of you being here. I hope we’ve addressed your questions. Thank you very much. And, really, congratulations to everyone, because this is, to me, a very significant moment in world history. And to be honest, I have to add that I want to see it fully carried through. So Mike and the entire team have to roll up their sleeves and finish the job. Otherwise, we’ve done something good, but if you don’t push the ball all the way over the goal line, it doesn’t count for enough.

So thank you, and congratulations to everyone here in the room. Thank you very much. I appreciate it. Thank you.

question: Does the election of two Muslim women to Congress, including one who wears a veil, contradict a particular political message?
interview_answer: I have no idea what you’re asking me. Huh?

question: Deliberation and strategy around issuing advance pardons
interview_answer: I’m not going to address pardons during this briefing today.

question: When exactly will that happen, Mr. President?
interview_answer: Honestly, I have no idea.

question: Explanation of what the President told French President Nicolas Sarkozy about Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu
interview_answer:
Let me start by pointing out that Chuck is the only one who’s managed to slip in two questions so far, so when I wrap up here, whoever was on deck next—[]—I’m just giving you a hard time, Chuck.

On the supercommittee, back in August we agreed to put in place a trillion dollars’ worth of cuts over the next decade, mainly from discretionary spending. But we also made clear that to put our finances on a more sustainable path, we needed at least another $1.2 trillion in deficit reduction. I actually pushed for going beyond that number. The entire purpose of the sequester was to ensure that both parties felt real pressure to abandon inflexible stances and do what was necessary for the country.

Since then, they’ve had a lot of meetings, but it still seems like too many people are clinging to their rigid positions instead of actually fixing the problem.

I’ve laid out a very detailed framework that produces more than $3 trillion in savings. It’s a balanced plan that matches what Americans say they want. It says nothing is off-limits. We have to trim discretionary spending the way we already have. We need nondefense cuts. We need defense cuts. We have to examine entitlements. We must tackle health care costs. And yes, we need additional revenue.

And if we have to raise revenue, it’s only reasonable to begin by asking those at the very top to contribute a bit more, rather than immediately demanding that seniors, for example, pay substantially more for Medicare.

This is the same basic proposal I walked through with Speaker Boehner in August. It’s the same kind of balanced approach every independent panel that’s studied this has said we need. Yet it feels like some folks are still trying to massage the numbers to avoid the inevitable conclusion.

But the math is what it is. However you slice it, if you want a balanced plan that doesn’t eviscerate Medicare and Medicaid and doesn’t stop us from investing in education, basic science, and research—the very things we’ve been discussing here at APEC that every leader recognizes are critical for long-term growth—then sensible spending cuts have to be paired with new revenue.

My hope is that over the coming days, the supercommittee leaders in Congress will face up to the reality and do what’s necessary, because the underlying numbers won’t suddenly change. There isn’t some secret shortcut. There aren’t any magic beans you can plant and grow a money tree. We have to take the responsible course. And I’m ready to sign a balanced bill that actually resolves this.

One more point: in my discussions with foreign leaders, what’s striking—whether in Europe or here in Asia—is that the structural changes they’re making, on both the revenue side and their public pension systems, are far more dramatic than anything we must do to straighten out our own balance sheet.

We don’t need to fundamentally remake America or our way of life. We just need to share the burden sensibly across all parts of society so it’s fair, so people don’t conclude yet again that the well-connected, the lobbyists, and the special interests get off easy while middle-class families already under strain are asked to carry the load. If other nations can do tough things, we can too, and we can do it responsibly.

I am not going to say whether I would veto some specific bill until an actual bill is in front of me, because I still think there’s a chance we’ll have that light‑bulb moment where everyone realizes: This is what we need to do.
