Regarding the open microphone incident in France, I am not going to discuss the private conversations I have with other heads of state. What I will say is this: The main subject of my discussion with President Sarkozy at that meeting was my deep disappointment that France supported the Palestinians’ bid to join UNESCO, fully aware that under U.S. law that decision would force us to cut off our funding to UNESCO, and after I had repeatedly made the case that the only viable way to resolve the Middle East dispute is for Israelis and Palestinians to negotiate directly at the table—that trying to go around that process via the United Nations is not going to succeed.

So I had a very candid and forceful exchange with President Sarkozy on that point. And that’s entirely in line with the private and public messages I’ve been delivering to everyone over these past several months.

Ed Henry, FOX News.

question: Are you actually able to cooperate with them?
interview_answer: Look, I’m going to have to get back to you on that—I’d prefer to give you a real answer in, say, about seven years, all right? As in, one plus seven. I do hope—John, I genuinely hope—I’ll be able to, because I like the idea of working across the aisle. But at the moment, all they’re doing is blocking everything. And if you think about it, they’re opposing major tax reductions that will strengthen our economy and boost competitiveness. That’s a tough stance to win elections on, and I think some Democrats will ultimately support us on these tax cuts.

question: When you met with President Castro, did you bring up Fidel Castro or talk about him?
interview_answer: Lesley, I’m going to stop you right there. I’m not getting into that, and we’re wasting too much time.

question: What kind of weapon was it?
interview_answer: I have no idea, sorry.

question: Have you been told of any confirmed coronavirus infections in the White House today?
interview_answer: You mean today?

question: When is Kosovo’s independence due?
interview_answer: I’ll pass.

question: Does the Speaker actually have a firm handle on his caucus?
interview_answer: That’s something you’d have to ask the Speaker himself; it isn’t a question I’m going to answer. In my dealings with John Boehner, things have gone well. I regard him as a decent person who wants to serve the country responsibly. As Chip mentioned, the political wave that elevated him to the Speakership worked well for a midterm race but makes day‑to‑day governing difficult. More broadly, the Republican conference needs to understand that our democracy functions when we hear one another out, extend good faith, presume the other side’s patriotism and good motives, and accept reasonable compromises to tackle major challenges. And there are still some in that caucus who, in my view, haven’t yet come around to that understanding.

question: Can you disclose the names of those five GOP Senators?
interview_answer: Of course not. Absolutely no way. Are you serious? I have to keep that private.

question: Will the United States pledge to ship more vaccine doses abroad?
interview_answer:
It could end up taking a bit longer globally. The United States intends to keep helping, and I think there’s a chance that over the course of 2022 and into 2023 we might be in a place where we can supply as many as another billion doses. But that’s not settled yet.

I’ve tried to be extremely cautious throughout this pandemic about only stating what I actually know and what I’m confident we can follow through on. When I’ve made announcements, I’ve then carried them out. What I don’t want to do is get ahead of where we really are by promising that I—or the United States—can deliver things that we have not yet secured.

So, there was strong agreement among all the G7 partners that this effort is not finished; we will keep at it until we can meet the world’s needs for vaccines. And that’s not just the morally right thing to do. It’s also the smart thing to do for our own health and national security. You can’t build a barrier high enough to stop new variants from coming in. You just can’t.

This is going to remain an ongoing effort for a long time, and we may well see other pandemics in the future. That’s why we’re working on systems to detect potential outbreaks—before a new virus spirals out of control.

We are not going to walk away while there are countries that still need access to vaccines. And beyond donations, as I’ve said and as my counterparts recognize from their own experience, this is an enormous logistical challenge. Sending country X a certain number of doses is one thing; having enough trained people on the ground to actually get shots into arms is another.

That’s why we’re also helping other nations build up their own manufacturing capacity. We’ve all agreed on that. India, for example, has the know-how but has so far lacked sufficient materials to ramp up full production. A lot of work is underway to not only provide vaccines but also enable countries to produce them themselves.

So I’m not going to stand here and give you a specific new commitment on additional shipments right now. I’m not going to answer it in those terms. We’ll keep doing our part, but I’m not announcing any new pledge today.

question: What actions, in your view, ought to be taken against Russia over the poisoning of Alexei Navalny?
interview_answer:
I can tell you this is something we’d have to treat very, very seriously if it turns out to be as reported, and I’m sure we will. But let me also say: nobody has been tougher on Russia than I have. At the same time, I maintain good relationships; I have good relations with almost every country. I’ve even had a good relationship with North Korea. Remember, everyone said we were headed for war with North Korea? If Hillary had won, you’d likely be in a major war with North Korea right now—a horrible, extremely bloody conflict with millions of casualties. Instead, under my Administration, we’ve avoided that war. People predicted that if I became President, we’d be in a war within a week; what’s happened has been exactly the opposite.

We’re doing very well in Afghanistan, drawing down troops to very low levels, and we have a lot of other positive developments. We’ve also launched a historic peace initiative in the Middle East with the U.A.E. and Israel. That agreement has gotten an incredible reception—almost no one has ever seen anything like it. More nations are going to announce that they’re joining, and you’re going to see many countries involved. Quite a few are saying, “Why didn’t you include us from day one?” They were eager to participate, and now they want in.

Beyond that, I was the one who really warned the United States and the world about Nord Stream 2. I asked why Germany is paying Russia billions and billions of dollars for energy while we’re expected to defend Germany from Russia. It doesn’t make sense. Then there are the sanctions and many other measures we’ve taken.

At the same time, with Russia, we’re now negotiating a nuclear nonproliferation agreement, which is extremely important—maybe the most important issue of all. Some people point to climate change; I don’t. I say this is much more critical. We’re the biggest nuclear power; they’re number two. China is third—a distant third for now, but they’re expanding quickly. We’ll bring China into these talks eventually, but our discussions with Russia on nuclear issues are going very well.

So, I do not yet know precisely what occurred in this case. It’s a horrible situation, it’s awful, it should never happen. But we don’t have definitive evidence at this point, and I’m not going to spell out what should be done to Russia until we have that. I’ll review it, but I’m not answering that specific question now.

question: Who exactly are U.S. oil companies?
interview_answer: Sorry, could you remind me what you just asked?

question: In what way does reforming the criminal justice system contribute to eliminating the remaining racial disparities in both state and federal governance?
interview_answer: That’s an awful lot to unpack there, April. [] You know what, I’m not going to get into that.

question: Should you have done more?
interview_answer: More about what?

question: If no new deal is reached, are you willing to resort to military action to curb Iran’s nuclear program?
interview_answer: I’m not going to say whether I would or wouldn’t use military force — that’s not something I consider suitable to discuss. What I will say is this: Iran will not end up with nuclear weapons. That much I guarantee you. They’re not going to get nuclear weapons. You can count on that. Alright, next.

question: Does this call for a signature?
interview_answer: Well, that actually raises a good procedural point about whether there even is a formal signature in this case, because, as I’ve noted, it isn’t a legally enforceable agreement. I really don’t know what the formal requirements are there. What I can say is that this reflects a pledge by the United States that we regard as highly significant. Okay, I’ll leave it there. Thanks, everyone.

question: Is the upcoming meeting going to be soon?
interview_answer: Look, I’m not able to say. It could happen in the near future, or it might not take place for quite some time. I’m not going to give you a specific time. I’d like it to be soon, but it could just as easily be much later.

question: Is there a chance you’ll head to Charlotte?
interview_answer: Anyone want to take a guess? We’ll reveal that in the near future.

question: So why didn’t you disclose that publicly back in February?
interview_answer: Look at what was already being reported. China had already announced it was transmitted through the air. I was hearing it was airborne and I recognized that early on. What was crucial was keeping things steady. You don’t want a president running around shouting, “There will be massive casualties,” and throwing the country into chaos. And if Bob Woodward truly believed my comments were so alarming, he should have gone straight to officials at the time so they could act on it and be informed.

question: Do you now regard Kim Jong Un as your peer?
interview_answer: What exactly do you mean?

question: Must President Zelenskyy surrender any part of Ukraine’s territory in order to secure a ceasefire with Russia?
interview_answer: On that second issue, my response is yes. It really hinges on what the G-20 decides. I—this came up earlier today. And I floated the idea that, if that’s not workable—if Indonesia and some others won’t go along—then, in my view, we ought to ask that both of them be allowed to take part in the sessions, or at least—well, essentially, to attend the G-20 gathering as observers. As for—sorry, could you remind me what your first question was?

question: Do you categorically oppose allowing John Bolton to testify?
interview_answer: Look, you're really piling on the questions here.

question: National sovereignty as a fundamental pillar of the global system.
interview_answer: I’m not going to address that directly—how am I supposed to square it with the duty to protect, which once meant what, exactly?

question: What military response would North Korea face if it failed to honor its commitments?
interview_answer: Look, I'm not going to go into that—and yes, I understand why you're asking. It's a very difficult subject to address because I don't want to sound like I'm issuing threats. I'm not interested in making threats. They were fully aware of that. And you've already seen what might have been on the verge of taking place. Remember, Seoul has about 28 million residents. We think our cities are large—New York has around 8 million and we call that huge. Seoul has 28 million people. Just think about that. And it's sitting right on the border, right on the DMZ, literally right there. If things had actually broken out, I’ve heard some people talk about casualties of a hundred thousand, but I think you could have seen 20 or 30 million deaths. It’s truly a privilege to be handling this, because I believe you might have been looking at 30, 40, even 50 million lives lost. Seoul, one of the world’s largest cities, is directly on that border.

question: Leaning toward withdrawing the military forces?
interview_answer: Not going there.

question: Does the President intend to reverse the Defense Department’s rule that bars media coverage of returning flag‑draped military coffins?
interview_answer:
That’s a very timely issue. We’ve just received word that four more of our men and women in uniform were killed in Iraq today, and of course our prayers are with their loved ones. I’ve mentioned before that when people ask, “When did it really sink in that you were President?” I tell them the most sobering part of the job is signing condolence letters to the families of those we’ve lost. It’s a stark reminder of the gravity of this office and the real‑world consequences of the choices you make as Commander in Chief.

Now, on the specific question of allowing press access when the remains of our fallen are brought home, we are actively reassessing that policy in close consultation with the Pentagon. Because that review is still underway, I am not going to give you a definitive answer at this point, before I’ve seen the results and fully weighed all of the ramifications.

On Afghanistan, we’re facing an enormous challenge. Thanks to the remarkable efforts of our troops and some very skillful diplomacy by Ambassador Crocker in Iraq, we’ve just witnessed an election there that proceeded relatively smoothly, and you can see a political process taking root in a meaningful way.

We don’t yet see that kind of functioning system in Afghanistan. They have elections on the horizon, but in practice the central government is largely removed from the daily realities in many communities outside the capital.

On top of that, the Taliban and Al Qaeda are still operating in the FATA and along the Afghan‑Pakistani border region, and we have not yet mounted the sustained, coordinated pressure on those safe havens that is essential if we’re going to succeed in our mission.

So we’re conducting a comprehensive review. General Petraeus, now at CENTCOM, is carrying out his own assessment and is working closely with my special representative, Richard Holbrooke—one of our most experienced diplomats—on a broader regional strategy. Success will require far better integration of our military operations with our diplomatic, development, and allied efforts.

What I want the American people to keep in mind—because this will not be easy—is that this is the part of the world from which an attack was launched that killed 3,000 of our citizens. This past week I met with families of those lost on 9/11, and it underscored again the price of allowing those sanctuaries to endure. My bottom line is that we cannot permit Al Qaeda to maintain those bases. We cannot tolerate those safe havens in that region. We will have to act intelligently and effectively, and we will have to stay at it, to ensure those sanctuaries are eliminated. I do not yet have a specific timetable for how long that will take. What I can say is that I will not allow Al Qaeda or bin Laden to operate freely, planning attacks on the United States.

Okay. Helene Cooper of the New York Times. Helene, where are you? There you are.

question: Your stance on imprisoning political rivals
interview_answer:
All right. On the question of refugees, remember that the Paris attacks occurred only a week ago. The news cycle moves at such speed that it’s easy to forget how recent and traumatic that was. Given how much it’s been in the headlines, and how closely Paris resembles many American cities, I understand why the House acted so quickly on refugee legislation.

My view, however, is that now that we’ve had a moment to step back and examine this carefully, people should recognize that refugees admitted to the United States undergo the most extensive, exhaustive, and rigorous screening of anyone who comes to our country. The vetting system, built over multiple administrations from both parties, is extremely detailed and currently takes roughly 18 to 24 months before anyone is approved.

So even though the House bill, on its surface, simply adds a certification requirement and doesn’t go as far as some of the more extreme ideas floated by certain presidential candidates earlier in the week, the reality is that if it clogs the system so badly that almost no cases can be processed, you effectively stop refugees from coming. If you just pile on layers of additional red tape that don’t improve security or strengthen screening, but make it practically impossible to move cases forward, you’re basically shutting down the refugee program for people in dire need.

When I spoke of betraying our values, I was referring specifically to comments suggesting, for example, that we might admit only Christians but bar Muslims, or that we are so afraid that even a 4‑year‑old orphan might be turned away. Those of you who came with me to the refugee center yesterday and saw those children know exactly who we’re talking about. If you’re a parent and you saw those kids, and you thought about what they’ve endured, the idea that we couldn’t find a place for them anywhere in the United States runs directly counter to who we are.

The encouraging thing is that the vast majority of Americans, once they understand how stringent the screening is and what safeguards are already in place, and once they actually see those children, would say we have an obligation to help them.

Now, regarding Prime Minister Najib, I have a clear practice: I do not speak about investigations underway in the United States, and I am certainly not going to speak about any investigations or legal matters that may be occurring in another country while I am a guest there. So I will not address that specific issue. What I did discuss with Prime Minister Najib—as I have with many leaders across Southeast Asia, as well as in Africa, Latin America, and elsewhere—is the critical importance of transparency and accountability, and of tackling corruption. These are all barriers to development; they hold nations back and contribute to poverty and stalled growth.

Countries that confront these challenges can succeed even if they lack natural resources or access to the sea. Countries that ignore them will fall behind, regardless of how resource‑rich or strategically located they are.

I also stressed to Prime Minister Najib that civil society and a free press are essential to a country’s success. Every nation here is at a different point in its social and economic development, and we don’t insist that everyone copy the American model. But there are some core principles we believe we should champion as friends and partners.

For example, look at Myanmar, which has just held a landmark election after four decades of military rule. They’re not going to become Sweden overnight in terms of how their democracy functions, but there is a major shift underway, and we want to support and deepen that.

Much of our work—the Open Government Partnership we launched through the United Nations, our bilateral efforts on transparency, and the standards we’ve embedded in TPP—is aimed at raising expectations and encouraging higher norms. Some countries will move ahead faster, some will slide back at times, but the overall direction we seek is the same: a world where ordinary people are treated fairly, the rule of law prevails, governments are open and answerable, citizens’ voices matter, women have equal standing, and minorities are not subjected to discrimination.

Those are deeply American values, but I believe they are also values shared by people everywhere.

Thank you all. Let’s wrap this up and head home.

question: What is your position on the matter of federal government–imposed busing?
interview_answer: Let me start by saying, before I touch that topic, I think she was given far more praise than she deserved. He didn’t perform well at all, and the facts may not really have backed him up, but I still believe she got more credit than her performance warranted. It wasn’t anything remarkable. And frankly, I think he—Biden—took more criticism than he should have. In my view, he was actually treated too harshly. Now, on that specific issue, I’m not going to get into it today. In around a month we’ll unveil some policy that’s going to be very interesting and, I think, quite surprising to many people. Jennifer, do you have a question?

question: What do you actually do to guarantee North Korea follows through on denuclearizing?
interview_answer:
Look, can you really guarantee anything at all? Can I guarantee you won’t trip when you stand up from that chair? You just can’t guarantee everything. What I can tell you is they’re interested in reaching an agreement. That’s what I do—I make deals. I’ve been very successful at it my entire life, that’s my background. And I can tell when someone is serious about a deal and when they’re not. A lot of politicians don’t have that sense—that’s not their strength, but it is mine.

Honestly, this probably could’ve been handled more easily years ago. But I know—very strongly, based on my instincts, my abilities, my talent—that they’re looking to strike a deal. And that’s good for the world. It’s also good for China, because I can’t see China being thrilled about nuclear weapons right on their doorstep. They were very cooperative here. So I believe he wants a deal. Can anyone be absolutely sure? No. But we’ll find out soon enough as the talks move forward. All right? Thanks a lot. Next question.

question: So, are the sanctions failing?
interview_answer:
At this point, we simply can’t say definitively whether the sanctions are achieving their ultimate objective. What we can say is that, in terms of design, they are functioning as intended by placing very heavy pressure on Russia’s economy. That’s not just my personal view; if you look at the financial markets, at the scale of capital outflows, at current forecasts for Russian economic growth, it’s clear their economy has largely stalled. Estimates suggest capital flight on the order of $100 to $200 billion. You’re also not seeing a wave of new foreign investors establishing operations there.

What the sanctions have done is confront President Putin with a fundamental choice: either move to address the crisis in eastern Ukraine through negotiations and non-military means, accepting that Ukraine is a sovereign nation whose people have the right to determine their own future; or push ahead with the current approach and, as a result, inflict long-term damage on Russia’s economy and on the Russian people themselves.

In that regard, we are following exactly the course we ought to be on, and we welcome the fact that our European partners have stood with us, along with many other countries worldwide.

But all that said, the underlying conflict is not yet settled. Fighting in eastern Ukraine continues, civilians are still being killed, and we have already witnessed one of the tragic outcomes of this war in the downing of the Malaysian airliner.

The faster we can return to a situation where genuine talks are underway—talks that ensure all Ukrainians have a voice, can use democratic channels, are properly represented, and see the commitments from the government in Kyiv put into practice to safeguard Russian speakers and enhance local autonomy—the sooner we will be able to say this crisis is truly resolved. That will also require President Putin to accept Ukraine’s independence. Only then can anyone honestly claim the problem is fully fixed. Until that time, what I can tell you is that the sanctions are operating exactly in the manner we set them up to operate, but I cannot say they have conclusively ‘worked’ in the broader sense you’re asking about.

question: Schedule for ultimately pulling U.S. forces out of Afghanistan
interview_answer:
That’s an important issue to raise. We’ve just received word that four more American service members were killed today in Iraq, and of course our hearts go out to their loved ones. I’ve mentioned before that people sometimes ask me, “When did it really sink in that you’re President?” And I’ve said the most sobering part of this job is signing condolence letters to the families of our fallen warriors. It’s a constant reminder of the weight of this office and the life‑or‑death consequences of the choices I make.

On the question of media access when the remains of our service members return home, we’re currently reviewing that policy in close consultation with the Department of Defense. Because that review is ongoing, I’m not going to give you a definitive position right now before I’ve seen the full analysis and weighed all of the ramifications.

Turning to Afghanistan, we’re facing a very serious test there. Thanks to the remarkable efforts of our troops, and very skillful diplomacy by Ambassador Crocker in Iraq, we’ve just witnessed elections in Iraq that were relatively peaceful, and you can see a political system that is beginning to function in a real way.

We simply do not yet see that kind of cohesion in Afghanistan. They have elections on the horizon, but in practice the central government is largely disconnected from daily life in many local communities.

On top of that, the Taliban and al‑Qa’ida are operating out of the FATA and other border areas between Afghanistan and Pakistan, and we have not yet seen the kind of sustained, coordinated effort to dismantle those sanctuaries that would be required for us to achieve our objectives.

So we are engaged in a comprehensive reassessment. General Petraeus, as head of CENTCOM, is conducting his own review, and he is working hand‑in‑hand with my special representative, Richard Holbrooke, one of our most experienced diplomats, to design a broader regional strategy. Success will demand much tighter integration of our military operations with our diplomatic and development work, and better coordination with our partners and allies.

The key point that I want the American people to keep in mind—because this is going to be hard—is that this part of the world was the launching pad for an attack that killed 3,000 Americans. Just this past week I sat down with families of those who died on 9/11, and it underscored again the price we pay if we allow those havens to exist. My bottom line is that we cannot permit al‑Qa’ida to enjoy safe refuge there. We cannot tolerate those sanctuaries, and we will have to act intelligently, effectively, and steadily to ensure they are eliminated.

I do not yet have a schedule for how long that will take, and I’m not in a position to lay out a withdrawal timetable for you now. What I can tell you is that I will not allow al‑Qa’ida or bin Laden to operate freely, planning attacks against the United States.

All right. Helene Cooper from the New York Times. Helene, where are you? There you are.

question: How do you view the confrontation that took place last night between White House Counsel Pat Cipollone and Jerrold Nadler?
interview_answer: Look, you’re throwing quite a few questions at me.

question: Can you verify whether drone attacks have taken place in Yemen?
interview_answer: I am not going to comment on matters of ongoing operations, Ed Henry.

question: Do you believe the markets will hold off for two years?
interview_answer: I really should’ve jotted all this down, Jackie. I’m honestly out of mental bandwidth to tackle that right now.

question: What steps should we take to advance the Merida Initiative?
interview_answer: I didn’t get that one interpreted. [] It really sounded like an excellent question, though. [] I think the translation is coming through now—please, go ahead.

question: The QAnon conspiracy scene is expanding during COVID. Your reaction?
interview_answer: Look, I really don’t know a lot about that group, other than I’m told they’re very fond of me, which I’m grateful for, but beyond that I don’t know much about them. I have heard they’ve been growing. And from what people tell me, these are folks who, when they see what’s happening on the streets in Portland, when they see what’s gone on in New York City over the last 6 or 7 months—and honestly, this started even before I got here almost 4 years ago, hard to believe—it’s people who are fed up with what they’re seeing in Portland, in Chicago, in New York, and in a lot of other cities and States. I’ve been told they’re people who love our country, and they don’t like watching what’s happening to it. So I really don’t, in truth, know anything about the movement itself, other than that they apparently support me. And they also want the problems in these places—especially the ones we’ve mentioned—to be solved. There’s no reason Democrats can’t manage a city. And if they won’t, we’ll send in whatever Federal resources—troops, law enforcement, whatever they prefer—and we’ll fix it in 24 hours or less. That’s what I’m focused on, not the movement.

question: Are we facing a hard Brexit?
interview_answer: Is it heart-wrenching?

question: Could you share some details or views about David Cameron?
interview_answer:
I haven’t had any personal dealings with him. And I really—I mean, I—this isn’t the moment for me to be offering guidance to the head of the Conservative Party, or to the leader of any other party, for that matter. It’s their responsibility to decide what they do, and it’s his choice whether he comes here or not.

But let me step back and say this: what we’re focused on isn’t these two particular party leaders; it’s the relationship between our two nations. Let’s recognize, even for those who vehemently opposed what we did in Iraq, that people do at least see there is a struggle going on across the globe right now. And let’s also accept that this is a struggle over which set of values will shape the world in the early twenty‑first century.

You don’t prevail in that kind of struggle by being an occasional, fair‑weather partner to your closest ally. You don’t succeed by wavering or pulling away from one another when things get hard. You don’t win by losing your determination to fight when your adversary’s determination is fierce and unwavering.

And in truth, the values our two countries stand for are reflected in what we’ve experienced here today. The President faces sharp questioning from the American press; I like to say I face questions that are every bit as sharp—if not sharper—from the British press. And—

If you choose to call them sharp.

And as we talk here at this press conference, I can’t quite hear what’s being shouted from the crowd, but I’m fairly sure they aren’t showering either of us with praise.

Well, maybe not all of them.

Some might be supporters we’ve brought along, but I rather doubt that’s the majority.

And that, at its core, is the point. It’s about democracy. It’s about people having the right to voice their opinions, and about elected leaders having to stand up, defend their choices, and accept the consequences if the public rejects those choices. The shared commitment to those values is what matters so profoundly in the world today.

So yes, of course, in any European country now, if you want the cheap applause line, you can get up and denounce America. You can win ovations by attacking the President.

A standing ovation.

Yes. And if people want to play that game, they can. But once all the showmanship is stripped away, you’re left with something very basic, very profound, and very clear: this contest of values is still unfolding.

We can argue over the errors, over specific decisions, over Iraq—whether this tactic or that policy was right or wrong. But what is undeniably happening in Iraq right now is that our enemy is confronting us. And if, whenever our enemy confronts us, our reaction is to edge away from our friends, to make small compromises so we can duck some of the burden, the criticism, or the unpopularity that comes with tough calls—if we do that—our enemy draws strength from it. They watch us constantly. They ask themselves, are these people prepared to stand by what they say they believe, or will they back off if we keep up the pressure, because it’s too hard, because polling shows it’s unpopular, because public opinion turns against them?

That is what leadership is about. And it’s not a question just for me and the President; it’s a question for anyone involved in politics. People are cynical about politics; they say it’s all posturing and sound bites and, at times, even untruths. But when politics is done properly, when people genuinely uphold their convictions, it is about serving the public, and there is no dishonor in that. Yes, the choices are hard—of course they’re hard.

We made a choice that was extremely difficult. I believed then, and I still believe now, that it was the right choice. History will deliver its verdict in due course. But I am certain of this: what we embody by standing here today in the Rose Garden, answering your questions and facing your scrutiny, represents a way of life that is far better than a brutal secular dictatorship or a fanatical religious regime. It is a better way to live, and whenever people are genuinely given the option, it is the way of life they select. Our duty, as two nations, is to extend that choice to as many people around the world as we can, and to do so with pride.

What I can say with confidence is that the world needs courage. And I can also say that this man beside me is a courageous leader.

Thank you all for coming. Thank you very much.

So, as I indicated at the outset, I’m not going to go into giving personal commentary or advice about David Cameron.

question: Did anyone make remarks about you personally, Mr. President?
interview_answer: I have no idea. I never inquired about that. It may have had nothing to do with me at all; it could have involved something entirely different. What we do know is that it was outside the grounds—on the wall, on the, you know, the barrier, and the upgraded barriers they installed are extremely strong. But this was beyond the White House perimeter. All right? And the Secret Service will be releasing a complete report on it shortly.

question: Does a congressional vote expressing disapproval weaken the president’s capacity to pursue their Iraq agenda?
interview_answer:
Yes, let me address that. A few things: First, I fully recognize that Congress intends to register its views, and it’s obvious where the Democrats stand, and some Republicans as well; I’m aware of that. They opposed the choice I made. That doesn’t mean I question their patriotism or integrity—they’re decent Americans who simply disagree. I weighed some of their arguments and concluded they wouldn’t produce an Iraq that can stand on its own, govern itself, and partner with us in the fight against terror—that’s my judgment.

Second, my concern is that this symbolic, nonbinding measure not be turned into an actual directive that blocks our troops from carrying out the mission I’ve assigned them. That’s why I keep pointing out that, on the one hand, you confirm David Petraeus unanimously, and on the other hand, you threaten not to fund the strategy he said he needed to fulfill his responsibilities, a strategy he explained under oath in the Senate. I’m going to be absolutely clear with Congress, starting now: they must finance our forces and ensure we have the room to maneuver required to accomplish the mission.

Also, I find it noteworthy that they’re issuing a judgment on a plan they haven’t even allowed time to take effect. As I said, I get it; I do understand. What was the second part of your question?

question: Have you obtained any new information about how Sergeant Bergdahl was taken?
interview_answer:
From the very beginning, the United States has held to a deeply held principle: we do not abandon our men and women in uniform. That tradition goes all the way back to the founding of our Republic.

For quite some time, we had been in consultation with Congress about the possibility that, to bring Sergeant Bergdahl home, we might have to carry out some form of prisoner exchange. When we saw an opening, we moved. We were worried about Sergeant Bergdahl’s condition, we had the Qatari government prepared to help us implement a swap, and we acted when that window appeared. The timeline was compressed because we were determined not to lose that chance.

Now, regarding the details of how Sergeant Bergdahl ended up in Taliban hands, we have very deliberately not been debriefing him. He has just emerged from five years in Taliban captivity. Right now, he is undergoing extensive medical and psychological evaluations and facing a major adjustment back to normal life. He hasn’t even been reunited with his family yet, and that should underscore how seriously we treat this reintegration phase, informed by lessons from the Vietnam era. So I’m not going to get into, or speculate about, the exact circumstances of his capture at this time.

Let me underscore something straightforward: no matter what the eventual facts about those circumstances may be, our obligation is the same. If an American service member is held as a prisoner, we bring that service member home. Period. We don’t make that duty conditional. That is what every parent who sends a son or daughter into harm’s way has a right to expect from the Commander in Chief and from this country.

As for the security implications, the Taliban detainees transferred out of Guantanamo are subject to conditions that require the Qataris to maintain oversight and put in place mechanisms for monitoring their activities. We will also be watching them. Could some of them attempt to reengage in actions that threaten us? That risk has always existed with Guantanamo releases; there is a known rate of reoffending. I would not have authorized this if I believed it undermined American national security, and we are confident we can respond if they again pose a threat.

But this is the reality of winding down wars. It was true in George Washington’s time, in Lincoln’s, in FDR’s, and in every major conflict: at some stage, you work to bring your people home. That is the appropriate course of action. And while that process is under way, I am not going to provide a detailed account of how Sergeant Bergdahl was captured.

question: A journalist asks President Biden to clarify why a travel ban still applies to arrivals from Germany and other EU countries, yet travelers from Turkey—where the rate of new infections is seven times higher—are allowed to enter the United States.
interview_answer:
We invited the head of our COVID response team here precisely because the Chancellor raised that point with me. The question of when we can remove the restriction is currently under active review. I’ll be in a position to give you a concrete answer in the coming days about what is likely to occur. Right now, I’m waiting on guidance from our experts and our COVID team on the appropriate timing, and, yes, the Chancellor and I did discuss it.

I brought it up as well and received essentially the same reply the President just gave you. The COVID team is still analyzing the situation. We spoke about it in both directions. The rise of the Delta variant is a new challenge for both our countries. Obviously, before any such step is taken, it has to be carefully considered and durable; it would make little sense to reverse course again only a few days later. I have full confidence in the American COVID experts.

I’ve visited here often, and if we don’t leave immediately, we will miss our scheduled dinner. The Chancellor and I are expected there with several guests very soon. So we must wrap up. Thank you all for your attention and for your questions. Thank you very much.

question: Do you believe all three of these women are lying—just yes or no?
interview_answer: I'm not able to give you an answer on that right now. I need to see tomorrow’s testimony, I need to read the statements. I only learned about one of them not long ago. What I can say is that her attorney is a real bottom-feeder, all right? So I cannot say whether they’re telling the truth or not until I actually hear them. I don’t know what went on today because I’ve been tied up with Japan, with South Korea, with , and about seven other countries, as you’re aware, and I was presiding over the Security Council. So I’m not familiar with what today’s accuser said. I am familiar with the lawyer, and it doesn’t get much worse than that—terrible reputation. Look into his record. As for the other women, I’ll wait to see what comes out tomorrow. I’ll be watching, believe it or not. I want to hear what’s said. It’s possible they may sound persuasive. Now, having said that, Judge Brett Kavanaugh has long been regarded as one of the most respected figures in Washington. He’s served on what you’d call the second-highest court, and everyone knows him; many know him very well, and they don’t buy these accusations. I can always be persuaded, but I need to hear the testimony before I decide.

question: Do you think Ghislaine Maxwell is going to expose influential men involved?
interview_answer: I really have no idea. I haven't been paying very close attention to it. Honestly, I just hope things turn out okay for her. I've run into her many times over the years, particularly when I was living in Palm Beach and, as I understand it, they were in Palm Beach too. But I truly wish her well, whatever the case may be. I don't know what's going on with Prince Andrew. I just don't know anything about that. I'm not informed on it. Next question, please, go ahead.

question: Do you believe you bear any responsibility for Tony Blair not remaining in office throughout your time as president?
interview_answer: I haven’t exactly surveyed Labour Party members on that, so it’s possible. [] But the real issue is whether I’m responsible for his departure, and I honestly can’t say.

question: Looking back on the difficult year of the Presidency, what does the President personally regard as the single greatest error in their choices and conduct?
interview_answer: Are you asking about mistakes on health care alone, or about everything overall?

question: From the President’s standpoint, is there any reason to be worried?
interview_answer: Honestly, I can’t say. I wasn’t part of negotiating that agreement. The idea behind it is fine, but I’m not going to answer right now. We’ll review it a bit and then see what it shows.

question: Can you say where that is?
interview_answer: I’m not able to reveal that right now. I need to wait and see how things develop. I haven’t taken part in that process. I’ve intentionally kept my distance. As, in effect, the nation’s top law enforcement official, I could step in if I chose. I decided it was wiser not to. I believe it’s best for our excellent Attorney General to oversee this. He has Durham, who is highly, highly regarded, handling it, and we’ll find out in due course. I can’t disclose that location.

question: So are you saying the temperature isn’t high?
interview_answer: At this point, I really can't say. What I can tell you is that I'm waiting to hear directly from President Putin about how he viewed the talks. What matters to me is whether he still shares the same concerns I have. I say “still” because when we met in Australia, he once again acknowledged that it is not in the global interest for Iran to be able to develop a nuclear weapon. Russia has backed us at the United Nations, and we’re cooperating with them on a possible third resolution. That’s where my focus lies—I’m not concerned about the photographs, and I understand why those meetings happen. I intend to keep working with Russia and other partners to maintain sustained pressure and make clear: if you persist in your nuclear weapons ambitions, you will remain isolated.

question: Do you have any approximate timeline for when Asad will leave?
interview_answer:
We would have liked to see Asad step down two years ago, a year ago, six months ago, even a couple of months ago. My administration has been absolutely consistent that Asad forfeited his legitimacy the moment he began shooting his own citizens and murdering people who were originally demonstrating peacefully just to secure a stronger voice in how their country is run. Since then, the violence has only intensified. So in terms of timing, our position is straightforward: the earlier he goes, the better.

Now, as for your question about when and how exactly that happens, we’ve already gone over this in broad terms. There is no simple, ready-made solution for an extremely brutal and complicated crisis like the one we’re seeing in Syria. If there were an easy answer or a clear schedule, the Prime Minister and I would already have acted on it and the conflict would be over.

Instead, what we can do is keep up steady, coordinated international pressure and continue to bolster the opposition. I believe that the talks in Geneva—bringing together the Russians and Syrian representatives to negotiate a genuine political transition that all sides can accept—could produce some progress. But while that plays out, our focus will be on supporting the opposition on the ground and addressing the humanitarian catastrophe, and we’ll pursue all of that in very close coordination with Turkey, which is directly affected and with whom we have an excellent partnership.

We’ll be meeting again this evening, as you know, and that will give us an opportunity to go into these questions in more depth. As I’ve said, our perspectives are very much aligned, and tonight we’ll keep working through what further steps we can take jointly, and what elements might form part of a roadmap tied to Geneva and whatever comes after it.

Having Russia and China engaged in this effort is crucial, especially given their roles as permanent members of the U.N. Security Council. Their involvement can add momentum to the process. The broad pressure from the international community remains essential, and if you look at the humanitarian aid we’ve provided so far, it now totals more than $1.5 billion.

Turkey will maintain its open-door policy, and we will continue that as well, because Turkey shares a 910‑kilometer border with Syria, and families are literally split across that border. Those ties matter, and so do these policies.

All of these efforts have real significance for regional stability. On one side, there are ongoing initiatives to normalize relations between Palestinians and Israelis; we don’t need fresh crises in the neighborhood. Turkey has also tried to help Syria and Israel address their disputes—we had five rounds of talks, though they ultimately stalled. My hope is that every step we take going forward in pursuit of regional peace will bear fruit, and that we can keep working resolutely with the United States to secure that peace.

We are actively examining all of these questions. The actions that might be taken by the U.N. Security Council and the next phases in Geneva are key elements, and we’ll continue reviewing them together.

Thank you. Thank you all. Thank you very much.

question: Have you reached a decision on whom you plan to appoint as Chair of the Federal Reserve Board? And if you haven’t, can you explain a bit what factors are guiding you as you weigh candidates for the Fed Chairmanship and the other vacant positions?
interview_answer: No, no, and still no. I’m not going to get into that with you, because that process is already underway. We’ll be rolling out those decisions fairly soon. It’s been moving forward for quite a while now, that’s the first point. Second, I’d also note that we will have ample time to ensure that all the key nominees can be confirmed before the current terms run out. Third, I’ve spent a great deal of time on this, talking with my economic team about who would be the strongest picks, and we do have many strong options. But I’m not going to offer any predictions at this stage. Nancy — from CBS — I believe you had your hand up. Is that right?

question: Do you still back Secretary Azar?
interview_answer: I’ll just wish him well. Back whom?

question: What timeframe have you been given for how long this is supposed to last?
interview_answer:
I haven’t been given any clear timeline at all. They’ve been in place for a very long period, under this leader and the one before. Eventually, I assume, the situation will shift. But we actually haven’t imposed the truly severe sanctions yet. We’re able to apply much tougher measures, and every option is still on the table, so we may decide to go that route. So far, as you know, we’ve stayed more or less in the middle range. We can escalate a lot further if it becomes necessary.

But the reality is extremely tragic, and our only aim is to help the many people who are starving and dying in the streets. What’s happening there is shameful. This used to be among the richest nations on earth, and now suddenly it’s devastated—crushing poverty, no food, no water, no air conditioning, nothing. Last week there was no electricity for a long stretch—no power. And that’s going to fail again, because everything is just barely holding together. So it’s a very heartbreaking situation, Roberta. Really heartbreaking to witness. All right?

question: What does the term torture mean?
interview_answer: Meaning of which one?

question: Should decisions within NATO on sending heavy weaponry to Ukraine require full consensus, and should Germany, as NATO’s leading European power, itself supply such heavy arms to Ukraine?
interview_answer:
There is no issue of having to rebuild confidence. He enjoys the full confidence of the United States. Germany is—and remains—one of our key allies worldwide. There is absolutely no question about Germany’s role as a partner of the United States. None whatsoever.

On support for Ukraine, Germany is among the biggest financial backers. It has been at the forefront in ensuring Ukraine receives robust economic aid. You actually raised several points—I’ve lost track of all of them—but as for some U.S. commentators claiming Germany isn’t dependable, that is simply wrong. Germany is entirely dependable—fully, completely, without reservation. I have zero doubts about Germany. We stand together. The transatlantic alliance between Germany and the United States is a lasting cornerstone of German policy and will remain so going forward—just as central as it is today—and it will always rank among our highest priorities.

Within NATO, on the European continent, we are the nation making the largest overall contribution, in both funding and military capability. And we play a major role in the financial support for Ukraine—we do not share your assessment of who carries the heaviest financial burden. Since 2014, we have provided roughly 2 billion U.S. dollars in direct bilateral assistance and, via the EU, helped make an additional 3.8 billion available. This is a very substantial effort to stabilize Ukraine’s economy, and we are prepared to continue in this way.

This reflects the deep, unshakable friendship between our two nations. As part of that, in light of the tense situation on Ukraine’s border due to Russian forces, we have stated very clearly that we will act in full unity on sanctions. I am not going to go into the specific issue of heavy weapons deliveries you mentioned.

question: Do you have any worries about how easily people are able to vote?
interview_answer: From what I’ve been told, things ran very smoothly in Georgia — very smoothly. But, once again, that’s really something you’d need to take up with the state officials, because I’m not the one to answer that. Next question. Yes, you there, go ahead.

question: Your response to news that Ruth Bader Ginsburg was hospitalized with an infection.
interview_answer: I’m not going to comment on that, other than to say I hope she recovers quickly. I actually hadn’t heard about it. She was admitted to the hospital? That’s unfortunate. But no, beyond wishing her a full recovery, I won’t say more.

question: Were you briefed today on any confirmed coronavirus infections inside the White House?
interview_answer: You mean today?

question: Have you come across any proof that Iran is speeding up its nuclear efforts?
interview_answer: I have not reviewed the particular report you’re mentioning. What I can say is that I am deeply worried about Iran’s desire to obtain a nuclear weapon. I am convinced that, if Iran were to acquire such a weapon, it would greatly destabilize the Middle East. That’s why we’ve been working to assemble an international coalition aimed at persuading Iran to abandon that effort, and to underscore that they face a fundamental choice between isolation and the opportunity to develop their economy. So while I’m not going to address that specific report, we do take Iran’s pursuit of a nuclear weapon very seriously.

question: Did this constitute a threat against the President?
interview_answer: We don't have that information right now. We simply don't know. The investigators will determine that.

question: What actions will you take to help them, sir, beyond Syria?
interview_answer: Look, what I can say is that we’ll be talking about that issue. Those conversations are already underway. But the Kurds have given us incredible backing in the fight to destroy ISIS. All right?

question: How do you plan to win Democratic backing for that proposal?
interview_answer: You'll need to put that question to them.

question: Will they continue receiving an extra $600 each week?
interview_answer: I'm not going to confirm that right now. You'll find out when the time comes, but we do have the funds.

question: What effect might this have on ongoing peace talks?
interview_answer: To start, what strikes me is that they chose to move forward with that decision only after the election, even though we had known for quite a while that this was in the works. That timing itself underlines the very real difficulties the Russian armed forces are facing. That’s the first point.The second point is that, at the very least, this will create a pause that lets all sides rethink their stances over the winter. And we simply don’t know yet whether that will result in any decision on Ukraine’s part to make concessions to Russia.I’ll be attending the G‑20 summit. My understanding is that President Putin probably won’t attend, but other major leaders will be in Indonesia. We’ll use that meeting to gauge what possible moves might come next.Nancy from CBS—Nancy Cordes.

question: Could you provide us with the Red Cross report?
interview_answer: I haven’t read it. Our policy forbids torture. Next question, Jim.

question: Do you personally consider homosexual conduct to be wrong?
interview_answer: I am not going to pass moral judgment on anyone’s sexual orientation. What I will say is that I support the ‘don’t ask, don’t tell’ policy. Sammon, from the Washington Times, go ahead.

question: Does it benefit a company to be concerned about the climate?
interview_answer: Apologies, but I’m not following what you’re asking.

question: Does that issue concern you?
interview_answer: Look, I’ve already said all I’m going to say on that. Mary Bruce, ABC News.

question: What was the reason Prime Minister Maliki did not appear at yesterday’s press conference with General Casey and Ambassador Khalilzad?
interview_answer: Elaine, I honestly don’t know the reason for his absence.

question: How much real influence do you believe he holds while Putin remains a central player?
interview_answer:
I don’t really have a deep understanding of Medvedev either. What’s going to be telling is who actually shows up—who officially speaks for Russia—at something like the G-8 summit. That will be one indicator; it should, I think, shed some light on how Russia plans to approach its foreign policy once Vladimir Putin is no longer President. And at this point I just can’t give you an answer to your question yet.

What I can say is that it remains in our national interest to maintain a working relationship with Russia. On issues like nonproliferation, for instance, it’s critical for us to cooperate to ensure that dangerous materials don’t spread. It’s also important that we keep working together on Iran. As I mentioned here the last time, I welcomed Vladimir Putin’s statement to the Iranians that Russia would supply the enriched uranium needed to fuel the Bushehr reactor, which removes their justification for enriching uranium themselves. I regarded that as a helpful step, and we need to be able to coordinate with Russia on the Iran file.

There are many other areas as well. Just yesterday, with the Czech Prime Minister, I discussed plans for a missile defense system in Europe. I believe it serves our interests to find a way to convince the Russians that this system isn’t directed at them but at the real dangers of this century, such as a possible launch of a weapon of mass destruction from a rogue regime.

So there are domains, David, where cooperation is essential—let me finish—and my aim is to leave things in a condition where my successor can sustain a constructive relationship with whoever is shaping Russian foreign policy. That’s simply in America’s interest. That doesn’t mean we’ll see eye to eye on everything—clearly, we didn’t on Kosovo, and other disagreements will arise. But it’s still vital for the country that there be a relationship, leader to leader and, ideally, broader than that.

question: How would you describe the unexplained aerial objects that former President Obama talked about?
interview_answer: I’d put that back to him, not me. Thanks. [] Alright, we’re done here, let’s move. []

question: Do you think the Justice Department’s collection of Associated Press reporters’ phone records this week—or earlier, as was just disclosed—amounted to an excessive use of power?
interview_answer:
I’ll leave it to you and your colleagues to make those kinds of comparisons, to look back at the record, and to reach your own judgments. My focus is on ensuring that if something is going wrong in the Government, we correct it. That’s my job, and that’s what we’re going to do. That applies to the IRS, where we have to be certain the laws are enforced as Congress intended. It applies to the safety of our diplomats, which is why we’ll have to partner with Congress to secure the funding needed to protect them adequately.

Now, when it comes to the Department of Justice, I’m not going to weigh in on a particular, ongoing matter. What I can do is speak more generally about the balance we have to maintain. Unauthorized disclosures involving national security can endanger people. They can put at risk the service members I’ve ordered into combat. They can endanger intelligence personnel working in difficult and fragile environments where their cover or their missions can easily be compromised.

Our national security depends on those individuals being able to do their jobs with the confidence that people back home support them and won’t leave them exposed or in even greater danger than they already face. So I make no apologies for being concerned about leaks that could jeopardize their operations or cost them their lives, and I don’t think the American people would want a Commander in Chief who wasn’t focused on that.

At the same time, we’re a democracy, and a free press, free speech, and the open circulation of information are vital to holding me and the rest of the Government accountable and to making our system work. My entire reason for entering politics was because I believe profoundly in that democratic process.

The aim of the media shield legislation—which had substantial backing from places like the Washington Post editorial board and from prosecutors—was to find a fair way to strike that balance. And if this case—about which we still don’t have the full picture—has renewed interest in how best to achieve that balance, then this is an appropriate moment to revisit that bill. That’s a conversation worth having, and it matters.

But it’s also crucial to understand that when I voice concern about leaks at a time when we still have over 60,000 troops in Afghanistan and many intelligence officers deployed worldwide in dangerous posts—some as hazardous as the one in Benghazi—part of my responsibility is to safeguard their work while still making room for the public’s right to know and to hold my office to account. So I’m not going to discuss the specifics of this particular case, but I do think we need to keep working on getting that broader balance right.

question: Do you believe the cap on Russian oil exports will actually reduce gasoline costs for drivers in the United States?
interview_answer: Let me just clarify here—the latter part of what you asked was, whether it would lower the cost?

question: Is the administration currently weighing options to prohibit or limit Chinese reporters’ access in America?
interview_answer: At this point, I really haven’t received a thorough briefing on that issue. We’re going to review it. We’ll take a look, absolutely. It’s not something I like to see happening. I don’t think it’s right, because we provide very strong access. Some might argue we allow too much access. I don’t agree with that at all. I think our access is very strong, so I don’t see it as fair. But we’ll reach a decision on it fairly soon. All right? Thank you. That was a very good question. Next, please. Next. Next.

question: What information does the President have regarding the accusations made against his nominee, Dr. Ronny Jackson?
interview_answer:
I’m not familiar with those specific accusations, and I’m not going to speak to them, but I can say this: he is one of the best people I’ve ever worked with, and I think my wife would say the same. He served as physician to President Obama, I believe also to President Bush, and to me as well. I’ve come to know him quite well. He’s an excellent doctor.

His nomination came as a recommendation. I understand people are raising concerns about his experience, that there’s a perceived lack of it. But I take the Veterans Administration extremely seriously. We’ve already done tremendous work there, as you know, with the Accountability Act and other measures. Now we’re moving forward on choice, and that will happen. We are going to look after our veterans incredibly well. That’s a top priority, and we’ve already made major progress.

I just said to Admiral Jackson not long ago, Why would you want to put yourself through this? These are very vicious critics who smear people—and they do it all the time. I’ve dealt with it myself; we’ve all seen it. You in the media are enjoying record ratings because of that, so congratulations. But I asked him, Why go through it? He’s an admiral, a strong leader, and they’ve been nitpicking him over every tiny thing.

And as you saw with what should soon be Secretary of State Pompeo, everyone was shocked. Ten minutes before the committee vote, I was told he would be rejected in committee—which would have been the first time in many decades for a Secretary of State nominee. But I spoke with Rand Paul—he’s almost never disappointed me, he’s a good person—and I knew some things others didn’t. Rand Paul said he would change his vote, and he did, surprising everyone. The final tally was 11–9, because, as you know, Senator Johnny Isakson’s vote is counted if it isn’t the deciding vote. So it ended up 11–9 with, I believe, one member voting present—what’s the exact term for that, John?

question: Have you obtained concrete intelligence indicating that President Putin is actually deploying these weapons, positioning them for action, or actively weighing their use?
interview_answer: On that first part, I’m not going to answer. I’m not prepared to share any intelligence information, that’s the first point. Second, if he uses such a weapon, we will respond. Our response would be calibrated to the specific way in which it was used. Next question, Josh from Bloomberg.

question: Is the United States prepared to tighten sanctions and potentially step up pressure on North Korea to make progress?
interview_answer: I'm not going to address that directly. What I can say is this: we already have very tough sanctions in place. I’m not interested in getting into a discussion about ramping them up. They’re already strong. There are many good, ordinary people in North Korea who have to live under those conditions too, and that matters to me. And I’ll add this: my overall approach has shifted quite a bit because, as you know, I’ve gotten to know Chairman Kim very well. They have their own perspective as well. So I really don’t want to get into that specific issue. I just believe that, hopefully, for the sake of the region, for Japan, and honestly for others—I was speaking with President Xi, who is someone widely respected—I said to him, you can’t be happy having a nuclear-armed neighbor on your border. And he’s not. He truly isn’t. I can tell you he also wants that situation resolved. And I’ll leave it there. Now, ladies and gentlemen, I’ve got a plane to catch back to a wonderful place called Washington, DC. So thank you all very much. Thank you. Thanks, everyone. Thank you very much.

question: Could the United States choose to halt its pursuit of Meng Wanzhou’s extradition?
interview_answer: Under which circumstances? According to what criteria?

question: In any case, do you believe that destroying those tapes was an appropriate and prudent action?
interview_answer: I think it’s been made quite plain that my earliest awareness of this came when Mike Hayden briefed me, and that should be understood as my initial point of reference. Beyond that, I trust that the preliminary review by the Attorney General and the CIA’s Inspector General, together with congressional oversight, will ultimately clarify precisely what took place. Because of that, as these reviews and hearings proceed, I’m going to withhold any judgment until I have a complete picture of the facts. I recognize this is an issue I’ll be pressed on repeatedly, so let me be upfront: until those investigations are wrapped up and the oversight process is finished, I will not be offering any opinion on this matter from this podium.

question: Why has Vice President Pence not adhered to the CDC’s self-quarantine recommendations?
interview_answer: On that point, I’m going to leave it to him to explain his actions. Whatever steps he’s taking, he’ll address those himself. I’ll see that they issue a statement about it. John, your question next, please.

question: Does the historic election of two Muslim women—one of whom wears a veil—to the House contradict the message you’ve been promoting?
interview_answer: I have no idea what you’re talking about. Huh?

question: Do you think Nancy Pelosi looks anything like Bob Bullock?
interview_answer: [] That’s more of an in-group joke, and I’m not going to weigh in on it. What I will say is that I’m fundamentally an optimist. I expected we’d lose some seats; I just didn’t know the exact number.

question: For how much longer should Americans expect to shoulder higher driving costs as a result of this war?
interview_answer: Let me go back to what you asked in the second half—about whether this would lower prices.

question: What led you to choose not to merge any agencies and instead expand the number of them? Isn't having so many agencies itself a major part of the problem?
interview_answer:
You're correct to notice that, and precisely because of that, I'm not going to preview the specifics before my formal announcement tomorrow. So let me limit myself to outlining the general principles, and then tomorrow you'll be able to press the administration on the exact details of what we're putting forward.

In broad terms, the core issue was that weak supervision and multiple regulatory blind spots let financial firms—not only traditional banks, but a range of non‑bank entities—take on excessive risks that didn’t just threaten those firms, but nearly brought down the U.S. economy and triggered a severe global downturn. We have to ensure that our regulatory framework—largely unchanged since the 1930s—is modernized to handle massive cross‑border capital flows and complex new financial products and risk‑taking that have proven extremely dangerous for ordinary Americans.

We will be unveiling a robust package of regulatory reforms that we believe can significantly reduce the chances of another crisis like this. We anticipate Congress will move promptly to enact these measures. I intend to sign them and see them implemented as soon as possible.

Once you see the full blueprint we’re releasing, you’ll see that we are not simply piling on a bunch of new regulators. In reality, there will be rationalization, some consolidation, and tighter coordination so that people no longer slip through the cracks—whether we’re talking about consumer protection, investor safeguards, or the systemic risks we must prevent. Taken together, it will be a far more coherent and effective system than what we had before.

But, as is often the case, this will be tough going. There will be powerful interests that prefer to keep making high‑risk bets while expecting U.S. taxpayers to rescue them if things go south. You’ll hear the familiar complaints that we don’t need additional rules and that government should just get out of the way. Many people have short memories, and some lobbyists are counting on that—hoping we overlook the catastrophe caused by their irresponsible behavior. I intend to keep reminding everyone of what happened so we put safeguards in place that stop this kind of crisis from recurring, but I’m not going to spell out tomorrow’s announcement today.

question: Would the upcoming meeting likely happen in the near future, or could it be delayed?
interview_answer: Honestly, I’m not in a position to say. It could happen fairly soon, or it could be pushed far down the road. I can’t give you a definite answer. I’d like it to be sooner rather than later, but it might still end up being much later on.

question: Your view on whether this could advance or undermine a two-state solution.
interview_answer:
All right. Let me start by saying I want to applaud the Senate for taking action to move ahead on granting me the authority not only to negotiate a forward-looking, progressive, pro-growth trade agreement with countries in the Asia-Pacific region, and possibly European partners as well, but also to equip this administration with the enforcement mechanisms to make sure those agreements actually have teeth—something that has too often been missing in prior deals.

So I’m grateful to every Senator who voted to give that authority, or at least to open the floor to a full debate so we can keep pushing the process along. For those who opposed it, I intend to keep engaging, continue laying out the facts, and give them the data and assurances they’re asking for—because while there have unquestionably been some very real problems with earlier trade agreements, I am convinced that the framework we’re putting forward here is the right one, not only for large U.S. corporations but also for small and mid-sized American businesses and, above all, for American workers. I simply would not be advocating any trade arrangement that I believed would end up destroying jobs or undercutting wages in this country. My core goal, from day one in office, has been to create employment, build real pathways to economic security, and raise incomes for working families—and every policy I support is judged against that standard.

Now, regarding this broader debate involving me and Elizabeth, it has never been about personalities. I know it’s tempting for the press to play up a rift when you see two long-time allies clashing on a major policy question. But quite a few of my closest friends in the Senate and the House—some of the very first people to support my campaigns—are on the other side of this issue. I get where they’re coming from. Like me, they saw factories shut down and jobs leave over the past few decades. Like me, they worry that labor protections and environmental standards overseas are not adequately enforced. Like me, they ask whether trade is truly fair, not just nominally free.

And like me, they’re responding to these global and domestic patterns we’ve all been watching: widening inequality, structural changes linked to automation and globalization, and a system that seems to concentrate gains at the top while middle-class wages stagnate and people striving to get into the middle class feel stuck. These are people whose fundamental values match mine almost point for point.

I took a look at a recent progressive manifesto that some of these colleagues put out—laying out what it means, in their view, to be a progressive today—and, frankly, it was essentially a restatement of my own governing agenda, except on the question of trade. That’s the one big divergence. What we’re dealing with here is a substantive policy disagreement and a different read of the evidence on what ultimately serves our constituents best.

My position is that, despite the track record of some earlier agreements, the United States is better off taking the lead in setting high-standard rules—with strong, enforceable provisions—on issues like child labor, illegal logging and deforestation, environmental damage, wildlife trafficking, and intellectual property for what will be the most dynamic, fast-growing markets in the world. If we decline to shape those rules, then China and other countries will fill that vacuum. And if that happens, our businesses will be at a competitive disadvantage, and in the long run our workers will pay the price.

On the concern about jobs being shipped overseas, the reality—based on the analyses I’ve seen—is that if a U.S. company is determined to chase the lowest wages, current rules don’t stop them from moving production abroad right now. That option already exists. What this agreement does is give us an opening to bring more production back to the United States.

We’re already seeing the beginnings of that. That’s one reason I traveled to Nike. I’m very aware that Nike has long relied on lower-wage labor in parts of the Asia-Pacific region and that this has cost U.S. footwear workers jobs over the last 30 years. But now the company is talking about using new manufacturing technologies that could create as many as 10,000 jobs here at home, precisely because we’re moving up the value chain and making things in new ways. That’s a real chance for us—but we still need to reach those overseas customers if we want to fully capture that upside.

So my message to my progressive friends is that, if we share the same priorities—and we do—then we should be putting our energy into the policies we all agree will make the biggest difference: a robust minimum wage, serious investments in job training, a major push on infrastructure that puts people to work, stronger protections for collective bargaining and worker voice, tougher enforcement on overtime rules, and guarantees like paid sick leave. On top of that, we need a straightforward debate about our fiscal choices so we’re not hacking away at future-oriented investments just to preserve tax loopholes for corporations that don’t actually help the broader economy.

Those are the tools that will directly confront the problems my friends are rightly worried about. Simply killing a trade agreement will not solve them—especially when these same critics are the first to point out that the status quo trade rules are failing American workers. If the existing system is already bad for us, it’s hard to see how freezing that system in place somehow improves things for working people in this country.

I strongly support real enforcement, including the enforcement pieces we’ve been discussing. I have flagged issues with the way the proposed currency provisions are currently drafted, and I’ve made clear I’m concerned they could unintentionally constrain our own ability to run an effective monetary policy. I’m going to keep talking with Senator Schumer, Senator Brown, and others to see how we can adjust that language so it doesn’t backfire.

Now, as for your earlier question, I’ve frankly lost track of what that second part was, so I’m not going to get into it.

question: Could President Lee clarify what stage the talks are at on lengthening South Korea’s missile range?
interview_answer:
Thank you. You asked about the upcoming launch of that country’s long‑range missile, and let me emphasize once more, ladies and gentlemen: if that state proceeds with its planned long‑range missile test, it will be in blatant violation of United Nations Security Council Resolution 1874. Moving forward with this test would directly contradict the commitments it has made to the international community. That is why the international community is calling on that regime to withdraw its announcement and cancel the long‑range missile test.

Now, you also asked how we intend to respond. I want to address that point only to say this: if that country presses ahead, it alone will bear full responsibility for the consequences. As I have already indicated, should it follow through, it will only increase its own isolation, and it will have to face all of the resulting repercussions by itself.

From the standpoint of its own citizens, they will see their leadership spending hundreds of millions of dollars on a long‑range missile launch. Watching that, they will not feel genuine pride that their country has fired a missile; instead, they will come to understand the true nature of their rulers and why they are enduring such severe hardship today.

Domestically, I am sure the leadership there hopes to achieve certain objectives with this missile test. But they must recognize that if they move ahead, they will be putting themselves in an extremely difficult situation. I urge that regime to act as a responsible member of the international community; that is the surest way to improve the lives of its own people.

On this matter, there is no division between the United States and South Korea. We will remain calm and reasonable, and we will respond wisely if, in fact, they carry out their plan.

As for your specific question about extending South Korea’s missile range: that topic was not taken up in our discussions. Yes, it is being examined by working‑level officials, but I am not going to go into it here. It needs to be considered in a broader context, as one element of strengthening our overall defensive capabilities vis‑à‑vis that country.

question: Will your administration restore the Houthi rebels or associated militias to the official terrorism list?
interview_answer: What I can say is that the matter is still being reviewed. Achieving an end to this war requires both sides to engage, and that process is extremely challenging. That’s all I can say on it.

question: Ask whether the Attorney General and the Deputy Attorney General can expect to remain in their roles long term.
interview_answer: I'm not going to get into that right now. We'll deal with questions like that at another point. We're reviewing a number of things, including Cabinet positions. Overall, I'm very pleased with the bulk of my Cabinet. We're considering various people for various roles, which, as you know, is very typical after the midterm elections. I chose not to make any moves before the midterms. But I can say that, in general, I'm extremely satisfied with my Cabinet.
