For example, Mike Pompeo has just been outstanding in his role; he's done a remarkable job——

question: When is the timing for changes to vaccine availability for young kids?
interview_answer: Right now, the medical data hasn’t advanced to the stage where experts are fully satisfied that it’s safe, and that’s exactly what they’re working on at the moment. I was asked something similar a few months ago about kids, I think it was those roughly 7 to 12 years old. Eventually the evidence reached a level where they were confident, based on the volume and results of the trials, that it was safe for that age group. The same process is happening here, and it will happen—it will. But I cannot give you a date; I’m not a scientist, so I’m not going to say when. What matters is that we get to that next step safely.

question: How do you personally regard North Korea’s leader, Kim Jong Un?
interview_answer:
On the broader question of North Korea, for South Korea, the United States, and the wider international community, the central goal we all must pursue is the complete dismantlement of North Korea’s nuclear program and its transformation into a responsible actor on the world stage. That outcome would advance peace on the Korean Peninsula and globally, and it would also serve North Korea’s own long‑term development. That is the perspective from which I approach this issue.

To encourage North Korea to choose that path and alter its current thinking, we need to act in close coordination. In this effort, China’s role and leverage are significant, so China’s active participation is essential, and we have reached a common understanding on that point.

Regarding the positions of China and Russia, I believe that both of them—as well as the rest of the international community—recognize the necessity of a nuclear‑free Korean Peninsula and are working together to steer North Korea toward the right course. Specifically, when it comes to North Korea’s missile launches and nuclear tests, China has played a proactive role in passing U.N. Security Council resolutions and is implementing those measures in earnest.

Russia, for its part, is also strongly committed to denuclearization on the Korean Peninsula. It has actively backed the adoption of U.N. Security Council resolutions on North Korea and has pushed for strong language directed at Pyongyang in the G‑8 foreign ministers’ joint statement. These constructive steps by China and Russia are crucial to presenting a united front to North Korea—making clear that its nuclear arsenal is unacceptable and pressing it to change course.

As for Kim Jong Un himself, I do not know him personally. I have never spoken with him and therefore cannot and will not offer an assessment of his personal traits. What we can judge are the measures his regime has taken, which have been confrontational and appear to lead nowhere.

I want to stress that I fully support maintaining a robust deterrent and not granting benefits in response to provocations. At the same time, the door remains open for North Korea to choose a peaceful route: giving up its nuclear weapons, honoring its international obligations, returning to the community of nations, and moving gradually toward greater security and prosperity for its people.

If the policies North Korea has followed so far have failed to create a strong and prosperous state, then this is an appropriate moment for Kim Jong Un to reconsider that record and opt for a different direction. Should he decide to do so, not only I but the international community as a whole would welcome such a change.

China, Russia, Japan, and the other key states involved in the six‑party talks have all indicated this very clearly. But what matters ultimately is concrete change in conduct. There is a saying in English: don’t focus on my words; pay attention to my actions. Up to now, we have not observed steps from North Korea that show it is ready to move onto a new path. So, to reiterate, I am not in a position to pass personal judgment on Kim Jong Un, and I will refrain from doing so.

question: If marriage should be treated as a basic civil right
interview_answer:
That was quite a mix of issues there. [] Chuck, we ought to have you sitting on the Supreme Court. []I'm not on the Supreme Court, and I'm not going to play constitutional scholar today. I'm not going to get into that legal characterization. Instead, let me start by addressing the underlying situation in . I want to deal with the actual facts on the ground, because there’s been a lot of chatter about procedure and consultations with Congress and so on. Let’s walk through, in concrete terms, what has taken place.

Muammar Qadhafi—who, before Usama bin Laden, was behind more American deaths than almost anyone else—was openly threatening to slaughter his own citizens. Acting as part of a broad international coalition, under a U.N. mandate that is close to without precedent, we moved in and disabled air defenses so that our partners could enforce a no‑fly zone and deliver humanitarian protection to civilians.

I went before the American public and explained what our role would be. I made clear there would be no American ground troops, and that we would not shoulder the bulk of the operation. Our role, as a NATO ally, would be to support this effort because it aligns with our national security interests and because it is simply the morally right course.

We have followed through exactly as promised. We have not deployed ground forces. Our NATO partners—who we’ve often criticized for not pulling their weight—have in this case taken on a substantial share of the burden. As a result, we’ve safeguarded thousands of lives in ; we have not lost a single U.S. service member; and we have avoided the prospect of further escalation. This mission has been tightly limited both in duration and in scope.

So I told the American people we had a focused objective. We have executed that focused mission extraordinarily well. Throughout, we have engaged Congress: there have been roughly 10 hearings; we’ve provided extensive documentation on our activities; and I’ve invited Members of Congress to the White House to discuss the operation. Much of the current controversy, frankly, is political.

On the merits, if you look at what we’ve actually done, we have carried out precisely what we set out to do, under a clear U.N. authorization, and in doing so have saved thousands of lives. Meanwhile, a leader who sponsored terrorist acts against the United States now finds himself cornered, with increasing pressure around him.

If you go back to the origins of the War Powers Resolution, it was a response to Vietnam, where we had about half a million troops deployed, tens of thousands of Americans killed, and hundreds of billions of dollars spent. In that context, Congress said, in essence, that it never wanted to see that kind of open‑ended commitment again, and that for those sorts of engagements, a President must fully consult Congress upfront.

I believe that level of consultation is entirely proper. But do I believe that what we have done here runs afoul of the War Powers Resolution? No, I do not. So there’s no need for me to wade into the constitutional law debate you’re inviting—I'm not going to answer that in the way you're framing it.

question: Is he explicitly denying that her departure could have been a firing?
interview_answer:
Look, Jackie, honestly, I can’t say. I’m being straight with you here—I only learned about this earlier today, so I don’t yet have the details, and Ken Salazar has been up on the Hill testifying. As for your first point, on MMS, Ken Salazar was already in the middle of trying to clean that agency up. But the larger point I’m making is that those reforms clearly weren’t moving fast enough. If they had been fully in place, maybe this would have been caught. Then again, maybe it still would have slipped through. We could have put a new environmental review process in place, had a team of technical experts pour over BP’s submission, and they might still have concluded that it met industry norms, that we hadn’t seen a disaster like this in 15 years, and that it was acceptable to proceed.

That’s precisely what this Commission has to get to the bottom of: Are we looking at a deep, structural failure, or a freak event? Is this the kind of thing that happens once in a million drilling operations, once in a thousand, once in every 5,000? What, specifically, are the probabilities and the dangers involved?

Let me also step back and make a broader observation about energy. The very fact that companies now have to drill a mile under the ocean and then go another three miles below the seabed just to reach oil tells you something about where the oil business is headed. The resource is harder and more expensive to get to, and that inherently raises the level of risk.

That’s part of why you never heard me chanting “Drill, baby, drill.” We are not going to solve this challenge simply by drilling more. Offshore drilling might play a role as a short‑term bridge while we move toward newer technologies and alternative sources, but we need to be realistic that the easy‑to‑reach oil has largely already been taken out of the ground.

As we go forward, the engineering gets tougher, the fields get more remote, and the hazards go up. As a country, we’re going to have to make some very tough choices about which risks we’re prepared to tolerate. And that’s another question this Commission is going to have to grapple with.

At the same time, we have to recognize that our economy and way of life will still rely on oil for some time, even as we start this transition. That’s why, when I met with the Republican caucus this week, I urged cooperation. Senators Lieberman and Kerry have been working on legislation that originally included Senator Graham—he’s not formally on the bill right now—but they’ve put together a framework that could draw bipartisan backing. It acknowledges that we’ll still require oil production, yet it also recognizes that we can already see the problems on the horizon if we fail to change course. So we need to begin shifting how we do business. All right?

Macarena Vidal, EFE News. She’s not here? Oh, there you are.

question: Request for details on the Al Qaida intel assessment.
interview_answer: Sorry, come again?

question: Did you instruct your Chief of Staff and the White House Counsel to go to John Ashcroft’s hospital room while he was ill in order to secure his approval for that program?
interview_answer: Kelly, there’s been a great deal of guessing about what did or didn’t occur, and I’m not going to get into that. This is an extremely sensitive operation. What I can say is, first, the program is vital for safeguarding the American people, and it remains vital because we still face enemies who want to harm us. That means I have a duty to put in place measures that both protect the country and respect Americans’ civil liberties, and this particular effort was, throughout, subject to ongoing review and regular briefings to Congress. As I’ve said, it is a critical tool in defending our nation. People will continue to talk about it in all sorts of ways, but I’m not going to advance that debate by discussing a highly sensitive, highly classified matter. What I will reiterate is that the program is necessary.

question: More concretely, have you achieved any tangible results from your diplomatic approach yet?
interview_answer: Look, I think by now you know me well enough to understand that I’m not someone who likes to sit here and declare whether something is a success or not in a situation like this. We prefer to keep our intentions and assessments somewhat under wraps. What I can say is this: I’m convinced that taking the course we’re taking is the right thing to do, not only for our country, but for people everywhere. So there are very strong, very sound reasons behind what we’re doing. Along those lines, yes, I believe we’re moving forward in a significant way. I think we’re demonstrating tremendous resolve. I think they recognize that our capabilities are unmatched. There’s never been power like what we have now. As you know, we’ve deployed three of the largest aircraft carriers in existence, and they’re in place as we speak. We’ve also positioned a nuclear submarine. There are many measures in motion that we hope—and I’ll go further, we pray—we never have to actually employ. Having said that, I truly think it’s in their interest to come to the table and reach an agreement that benefits their people and the wider world. I do see some signs of movement, yes. But I’m not going to spell out or label that as ‘success’ right now; we’ll have to wait and see how it unfolds.

question: Could you clarify whether your very first priority would be to apply conditions on federal contractors dealing with advancing gay rights or contraception policies?
interview_answer: Margaret, I’m not going to roll out concrete details bit by bit. Once we’ve finished a full review and know exactly what powers we have, I’ll share that information with you.

question: And secondly, regarding Operation Fast and Furious, lawmakers here and officials in Mexico say they still lack clear information. Do you intend to make changes in ATF leadership, and when should we anticipate seeing the findings of the current probe?
interview_answer:
On your second point, as you’re aware, my Attorney General has already stated unequivocally that he never would have authorized firearms trafficking to move into Mexico. The inquiry is still ongoing. I am not going to discuss an active investigation. I’ve been very clear that such an approach by the ATF would be unacceptable, and we need to determine how it was allowed to occur. Once the investigation is finished, I expect that whatever steps are warranted will be taken.

On E‑Verify, what we really need is comprehensive immigration reform. I’ve said this before, I’m saying it now, and I’ll still be saying it months from today. We must have a system that both maintains our identity as a nation governed by laws and honors our identity as a nation of immigrants. That requires strong border enforcement, cracking down on employers who illegally hire and exploit workers, and also creating a realistic path to legal status for people currently living in the shadows.

We may not achieve every element I’d like in one bill, but the package must be balanced. E‑Verify can be a useful enforcement mechanism if it’s accurate and if U.S. citizens’ rights are safeguarded, because I don’t want employers compelled to use a system they can’t trust. And we don’t want companies rejecting qualified applicants because a faulty database wrongly labels them as undocumented when they are not—that’s unfair to the worker and could put the employer at legal risk too.

So the objective now is to keep improving the E‑Verify system and ensure there are protections that prevent the kinds of problems I just outlined. At the same time, we can’t lose sight of the other parts of immigration reform—for instance, ensuring that DREAMers—young people raised here who see themselves as Americans, who lack legal status through no fault of their own, and who want to study, serve in our military, start businesses, and contribute—are able to remain here. We need a broader, more balanced strategy than simply a verification tool. Okay?

question: Now that Mr. Trump has been elected, wouldn’t basic political courtesy require you to publicly announce right away that you will be running for office again?
interview_answer:
Let me start by saying that I make it a point not to interfere in the domestic politics of other countries. What I can say is that Chancellor Merkel has been an exceptional counterpart. She’s essentially the only one of our closest partners who was already in office when I took over, so in a sense, we’ve both become the old hands who have weathered a great many tests together over the past eight years. And while we haven’t agreed on every single issue, when it comes to fundamental values—her honesty, her seriousness, the way she digs into the briefings, understands the data, and keeps the well‑being of the German people front and center, while still understanding that good national leadership requires engagement with the broader world and effective work in multilateral forums—I consider her performance exemplary.

So whether she chooses to run again is her decision alone, and ultimately, it is for the German public to determine what lies ahead. If I were living here as a German citizen and had a ballot, I could imagine casting it for her. [] But I’m not sure whether that kind of statement is actually helpful or not. []

On Russia, my basic stance has not changed since I first assumed office. Russia is a major country, a nuclear power, with regional and global reach. For us to tackle many of the world’s larger challenges, it is in our interest to seek cooperation with Russia where we can.

We should all want a Russia that is stable and prosperous, where people have jobs, the economy expands, relations with neighbors are constructive, and Moscow plays a positive role on global issues like climate change. That’s why I’ve tried to build a constructive relationship, while also being clear‑eyed about areas where our worldviews diverge significantly.

The principles we’ve discussed—democracy, free expression, adherence to international rules and the rule of law, respect for other nations’ right to chart their own course and to have their territorial integrity safeguarded—these are not negotiable. So on matters such as Ukraine and Syria, there have been very sharp disagreements. My hope is that the incoming President adopts a similarly pragmatic but principled approach: working with Russia where our interests and values overlap, but also being prepared to push back when Russia steps outside accepted norms and our shared values.

I don’t assume the President‑elect will copy our policies step for step. My concern—and my hope—is that he will not default to a purely transactional, power‑politics mindset that says we should just strike deals with Russia even if it harms people, flouts international law, undermines vulnerable states, or plants the seeds of long‑term instability in places like Syria, simply because it seems expedient in the moment. How he approaches that will become clearer as he selects his team.

I do take some comfort from his repeated pledge that NATO remains a firm, unchanging obligation. His explicit affirmation of NATO as the bedrock of our collective security is, in my view, of real significance.

Finally, as for my own discussions with President Putin, those all occurred before the U.S. election. As I’ve already said, there is firm evidence that Russian actors have carried out cyber intrusions. This is not a brand‑new phenomenon, nor is it something unique to Russia. We have seen similar low‑level cyber activity, espionage, and other problematic behavior from several states that, in our view, crosses lines that should not be crossed. I conveyed a direct and strong warning that, while we recognize intelligence services will always do some information‑gathering, there is a clear distinction between that and attempts to interfere in elections, or attacks on private groups or businesses. I made clear we are watching this closely and will react in an appropriate way if and when such activities occur.

More broadly, the cyber realm is an area where we urgently need to develop international standards, so that it does not become an unregulated, constant battlefield. Many countries now possess sophisticated cyber capabilities, and given how exposed our modern economies and infrastructures are to digital disruption, we must ensure we don’t drift into a cyber arms race without rules.

We’ve begun to shape some basic principles, which have been taken up by the G‑20, the G‑7, and the United Nations, but there is a lot more work required.

If I may add one more point, I am genuinely struck by how, despite a very rough election campaign, the transition in the United States is proceeding in an orderly fashion in accordance with democratic norms. This is about the American people and their future, and the outgoing administration is actively passing on its experience and knowledge to the incoming team. For us, that is an encouraging sign that the strong partnership between the United States and the Federal Republic of Germany can and will continue, because it serves both sides.

So I will carry on this cooperation, with President‑elect Trump as well, in a spirit of openness and with deep conviction.

On Russia, from a European—and specifically a German—perspective, I can only echo what the President has laid out. The last seven decades of peace in Europe have depended on all states’ borders and sovereignty being respected. Given our continent’s history, abandoning that principle would set us on a very dangerous path, one we must stop at the outset by resisting any such attempts firmly and clearly.

Our focus remains on political solutions. That is why we initiated the Normandy process in close coordination with the United States. From where we stand in Europe—and again, from Germany’s vantage point—I want to stress that Russia is our neighbor. You need only look at Poland to understand how this looks from here. We have every interest in a constructive relationship with Russia. We share a complex history. But that cannot prevent us from speaking plainly about deep disagreements when they arise—always by political and diplomatic means, always in search of negotiated outcomes.

And that is exactly what I will continue to devote myself to.

As for your specific question about whether I will stand as a candidate again, I will address that decision publicly when the time is right—and that time is not today.

question: Do you still intend to move forward with an executive order on birthright citizenship?
interview_answer: You can put that to me again later; I’m not addressing it now. Next question.

question: Is President Putin going to order an attack on Ukraine before this winter is over?
interview_answer:
I've spoken many times with Russian officials and with President Putin himself. I honestly don't believe he has even fully decided what course he's going to take. And he must understand that launching an assault on Ukraine would be an enormous error in judgment. The consequences for Europe and the broader international community would be severe, and he would face a very steep cost for such a move.

I've been extremely direct with President Putin, both over the phone and face to face. We are prepared to enact the toughest economic sanctions ever used, and there will be a serious price to pay over time for any aggression. Those measures would have spillover effects. They would impact us to a degree and they would impact our European partners, but they would hit his economy especially hard.

That said, I cannot tell you what he will ultimately choose to do. He’s now in a position where he could launch an invasion, provided conditions on the ground near Kyiv allow it. But what he will decide, I do not know—and I don’t think anyone but him does.

question: Advance presidential pardons for members of your administration
interview_answer: I'm not going to talk about pardons during this briefing.

question: Is this going to be included?
interview_answer:
I don’t make my complete movie list public. But let’s focus on what we do know right now. Earlier today the FBI made public, and we can now verify, that North Korea was behind this attack. I think it says something quite telling about that regime that it chose to have the state launch a full-scale strike on a film studio over a comedy featuring Seth Rogen and James Franco. They inflicted serious damage, and we will take action in response. Our response will be measured, and it will happen at a time, place, and in a manner of our choosing. That is not something I’m going to spell out here at a press conference.

More generally, this underscores the need to work with other nations to establish clearer rules of the road for how the Internet and cyberspace are used. At the moment it’s essentially a Wild West environment. Part of the challenge is that weaker countries can carry out these kinds of operations, and nonstate groups can also cause massive harm. That’s a big reason why cybersecurity is such an urgent issue.

Once again, this is why it’s critical that Congress partner with us and actually pass legislation that enables the kind of information sharing that’s required. If we don’t build the structures needed to prevent these sorts of intrusions, we’re not just talking about disruptions to entertainment; we’re talking about potentially vast consequences for our whole economy.

And I’ve also heard you’re heading over to Europe. Where are you going to be living?

question: How do you respond to commentators like Novak who claim you’re now more politically alone than Richard Nixon was during Watergate?
interview_answer: What exactly did he mean by ‘isolated’?

question: What is the President’s view of this movement, and what does he want its supporters to hear from him?
interview_answer: Look, I really don’t know much about this movement, other than I’m told they’re very fond of me, which I’m grateful for, but beyond that I don’t know a lot about them. I’ve been told they’re growing fast. And from what I’m hearing, these are folks who, when they see what’s happening on the streets in Portland, when they look at what’s gone on in New York over the last 6 or 7 months—and honestly, this started back when I first got here almost 4 years ago, hard to believe—these are people who are fed up with that in cities like Portland, Chicago, New York, and many other places. I’ve heard they’re very pro‑America, and they just don’t like seeing this chaos. So I really don’t actually know anything concrete about them, except they reportedly support me. And I’m also told they want the problems in these places—especially the ones we’re talking about—to stop. There’s no excuse for Democrats not being able to manage a city. If they won’t, we’ll send in any Federal resources needed—troops, law enforcement, whatever they prefer—and we’ll fix it in 24 hours or less. But as far as the movement itself, I really don’t know anything more than that.

question: What about Michael Gove?
interview_answer: Look, I’ve known Boris for years and I’m very fond of him. I think he’d do an excellent job. I know Jeremy as well, and I believe he’d be excellent too. Michael, I don’t really know. So would Jeremy be good—what do you think? You tell me. All right? All right. Thanks a lot, everyone. Thank you, thank you.

question: Can you update us on President Zelenskyy’s messaging about the major counteroffensive and clarify if it has already begun?
interview_answer: Sure, I’ll lay out the exact time they’re launching and the precise locations they’ll hit. He briefed me—[]—come on now. Bless your heart. Even if I had all that information, do you really think I’d spell out their battlefield plans and describe their offensive for you?
